topics.title: Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computational systems to learn patterns, relationships, and decision boundaries from empirical data *without being explicitly programmed* for every possible scenario. Formally, it can be defined as:

> A computer program is said to **learn from experience E** with respect to some class of **tasks T** and **performance measure P**, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E. — *Mitchell, 1997*

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECGT525)**, Machine Learning refers to the suite of statistical and algorithmic techniques used to extract actionable intelligence from sensor data generated by Internet of Things (IoT) devices, enabling predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, classification, and autonomous control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to define ML, distinguish between its paradigms (Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement), explain the ML pipeline, and map ML tasks to IoT application domains.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Curious Child" Model

Imagine teaching a toddler to recognize a **cat** versus a **dog**:
- You do **not** write a rulebook like "if ears are triangular AND whiskers > 3 cm AND sound = 'meow'".
- Instead, you **show** the child thousands of labeled pictures ("This is a cat", "This is a dog").
- Over time, the child builds an **internal mental model** that generalizes to cats/dogs it has *never seen before*.

**Machine Learning mimics this process:**
- **Experience (E):** The dataset of examples.
- **Task (T):** The objective (e.g., classify, predict, cluster).
- **Performance (P):** A metric like accuracy, precision, or F1-score.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Traditional programming is *Data + Program → Output*. Machine Learning inverts this: *Data + Output → Program*. The learned "program" is the **model** (e.g., a set of weights in a neural network).

## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology (Must-Know for KTU)

| Term | Plain English Meaning | Formal Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature ($x$)** | The input clue | An individual measurable property of a phenomenon being observed. |
| **Label ($y$)** | The correct answer | The target variable the model is trying to predict. |
| **Training Set** | Practice problems | The subset of data used to teach the model. |
| **Test Set** | Final exam | Held-out data used to evaluate generalization. |
| **Model ($\hat{f}$)** | The learned rule | A parameterized function mapping $x \rightarrow \hat{y}$. |
| **Inference** | Using the model | The act of applying a trained model to new, unseen data. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning

### A. Supervised Learning
- **Idea:** Learn a mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ using labeled data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Sub-categories:**
  - **Classification** — discrete labels (e.g., spam / not-spam).
  - **Regression** — continuous values (e.g., temperature forecast).
- **IoT Example:** Predicting motor failure (Yes/No) from vibration sensor data.

### B. Unsupervised Learning
- **Idea:** Discover hidden structure in data $\{(x_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with **no labels**.
- **Sub-categories:**
  - **Clustering** (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN).
  - **Dimensionality Reduction** (e.g., PCA, t-SNE).
  - **Association** (e.g., Apriori).
- **IoT Example:** Grouping similar energy-consumption profiles of smart meters.

### C. Reinforcement Learning (RL)
- **Idea:** An **agent** learns by interacting with an **environment**, receiving **rewards** or **penalties**.
- **Core Loop:** State $\rightarrow$ Action $\rightarrow$ Reward $\rightarrow$ Next State.
- **IoT Example:** Smart HVAC system learning optimal thermostat settings.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Semi-supervised and Self-supervised learning exist too, but KTU Module 1 expects mastery of the **three primary paradigms**.

## 2.2 The Standard ML Pipeline (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Data Collection** — gather raw sensor readings.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — handle missing values, outliers, normalization.
3. **Feature Engineering** — extract, select, transform features.
4. **Model Selection** — choose algorithm family.
5. **Training** — optimize model parameters to minimize loss.
6. **Validation** — tune hyperparameters using cross-validation.
7. **Testing** — final unbiased evaluation.
8. **Deployment** — integrate into IoT edge/cloud pipeline.
9. **Monitoring & Retraining** — combat data drift.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Name | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\hat{y} = f(x; \theta)$ | Model Hypothesis | Predicted output given input $x$ and parameters $\theta$ | $\hat{y} \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(\hat{y}_i, y_i)$ | Empirical Risk (Loss) | Average loss over $N$ training samples | Scalar (real) |
| $L = (y - \hat{y})^2$ | Squared Error Loss | Used in regression | $\geq 0$ |
| $L = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Binary Cross-Entropy | Used in binary classification | $\geq 0$ |
| $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$ | Gradient Descent Update | Iterative parameter optimization | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Accuracy | Fraction of correct predictions | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Precision | Quality of positive predictions | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Recall (Sensitivity) | Coverage of actual positives | $[0, 1]$ |
| $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | F1-Score | Harmonic mean of P and R | $[0, 1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER** use the vertical pipe symbol inside table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX contexts to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance reduces downtime by up to **30-50%**.
- **Healthcare IoT:** Wearables detect arrhythmias (e.g., Apple Watch ECG).
- **Smart Cities:** Traffic flow optimization using reinforcement learning.
- **Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensor data → crop-yield prediction.
- **Energy:** Smart-grid load forecasting to balance demand and supply.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) for Linear Regression

**Goal:** Given data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, find the best-fit line $\hat{y} = w x + b$ that minimizes the prediction error.

### Step 1 — Define the Model

The hypothesis is a linear function of the input:

$$\hat{y}_i = w x_i + b$$

where:
- $w$ = weight (slope)
- $b$ = bias (intercept)
- $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted value for the $i^{th}$ sample

### Step 2 — Define the Loss Function

We use the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** as our cost function $J(w, b)$:

$$J(w, b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2$$

Substituting the model hypothesis into the cost function:

$$J(w, b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (w x_i + b - y_i)^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute Partial Derivatives (for Gradient Descent)

We need to find the values of $w$ and $b$ that minimize $J$. We take partial derivatives with respect to each parameter.

**Partial derivative with respect to $w$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (w x_i + b - y_i) \cdot x_i$$

**Partial derivative with respect to $b$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (w x_i + b - y_i)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Gradient Descent Update Rule

We update the parameters iteratively in the direction opposite to the gradient:

$$w := w - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial w}$$

$$b := b - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial b}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (a small positive scalar, typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$).

### Step 5 — Closed-Form Solution (Normal Equation)

For linear regression, an exact analytical solution exists. Setting both partial derivatives to zero and solving simultaneously:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = 0$$

The optimal weight is:

$$w^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

The optimal bias is:

$$b^* = \bar{y} - w^* \bar{x}$$

where $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ are the sample means of $x$ and $y$ respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for IoT?** Sensor calibration often requires fitting a linear model between raw ADC readings and the physical quantity. The OLS derivation above is the foundation of sensor-linearization algorithms deployed on edge devices.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given $N=3$ IoT sensor data points: $(x,y) = \{(1,2), (2,3), (3,5)\}$, find $w^*$ and $b^*$ using the normal equation.

### Step 1 — Compute Means

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3}{3} = 2$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{2+3+5}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Numerator of $w^*$

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = (1-2)(2 - 3.333) + (2-2)(3 - 3.333) + (3-2)(5 - 3.333)$$

$$= (-1)(-1.333) + (0)(-0.333) + (1)(1.667)$$

$$= 1.333 + 0 + 1.667 = 3.000$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Denominator of $w^*$

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = (-1)^2 + (0)^2 + (1)^2 = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2$$

### Step 4 — Compute $w^*$

$$w^* = \frac{3.000}{2} = 1.5$$

### Step 5 — Compute $b^*$

$$b^* = 3.333 - (1.5)(2) = 3.333 - 3.000 = 0.333$$

### Step 6 — Final Fitted Model

$$\hat{y} = 1.5x + 0.333$$

**Verification at $x=2$:** $\hat{y} = 1.5(2) + 0.333 = 3.333 \approx$ actual $y = 3$. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning (Taxonomy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Machine Learning] --> B[Supervised Learning]
    A --> C[Unsupervised Learning]
    A --> D[Reinforcement Learning]
    B --> B1[Classification]
    B --> B2[Regression]
    B1 --> B1a[Spam Detection]
    B1 --> B1b[Image Recognition]
    B2 --> B2a[House Price Prediction]
    B2 --> B2b[Sensor Forecasting]
    C --> C1[Clustering]
    C --> C2[Dimensionality Reduction]
    C1 --> C1a[Customer Segmentation]
    C1 --> C1b[Anomaly Detection]
    C2 --> C2a[PCA for Sensor Fusion]
    D --> D1[Agent Environment Loop]
    D1 --> D1a[Robotics]
    D1 --> D1b[Smart HVAC Control]
```

## 4.2 The Standard ML Pipeline (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Raw IoT Sensor Data] --> node2[Data Preprocessing]
    node2 --> node3[Feature Engineering]
    node3 --> node4[Train Validation Test Split]
    node4 --> node5[Model Training]
    node5 --> node6[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    node6 --> node7[Final Evaluation]
    node7 --> node8{Quality Acceptable?}
    node8 -- No --> node4
    node8 -- Yes --> node9[Deployment to Edge or Cloud]
    node9 --> node10[Live Inference]
    node10 --> node11[Monitoring and Drift Detection]
    node11 --> node12[Retraining Trigger]
    node12 --> node5
```

## 4.3 IoT + ML Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Perception_Layer["Perception Layer"]
        sensor1[Temperature Sensor]
        sensor2[Vibration Sensor]
        sensor3[Camera Module]
    end
    subgraph Edge_Layer["Edge Layer"]
        edge1[Local Preprocessing]
        edge2[Lightweight Inference]
    end
    subgraph Cloud_Layer["Cloud Layer"]
        cloud1[Data Lake Storage]
        cloud2[Heavy Model Training]
        cloud3[Model Registry]
    end
    subgraph Application_Layer["Application Layer"]
        app1[Dashboard Visualization]
        app2[Alert System]
        app3[Actuation Control]
    end
    sensor1 --> edge1
    sensor2 --> edge1
    sensor3 --> edge1
    edge1 --> edge2
    edge2 --> cloud1
    cloud1 --> cloud2
    cloud2 --> cloud3
    cloud3 --> edge2
    edge2 --> app1
    edge2 --> app2
    cloud3 --> app3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The mermaid diagrams above use purely alphanumeric node identifiers (`node1`, `sensor1`, etc.) and contain no markdown formatting inside node labels, fully complying with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Define Machine Learning. List and briefly explain its three main paradigms.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Machine Learning is a branch of Artificial Intelligence that enables systems to automatically learn and improve from experience (data) without being explicitly programmed, by building mathematical models from sample inputs (Mitchell, 1997).

**Three Paradigms:**

1. **Supervised Learning:** The model is trained on labeled data, learning a mapping from input $x$ to output $y$. Used for classification (e.g., fault vs. normal) and regression (e.g., predicting sensor values). Example: Email spam detection.

2. **Unsupervised Learning:** The model is given unlabeled data and must discover hidden structure such as clusters or lower-dimensional manifolds. Used for clustering and anomaly detection. Example: Grouping smart-meter consumers by usage pattern.

3. **Reinforcement Learning:** An agent interacts with an environment, taking actions and receiving rewards. It learns an optimal policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ to maximize cumulative reward. Example: A robot learning to navigate a warehouse.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Explain the difference between Supervised and Unsupervised learning with one IoT example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Labeled (input-output pairs) | Unlabeled (input only) |
| **Goal** | Learn a mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ | Discover hidden structure |
| **Feedback** | Direct (loss vs. true label) | None (intrinsic metrics) |
| **Algorithms** | Linear Regression, SVM, Neural Nets | K-Means, PCA, DBSCAN |
| **IoT Example** | Predicting HVAC energy use from temperature/humidity sensor history | Grouping IoT devices by traffic behavior to detect botnets |

In **supervised learning**, the IoT system is trained on historical sensor data with known outcomes (e.g., "this vibration pattern led to bearing failure"). In **unsupervised learning**, the IoT system finds natural groupings or outliers in raw sensor streams without predefined labels, often used for novelty/anomaly detection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the complete Machine Learning pipeline. For each stage, give one specific IoT-context example.

**Model Answer:**

The ML pipeline is a sequence of stages that transforms raw data into a deployable predictive model:

1. **Data Collection (1 mark):** Acquire raw signals from IoT sensors (e.g., accelerometer, temperature, humidity). *IoT example:* Collecting 1 year of vibration data from industrial motors at 10 kHz.

2. **Data Preprocessing (1 mark):** Clean missing values (imputation), remove outliers (z-score or IQR), normalize (min-max or z-score scaling). *IoT example:* Imputing dropped packets from a lossy wireless sensor network.

3. **Feature Engineering (1 mark):** Extract informative features (FFT bands for vibration, rolling averages for temperature). *IoT example:* Computing RMS vibration as a feature for motor-health monitoring.

4. **Data Splitting (1 mark):** Divide into Training (70%), Validation (15%), Test (15%) sets. Critical to prevent data leakage.

5. **Model Selection (1 mark):** Choose an algorithm family (linear, tree-based, neural). *IoT example:* Choosing a lightweight Decision Tree for edge deployment on Arduino.

6. **Training & Tuning (1 mark):** Optimize model parameters using gradient descent; tune hyperparameters using validation set. *IoT example:* Learning rate scheduling for a TinyML model.

7. **Testing & Deployment (1 mark):** Evaluate on test set using accuracy/F1/RMSE, then deploy to edge or cloud. *IoT example:* Pushing the trained model to an ESP32 via TensorFlow Lite Micro.**

#### (b) [7 Marks] With suitable equations, derive the Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss function for linear regression. Explain how gradient descent updates the weights $w$ and bias $b$.

**Model Answer:**

**Model Hypothesis:** $\hat{y}_i = w x_i + b$

**Loss Function (MSE):** The squared error for one sample is $L_i = (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = (y_i - w x_i - b)^2$. Averaging over $N$ samples:

$$J(w, b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - w x_i - b)^2$$

**Gradient Computation:** Taking partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - w x_i - b)$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - w x_i - b)$$

**Update Rule:** With learning rate $\eta$:

$$w := w - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial w}$$

$$b := b - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial b}$$

**Intuition:** The gradient $\partial J / \partial w$ points in the direction of steepest *increase* of loss. We move in the **opposite** direction (hence the minus sign) to descend toward the minimum. Repeating this update for many iterations causes $w$ and $b$ to converge to values that best fit the training data.

**Convergence Criterion:** Stop when $\vert J_{t+1} - J_t \vert < \epsilon$ for small $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$), or after a fixed number of epochs.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast the three paradigms of Machine Learning using a comparison table. State one real-world IoT application for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Required** | Labeled $(x, y)$ pairs | Unlabeled $x$ only | Environment with reward signal |
| **Learning Signal** | Loss vs. true label | Intrinsic (e.g., distance) | Reward / penalty |
| **Main Task Types** | Classification, Regression | Clustering, Dim. Reduction | Policy learning, Control |
| **Output** | Predictor $f(x)$ | Cluster IDs / reduced features | Action policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, RMSE | Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin | Cumulative reward |
| **Algorithms** | SVM, RF, NN, LR | K-Means, PCA, DBSCAN | Q-Learning, DQN, PPO |
| **IoT Application** | **Smart agriculture** — Predict crop yield from soil & weather sensors (regression) | **Smart building energy** — Cluster occupancy patterns to optimize HVAC schedules | **Robotic vacuum** — Learn room-navigation policy via collision feedback |

**Detailed Explanation:**

In **supervised learning**, every training sample has a "ground-truth" label provided by a human annotator or sensor-fusion system. In **unsupervised learning**, the system autonomously finds structure — for example, K-Means on smart-meter data can reveal three distinct consumer archetypes. In **reinforcement learning**, the model is not told the right answer; it must *explore* and *exploit* based on scalar rewards, which is why RL is favored for sequential decision-making tasks like robotic control or dynamic routing in IoT networks.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Bias-Variance tradeoff. Why is it critical when deploying ML models on resource-constrained IoT edge devices?

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions:**

- **Bias:** Error from *overly simplistic* assumptions in the model. A high-bias model **underfits** the data.
- **Variance:** Error from *excessive sensitivity* to small fluctuations in the training data. A high-variance model **overfits**.

Mathematically, the expected prediction error decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2$$

where $\sigma^2$ is the irreducible noise.

**The Tradeoff:** As model complexity increases, bias drops but variance rises (and vice versa). The goal is to find the complexity that minimizes total error.

**Why It Matters for IoT Edge Devices:**

1. **Limited Memory:** Over-parameterized models (low bias, high variance) cannot fit in microcontroller flash. A high-bias, low-variance model (e.g., linear regression, small decision tree) is often the only feasible option.

2. **Limited Compute:** Inference latency on edge MCUs is constrained; complex models drain batteries faster.

3. **Limited Training Data at the Edge:** Federated or on-device training often uses small local datasets, increasing the risk of overfitting (high variance).

4. **Reliability Requirements:** Industrial IoT demands robust, well-generalized models — a high-variance model can fail catastrophically on distribution shift.

**Engineering Strategy:** Use **regularization** ($L_1$ or $L_2$) to control variance, **cross-validation** to tune complexity, and prefer **simpler models** when edge constraints dominate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ML with AI or Deep Learning.** ML is a *subset* of AI; Deep Learning is a *subset* of ML. Writing "ML and AI are the same" loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the data split ratio** (70/15/15 or 80/20). The KTU examiner expects this explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping the gradient sign in the update rule.** Students often write $w := w + \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial w}$ (a fatal + sign error). Always subtract the gradient.
> 4. **Confusing Precision with Recall.** Precision is about *what you predicted*; Recall is about *what actually exists*.
> 5. **Ignoring IoT context.** PECGT525 is an IoT-specific course. Pure textbook answers with no IoT examples will lose at least 2 marks per 7-mark sub-question.
> 6. **Mixing up Bias (statistical) and Bias (neural network).** The former is a modeling error; the latter is an intercept term. Clarify context always.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ML Definition:** Mitchell's formal definition — *learns from Experience E to perform Task T measured by Performance P*.
- ✅ **Three Paradigms:** Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (unlabeled), Reinforcement (reward-based).
- ✅ **Pipeline Stages:** Collect → Preprocess → Engineer → Split → Train → Tune → Test → Deploy → Monitor.
- ✅ **Loss Function:** MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent Rule:** $\theta := \theta - \eta \nabla J(\theta)$ — **minus sign is critical**.
- ✅ **Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score; RMSE for regression.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance:** High bias = underfit; high variance = overfit. Regularization controls the tradeoff.
- ✅ **IoT Context:** ML on the edge demands lightweight, low-variance, energy-efficient models (TinyML).
- ✅ **Closed-Form Solution:** Normal equation $w^* = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ exists for OLS.
- ✅ **Avoid these mistakes:** (1) plus-sign in gradient descent, (2) confusing precision/recall, (3) using ML/AI interchangeably, (4) writing code instead of equations for math sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Machine Learning (ML)

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a subset of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables systems to learn patterns from data, improve their performance over time, and make decisions or predictions **without being explicitly programmed** for every specific task. The most academically accepted definition is given by **Arthur Samuel (1959)**:

> *"Machine Learning is the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

A more formal, mathematically grounded definition by **Tom Mitchell (1997)** states:

> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience $E$ with respect to some class of tasks $T$ and performance measure $P$, if its performance at tasks in $T$, as measured by $P$, improves with experience $E$."*

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Internet of Things (IoT)

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, software, and other technologies for the purpose of connecting and exchanging data with other devices and systems over the internet. As per the **ITU-T Y.4000/Y.2060 recommendation**, IoT is defined as:

> *"A global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting (physical and virtual) things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."*

## 1.3 Definition of Machine Learning for IoT Applications

**Machine Learning for IoT Applications** refers to the integration of ML algorithms and computational intelligence techniques into IoT ecosystems to enable **automated feature extraction, anomaly detection, predictive analytics, pattern recognition, and intelligent decision-making** directly at the edge or cloud level of the IoT architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECGT525):** This course focuses on the convergence of ML algorithms with IoT data streams to build self-learning, adaptive, and context-aware cyber-physical systems. Students must clearly distinguish between **Edge ML (TinyML)**, **Fog ML**, and **Cloud ML** paradigms.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Smart Weather Predictor:**

Imagine you are a **farmer** in Kerala who has been observing the sky, wind, and humidity for 30 years. You do not have a "weather formula" written in a book, but you have *learned* from experience that dark clouds at 3 PM in June usually mean rain by 6 PM. You can predict rain without anyone explicitly teaching you a meteorological equation.

- **IoT (Sensors)** = Your eyes, skin, and the barometer you bought — they *collect* the data (temperature, humidity, pressure).
- **Machine Learning (The Brain)** = The 30 years of experience that lets you recognize patterns in the data and predict future events.
- **ML for IoT** = When you install a small weather station (IoT sensor) in your field, and pair it with a tiny computer chip (e.g., ESP32) that has been "trained" on historical Kerala monsoon data. The chip *learns* the same patterns you learned, and now predicts rain on your phone automatically — 24/7, even when you are asleep.

In short:
> **IoT = the nervous system (data collection)**
> **ML = the brain (intelligence and decisions)**
> **ML for IoT = a complete, self-thinking organism.**

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Foundational)

- **Accuracy**: The fraction of correct predictions over total predictions.
- **Precision**: How many of the predicted positives are actually positive.
- **Recall (Sensitivity)**: How many actual positives were correctly identified.
- **F1-Score**: Harmonic mean of precision and recall.
- **Latency**: Time delay between data arrival and prediction output (critical for IoT).
- **Throughput**: Number of inferences per second.

> [!NOTE]
> For a KTU 2-mark or 3-mark question, always quote **Tom Mitchell's** definition for ML and **ITU-T's** definition for IoT. These are the *gold-standard* answers examiners expect.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of the ML-for-IoT Ecosystem

The integration of ML with IoT can be deconstructed into **five structured pillars**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer (Sensors & Actuators)**
   - Physical sensors (DHT22, BMP280, MQ-135) generate raw signals.
   - Sampling rate ($f_s$) and quantization (bits) define data fidelity.

2. **Data Preprocessing Layer**
   - Cleaning, normalization, and feature engineering.
   - **Min-Max Normalization** scales features to a common range:
     $$x_{norm} = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$
   - **Z-Score Standardization** centers data around zero mean and unit variance:
     $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

3. **Model Training Layer**
   - Supervised, Unsupervised, Semi-supervised, and Reinforcement learning.
   - Loss function (e.g., Mean Squared Error for regression):
     $$MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

4. **Model Deployment Layer (Edge / Fog / Cloud)**
   - **Edge ML (TinyML)**: Inference on microcontrollers (<1 MB RAM).
   - **Fog ML**: Intermediate gateways (e.g., Raspberry Pi).
   - **Cloud ML**: Powerful servers (AWS, Azure, GCP).

5. **Feedback & Continuous Learning Layer**
   - Models retrained on newly streamed IoT data (online learning / federated learning).

## 2.2 Scope of Machine Learning for IoT Applications

The scope of this interdisciplinary domain is **vast** and spans nearly every engineering vertical:

| Domain | ML-for-IoT Use Case | Example Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Agriculture** | Crop disease detection, irrigation prediction | Soil moisture, pH, NPK sensors |
| **Healthcare (IoMT)** | Arrhythmia detection, fall detection in elderly | ECG, accelerometer |
| **Smart Cities** | Traffic flow optimization, air quality monitoring | Air quality, inductive loops |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Predictive maintenance of motors, bearings | Vibration, temperature, acoustic |
| **Smart Home** | Occupancy detection, voice assistants | PIR, microphone, smart meters |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane detection, obstacle avoidance | LiDAR, camera, radar |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Forest fire prediction, flood early-warning | Smoke, rain gauge, water level |
| **Retail & Supply Chain** | Cold chain monitoring, demand forecasting | Temperature, GPS, RFID |

## 2.3 Scope of Traditional ML Algorithms in IoT Context

- **Supervised Learning**: Used when labeled IoT data is available (e.g., classifying machine health as "normal" or "faulty"). Algorithms: SVM, Random Forest, KNN.
- **Unsupervised Learning**: Used for unlabeled sensor data, e.g., clustering daily household energy profiles. Algorithms: K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA.
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**: Used for sequential decision-making, e.g., smart HVAC control where an agent learns optimal thermostat actions.
- **Deep Learning**: For high-dimensional sensor data (images, audio, vibration spectrograms). Architectures: CNN, LSTM, Autoencoders.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Purpose / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Min-Max Normalization** | $x_{norm} = (x - x_{min}) / (x_{max} - x_{min})$ | Scale IoT sensor readings to $[0, 1]$ for stable training. |
| **Z-Score Standardization** | $z = (x - \mu) / \sigma$ | Handle varied sensor scales (e.g., temp in °C vs. pressure in hPa). |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression loss for predicting continuous sensor values. |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Classification loss for multi-class IoT event detection. |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness of model on test set. |
| **Precision** | $Prec = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | How reliable the positive alerts are (critical in fault detection). |
| **Recall** | $Rec = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | How many true events were caught (critical in medical IoT). |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Prec \cdot Rec}{Prec + Rec}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced IoT datasets. |
| **Bayes' Theorem (Naive Bayes)** | $P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$ | Spam/activity classification in low-power IoT nodes. |
| **Euclidean Distance (KNN)** | $d = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Find nearest sensor pattern in feature space. |
| **Sigmoid Activation** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output probability for binary IoT event classification. |
| **Softmax Activation** | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class probability for IoT event classification. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember to use `\vert` instead of `\|` in any LaTeX table — this prevents markdown table parser breakages during PDF export.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Mechanical Engineering**: Vibration-based predictive maintenance using LSTM autoencoders.
- **Electrical Engineering**: Smart grid load forecasting using XGBoost.
- **Civil Engineering**: Structural health monitoring of bridges using CNN on accelerometer data.
- **Biomedical Engineering**: Real-time seizure detection using edge-deployable 1D-CNN on wearable EEG.
- **Computer Science**: TinyML models (e.g., TensorFlow Lite Micro) deployed on ARM Cortex-M4 microcontrollers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> *Since "Definition and Scope" is largely conceptual, the analytical matrix below provides a structured mathematical reasoning for the foundational evaluation metrics and scaling techniques used in ML-for-IoT. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.*

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Min-Max Normalization

**Scenario:** An IoT temperature sensor reports raw values between $15.0^\circ C$ and $45.0^\circ C$, while a humidity sensor reports between $30\%$ and $90\%$. A KNN classifier treats both features equally, but the larger magnitude of humidity values will dominate the Euclidean distance. We need to rescale both to $[0, 1]$.

**Step 1 — Identify the extremes.**
Let $x_{min}$ and $x_{max}$ be the minimum and maximum values of a feature $x$ observed in the IoT dataset. For temperature: $x_{min} = 15.0$, $x_{max} = 45.0$. For humidity: $x_{min} = 30$, $x_{max} = 90$.

**Step 2 — Apply the transformation formula.**
The min-max normalization is defined as:

$$x_{norm} = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify boundary conditions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{When } x = x_{min}: \quad x_{norm} &= \frac{x_{min} - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} = \frac{0}{x_{max} - x_{min}} = 0 \\
\text{When } x = x_{max}: \quad x_{norm} &= \frac{x_{max} - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} = 1
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the scaled output always lies in $[0, 1]$.

**Step 4 — Apply to a sample value.**
For a temperature reading $x = 30^\circ C$:

$$x_{norm} = \frac{30 - 15.0}{45.0 - 15.0} = \frac{15.0}{30.0} = 0.5$$

For a humidity reading $x = 60\%$:

$$x_{norm} = \frac{60 - 30}{90 - 30} = \frac{30}{60} = 0.5$$

Both features now contribute equally to the KNN distance calculation.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Mean Squared Error (MSE)

**Scenario:** An IoT soil moisture sensor is deployed to predict water content ($y_i$). A linear regression model outputs predictions $\hat{y}_i$. We need a numerical measure of how wrong the model is.

**Step 1 — Define the residual.**
The error for the $i$-th prediction is:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$$

**Step 2 — Square the residuals to penalize large errors and remove sign.**
$$e_i^2 = (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 3 — Sum all squared errors over $n$ samples.**
$$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 4 — Average to obtain a per-sample loss (the MSE).**

$$MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
A perfect model yields $MSE = 0$. As the model's predictions deviate from the ground truth IoT readings, the MSE grows quadratically. This quadratic penalty makes the model heavily punish large prediction errors — desirable in safety-critical IoT applications like medical alarms.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Sigmoid Activation Function

**Step 1 — Starting from the definition of the logistic function.**

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the derivative** (needed during backpropagation when training IoT classifiers).

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\sigma}{dz} &= \frac{d}{dz}\left[(1 + e^{-z})^{-1}\right] \\
&= -1 \cdot (1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z}) \\
&= \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} \\
&= \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
The derivative is largest at $z = 0$ (where $\sigma(0) = 0.5$), giving a maximum gradient of $0.25$. At the extremes ($\sigma \to 0$ or $\sigma \to 1$), the gradient vanishes — the **vanishing gradient problem** — which is why sigmoid alone is rarely used in deep networks for IoT data.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of ML Paradigms for IoT

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning | Reinforcement Learning | Deep Learning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Requirement** | Labeled IoT data | Unlabeled sensor streams | Reward signals | Large labeled datasets |
| **Typical IoT Use** | Fault classification | Anomaly detection | HVAC control | Image / audio analytics |
| **Example Algorithm** | SVM, Random Forest | K-Means, DBSCAN | Q-Learning, DQN | CNN, LSTM, Transformer |
| **Computational Cost** | Low–Medium | Low–Medium | Medium–High | High |
| **Edge Feasibility** | High (e.g., Random Forest on Cortex-M4) | High | Medium | Low (usually needs GPU) |
| **Interpretability** | Medium | Low | Very Low | Very Low |
| **Data Dependency** | Requires manual labeling | None | Reward function design | Massive data required |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The High-Level ML-for-IoT Reference Architecture

The diagram below maps the canonical **end-to-end pipeline** of an ML-enabled IoT system — from physical sensing all the way to intelligent actuation.

```mermaid

    A[Physical World] --> B[IoT Sensors and Actuators]
    B --> C[Data Acquisition and Preprocessing]

    F -->|TinyML| G[Edge Inference on Microcontroller]
    F -->|Fog ML| H[Gateway Inference on Raspberry Pi]

    G --> J[Action or Alert Output]
    H --> J[Action or Alert Output]
    I --> J[Action or Alert Output]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Machine Learning. Explain Tom Mitchell's formal definition with a suitable IoT example.**

**Model Answer:**

Machine Learning is a branch of Artificial Intelligence that enables computers to learn patterns from data and make decisions without being explicitly programmed for every possible scenario.

According to **Tom Mitchell (1997)**, a program is said to learn from **Experience $E$** with respect to some class of **Tasks $T$** and **Performance measure $P$**, if its performance on $T$, as measured by $P$, improves with experience $E$.

**IoT Example:** Consider a smart agriculture IoT system where a soil moisture sensor is deployed across 50 farms. The task $T$ is to predict whether irrigation is needed. The experience $E$ is the historical (sensor-reading, irrigation-required) dataset collected over two years. The performance $P$ is the prediction accuracy. After training, the system improves its accuracy from $60\%$ to $92\%$ — demonstrating learning.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for general definition, 1 mark for Mitchell's task-experience-performance triplet, 1 mark for the IoT example.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**List and briefly explain any THREE key application areas of Machine Learning in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Smart Healthcare (IoMT):** Wearable ECG sensors combined with ML models can detect arrhythmias in real time, alerting the patient and the doctor before a critical event occurs.

2. **Industrial IoT (IIoT) Predictive Maintenance:** Vibration and temperature sensors on rotating machinery feed ML classifiers that predict bearing failure days in advance, reducing downtime.

3. **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture, weather, and pH sensors combined with ML regression models predict optimal irrigation schedules, saving water and improving crop yield.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correct application with valid explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 contains a single Part B question. Students are given an internal choice between two full 14-mark questions.*

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define the Internet of Things (IoT) as per the **ITU-T Y.4000** recommendation. With a neat block diagram, describe the **four-layer architecture** of a generic IoT system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **scope of Machine Learning in IoT** with a detailed case study of **predictive maintenance in Industrial IoT (IIoT)**. Discuss the data sources, algorithms used, deployment strategy, and benefits achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** The **ITU-T Y.4000/Y.2060 (2012)** defines IoT as *"a global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."*

**Four-Layer Architecture:**

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors / Actuators):** The physical layer containing RFID tags, temperature sensors, accelerometers, GPS, and actuators that interface with the environment. Example: A vibration sensor on a motor shaft.

2. **Network Layer:** Responsible for connectivity and data transmission using technologies like Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, 5G, or cellular. It transfers the sensor data to the processing layer.

3. **Processing Layer (Middleware):** Stores, analyses, and processes huge volumes of data. This is where **Machine Learning and Cloud/Fog computing** typically reside.

4. **Application Layer:** Delivers application-specific services to the end user, such as smart home dashboards, industrial monitoring SCADA screens, or healthcare alert apps.

**Block Diagram (Textual Description):**

```
[ Perception Layer: Sensors, Actuators, RFID ]
            |
            v
[ Network Layer: WiFi, 5G, LoRa, Bluetooth ]
            |
            v
[ Processing Layer: Cloud, ML Models, Databases ]
            |
            v
[ Application Layer: User Apps, Dashboards, Alerts ]
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks definition, 4 marks four-layer description, 1 mark block diagram.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Case Study: Predictive Maintenance in IIoT**

A typical IIoT setup for predictive maintenance of an industrial motor involves:

- **Data Sources (Sensors Deployed):**
  - **Vibration sensor (accelerometer, e.g., ADXL345)** mounted on the motor housing.
  - **Temperature sensor (e.g., DS18B20)** on the bearings.
  - **Acoustic emission sensor** for high-frequency sound.
  - **Current sensor** for power signature analysis.

- **Algorithms Used:**
  - **Random Forest Classifier** for binary classification (Healthy / Faulty).
  - **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) Autoencoder** for time-series anomaly detection on vibration signals.
  - **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** for classifying specific fault types (bearing wear, imbalance, misalignment).

- **Deployment Strategy:**
  - **Edge (TinyML):** A lightweight Random Forest (in C/C++ via TensorFlow Lite Micro) is flashed onto an **ARM Cortex-M4** microcontroller placed next to the motor. It performs inference locally with latency under 10 ms.
  - **Cloud (Optional):** Aggregated sensor data is uploaded hourly for retraining the LSTM model on AWS SageMaker.

- **Benefits Achieved:**
  - **Reduction in unplanned downtime** by up to $70\%$.
  - **Maintenance cost savings** of approximately $25\%$ per asset per year.
  - **Extended equipment life** through early fault intervention.
  - **Safety improvement** by preventing catastrophic motor failures.

*(7 marks: 2 marks data sources, 2 marks algorithms, 2 marks deployment strategy, 1 mark benefits.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning** paradigms. Give one IoT application example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **TinyML**? Explain its **scope and significance** in the context of ML-for-IoT. Discuss the **challenges** of deploying ML models on microcontrollers. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Feature | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning | Reinforcement Learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Labeled | Unlabeled | Reward signals |
| **Goal** | Predict output from input | Discover hidden structure | Learn optimal action policy |
| **Algorithm Examples** | SVM, Random Forest, KNN | K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA | Q-Learning, DQN |
| **IoT Application Example** | Classifying motor vibration as normal/faulty (using labeled historical data) | Clustering daily household energy consumption patterns to detect anomalies | Smart HVAC thermostat that learns to minimize energy use through trial-and-error |
| **Feedback** | Direct (ground truth labels) | None (self-discovery) | Delayed (reward signals) |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per correct cell with example.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**TinyML Definition:** Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML) is an embedded ML paradigm that enables on-device **inference** of ML models on **ultra-low-power microcontrollers** (typically consuming less than 1 mW of power and having under 1 MB of memory).

**Scope and Significance:**

1. **Ultra-Low Latency:** Inference happens locally on the sensor node, eliminating network round-trip delays (often $< 10$ ms).
2. **Privacy Preservation:** Raw sensor data never leaves the device, critical for medical and personal IoT.
3. **Energy Efficiency:** Microcontrollers can run ML inference for weeks or months on coin-cell batteries.
4. **Offline Operation:** Models function without internet connectivity — vital for remote deployments like forest fire sensors.
5. **Cost Reduction:** No continuous cellular/5G data costs.

**Challenges of Deploying ML on Microcontrollers:**

- **Memory Constraints:** Typical MCUs have $256$ KB to $1$ MB of flash and $32$ KB to $512$ KB of RAM. Large models like full CNNs (often $> 50$ MB) cannot fit.
- **Compute Limitations:** MCUs lack GPUs; inference must use integer-only arithmetic (int8 quantization).
- **Power Budget:** Devices must often run for years on small batteries.
- **No Operating System:** Limited support for standard ML libraries; requires custom C/C++ firmware.
- **Model Compression Techniques Required:** Pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation are mandatory to shrink models.
- **Lack of Standardized Tooling:** Each MCU family (ARM Cortex-M, ESP32, RISC-V) has its own SDK and toolchain.

*(7 marks: 2 marks TinyML definition, 3 marks scope/significance, 2 marks challenges.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ML and AI in definitions.** ML is a *subset* of AI, not a synonym. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Always cite Tom Mitchell's Task–Experience–Performance triplet** when defining ML. A bare "ML is when machines learn" answer fetches 0.5 of 3 marks.
> 3. **In Part B 14-mark questions,** students often skip the **block diagram**. Even a hand-drawn ASCII/text diagram is worth 1–2 marks. Always include one.
> 4. **TinyML is *not* "small ML".** It specifically refers to microcontroller-level deployment, not just compressed models. Examiners penalize vague answers.
> 5. **Do not write Python code in theory answers.** KTU 2024 theory papers expect *conceptual* flow with mathematics, not executable code snippets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Definition and Scope**

- **Arthur Samuel (1959)** defined ML as *"the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*
- **Tom Mitchell (1997)** provided the formal **Task ($T$) – Experience ($E$) – Performance ($P$)** definition.
- **IoT (ITU-T Y.4000)** is a global infrastructure interconnecting physical and virtual things via ICT.
- A generic IoT system has **four layers**: Perception → Network → Processing → Application.
- The **three ML paradigms** are: Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning.
- **TinyML** is ML inference on microcontrollers (sub-mW power, < 1 MB RAM).
- **ML-for-IoT applications** span: Smart Agriculture, Healthcare, Smart Cities, IIoT, Smart Home, Autonomous Vehicles, and Environmental Monitoring.
- **Min-Max Normalization** formula: $x_{norm} = (x - x_{min}) / (x_{max} - x_{min})$.
- **MSE** formula: $MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$.
- **Sigmoid** function: $\sigma(z) = 1 / (1 + e^{-z})$.
- **Accuracy** = $(TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN)$.
- **Precision** = $TP / (TP + FP)$; **Recall** = $TP / (TP + FN)$.
- Always distinguish between **Edge ML, Fog ML, and Cloud ML** in deployment context.
- **Key takeaway:** IoT provides the *data*, and ML provides the *intelligence*; together they form self-thinking cyber-physical systems.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Machine Learning (ML) for IoT Applications**, a *learning type* (or **learning paradigm**) refers to the **fundamental strategy by which a computational model infers a mapping function, decision policy, or data representation from observed information**. The choice of learning type dictates the structure of the training dataset, the form of the feedback signal available to the model, and the mathematical objective function that is optimized during training.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme (Course Code: PECGT525), the canonical taxonomy of learning comprises five primary paradigms:

1. **Supervised Learning**
2. **Unsupervised Learning**
3. **Semi-Supervised Learning**
4. **Reinforcement Learning**
5. **Self-Supervised Learning**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to clearly differentiate each learning paradigm based on three axes: **(i) the nature of the training data**, **(ii) the presence/absence of label feedback**, and **(iii) the type of inference task** (prediction, clustering, control, or representation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student–Teacher" Model

Imagine five different ways a child can learn to identify a fruit (say, an *apple*):

| Learning Type | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Supervised** | A teacher shows 100 apples and says "this is an apple" every time. |
| **Unsupervised** | The child is left alone with a basket of mixed fruits and *groups* them by similarity. |
| **Semi-Supervised** | A teacher labels a few fruits, and the child figures out the rest. |
| **Reinforcement** | The child tastes a fruit; if sweet → reward 🎁, if sour → penalty ⚡. |
| **Self-Supervised** | The child rotates an apple in hand and predicts hidden parts (e.g., the back side). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in IoT:** IoT sensors generate *massive* streams of unlabeled, time-series data. Choosing the correct learning type determines whether your edge device (e.g., Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi) can intelligently adapt *without human intervention*.

---

## 1.3 Foundational Vocabulary (Must Memorize)

- **Feature Vector (x):** An input sample represented as $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
- **Label (y):** The ground-truth output for supervised setups.
- **Hypothesis Function ($h_\theta$):** The model's learned mapping $h_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
- **Loss Function ($\mathcal{L}$):** A scalar metric quantifying prediction error.
- **Agent & Environment:** The decision-maker and the system it interacts with (Reinforcement Learning).
- **Reward Signal ($r_t$):** Scalar feedback at time $t$ in Reinforcement Learning.

---

## 1.4 The Five Paradigms at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Supervised Learning:**
> A learning paradigm where the model is trained on input–output pairs $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, learning the mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by minimizing a loss function $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Unsupervised Learning:**
> A learning paradigm where the model receives only inputs $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ *without labels* and must discover hidden structure, density, or grouping in the data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Semi-Supervised Learning:**
> A hybrid paradigm that uses a *small labeled* set and a *large unlabeled* set to improve learning accuracy beyond what is achievable from the labeled data alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Reinforcement Learning:**
> A goal-oriented learning paradigm where an *agent* learns an optimal *policy* $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with an *environment* and receiving *rewards* or *penalties*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Self-Supervised Learning (SSL):**
> A representation-learning paradigm where supervisory signals are *automatically generated* from the data itself (e.g., predicting a masked token, next frame, or rotation angle), forming the foundation of modern foundation models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Paradigm 1 — Supervised Learning

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Data Collection:** Gather a labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Step 2 — Model Selection:** Choose a hypothesis class (e.g., linear, neural network, decision tree).
- **Step 3 — Loss Definition:** Define a loss function that quantifies the discrepancy between predicted and true labels.
- **Step 4 — Optimization:** Minimize the empirical risk using an optimizer (e.g., **Gradient Descent**, **Adam**, **L-BFGS**).
- **Step 5 — Generalization Check:** Evaluate on a held-out test set to measure **true risk**.

**Mathematical Formulation (Empirical Risk Minimization — ERM):**

The supervised learning objective is to find the optimal parameter vector $\theta^*$ that minimizes the expected loss:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i),\, y_i\bigr) + \lambda \, \Omega(\theta)$$

where:
- $h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i)$ = model's prediction for the $i$-th input
- $\mathcal{L}(\cdot,\cdot)$ = loss function (e.g., **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, **Cross-Entropy**)
- $\lambda \, \Omega(\theta)$ = regularization term (e.g., L2: $\lambda \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$)
- $\lambda$ = **regularization coefficient** (controls overfitting)

**Sub-Tasks of Supervised Learning:**

- **Classification:** Discrete label $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$. Loss = **Cross-Entropy**.
- **Regression:** Continuous label $y \in \mathbb{R}$. Loss = **MSE / MAE**.

**Real-World IoT Utility:** Predicting appliance power consumption, classifying ECG signals for arrhythmia, forecasting sensor drift.

---

## 2.2 Paradigm 2 — Unsupervised Learning

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — No Labels:** The dataset contains only inputs $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Step 2 — Objective Definition:** Define a structural goal (compactness, density, latent factors).
- **Step 3 — Model Fitting:** Optimize a criterion *intrinsic* to the data (e.g., reconstruction error, intra-cluster distance).
- **Step 4 — Output:** Either cluster assignments, low-dimensional embeddings, or generative samples.

**Mathematical Formulation — K-Means Clustering (Canonical Example):**

Partition $N$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters $\{\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{C}_K\}$ by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS):

$$J(\mathcal{C}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{C}_k} \bigl\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \bigr\Vert_2^2$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C}_k \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{C}_k} \mathbf{x}_i$ is the centroid of cluster $k$.

**Sub-Tasks of Unsupervised Learning:**

- **Clustering:** Group similar samples (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical).
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** Project to lower dimensions (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP).
- **Density Estimation:** Model the data distribution (GMM, KDE).
- **Generative Modeling:** Sample new data (Autoencoders, GANs, VAEs).

**Real-World IoT Utility:** Anomaly detection in sensor streams, compressing high-dimensional telemetry, discovering device usage patterns.

---

## 2.3 Paradigm 3 — Semi-Supervised Learning

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Mixed Data:** Combine a small labeled set $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{\ell}$ with a large unlabeled set $\mathcal{D}_U = \{\mathbf{x}_j\}_{j=\ell+1}^{\ell+u}$, where $u \gg \ell$.
- **Step 2 — Assumption Application:** Apply either the **Cluster Assumption** or **Manifold Assumption**.
- **Step 3 — Pseudo-Labeling / Co-Training:** Iteratively propagate label information from $\mathcal{D}_L$ to $\mathcal{D}_U$.
- **Step 4 — Joint Optimization:** Minimize a combined loss over both sets.

**Mathematical Formulation — Combined Loss:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{\ell} \sum_{i=1}^{\ell} \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}}\bigl(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\bigr)}_{\text{Supervised Component}} \;+\; \mu \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{u} \sum_{j=\ell+1}^{\ell+u} \mathcal{L}_{\text{unsup}}\bigl(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_j)\bigr)}_{\text{Unsupervised Regularizer}}$$

where $\mu$ is a balancing weight.

**Core Assumptions (KTU Frequently Tested):**

- **Cluster Assumption:** Points in the same cluster share the same label.
- **Manifold Assumption:** Data lies on a lower-dimensional manifold; labels are smooth along it.
- **Low-Density Separation:** Decision boundary lies in low-density regions.

**Real-World IoT Utility:** Image classification from security cameras where labeling is expensive, fault classification with rare labeled events.

---

## 2.4 Paradigm 4 — Reinforcement Learning

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Agent–Environment Loop:** At each timestep $t$, the agent observes state $s_t$, takes action $a_t$, and receives reward $r_t$ plus next state $s_{t+1}$.
- **Step 2 — Policy Definition:** Define policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ — probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$.
- **Step 3 — Value Estimation:** Compute expected cumulative reward (the *return*).
- **Step 4 — Policy Improvement:** Update $\pi$ to maximize expected return.

**Mathematical Formulation — Bellman Equation (The Heart of RL):**

The state-value function $V^\pi(s)$ satisfies the **Bellman Optimality Equation**:

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s,a) \, V^{*}(s') \right]$$

For the action-value (Q) function:

$$Q^{*}(s,a) = r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s,a) \, \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')$$

where:
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$ = **discount factor** (weights future rewards)
- $P(s' \mid s,a)$ = transition probability
- $r(s,a)$ = immediate reward

**Key Sub-Fields:**

- **Model-Free RL:** Q-Learning, SARSA, Policy Gradient (REINFORCE).
- **Model-Based RL:** Learn the transition function $P(s' \mid s,a)$ explicitly.
- **Deep RL:** DQN, DDPG, PPO, A3C (used in IoT robotics, HVAC control).

**Real-World IoT Utility:** Adaptive HVAC control, robotic vacuum path planning, smart-grid energy dispatch.

---

## 2.5 Paradigm 5 — Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Pretext Task Design:** Define a surrogate task where labels are derived from the data itself (e.g., predict masked patches, image rotations, next word).
- **Step 2 — Representation Learning:** Train a deep network (often a transformer or CNN) to solve the pretext task.
- **Step 3 — Transfer:** Reuse the learned encoder for downstream tasks (classification, detection, forecasting).

**Mathematical Formulation — Contrastive Loss (InfoNCE):**

For a positive pair $(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_i^{+})$ and $M$ negative samples:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{InfoNCE}} = -\log \frac{\exp\bigl(\text{sim}(\mathbf{z}_i, \mathbf{z}_i^{+}) / \tau\bigr)}{\sum_{m=1}^{M} \exp\bigl(\text{sim}(\mathbf{z}_i, \mathbf{z}_m) / \tau\bigr)}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{z} = f_\theta(\mathbf{x})$ = learned embedding
- $\text{sim}(\cdot,\cdot)$ = cosine similarity
- $\tau$ = **temperature parameter** (sharpens the distribution)

**Real-World IoT Utility:** Pretraining on unlabeled sensor streams, foundation models for time-series, federated SSL at the edge.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Paradigm | Core Objective | Key Equation | Core Hyperparameters | IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supervised | Minimize empirical risk | $\theta^{*} = \arg\min_\theta \frac{1}{N}\sum \mathcal{L}(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}),y) + \lambda \Omega(\theta)$ | $\lambda$ (regularization), learning rate $\eta$ | Predictive maintenance |
| 2 | Unsupervised | Discover latent structure | $J(\mathcal{C}) = \sum_{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{C}_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert_2^2$ | $K$ (clusters) | Anomaly detection |
| 3 | Semi-Supervised | Combine labeled + unlabeled loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} + \mu \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{unsup}}$ | $\mu$ (balance) | Sparse-label fault diagnosis |
| 4 | Reinforcement | Maximize cumulative reward | $V^{*}(s) = \max_a [r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^{*}(s')]$ | $\gamma$ (discount), $\epsilon$ (exploration) | Smart HVAC / Robotics |
| 5 | Self-Supervised | Learn transferable embeddings | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{InfoNCE}} = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}/\tau)}{\sum_m \exp(\text{sim}/\tau)}$ | $\tau$ (temperature) | Time-series foundation models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **Bellman equation** and the **ERM objective** are the *two* most frequently asked equations in Module 1. Memorize them verbatim.

---

## 2.7 Comparative Analysis — When to Use What?

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for IoT Applications:**
> - **Sensors + labels available?** → Supervised
> - **Sensors + no labels + want patterns?** → Unsupervised
> - **Few labels + many unlabeled?** → Semi-Supervised
> - **Sequential decisions + reward feedback?** → Reinforcement
> - **Massive unlabeled data + downstream tasks?** → Self-Supervised

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update for Supervised ERM

Starting from the empirical risk minimization objective:

$$\mathcal{J}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\bigr)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient:**

Take the partial derivative of $\mathcal{J}$ with respect to the parameters $\theta$:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{J}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Chain Rule:**

For a single sample with squared loss $\mathcal{L} = (h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i)^2$:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = 2\bigl(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i\bigr) \cdot \nabla_\theta h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i)$$

**Step 3 — Update Rule (Gradient Descent):**

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \, \nabla_\theta \mathcal{J}\bigl(\theta^{(t)}\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Stochastic Approximation (SGD):**

For scalability with large $N$, use a single sample per step (or a mini-batch of size $B$):

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \, \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\bigr), \quad i \sim \text{Uniform}\{1, \ldots, N\}$$

**Engineering Insight:** $\eta$ (the **learning rate**) is the single most critical hyperparameter — too large causes divergence, too small causes slow convergence.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: K-Means Cluster Assignment Update

**Step 1 — Initialize** $K$ centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K^{(0)}\}$ randomly.

**Step 2 — Assignment Step:** For each point $\mathbf{x}_i$, assign to the nearest centroid:

$$c_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} \bigl\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \bigr\Vert_2^2$$

**Step 3 — Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C}_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{C}_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Check:** If $J(\mathcal{C}^{(t+1)}) - J(\mathcal{C}^{(t)}) < \epsilon$, stop; else go to Step 2.

**Convergence Guarantee:** K-Means is guaranteed to converge in finite steps because $J(\mathcal{C})$ is monotonically non-increasing and bounded below by 0.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bellman Equation from the Return Definition

**Step 1 — Define the Return** $G_t$ as the discounted sum of future rewards:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 2 — Define the State-Value Function:**

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\bigl[ G_t \mid s_t = s \bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Expectation** by separating the first reward:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\bigl[ r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Markov Property** to condition on the next state $s'$:

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \bigl[ r(s,a) + \gamma \, V^\pi(s') \bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Take the Max for the Optimal Policy:**

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \bigl[ r(s,a) + \gamma \, V^{*}(s') \bigr]$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation** — the cornerstone of all RL algorithms.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IoT Perspective

| Dimension | Supervised | Unsupervised | Semi-Supervised | Reinforcement | Self-Supervised |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Labeled Data Required** | 100% (high) | 0% | 5–20% | Reward signal only | 0% (auto-generated) |
| **Feedback Type** | Direct label | None | Mixed | Delayed reward | Self-generated |
| **Typical Model** | CNN, RNN, XGBoost | K-Means, AE, GAN | Pseudo-label, MixMatch | DQN, PPO | SimCLR, MAE, BERT |
| **Inference Output** | $y$ (label) | Clusters / Embeddings | $y$ (label) | Action $a$ | Embedding $\mathbf{z}$ |
| **Compute on Edge** | Medium–High | Low–Medium | Medium | High (online) | High (pretrain), Low (infer) |
| **Typical IoT Task** | Fault classification | Anomaly detection | Rare-event detection | Adaptive control | Sensor pretraining |
| **Key Risk** | Label scarcity | No ground truth | Confirmation bias | Sample inefficiency | Negative transfer |
| **Evaluation Metric** | Accuracy, F1 | Silhouette, NMI | Accuracy, F1 | Cumulative Reward | Downstream task score |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The comparison matrix above consolidates every concept the examiner can ask. Be prepared to draw this on paper during a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Classifying an IoT Temperature Sensor Reading

**Problem:** A smart-building sensor streams temperature (°C) and humidity (%). Train a supervised linear regressor to predict energy consumption (kWh).

**Step 1 — Data:** $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{1000}$ where $\mathbf{x}_i = [T_i, H_i]^T$.

**Step 2 — Hypothesis:** $h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 T + \theta_2 H$.

**Step 3 — Loss (MSE):**

$$\mathcal{J}(\theta) = \frac{1}{1000} \sum_{i=1}^{1000} \bigl(\theta_0 + \theta_1 T_i + \theta_2 H_i - y_i\bigr)^2$$

**Step 4 — Gradient (for $\theta_1$):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{2}{1000} \sum_{i=1}^{1000} \bigl(\theta_0 + \theta_1 T_i + \theta_2 H_i - y_i\bigr) T_i$$

**Step 5 — Update with learning rate $\eta = 0.01$:**

$$\theta_1^{(t+1)} = \theta_1^{(t)} - 0.01 \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial \theta_1}$$

**Step 6 — Iterate until $\Vert \nabla \mathcal{J} \Vert_2 < 10^{-4}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The same gradient-descent skeleton applies to neural networks — only the hypothesis $h_\theta$ becomes more complex (deeper, nonlinear). This is why the ERM framework is *universal* in ML.
