# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define supervised learning. Differentiate between classification and regression with one IoT example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm where a model is trained on a labelled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ to learn a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that predicts outputs for unseen inputs.

| Aspect | Classification | Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Discrete label | Continuous value |
| IoT Example | Predicting whether a motor is "faulty" or "normal" from vibration data | Predicting the remaining useful life (in hours) of a bearing from temperature history |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for comparison with examples)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the role of the loss function in supervised learning. State the loss function used in linear regression.**

**Model Answer:**
The loss function $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ quantifies the discrepancy between the predicted output $\hat{y}$ and the true label $y$. The objective of training is to find model parameters $\theta^*$ that minimize the average loss (Empirical Risk):

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)})$$

For **linear regression**, the standard loss is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$$

The choice of loss function directly shapes the learned model — different losses lead to different optimal solutions.

*(3 marks: 1 for role, 1 for formula, 1 for explanation)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain the working of the **Linear Regression** algorithm. Derive the **Normal Equation** for the optimal weight vector $\theta$. Discuss **two limitations** of linear regression. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Linear regression models the relationship between input features $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and a continuous target $y \in \mathbb{R}$ as a linear function:

$$\hat{y} = w^T x + b = \theta^T x \quad \text{(with bias absorbed into } \theta \text{ via } x_0 = 1\text{)}$$

**Derivation of the Normal Equation:**

The cost function (MSE, written as $\frac{1}{2N}$ form for convenience) is:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(\theta) &= \frac{1}{2N} \left( \theta^T X^T X \theta - 2\theta^T X^T y + y^T y \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\theta$ and setting to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) &= \frac{1}{N} (X^T X \theta - X^T y) = 0 \\
X^T X \theta &= X^T y \\
\boxed{\theta &= (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Linearity Assumption**: Cannot capture non-linear relationships without explicit feature engineering (e.g., polynomial features, basis expansion).
2. **Sensitivity to Outliers**: MSE squares the residuals, so outliers have disproportionately large influence on the learned parameters.
3. *(Bonus)* Multicollinearity: Highly correlated features cause $(X^T X)$ to be nearly singular, making the inverse unstable.
4. *(Bonus)* Computationally expensive for large $d$ because $(X^T X)^{-1}$ is $\mathcal{O}(d^3)$.

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Decision Tree Classifier**. How does it handle **continuous** and **categorical** features? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A decision tree is a non-parametric, hierarchical model that recursively partitions the feature space based on attribute tests, forming a tree of decision nodes and leaf nodes (predictions).

**Working (Top-Down Inductive Construction):**

1. Start at the **root node** with the entire training set.
2. At each node, evaluate all possible splits on all features using an impurity measure (**Gini** or **Entropy**).
3. Select the split that maximizes **Information Gain (IG)** or minimizes weighted impurity.
4. Recursively split the resulting child nodes.
5. Stop when a stopping criterion is met (max depth, min samples per leaf, pure node).
6. Assign the **majority class** (classification) or **mean value** (regression) of training samples in each leaf as the prediction.

**Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $H(S) = -\sum_c p_c \log_2 p_c$ is Shannon entropy.

**Handling Different Feature Types:**
- **Categorical Features**: Each possible category becomes a branch (multi-way split) OR categories are grouped into binary subsets.
- **Continuous Features**: All possible threshold values $t$ are evaluated: the feature $x_j$ is split as $x_j \leq t$ vs $x_j > t$. The threshold $t^*$ that maximizes IG is chosen.

**Advantages**: Highly interpretable (white-box), handles both feature types natively, requires little preprocessing.
**Disadvantages**: Prone to overfitting, unstable (small data changes → different trees), greedy splitting is not globally optimal.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Naive Bayes Classifier** algorithm. State the **naive conditional independence assumption**. Derive the **decision rule** and discuss **two advantages** for IoT applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Naive Bayes is a **probabilistic classifier** based on Bayes' theorem with the strong assumption that features are conditionally independent given the class label.

**Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(y = c \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid y = c) \cdot P(y = c)}{P(x)}$$

**Naive Conditional Independence Assumption:**

Given the class $c$, the features $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d$ are mutually independent:

$$P(x \mid y = c) = \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = c)$$

**Derivation of the Decision Rule (MAP):**

The predicted class is the one maximizing the posterior:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y} &= \arg\max_{c} P(y = c \mid x) \\
&= \arg\max_{c} \frac{P(y = c) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = c)}{P(x)} \\
&= \arg\max_{c} P(y = c) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = c)
\end{aligned}
$$

The denominator $P(x)$ is dropped because it is constant across classes.

**Probability Estimation:**
- For **categorical features**: $P(x_j = v \mid y = c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_j = v, y = c)}{\text{count}(y = c)}$
- For **continuous features**: Assume Gaussian distribution and estimate mean/variance from data per class.

**Laplace Smoothing** is added to handle zero-frequency issues:
$$P(x_j = v \mid y = c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_j = v, y = c) + 1}{\text{count}(y = c) + V_j}$$

**Two Advantages for IoT:**
1. **Extremely Fast Training & Inference** — Only requires counting occurrences; ideal for resource-constrained edge devices in IoT.
2. **Handles High-Dimensional Data Well** — Performs robustly even with thousands of features (e.g., sensor arrays) and small training sets.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** algorithm with a suitable example. Discuss the effect of the parameter $k$, distance metrics used, and its **computational complexity**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

KNN is a **non-parametric, instance-based (lazy)** learning algorithm. It makes no explicit model; instead, it stores the entire training dataset and predicts the label of a new query point based on its $k$ closest training samples.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Choose $k$ (number of neighbors) and a distance metric $d(x, x')$.
2. For a new query point $x_q$, compute $d(x_q, x^{(i)})$ for all training samples.
3. Identify the $k$ training samples with smallest distances → the $k$ nearest neighbors $N_k(x_q)$.
4. **Classification**: Predict $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y^{(i)} : x^{(i)} \in N_k(x_q)\}$ (majority voting).
5. **Regression**: Predict $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i \in N_k} y^{(i)}$.

**Example**: Given a dataset of IoT sensor readings (temperature, humidity) labelled "Comfortable" or "Uncomfortable", a new reading is classified based on majority class of its 5 closest neighbours in feature space.

**Effect of $k$:**
- **Small $k$ (e.g., 1)**: Low bias, **high variance** — sensitive to noise (overfitting).
- **Large $k$**: High bias, **low variance** — smoother decision boundary (may underfit).
- **Odd $k$** is preferred in binary classification to avoid ties.
- Optimal $k$ is typically chosen via **cross-validation**.

**Distance Metrics:**
- **Euclidean**: $d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - x_j')^2}$ (most common)
- **Manhattan**: $d(x, x') = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - x_j' \vert$
- **Minkowski**: Generalized form with parameter $p$.
- **Cosine Similarity**: For text/high-dimensional sparse data.

**Computational Complexity:**
- **Training**: $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — only stores the data.
- **Inference**: $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot d)$ per query — must scan all $N$ training points and compute $d$-dimensional distances.
- **Space**: $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot d)$ — must store the entire dataset.

This makes KNN **impractical for large-scale IoT deployments** without dimensionality reduction or approximate nearest neighbor methods (e.g., KD-trees, LSH).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MSE with RMSE** — RMSE is the square root of MSE; report the correct formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the bias term** in the normal equation derivation — always augment $X$ with a column of 1s.
> 3. **Mixing up Gini and Entropy** — both measure node impurity, but Gini ranges $[0, 0.5]$ for binary, while Entropy ranges $[0, 1]$.
> 4. **Not mentioning Laplace smoothing** in Naive Bayes — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if zero-frequency handling is omitted.
> 5. **Skipping the MAP derivation** in Bayes problems — always derive $\hat{y} = \arg\max P(y)\prod P(x_j \mid y)$ explicitly.
> 6. **Reporting only the algorithm description without complexity** — KTU Part B questions nearly always ask for complexity analysis.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Supervised learning requires **labelled data** $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ and learns mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
- ✅ **Classification** → discrete outputs; **Regression** → continuous outputs.
- ✅ **Linear Regression** uses MSE loss and has a closed-form **Normal Equation** $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$.
- ✅ **Logistic Regression** uses the **sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ and **cross-entropy** loss.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent** iteratively updates $\theta := \theta - \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ where $\alpha$ is the learning rate.
- ✅ **Naive Bayes** applies Bayes' theorem + conditional independence assumption; needs **Laplace smoothing**.
- ✅ **KNN** is a lazy learner with inference complexity $\mathcal{O}(Nd)$; sensitive to $k$ and feature scaling.
- ✅ **Decision Trees** split using **Gini** or **Entropy/IG**; handle both categorical and continuous features.
- ✅ **SVM** finds the maximum-margin hyperplane; uses **kernel trick** for non-linear data.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** is fundamental: high complexity → overfitting; low complexity → underfitting.
- ✅ **Regularization (L1/L2)** penalizes large weights to reduce overfitting.
- ✅ Standard split: **70/15/15** or **80/10/10** for train/validation/test.
- ✅ For IoT: prefer lightweight models (Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, shallow trees) for edge deployment.
- ✅ Always **normalize/standardize features** before applying KNN or gradient-descent-based models.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression Analysis** is a supervised statistical machine learning technique used to model and analyze the quantitative relationship between a **dependent variable** (target/response) and one or more **independent variables** (predictors/features). It estimates a continuous mapping function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ such that $y \approx f(X; \beta) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ denotes the irreducible random error term assumed to follow a Normal distribution with mean zero and constant variance $\sigma^{2}$.

In the context of IoT applications, regression models ingest streams of sensor telemetry (temperature, humidity, vibration, voltage, current) to forecast future readings, predict equipment Remaining Useful Life (RUL), estimate energy consumption, and detect anomalies.

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Regression Models

| **Type** | **Functional Form** | **Typical IoT Use-Case** |
|---|---|---|
| Simple Linear | $y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x$ | Linear sensor calibration |
| Multiple Linear | $y = \beta_{0} + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_{j}x_{j}$ | Multi-sensor fusion prediction |
| Polynomial | $y = \beta_{0} + \sum_{j=1}^{d}\beta_{j}x^{j}$ | Non-linear growth forecasting |
| Ridge / Lasso | Penalized linear forms | High-dimensional sensor arrays |
| Logistic | $P(y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(z)$ | Binary fault classification |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Salt-Water Solution":** Imagine you are a chef preparing a brine solution. You add 1 gram of salt → it tastes mild. 2 grams → noticeable. 5 grams → very salty. If you plot "salt grams" (X-axis) against "saltiness score" (Y-axis), you can draw a *best-fit line* through these data points. **Regression** is precisely that line — it summarizes the *average trend* so that for any new salt quantity, you can *predict* the saltiness without tasting it. The line is fitted to minimize the squared vertical gaps (errors) between itself and the actual observations.

## 1.4 Why Regression Matters in IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Forecasting**: Predict future sensor readings (e.g., next-hour room temperature).
> - **Predictive Maintenance**: Estimate Remaining Useful Life of motors/pumps from vibration and temperature sensors.
> - **Energy Optimization**: Forecast power consumption from environmental + occupancy features.
> - **Anomaly Detection**: Large residuals $\vert y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i} \vert$ flag faulty sensors.
> - **Calibration**: Convert raw ADC counts into physically meaningful units.

## 1.5 Key Terminology

- **Dependent Variable ($y$)**: The target we wish to predict (continuous).
- **Independent Variable ($x$)**: The input feature(s) used for prediction.
- **Coefficients ($\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}, \ldots, \beta_{p}$)**: Learnable parameters (intercept + slopes).
- **Residual ($e_{i} = y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i}$)**: Difference between observed and predicted value.
- **Hypothesis Function $h_{\beta}(x)$**: The model that maps inputs to outputs.
- **Cost Function $J(\beta)$**: Scalar measure of total prediction error to be minimized.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Simple Linear Regression Model

The simplest regression form models a straight-line relationship:

$$
h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1} x
$$

**Assumptions of the Classical Linear Model (Gauss-Markov):**

1. **Linearity**: The relationship between $X$ and $Y$ is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence**: Observations are independently sampled.
3. **Homoscedasticity**: The error variance $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_{i}) = \sigma^{2}$ is constant for all $i$.
4. **Normality**: $\varepsilon_{i} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$.
5. **No Multicollinearity** (for multiple regression): Predictors are not perfectly correlated.

## 2.2 The Least Squares Criterion (Why "Least Squares"?)

We seek coefficients $\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}$ that **minimize the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$
J(\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i} \right)^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( y_{i} - \beta_{0} - \beta_{1} x_{i} \right)^{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why squares and not absolute values?** Squaring (i) penalizes large errors more heavily, (ii) yields a smooth, differentiable function amenable to closed-form calculus, and (iii) leads to the BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) property under Gauss-Markov conditions.

## 2.3 Closed-Form Solution (Normal Equation)

Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_{0}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1}\bar{x}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_{1}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_{1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_{i} - \bar{x})(y_{i} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_{i} - \bar{x})^{2}}
$$

**Vectorized form for multiple regression:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\beta} = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y
$$

## 2.4 Gradient Descent Optimization

For high-dimensional data, computing $(X^{T}X)^{-1}$ becomes expensive ($\mathcal{O}(n^{3})$). Instead, we iteratively update parameters:

$$
\beta_{j} := \beta_{j} - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta_{j}}
$$

Where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (a small positive scalar typically in $[10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$). The update rule for linear regression is:

$$
\beta_{j} := \beta_{j} - \frac{\alpha}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)}
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **examiner's quick-reference**. Memorize every entry.

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Hypothesis | $h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x$ | Output units match $y$ |
| Slope | $\beta_{1} = \dfrac{\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})(y_{i}-\bar{y})}{\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}$ | Dimensionless if both scaled |
| Intercept | $\beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1}\bar{x}$ | Output units |
| MSE Cost | $J(\beta) = \dfrac{1}{2m}\sum (h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$ | Squared output units |
| Normal Equation | $\beta = (X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}y$ | Vector form |
| Gradient Update | $\beta_{j} := \beta_{j} - \alpha \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_{j}}$ | $\alpha > 0$ |
| MAE | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i} \vert$ | Same as $y$ |
| MSE | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}$ | Squared units of $y$ |
| RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same as $y$ |
| $R^{2}$ (Coefficient of Determination) | $R^{2} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}}{\sum (y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2}}$ | Range: $(-\infty, 1]$, ideally $\geq 0.8$ |
| Adjusted $R^{2}$ | $R^{2}_{adj} = 1 - (1-R^{2})\dfrac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ | Penalizes extra predictors |
| Total Sum of Squares | $\text{SST} = \sum (y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2}$ | Variance proxy |
| Residual Sum of Squares | $\text{SSR} = \sum (y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}$ | Model error |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Smart Grids**: Forecasting solar irradiance to predict photovoltaic output.
> - **Predictive Maintenance**: Estimating RUL of bearings from vibration spectral features.
> - **Environmental Monitoring**: Forecasting PM2.5 from humidity, wind speed, and traffic counts.
> - **Healthcare IoT**: Predicting blood glucose from continuous glucose monitor (CGM) trends.
> - **Industrial Process Control**: Modeling temperature–pressure relationships in chemical reactors.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Least Squares Estimators

**Starting Objective** (Sum of Squared Errors):

$$
J(\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( y_{i} - \beta_{0} - \beta_{1} x_{i} \right)^{2}
$$

### Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to $\beta_{0}$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_{0}} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( y_{i} - \beta_{0} - \beta_{1} x_{i} \right)
$$

Setting the derivative to zero (first-order optimality condition):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m} y_{i} = m \beta_{0} + \beta_{1} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{i}
$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$
\bar{y} = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1} \bar{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1} \bar{x}
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\beta_{1}$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_{1}} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{i} \left( y_{i} - \beta_{0} - \beta_{1} x_{i} \right)
$$

Setting equal to zero:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{i} y_{i} = \beta_{0} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{i} + \beta_{1} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{i}^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $\beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1}\bar{x}$

$$
\sum x_{i} y_{i} = (\bar{y} - \beta_{1}\bar{x}) \sum x_{i} + \beta_{1} \sum x_{i}^{2}
$$

$$
\sum x_{i} y_{i} = \bar{y} m \bar{x} - \beta_{1} m \bar{x}^{2} + \beta_{1} \sum x_{i}^{2}
$$

$$
\beta_{1} \left( \sum x_{i}^{2} - m \bar{x}^{2} \right) = \sum x_{i} y_{i} - m \bar{x} \bar{y}
$$

Using the identity $\sum (x_{i} - \bar{x})^{2} = \sum x_{i}^{2} - m\bar{x}^{2}$:

$$
\beta_{1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_{i} - \bar{x})(y_{i} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_{i} - \bar{x})^{2}}
$$

This is the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** estimator — the cornerstone of classical regression. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Computation)

**Problem:** An IoT weather station records the following daily ambient temperature ($x$, in °C) and soil moisture percentage ($y$, in %):

| Day ($i$) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_{i}$ | 20 | 22 | 25 | 27 | 30 |
| $y_{i}$ | 35 | 32 | 30 | 28 | 25 |

Fit a linear regression model $y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x$ using OLS.

### Step 1 — Compute Means

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{20 + 22 + 25 + 27 + 30}{5} = \frac{124}{5} = 24.8
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{35 + 32 + 30 + 28 + 25}{5} = \frac{150}{5} = 30.0
$$

### Step 2 — Build the Deviation Table

| $i$ | $x_{i}$ | $y_{i}$ | $x_{i} - \bar{x}$ | $y_{i} - \bar{y}$ | $(x_{i}-\bar{x})(y_{i}-\bar{y})$ | $(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 35 | $-4.8$ | $+5.0$ | $-24.00$ | $23.04$ |
| 2 | 22 | 32 | $-2.8$ | $+2.0$ | $-5.60$ | $7.84$ |
| 3 | 25 | 30 | $+0.2$ | $0.0$ | $0.00$ | $0.04$ |
| 4 | 27 | 28 | $+2.2$ | $-2.0$ | $-4.40$ | $4.84$ |
| 5 | 30 | 25 | $+5.2$ | $-5.0$ | $-26.00$ | $27.04$ |
| **Sum** | | | | | $\mathbf{-60.00}$ | $\mathbf{62.80}$ |

### Step 3 — Compute $\beta_{1}$

$$
\beta_{1} = \frac{\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})(y_{i}-\bar{y})}{\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}} = \frac{-60.00}{62.80} = -0.9554
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\beta_{0}$

$$
\beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1}\bar{x} = 30.0 - (-0.9554)(24.8) = 30.0 + 23.694 = 53.694
$$

### Step 5 — Final Regression Equation

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = 53.694 - 0.9554 \, x}
$$

### Step 6 — Predict at $x = 28\,°C$

$$
\hat{y} = 53.694 - 0.9554 \times 28 = 53.694 - 26.751 = 26.94\,\%
$$

### Step 7 — Evaluate $R^{2}$ (Goodness of Fit)

| $i$ | $x_{i}$ | $y_{i}$ | $\hat{y}_{i}$ | $(y_{i}-\hat{y}_{i})^{2}$ | $(y_{i}-\bar{y})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 35 | 34.586 | 0.171 | 25.00 |
| 2 | 22 | 32 | 32.675 | 0.456 | 4.00 |
| 3 | 25 | 30 | 29.809 | 0.036 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 27 | 28 | 27.898 | 0.011 | 4.00 |
| 5 | 30 | 25 | 24.932 | 0.005 | 25.00 |
| **Sum** | | | | $\mathbf{0.679}$ | $\mathbf{58.00}$ |

$$
R^{2} = 1 - \frac{0.679}{58.00} = 1 - 0.0117 = 0.9883
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $R^{2} = 0.9883$ indicates that $\approx 98.83\%$ of the variability in soil moisture is explained by temperature — an excellent fit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Normal Equation (Matrix Form)

For multiple regression with $m$ samples and $n$ features, the design matrix is:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_{1}^{(1)} & x_{2}^{(1)} & \cdots & x_{n}^{(1)} \\ 1 & x_{1}^{(2)} & x_{2}^{(2)} & \cdots & x_{n}^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{1}^{(m)} & x_{2}^{(m)} & \cdots & x_{n}^{(m)} \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} y^{(1)} \\ y^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ y^{(m)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The cost function in matrix form:

$$
J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m}(X\beta - y)^{T}(X\beta - y)
$$

Gradient with respect to $\beta$:

$$
\nabla_{\beta} J(\beta) = \frac{1}{m} X^{T}(X\beta - y)
$$

Setting $\nabla_{\beta} J = 0$:

$$
X^{T} X \beta = X^{T} y
$$

Multiplying both sides by $(X^{T}X)^{-1}$ (assuming invertibility):

$$
\boxed{\beta = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 4.1 End-to-End Regression Pipeline (Block Architecture)

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguard: All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes; no reserved keywords used as IDs; clean uppercase labels inside quotes. -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Sensor Node] --> B[Data Acquisition Layer]
    B --> C[Data Preprocessing]
    C --> C1[Missing Value Imputation]
    C --> C2[Outlier Removal]

    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Train Test Split]
    E --> E1[Training Set 80 percent]
    E --> E2[Test Set 20 percent]
    E1 --> F[Regression Model Training]
    F --> F1[Initialize Coefficients]
    F1 --> F2[Compute Cost Function MSE]
    F2 --> F3{Convergence Check}
    F3 -->|No| F4[Update Coefficients Gradient Descent]
    F4 --> F2
    F3 -->|Yes| G[Trained Model]
    G --> H[Prediction Phase]
    E2 --> H
    H --> I[Model Evaluation]
    I --> I1[Compute MAE MSE RMSE R squared]
    I1 --> J{Model Acceptable}
    J -->|Yes| K[Deploy to Edge or Cloud]

    K --> M[Real Time IoT Inference]

```

## 4.2 Gradient Descent Convergence Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start with random beta] --> P2[Compute gradient of J]
    P2 --> P3[Move opposite to gradient]
    P3 --> P4[Update beta new]
    P4 --> P5{Delta J less than tolerance}
    P5 -->|No| P2
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Optimal beta reached]
```

## 4.3 Regression Error Decomposition (Bias-Variance View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Total Prediction Error] --> T1[Bias Squared]
    T --> T2[Variance]
    T --> T3[Irreducible Noise epsilon]
    T1 --> R1[Underfitting: Model Too Simple]
    T2 --> R2[Overfitting: Model Too Complex]
    R1 --> SOL[Solution: Add Features / Polynomial Terms]
    R2 --> SOL2[Solution: Regularization Ridge Lasso]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The IoT sensor stream flows through preprocessing, training, and evaluation. The gradient descent loop iterates until the cost function converges, after which the model is deployed for real-time inference — closing the feedback loop with the physical sensor network.
