# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question A1]**
> **Q1. Define Regression Analysis. Differentiate between Simple and Multiple Linear Regression with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Regression analysis is a supervised statistical learning method that models the quantitative relationship between a dependent variable $y$ and one or more independent variables $X$, producing a continuous-valued prediction function $h_{\beta}(x)$.

| **Aspect** | **Simple Linear Regression** | **Multiple Linear Regression** |
|---|---|---|
| No. of predictors | One $(x)$ | Two or more $(x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{p})$ |
| Equation | $y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x$ | $y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x_{1} + \cdots + \beta_{p}x_{p}$ |
| IoT Example | Predicting soil moisture from a single temperature sensor | Predicting motor vibration from temperature, RPM, and current sensors |

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question A2]**
> **Q2. What is the Coefficient of Determination ($R^{2}$)? Mention its ideal range and the formula.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Coefficient of Determination $R^{2}$** quantifies the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the regression model.

$$
R^{2} = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{\text{res}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{tot}}} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i})^{2}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2}}
$$

- **Range:** $(-\infty, 1]$; ideally $\geq 0.8$ for engineering IoT applications.
- **Interpretation:** $R^{2} = 0.92$ means 92% of variability in $y$ is explained by the predictors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Derive the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) estimators $\beta_{0}$ and $\beta_{1}$ for a simple linear regression model $y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x + \varepsilon$ by minimizing the Sum of Squared Errors. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following dataset collected from an industrial IoT pressure sensor:

| Observation | $x$ (V) | $y$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 2.1 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 4.0 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 5.9 |
| 4 | 4.0 | 8.1 |

Fit a linear regression model using the OLS formulas. Compute the predicted value at $x = 5.0$ V and evaluate the $R^{2}$ score. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the exhaustive derivation.)*

Key steps expected:
1. Write the SSE cost function: $J(\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}) = \sum (y_{i} - \beta_{0} - \beta_{1}x_{i})^{2}$.
2. Take partial derivatives $\partial J / \partial \beta_{0}$ and $\partial J / \partial \beta_{1}$.
3. Set them to zero, derive $\beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1}\bar{x}$ and $\beta_{1} = \frac{\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})(y_{i}-\bar{y})}{\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}$.
4. State the four Gauss-Markov assumptions (Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Means:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4}{4} = 2.5, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2.1+4.0+5.9+8.1}{4} = 5.025
$$

**Step 2 — Deviations and Products:**

| $i$ | $x_{i}-\bar{x}$ | $y_{i}-\bar{y}$ | $(x_{i}-\bar{x})(y_{i}-\bar{y})$ | $(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-1.5$ | $-2.925$ | $4.3875$ | $2.25$ |
| 2 | $-0.5$ | $-1.025$ | $0.5125$ | $0.25$ |
| 3 | $+0.5$ | $+0.875$ | $0.4375$ | $0.25$ |
| 4 | $+1.5$ | $+3.075$ | $4.6125$ | $2.25$ |
| **Σ** | | | **$9.95$** | **$5.00$** |

**Step 3 — Coefficients:**

$$
\beta_{1} = \frac{9.95}{5.00} = 1.99, \quad \beta_{0} = 5.025 - (1.99)(2.5) = 5.025 - 4.975 = 0.05
$$

**Step 4 — Fitted Model:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = 0.05 + 1.99\,x}
$$

**Step 5 — Prediction at $x = 5.0$:**

$$
\hat{y} = 0.05 + 1.99 \times 5.0 = 0.05 + 9.95 = 10.00 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 6 — $R^{2}$ Calculation:**

| $i$ | $y_{i}$ | $\hat{y}_{i}$ | $(y_{i}-\hat{y}_{i})^{2}$ | $(y_{i}-\bar{y})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.1 | 2.04 | 0.0036 | 8.5556 |
| 2 | 4.0 | 4.03 | 0.0009 | 1.0506 |
| 3 | 5.9 | 6.02 | 0.0144 | 0.7656 |
| 4 | 8.1 | 8.01 | 0.0081 | 9.4556 |
| **Σ** | | | **$0.0270$** | **$19.8274$** |

$$
R^{2} = 1 - \frac{0.0270}{19.8274} = 1 - 0.00136 = 0.9986
$$

**Conclusion:** The model explains 99.86% of variance — an excellent linear fit for the IoT pressure sensor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain the Gradient Descent algorithm for optimizing linear regression parameters. Write the update rule and discuss the effect of learning rate $\alpha$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the following 2-feature dataset (multi-sensor IoT data), apply the **Normal Equation** $\beta = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y$ to compute regression coefficients. Features: $x_{1}$ = humidity (%), $x_{2}$ = light intensity (lux); Target: $y$ = power consumption (W).

| $i$ | $x_{1}$ | $x_{2}$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 200 | 15 |
| 2 | 50 | 400 | 25 |
| 3 | 70 | 600 | 35 |

Predict $\hat{y}$ at $(x_{1}, x_{2}) = (60, 500)$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Gradient Descent Explanation (7 Marks):**

1. **Initialize** $\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}$ to zero (or small random values).
2. **Loop until convergence:**
   - Compute predictions $h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1} x^{(i)}$.
   - Compute cost $J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$.
   - Update simultaneously: $\beta_{j} := \beta_{j} - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_{j}^{(i)}$.
3. **Learning Rate Effects:**
   - $\alpha$ too small → slow convergence.
   - $\alpha$ too large → divergence / oscillation.
   - $\alpha$ optimal → reaches global minimum efficiently (since $J$ is convex for linear regression).
4. **Stopping Criteria:** Maximum iterations reached OR $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$).

**(b) Normal Equation Computation (7 Marks):**

Construct the augmented design matrix:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 30 & 200 \\ 1 & 50 & 400 \\ 1 & 70 & 600 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 25 \\ 35 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $X^{T} X$:

$$
X^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 30 & 50 & 70 \\ 200 & 400 & 600 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
X^{T} X = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 150 & 1200 \\ 150 & 8300 & 66000 \\ 1200 & 66000 & 560000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $X^{T} y$:

$$
X^{T} y = \begin{bmatrix} 1+1+1 & 15 \\ 30\cdot15+50\cdot25+70\cdot35 \\ 200\cdot15+400\cdot25+600\cdot35 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 75 \\ 4200 \\ 32000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Invert $X^{T}X$ and multiply by $X^{T}y$ (computed via numerical linear algebra — students may show the augmented matrix and Gaussian elimination steps):

$$
\beta = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> *(Note: for this particular problem, $\beta_{0}=0$, $\beta_{1}=0.5$, $\beta_{2}=0$ — the data is generated from $y = 0.5 x_{1}$.)*

**Prediction at $(x_{1}, x_{2}) = (60, 500)$:**

$$
\hat{y} = 0 + 0.5(60) + 0(500) = 30 \text{ W}
$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on Regression Questions:**
> 1. **Arithmetic slips in deviation sums** — Always recompute $\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}$ and $\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})(y_{i}-\bar{y})$; a 0.5 sign error propagates into the entire $\beta_{1}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the intercept column of 1's** in the design matrix $X$ for the Normal Equation. This is a *fatal* error in 14-mark derivations.
> 3. **Mis-stating $R^{2}$ formula** — confusing $\text{SS}_{\text{res}}$ with $\text{SS}_{\text{tot}}$ in the denominator. KTU examiners specifically test this trap.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** of linear regression. Even if the derivation is correct, omitting the Gauss-Markov assumptions costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Gradient Descent vs Normal Equation**: Normal Equation gives a closed-form solution (no iterations); Gradient Descent is iterative and requires $\alpha$. Examiners may ask "which is preferable when $n > 10{,}000$?" — answer: **Gradient Descent** (since matrix inversion is $\mathcal{O}(n^{3})$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Regression Analysis:**
> - Regression predicts a **continuous** value (vs. classification, which predicts discrete labels).
> - Simple Linear Model: $y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x + \varepsilon$.
> - **OLS Slope Formula**: $\beta_{1} = \dfrac{\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})(y_{i}-\bar{y})}{\sum (x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}$ — memorize verbatim.
> - **Intercept Formula**: $\beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1}\bar{x}$.
> - **Normal Equation** (vector form): $\beta = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y$.
> - **Gradient Descent Update**: $\beta_{j} := \beta_{j} - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_{j}}$.
> - **MSE Cost Function**: $J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum (h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$.
> - **$R^{2}$** ranges in $(-\infty, 1]$; **Adjusted $R^{2}$** penalizes unnecessary features.
> - **Assumptions** (Gauss-Markov): Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality of errors, No Multicollinearity.
> - **Key trade-off**: Normal Equation is exact but $\mathcal{O}(n^{3})$; Gradient Descent scales to large $n$ but requires tuning $\alpha$.
> - **IoT Relevance**: Sensor calibration, energy forecasting, predictive maintenance, anomaly detection via residual analysis.
> - **Pitfall to avoid**: Always add the column of 1's in $X$ for the intercept term.
> - **Numerical check**: After computing $\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}$, plug in $\bar{x}$ to verify $\hat{y}(\bar{x}) = \bar{y}$ — a built-in sanity check examiners love to see.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Linear Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Linear Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Linear Regression (SLR)** is a fundamental supervised machine learning algorithm used in predictive analytics that establishes a **linear relationship** between a single independent (predictor) variable $X$ and a continuous dependent (response) variable $Y$ by fitting a straight line through the observed data points using the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method.

The mathematical model is formally expressed as:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

Where:
- $Y_i$ = Dependent (response) variable for the $i^{th}$ observation
- $X_i$ = Independent (predictor/explanatory) variable for the $i^{th}$ observation
- $\beta_0$ = **Intercept** (population parameter) — value of $Y$ when $X = 0$
- $\beta_1$ = **Slope** (population parameter) — change in $Y$ per unit change in $X$
- $\varepsilon_i$ = **Random error term** (residual) — unobserved stochastic component
- $n$ = Number of observations in the dataset

The estimated (predicted) form of the model is:

$$\hat{Y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X_i$$

Where $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ are the **sample estimates** of the true population parameters $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Best-Fit Line Through a Scatter Cloud"**

Imagine you are an **IoT engineer** who has deployed temperature sensors ($X$) across a smart greenhouse and recorded the corresponding tomato plant growth rate ($Y$) over 30 days. When you plot these 30 points on a graph, they form a scattered cloud. Your goal is to draw **one single straight line** that best summarizes the trend — passing as close as possible to all points simultaneously.

**Simple Linear Regression is precisely this process:**
- The line represents the **average trend** of how growth changes with temperature.
- The **slope** $\beta_1$ tells you: *"For every 1°C increase in temperature, by how many cm does the plant grow?"*
- The **intercept** $\beta_0$ tells you: *"What would the growth rate be if temperature were 0°C?"* (often theoretical in IoT contexts).
- The **error term** $\varepsilon_i$ captures all the noise — humidity variations, soil nutrients, sunlight, and other unmeasured factors.

> [!TIP]
> **IoT Context Snapshot:** In Industrial IoT (IIoT), SLR is used to model the relationship between **motor vibration frequency** and **bearing wear**, between **ambient temperature** and **battery discharge rate**, or between **time-of-day** and **HVAC energy consumption**. The simplicity and interpretability of SLR make it the **first-line analytical tool** in edge analytics dashboards.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Inventory

| Term | Symbol | Description |
|------|--------|-------------|
| Independent Variable | $X$ | Predictor, regressor, feature, input |
| Dependent Variable | $Y$ | Response, target, output, label |
| True Slope | $\beta_1$ | Population regression coefficient |
| Estimated Slope | $\hat{\beta}_1$ | Sample-based least squares estimate |
| True Intercept | $\beta_0$ | Y-intercept of population line |
| Estimated Intercept | $\hat{\beta}_0$ | Sample-based intercept estimate |
| Observed Value | $y_i$ | Actual measured data point |
| Predicted Value | $\hat{y}_i$ | Value predicted by the model |
| Residual | $e_i$ | Difference: $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ |
| Mean of $X$ | $\bar{X}$ | Sample mean of independent variable |
| Mean of $Y$ | $\bar{Y}$ | Sample mean of dependent variable |
| **Sample Size** | $n$ | **Number of observations** |
| **Degrees of Freedom** | $n - 2$ | **Critical for SLR (2 parameters estimated)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Statistical Philosophy

The entire edifice of Linear Regression rests upon **five classical Gauss-Markov assumptions** that must be validated before trusting the model in any real engineering deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Gauss-Markov Assumptions (Mandatory for KTU 14-mark derivations):**

1. **Linearity**: The relationship between $X$ and $Y$ is linear in parameters ($\beta_0, \beta_1$).
2. **Independence**: Observations (and hence residuals) are mutually independent — no autocorrelation.
3. **Homoscedasticity**: The variance of the error term is **constant** across all levels of $X$: $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i \mid X_i) = \sigma^2$.
4. **Normality**: The error term is **normally distributed**: $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
5. **No Multicollinearity**: For SLR, this is automatically satisfied since there is only one predictor.

If assumptions 1–4 hold, the OLS estimators are **BLUE** — *Best Linear Unbiased Estimators* — meaning they have the **minimum variance** among all linear unbiased estimators.

---

## 2.2 The Least Squares Criterion — Why "Least"?

The **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method finds $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ by **minimizing the sum of squared residuals (SSR)** — the total squared vertical distance from each data point to the regression line.

$$\text{SSR} = S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why square the residuals instead of taking absolute values?**
> - Squaring **penalizes large errors heavily** (quadratic penalty).
> - Squared residuals are **mathematically differentiable everywhere**, enabling clean calculus-based optimization.
> - Squaring yields a **closed-form analytical solution** (no iterative numerical methods required).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examiner favorites. Memorize the boxed ones with absolute precision.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | Sample Mean of $X$ | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Same units as $X$ |
| 2 | Sample Mean of $Y$ | $\bar{Y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$ | Same units as $Y$ |
| 3 | **Slope Estimator (OLS)** | $\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{X})(y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{X})^2} = \dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}$ | **MOST CRITICAL** |
| 4 | **Intercept Estimator (OLS)** | $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$ | Passes through $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$ |
| 5 | Sum of Squares — $X$ | $S_{xx} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{X})^2 = \sum x_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}$ | Variance scaling |
| 6 | Sum of Cross-Products | $S_{xy} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{X})(y_i - \bar{Y}) = \sum x_i y_i - \dfrac{\sum x_i \sum y_i}{n}$ | Covariance scaling |
| 7 | Sum of Squares — $Y$ | $S_{yy} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = \sum y_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n}$ | Total variation |
| 8 | Predicted Value | $\hat{y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_i$ | Point estimate |
| 9 | Residual | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | Observed minus predicted |
| 10 | Sum of Squared Residuals | $\text{SSR} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2$ | Also called **RSS** |
| 11 | Total Sum of Squares | $\text{SST} = S_{yy} = \sum (y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Total variance in $Y$ |
| 12 | Explained Sum of Squares | $\text{SSR}_{reg} = \sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{Y})^2 = \hat{\beta}_1^2 S_{xx}$ | Variance explained |
| 13 | **Variance Identity (ANOVA)** | $\text{SST} = \text{SSR}_{reg} + \text{SSR}$ | **Telescoping identity** |
| 14 | **Coefficient of Determination** | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \dfrac{\text{SSR}_{reg}}{\text{SST}}$ | Range: $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ |
| 15 | Residual Standard Error | $s_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SSR}}{n-2}}$ | Degrees of freedom = **$n-2$** |
| 16 | **Standard Error of Slope** | $\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \dfrac{s_e}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}}$ | Used in hypothesis testing |
| 17 | **Standard Error of Intercept** | $\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_0) = s_e \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} + \dfrac{\bar{X}^2}{S_{xx}}}$ | Depends on $\bar{X}$ |
| 18 | t-statistic for slope | $t = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_1}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)}$ | Tested against $t_{n-2, \alpha/2}$ |
| 19 | t-statistic for intercept | $t = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_0}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_0)}$ | Hypothesis testing |
| 20 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Average squared error |

> **Critical Convention Note:** All pipes $|$ are written as $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 The Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$) — The "Goodness-of-Fit" Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$R^2$ answers the question:** *"What proportion of the total variation in $Y$ is explained by the linear relationship with $X$?"*

**Interpretation Table:**

| $R^2$ Value | Engineering Interpretation | Model Quality |
|-------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| $R^2 = 1.0$ | Perfect linear fit — all points lie on the line | **Ideal (rare in real IoT data)** |
| $0.8 \leq R^2 < 1.0$ | Strong predictive power | **Excellent for deployment** |
| $0.5 \leq R^2 < 0.8$ | Moderate predictive power | **Acceptable for exploratory IoT analytics** |
| $0 < R^2 < 0.5$ | Weak linear relationship | **Consider non-linear models** |
| $R^2 = 0$ | No linear relationship captured | **Model is useless** |
| $R^2 < 0$ | Model is worse than predicting the mean | **Severe misspecification** |

> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common pitfall is that $R^2$ **always increases** as you add more predictors. For SLR, this isn't an issue, but students must know that a high $R^2$ does not guarantee causation — only correlation.

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering Fields

| Application Domain | Predictor ($X$) | Response ($Y$) | Use Case |
|--------------------|----------------|----------------|----------|
| **Smart Energy** | Outdoor temp | HVAC power draw | Demand forecasting |
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Vibration amplitude | Bearing wear | Failure prediction |
| **Precision Agriculture** | Soil moisture | Crop yield | Irrigation optimization |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | PM2.5 levels | Respiratory admissions | Public health alert |
| **Smart Manufacturing** | Machine speed cycle | Product defect rate | Quality control |
| **Wearable IoT** | Daily steps | Resting heart rate | Health analytics |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the OLS Slope Estimator ($\hat{\beta}_1$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a KTU 14-mark favorite. Master every line.**

**Step 1 — Define the objective function (Sum of Squared Residuals):**

$$S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order optimality conditions (partial derivatives set to zero):**

For a minimum, we require:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0}$:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)(-1) = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)$$

Setting this to zero:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the normal equation for $\beta_0$:**

Expanding:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - n\beta_0 - \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 0$$

$$n\beta_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1}$:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)(-x_i) = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)$$

Setting this to zero:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$ into the second normal equation:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \left[ y_i - (\bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}) - \beta_1 x_i \right] = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (y_i - \bar{Y}) - \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (x_i - \bar{X}) = 0$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the two sums as $S_{xy}$ and $S_{xx}$:**

$$S_{xy} - \beta_1 S_{xx} = 0$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $\hat{\beta}_1$:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{X})(y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{X})^2}}$$

**Step 9 — Variance form (computational shortcut):**

$$S_{xy} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - \frac{\left(\sum x_i\right)\left(\sum y_i\right)}{n}$$

$$S_{xx} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - \frac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^2}{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convexity Justification:** The Hessian of $S(\beta_0, \beta_1)$ is the matrix:
> $$H = \begin{pmatrix} 2n & 2\sum x_i \\ 2\sum x_i & 2\sum x_i^2 \end{pmatrix}$$
> whose determinant is $4n\sum x_i^2 - 4(\sum x_i)^2 = 4nS_{xx} > 0$ (provided $X$ has non-zero variance). Hence $S$ is **strictly convex**, and the critical point is the **unique global minimum**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)

**Step 1 — Start with the identity for any data point:**

$$y_i - \bar{Y} = (y_i - \hat{y}_i) + (\hat{y}_i - \bar{Y})$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides and sum over all $i$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{y}_i - \bar{Y})^2 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)(\hat{y}_i - \bar{Y})$$

**Step 3 — Show the cross-term vanishes:**

Substituting $\hat{y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_i$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)(\hat{y}_i - \bar{Y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i (\hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_i - \bar{Y})$$

Since $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$:

$$= \sum e_i (\hat{\beta}_1(x_i - \bar{X})) = \hat{\beta}_1 \sum e_i (x_i - \bar{X}) = \hat{\beta}_1 \sum e_i x_i - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X} \sum e_i$$

By the normal equations, $\sum e_i = 0$ and $\sum e_i x_i = 0$, so the cross-term is **exactly zero**.

**Step 4 — The Pythagorean Decomposition (ANOVA identity):**

$$\text{SST} = \text{SSR} + \text{SSR}_{reg}$$

Where:
- $\text{SST} = \sum(y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ (Total Sum of Squares)
- $\text{SSR} = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ (Residual Sum of Squares)
- $\text{SSR}_{reg} = \sum(\hat{y}_i - \bar{Y})^2$ (Regression Sum of Squares)

**Step 5 — Define $R^2$:**

$$\boxed{R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \frac{\text{SSR}_{reg}}{\text{SST}}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplified $R^2$ for SLR:**

Since $\text{SSR}_{reg} = \hat{\beta}_1^2 S_{xx} = \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy}$:

$$R^2 = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1^2 S_{xx}}{S_{yy}} = \frac{S_{xy}^2}{S_{xx} \cdot S_{yy}}$$

The last expression is the **square of the Pearson correlation coefficient** between $X$ and $Y$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU-Style Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample data: $n = 5$ IoT sensor observations. Find the regression equation and $R^2$.**

| $i$ | $x_i$ (Humidity %) | $y_i$ (Soil Moisture mV) |
|-----|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1   | 20                 | 50                       |
| 2   | 30                 | 45                       |
| 3   | 40                 | 40                       |
| 4   | 50                 | 35                       |
| 5   | 60                 | 25                       |

**Step 1 — Compute the necessary summations:**

$$\sum x_i = 20 + 30 + 40 + 50 + 60 = 200$$

$$\sum y_i = 50 + 45 + 40 + 35 + 25 = 195$$

$$\sum x_i^2 = 400 + 900 + 1600 + 2500 + 3600 = 9000$$

$$\sum y_i^2 = 2500 + 2025 + 1600 + 1225 + 625 = 7975$$

$$\sum x_i y_i = (20)(50) + (30)(45) + (40)(40) + (50)(35) + (60)(25)$$

$$= 1000 + 1350 + 1600 + 1750 + 1500 = 7200$$

**Step 2 — Compute sample means:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{200}{5} = 40, \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{195}{5} = 39$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_{xx}, S_{xy}, S_{yy}$:**

$$S_{xx} = 9000 - \frac{(200)^2}{5} = 9000 - 8000 = 1000$$

$$S_{xy} = 7200 - \frac{(200)(195)}{5} = 7200 - 7800 = -600$$

$$S_{yy} = 7975 - \frac{(195)^2}{5} = 7975 - 7605 = 370$$

**Step 4 — Compute the slope:**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} = \frac{-600}{1000} = -0.6$$

**Step 5 — Compute the intercept:**

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X} = 39 - (-0.6)(40) = 39 + 24 = 63$$

**Step 6 — The fitted regression line:**

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 63 - 0.6x}$$

**Step 7 — Compute $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = \frac{S_{xy}^2}{S_{xx} \cdot S_{yy}} = \frac{(-600)^2}{(1000)(370)} = \frac{360000}{370000} = \frac{36}{37} \approx 0.9730$$

**Step 8 — Compute residual standard error $s_e$:**

First, compute predicted values:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i = 63 - 0.6x_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $e_i^2$ |
|-------|-------|---------------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| 20    | 50    | 51                        | -1                       | 1        |
| 30    | 45    | 45                        | 0                        | 0        |
| 40    | 40    | 39                        | 1                        | 1        |
| 50    | 35    | 33                        | 2                        | 4        |
| 60    | 25    | 27                        | -2                       | 4        |

$$\text{SSR} = 1 + 0 + 1 + 4 + 4 = 10$$

$$s_e = \sqrt{\frac{\text{SSR}}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{10}{3}} \approx 1.826$$

**Step 9 — Test the significance of the slope (t-test):**

$$\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \frac{s_e}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}} = \frac{1.826}{\sqrt{1000}} \approx 0.0577$$

$$t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)} = \frac{-0.6}{0.0577} \approx -10.40$$

With $n - 2 = 3$ degrees of freedom, the critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) is $t_{3, 0.025} = 3.182$. Since $\vert t \vert = 10.40 > 3.182$, the slope is **highly significant** ($p < 0.01$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram of the SLR Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Sensor Data Stream] --> B[Data Acquisition Module]
    B --> C[Data Preprocessing]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering]
    D --> E[Training Set X and Y]
    E --> F[OLS Estimator]
    F --> G[Compute Beta1 Slope]
    F --> H[Compute Beta0 Intercept]
    G --> I[Regression Equation y hat]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Prediction Engine]
    J --> K[IoT Decision Output]
    K --> L[Dashboard Alert]
    K --> M[Actuator Control]
    K --> N[Cloud Telemetry]
```

> **Figure 4.1 — End-to-end architecture of a Simple Linear Regression system embedded within an IoT analytics pipeline.**

---

## 4.2 Mathematical Flow of the OLS Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Input n data pairs x_i, y_i] --> P2[Compute mean of X]
    P2 --> P3[Compute mean of Y]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Sxx and Sxy]
    P4 --> P5[Compute slope beta1 equals Sxy divided by Sxx]
    P5 --> P6[Compute intercept beta0 equals Ybar minus beta1 times Xbar]
    P6 --> P7[Form regression equation y hat]
    P7 --> P8[Compute residuals e_i]
    P8 --> P9[Compute SSR and R squared]
    P9 --> P10[Output Model Parameters and Fit Diagnostics]
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Procedural flow of the Ordinary Least Squares computation sequence.**

---

## 4.3 Graphical Schematic — Best-Fit Line and Residuals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observed data point y_i] -->|vertical distance e_i| B[Predicted point on line y hat]
    B -->|lies on| C[Regression Line y hat equals beta0 plus beta1 x]
    C -->|passes through| D[Centroid Xbar Ybar]
    D -->|anchors| C
    E[X axis predictor variable] --> C
    F[Y axis response variable] --> A
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Geometric interpretation of residuals, the regression line, and the centroid anchor point.**

---

## 4.4 Variance Decomposition Schematic (ANOVA Identity)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Variation SST] --> B[Unexplained Variation SSR]
    A --> C[Explained Variation SSRreg]
    B --> D[Residuals from data noise]
    C --> E[Captured by linear trend]
    A --> F[Ratio gives R squared]
    C --> F
    B --> F
```

> **Figure 4.4 — Visual representation of the partition of total variance into explained and unexplained components, which yields $R^2$.**

---

## 4.5 Model Diagnostics Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fit SLR Model] --> B{Check Assumptions}
    B -->|Linearity| C[Scatter plot inspection]
    B -->|Independence| D[Durbin Watson test]
    B -->|Homoscedasticity| E[Residual vs Fitted plot]
    B -->|Normality| F[Q Q plot and Shapiro Wilk]
    C --> G{All assumptions satisfied?}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Deploy Model for IoT Inference]
    G -->|No| I[Apply Transformations or Use Polynomial Regression]
```

> **Figure 4.5 — Diagnostic decision tree for validating the four Gauss-Markov assumptions before deploying an SLR model in production IoT environments.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Each question carries 3 marks. Answers should be precise, definition-oriented, and within 80–100 words.**

---

### **Question 1:** Define Simple Linear Regression. State the assumptions underlying the OLS estimation method.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Simple Linear Regression is a statistical method that models the relationship between a single independent variable $X$ and a continuous dependent variable $Y$ using a straight line of the form $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$.

The five Gauss-Markov assumptions are:
1. **Linearity** — the relationship is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence** — observations are mutually independent.
3. **Homoscedasticity** — constant variance of errors: $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$.
4. **Normality** — errors are normally distributed: $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
5. **No measurement error** in the predictor variable $X$.

---

### **Question 2:** What is the Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)? Explain its significance in evaluating a regression model.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)** measures the proportion of total variation in the dependent variable $Y$ that is explained by the linear relationship with the independent variable $X$.

It is defined as:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \frac{\text{SSR}_{reg}}{\text{SST}}$$

**Significance:**
- $R^2$ ranges from $0$ to $1$.
- A value close to $1$ indicates that the model explains a large portion of the variability in $Y$ (good fit).
- A value close to $0$ suggests the model fails to capture the underlying relationship (poor fit).
- In IoT applications, $R^2$ is critical for validating predictive maintenance and sensor calibration models.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **As per KTU 2024 scheme, students answer ONE full question per module. Each has two 7-mark sub-parts.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Fitting and Interpreting an SLR Model

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper]`

The following data represents the **daily ambient temperature (°C)** ($X$) recorded by an IoT weather sensor and the **corresponding power consumption (kWh)** ($Y$) of a smart building's HVAC system over 6 days:

| Day | $x_i$ (°C) | $y_i$ (kWh) |
|-----|------------|-------------|
| 1   | 28         | 120         |
| 2   | 30         | 135         |
| 3   | 32         | 145         |
| 4   | 34         | 160         |
| 5   | 36         | 170         |
| 6   | 38         | 185         |

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Estimate the parameters of the simple linear regression model $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$ using the Ordinary Least Squares method. Write the fitted regression equation.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the required summations:**

$$\sum x_i = 28 + 30 + 32 + 34 + 36 + 38 = 198$$

$$\sum y_i = 120 + 135 + 145 + 160 + 170 + 185 = 915$$

$$\sum x_i^2 = 784 + 900 + 1024 + 1156 + 1296 + 1444 = 6604$$

$$\sum y_i^2 = 14400 + 18225 + 21025 + 25600 + 28900 + 34225 = 142375$$

$$\sum x_i y_i = (28)(120) + (30)(135) + (32)(145) + (34)(160) + (36)(170) + (38)(185)$$

$$= 3360 + 4050 + 4640 + 5440 + 6120 + 7030 = 30640$$

**Step 2 — Compute means:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{198}{6} = 33 \quad ; \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{915}{6} = 152.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_{xx}$ and $S_{xy}$:**

$$S_{xx} = 6604 - \frac{(198)^2}{6} = 6604 - 6534 = 70$$

$$S_{xy} = 30640 - \frac{(198)(915)}{6} = 30640 - 30195 = 445$$

**Step 4 — Compute the slope $\hat{\beta}_1$:**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} = \frac{445}{70} = 6.357$$

**Step 5 — Compute the intercept $\hat{\beta}_0$:**

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X} = 152.5 - (6.357)(33) = 152.5 - 209.786 = -57.286$$

**Step 6 — Fitted Regression Equation:**

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = -57.286 + 6.357 x}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** For every 1°C increase in ambient temperature, the HVAC power consumption increases by approximately **6.357 kWh**.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate $R^2$, the residual standard error $s_e$, and test the significance of the slope at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $S_{yy}$:**

$$S_{yy} = 142375 - \frac{(915)^2}{6} = 142375 - 139537.5 = 2837.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = \frac{S_{xy}^2}{S_{xx} \cdot S_{yy}} = \frac{(445)^2}{(70)(2837.5)} = \frac{198025}{198625} = 0.9970$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **99.70%** of the variation in HVAC power consumption is explained by the ambient temperature — indicating an excellent linear fit.

**Step 3 — Compute predicted values and residuals:**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $e_i^2$ |
|-------|-------|-------------|--------------------------|----------|
| 28    | 120   | 120.71      | -0.71                    | 0.50     |
| 30    | 135   | 133.43      | 1.57                     | 2.46     |
| 32    | 145   | 146.14      | -1.14                    | 1.30     |
| 34    | 160   | 158.86      | 1.14                     | 1.30     |
| 36    | 170   | 171.57      | -1.57                    | 2.46     |
| 38    | 185   | 184.29      | 0.71                     | 0.50     |

$$\text{SSR} = 0.50 + 2.46 + 1.30 + 1.30 + 2.46 + 0.50 = 8.52$$

**Step 4 — Residual Standard Error:**

$$s_e = \sqrt{\frac{\text{SSR}}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.52}{4}} = \sqrt{2.13} = 1.459$$

**Step 5 — Standard Error of the Slope:**

$$\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \frac{s_e}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}} = \frac{1.459}{\sqrt{70}} = \frac{1.459}{8.367} = 0.1744$$

**Step 6 — t-statistic:**

$$t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)} = \frac{6.357}{0.1744} = 36.45$$

**Step 7 — Hypothesis Test Decision:**

With $n - 2 = 4$ degrees of freedom and $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed), the critical value is $t_{4, 0.025} = 2.776$.

Since $\vert t \vert = 36.45 \gg 2.776$, **we reject the null hypothesis** $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$.

**Conclusion:** The slope coefficient is **highly statistically significant** ($p < 0.001$). The relationship between temperature and HVAC power consumption is real and not due to random chance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** Theoretical Foundations and Properties

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the OLS estimators $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ for the simple linear regression model by minimizing the sum of squared residuals. State clearly the normal equations and the final closed-form solutions.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the model and objective:**

$$y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

The OLS objective is to minimize:

$$S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2$$

**Step 2 — Take partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Form the two normal equations:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \quad \text{...(I)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i = \beta_0 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve Equation (I) for $\beta_0$:**

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into Equation (II) and solve for $\hat{\beta}_1$:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{X})(y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{X})^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Confirm the Hessian is positive definite** (already done in Section 3.1 Step 9), proving this is a global minimum.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Prove that under the Gauss-Markov assumptions, the OLS estimators are unbiased, i.e., $E(\hat{\beta}_1) = \beta_1$ and $E(\hat{\beta}_0) = \beta_0$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rewrite $\hat{\beta}_1$ in a useful form:**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{X})(y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{X})^2} = \sum k_i y_i$$

where $k_i = \dfrac{x_i - \bar{X}}{\sum(x_j - \bar{X})^2}$. Note that $\sum k_i = 0$ and $\sum k_i x_i = 1$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i$:**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \sum k_i (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i) = \beta_0 \sum k_i + \beta_1 \sum k_i x_i + \sum k_i \varepsilon_i$$

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = 0 + \beta_1 (1) + \sum k_i \varepsilon_i = \beta_1 + \sum k_i \varepsilon_i$$

**Step 3 — Take expectation:**

$$E(\hat{\beta}_1) = \beta_1 + \sum k_i E(\varepsilon_i) = \beta_1 + \sum k_i (0) = \beta_1$$

This proves $\hat{\beta}_1$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\beta_1$.

**Step 4 — Show unbiasedness of $\hat{\beta}_0$:**

$$E(\hat{\beta}_0) = E(\bar{Y}) - E(\hat{\beta}_1) \cdot \bar{X} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{X} - \beta_1 \bar{X} = \beta_0$$

(Since $E(\bar{Y}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{X}$ from the model assumptions.)

**Step 5 — Variance of $\hat{\beta}_1$ (additional bonus derivation):**

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \text{Var}\left(\sum k_i \varepsilon_i\right) = \sigma^2 \sum k_i^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum(x_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

This proves that the OLS estimator has the **minimum variance** among all linear unbiased estimators, completing the **Gauss-Markov theorem**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Degrees of Freedom Error (–1 mark):** Students often use $n$ instead of $n - 2$ in the denominator of $s_e$. KTU examiners specifically check this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Compute $\bar{X}$ and $\bar{Y}$ First (–2 marks):** Direct substitution into the deviation form without first computing the means is a common error.
>
> 3. **Sign Errors in $\hat{\beta}_0$ (–1 mark):** The intercept is $\bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$, NOT $\bar{Y} + \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$.
>
> 4. **Confusing $R^2$ with $r$ (correlation coefficient):** $R^2$ is the **square** of the Pearson correlation for SLR. State this explicitly.
>
> 5. **Failing to Verify Assumptions:** In 14-mark questions, examiners expect a brief mention of the Gauss-Markov assumptions — omitting them may cost 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Not Stating the Critical $t$-value:** When conducting hypothesis tests, you must compare against $t_{n-2, \alpha/2}$ from the t-distribution table. Do not use $z$-values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- **Model Form:** $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i$; Estimated form: $\hat{Y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X_i$
- **Slope Formula:** $\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}$ (covariance over variance of $X$)
- **Intercept Formula:** $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$ (the line passes through the centroid)
- **Computational Formulas:** $S_{xx} = \sum x_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}$ and $S_{xy} = \sum x_i y_i - \dfrac{\sum x_i \sum y_i}{n}$
- **Degrees of Freedom:** Always $n - 2$ for SLR (two parameters estimated)
- **Residual Standard Error:** $s_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SSR}}{n-2}}$
- **Coefficient of Determination:** $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = \dfrac{\text{SSR}_{reg}}{\text{SST}}$
- **For SLR specifically:** $R^2 = r^2$ (square of Pearson correlation coefficient)
- **Standard Error of Slope:** $\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \dfrac{s_e}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}}$
- **t-statistic:** $t = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_1}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)}$; critical value = $t_{n-2, \alpha/2}$
- **ANOVA Identity:** $\text{SST} = \text{SSR}_{reg} + \text{SSR}$ (always holds exactly)
- **Gauss-Markov Assumptions:** Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality, No measurement error
- **BLUE Property:** OLS estimators are Best (minimum variance), Linear, Unbiased Estimators
- **IoT Applications:** Predictive maintenance, energy forecasting, sensor calibration, environmental monitoring
- **Geometric Fact:** The OLS regression line always passes through the point $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$
- **Sign of Slope:** Positive slope → positive correlation; negative slope → negative correlation
- **Interpretation:** $\hat{\beta}_1$ = expected change in $Y$ for a unit increase in $X$
- **Limitation:** SLR captures only linear trends; for non-linear relationships, use polynomial or other regression variants
- **No Causation Rule:** High $R^2$ does NOT imply $X$ causes $Y$ — only association

> **Final Exam Mantra:** "The slope is covariance-over-variance, the intercept is mean-of-Y minus slope times mean-of-X, and $R^2$ is one minus residual-over-total variation. Master the formulas — every KTU paper has at least one SLR problem."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Linear Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Linear Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)** is a supervised machine learning regression algorithm that models the linear relationship between a scalar continuous dependent (target) variable and **two or more** independent (predictor) variables by fitting a linear equation to the observed data.

Mathematically, MLR extends Simple Linear Regression by generalizing the predictor space from one dimension to a $p$-dimensional hyperplane, expressed as:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon$$

where:
- $Y$ is the dependent (response) variable
- $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p$ are the $p$ independent (explanatory) variables
- $\beta_0$ is the **intercept** (value of $Y$ when all $X = 0$)
- $\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_p$ are the **partial regression coefficients** (slopes)
- $\varepsilon$ is the **error term** (residual) — assumed i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) with $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$
- $p$ is the number of predictors (features)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Simple Linear Regression:**
> - **Simple Linear Regression** uses **one** independent variable ($p = 1$).
> - **Multiple Linear Regression** uses **two or more** independent variables ($p \geq 2$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Predicting House Prices in Kerala:**
> Imagine you are a real-estate analyst in **Kochi** trying to predict the price of a house.
> - In **Simple Linear Regression**, you only look at one feature, say "area of the house in sq.ft."
> - In **Multiple Linear Regression**, you simultaneously consider **area, number of bedrooms, distance to the city center, age of the house, and number of floors**.
>
> Each factor contributes **a weighted amount (the $\beta$ coefficient)** to the final price. The model finds the *best combination of weights* that minimizes the total prediction error across hundreds of historical house sales.

**Another Analogy — IoT Smart Home Energy Prediction:**
A smart home IoT system tries to predict monthly electricity consumption using:
- $X_1$ = outdoor temperature (from weather API)
- $X_2$ = number of occupants (from smart sensors)
- $X_3$ = hours of AC usage (from smart meter)
- $X_4$ = day-time vs night-time tariff hours

MLR finds the optimal weight for each sensor input to predict consumption accurately.

---

## 1.3 Why Multiple Linear Regression is Critical for IoT

| IoT Application | Predictor Variables ($X$) | Target ($Y$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Agriculture** | Soil moisture, humidity, temperature, pH | Crop yield |
| **Smart Healthcare** | Heart rate, SpO₂, body temp, age | Risk score |
| **Industrial IoT** | Vibration, RPM, temperature, pressure | Machine RUL |
| **Smart City** | Traffic density, weather, time of day | Pollution level |
| **Energy Mgmt** | Temp, occupancy, solar irradiance | Power consumption |

The term **"linear"** refers to linearity **in the parameters $\beta$**, not necessarily in the variables. So $X^2$, $\log(X)$, or interaction terms $X_1 X_2$ are still permitted because the model remains linear in $\beta$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MLR Model — Structural Decomposition

The model is built layer-by-layer as follows:

1. **Vector of Predictors:** $\mathbf{x}_i = (1, x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{ip})^T$ — includes an explicit $1$ for the intercept.
2. **Vector of Parameters:** $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p)^T$.
3. **Prediction for observation $i$:** $\hat{y}_i = \mathbf{x}_i^T \boldsymbol{\beta} = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij}$.
4. **Residual for observation $i$:** $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$.
5. **Error assumption:** $e_i \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the column of 1s?** The leading $1$ in $\mathbf{x}_i$ multiplies $\beta_0$ to allow the regression hyperplane to shift away from the origin. Without it, the plane would be forced through $\mathbf{0}$.

---

## 2.2 Matrix Formulation (Compact Representation)

For $n$ observations and $p$ predictors, define:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1p} \\ 1 & x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2p} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{np} \end{bmatrix}_{n \times (p+1)}, \quad \boldsymbol{\beta} = \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \vdots \\ \beta_p \end{bmatrix}_{(p+1) \times 1}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_n \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 1}
$$

The model in matrix form becomes:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

The **predicted values** vector is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$$

The **residual vector** is:

$$\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$$

---

## 2.3 Cost Function: Mean Squared Error (MSE)

The objective is to find $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ that minimizes the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

The **Mean Squared Error** (per-sample version, used in gradient descent) is:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \frac{1}{n} \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why squared errors?**
> - Squaring **penalizes large errors more** (no cancellation between positive and negative residuals).
> - Squaring yields a **convex, differentiable** function with a unique global minimum — guaranteeing convergence.
> - Under Gaussian noise assumptions, SSE minimization is equivalent to **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | MLR Model | $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon$ | $p$ predictors |
| 2 | Matrix Form | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ | $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (p+1)}$ |
| 3 | Cost Function (SSE) | $J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$ | Scalar |
| 4 | **Normal Equation** | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form |
| 5 | Predictions | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ | Vector |
| 6 | Residuals | $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ | Vector |
| 7 | SSE | $\text{SSE} = \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2$ | Sum |
| 8 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean |
| 9 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Square root of MSE |
| 10 | SST (Total) | $\text{SST} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Total variance |
| 11 | SSR (Regression) | $\text{SSR} = \sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Explained |
| 12 | R² (Coefficient of Det.) | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}$ | $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ |
| 13 | Adjusted R² | $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2) \frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ | Penalizes extra vars |
| 14 | Gradient Descent Update | $\beta_j \leftarrow \beta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j}$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate |
| 15 | Partial Derivative | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = -\frac{2}{n} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i) x_{ij}$ | Per-feature |
| 16 | Variance of $\hat{\beta}_j$ | $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j) = \sigma^2 [(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}]_{jj}$ | Diagonal element |
| 17 | F-Statistic | $F = \frac{\text{SSR}/p}{\text{SSE}/(n-p-1)}$ | Overall model fit |
| 18 | t-Statistic (per coef) | $t_j = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_j)}$ | Tests $\beta_j = 0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **MSE** (mean) with **SSE** (sum). The Normal Equation minimizes **SSE**, while Gradient Descent often minimizes **MSE**. They differ only by a factor of $\frac{1}{n}$, so the optimal $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is identical.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of MLR

- **Interpretability**: Each $\beta_j$ gives the *marginal* effect of $X_j$ on $Y$, holding all other variables constant — invaluable in scientific and engineering decision-making.
- **Baseline model**: Almost always the **first** model tried in any regression problem.
- **Statistical inference**: Enables hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, and p-values for each predictor.
- **Feature importance**: Standardized coefficients $|\beta_j^*|$ rank predictors by influence.
- **Closed-form solvability**: The Normal Equation guarantees an exact analytical solution (unlike deep learning models).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Equation (Closed-Form Solution)

**Goal:** Find $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ that minimizes $J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$.

### Step 1 — Expand the Cost Function

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T\mathbf{y} + (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

**Explanation:** We used the distributive property of matrix multiplication. Since $\mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is a scalar, it equals its transpose: $(\mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T = \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$.

### Step 2 — Simplify the Scalar Terms

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

**Explanation:** Because $\mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ (both are scalars), we combined the two cross terms into $-2\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$.

### Step 3 — Take the Gradient with Respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

**Explanation:** Using the standard matrix calculus rules:
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} (\mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{0}$ (constant w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\beta}$)
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} (\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{A}) = \mathbf{A}$ (here $\mathbf{A} = -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$)
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} (\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}) = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ (here $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$, which is symmetric)

### Step 4 — Set the Gradient to Zero (First-Order Optimality Condition)

$$-2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Explanation:** At a minimum, the gradient vector must be zero (necessary condition). This is the **Normal Equation**.

### Step 5 — Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$

$$\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}}$$

**Explanation:** We assume $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ is **invertible** (i.e., non-singular, full rank). This requires $n \geq p+1$ and no perfect multicollinearity. The unique solution is obtained by left-multiplying both sides by $(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence Condition for Normal Equation:** $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ must be invertible $\iff$ $\mathbf{X}$ has **full column rank** $\iff$ no feature is a perfect linear combination of others $\iff$ $n > p$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Gradient Descent Update Rule

**Goal:** Iteratively update $\beta_j$ to minimize MSE $J = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Partial Derivative of MSE w.r.t. $\beta_j$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_j} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Chain Rule

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(y_i - \hat{y}_i) \cdot \left(-\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial \beta_j}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $\hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \cdots + \beta_p x_{ip}$

Since $\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial \beta_j} = x_{ij}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = -\frac{2}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i) x_{ij}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Gradient Descent Update

$$\beta_j^{\text{new}} = \beta_j^{\text{old}} - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = \beta_j^{\text{old}} + \frac{2\alpha}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i) x_{ij}$$

**Explanation:** The negative gradient points in the direction of *steepest descent*. The learning rate $\alpha$ controls step size:
- $\alpha$ too small → slow convergence
- $\alpha$ too large → divergence/oscillation

> [!NOTE]
> **Vectorized form (more efficient):**
> $$\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\text{new}} = \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\text{old}} + \frac{2\alpha}{n} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\text{old}})$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** Given $n = 4$ observations with $p = 2$ predictors, find $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$.

| $i$ | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $Y$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-----|
| 1   | 1     | 2     | 5   |
| 2   | 2     | 1     | 7   |
| 3   | 3     | 3     | 11  |
| 4   | 4     | 2     | 13  |

### Step 1 — Construct the Design Matrix $\mathbf{X}$ and Response Vector $\mathbf{y}$

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \\ 11 \\ 13 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$

$$
\mathbf{X}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 & 8 \\ 10 & 30 & 19 \\ 8 & 19 & 18 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Explanation of $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ entries (sample):**
- $(1,1)$: $\sum 1^2 = 4$
- $(1,2)$: $\sum x_{i1} = 1+2+3+4 = 10$
- $(2,2)$: $\sum x_{i1}^2 = 1+4+9+16 = 30$
- $(2,3)$: $\sum x_{i1} x_{i2} = (1)(2)+(2)(1)+(3)(3)+(4)(2) = 2+2+9+8 = 21$

> [!WARNING]
> **Recheck above** — let me recompute $(2,3)$: $1\cdot2 + 2\cdot1 + 3\cdot3 + 4\cdot2 = 2+2+9+8 = 21$, not 19. Let me recompute all entries carefully.

Recomputing $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ accurately:
- $(1,1) = 1^2 \cdot 4 = 4$
- $(1,2) = (1)(1) + (1)(2) + (1)(3) + (1)(4) = 10$
- $(1,3) = (1)(2) + (1)(1) + (1)(3) + (1)(2) = 8$
- $(2,2) = 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 = 1+4+9+16 = 30$
- $(2,3) = (1)(2) + (2)(1) + (3)(3) + (4)(2) = 2+2+9+8 = 21$
- $(3,3) = 2^2 + 1^2 + 3^2 + 2^2 = 4+1+9+4 = 18$

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 & 8 \\ 10 & 30 & 21 \\ 8 & 21 & 18 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 5+7+11+13 \\ 1\cdot5 + 2\cdot7 + 3\cdot11 + 4\cdot13 \\ 2\cdot5 + 1\cdot7 + 3\cdot11 + 2\cdot13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 36 \\ 5+14+33+52 \\ 10+7+33+26 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 36 \\ 104 \\ 76 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}$

Determinant: $\det = 4(30\cdot18 - 21\cdot21) - 10(10\cdot18 - 21\cdot8) + 8(10\cdot21 - 30\cdot8)$
$= 4(540 - 441) - 10(180 - 168) + 8(210 - 240)$
$= 4(99) - 10(12) + 8(-30)$
$= 396 - 120 - 240 = 36$

$$
(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{36} \begin{bmatrix} (30\cdot18 - 21^2) & -(10\cdot18 - 8\cdot21) & (10\cdot21 - 30\cdot8) \\ -(10\cdot18 - 8\cdot21) & (4\cdot18 - 8^2) & -(4\cdot21 - 8\cdot10) \\ (10\cdot21 - 30\cdot8) & -(4\cdot21 - 8\cdot10) & (4\cdot30 - 10^2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{36} \begin{bmatrix} 99 & -12 & -30 \\ -12 & 8 & -4 \\ -30 & -4 & 20 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \frac{1}{36} \begin{bmatrix} 99 & -12 & -30 \\ -12 & 8 & -4 \\ -30 & -4 & 20 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 36 \\ 104 \\ 76 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute each row:
- Row 1: $99(36) - 12(104) - 30(76) = 3564 - 1248 - 2280 = 36$
- Row 2: $-12(36) + 8(104) - 4(76) = -432 + 832 - 304 = 96$
- Row 3: $-30(36) - 4(104) + 20(76) = -1080 - 416 + 1520 = 24$

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \frac{1}{36} \begin{bmatrix} 36 \\ 96 \\ 24 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 8/3 \\ 2/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Regression Equation

$$\hat{Y} = 1 + \frac{8}{3} X_1 + \frac{2}{3} X_2$$

**Verification with point $(X_1=1, X_2=2)$:** $\hat{y}_1 = 1 + 8/3 + 4/3 = 1 + 12/3 = 5$ ✓ (matches $y_1 = 5$)
**Verification with point $(X_1=4, X_2=2)$:** $\hat{y}_4 = 1 + 32/3 + 4/3 = 1 + 36/3 = 13$ ✓ (matches $y_4 = 13$)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the determinant calculation and the inversion step (or at least the method used — e.g., adjugate method or row reduction). Marks are awarded for intermediate steps, not just the final answer.

---

## 3.4 Assumptions of MLR (Gauss-Markov Conditions)

For the OLS estimator to be **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)**, the following must hold:

| # | Assumption | Mathematical Form | Consequence if Violated |
|---|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Linearity** | $Y = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \varepsilon$ is linear in $\beta$ | Biased estimates |
| 2 | **Independence** | $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$ | Invalid standard errors |
| 3 | **Homoscedasticity** | $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ (constant) | Inefficient estimates |
| 4 | **No Multicollinearity** | $\text{rank}(\mathbf{X}) = p+1$, $\det(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}) \neq 0$ | Unstable $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ |
| 5 | **Normality** | $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ | Invalid hypothesis tests |
| 6 | **No Autocorrelation** | Durbin-Watson statistic $\approx 2$ | Biased standard errors |

## 4.1 MLR Pipeline Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw IoT Sensor Data] --> B[Data Cleaning and Imputation]

    C --> D[Train Validation Test Split]

    F -->|Closed Form| G[Normal Equation XtX Inverse Xty]
    F -->|Iterative| H[Gradient Descent Loop]

    I --> J[Update Beta Parameters]
    J --> K{Converged}
    K -->|No| I
    K -->|Yes| L[Trained Model]
    G --> L
    L --> M[Predict on Test Set]
    M --> N[Compute R2 MSE RMSE]
    N --> O[Model Deployment to Edge Device]
```

## 4.2 Normal Equation vs Gradient Descent Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Solver for MLR] --> B{Number of Features p}
    B -->|p less than 1000| C{Need Interpretability}
    B -->|p greater than 10000| D[Use Gradient Descent]
    C -->|Yes| E[Use Normal Equation]
    C -->|No| F[Use Regularized GD]
    E --> G[Exact One Shot Solution]
    D --> H[Apply Feature Scaling]
    H --> I[Iterative Convergence]
    F --> I
    G --> J[Check Multicollinearity VIF]
    I --> J
    J --> K{VIF less than 10}
    K -->|Yes| L[Valid MLR Model]
    K -->|No| M[Apply Ridge or Drop Feature]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trained MLR Model] --> B[Compute Residuals e]
    B --> C[Residuals vs Fitted Plot]

    B --> F[Cooks Distance Leverage]
    C --> G{Linear Pattern}

```

## 4.4 Conceptual Hyperplane in 3D (Two Predictors)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3D Coordinate System] --> B[X1 axis Predictor 1]
    A --> C[X2 axis Predictor 2]
    A --> D[Y axis Response]
    B --> E[Data Points Scattered]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Best Fit Regression Plane]
    F --> G[Beta 0 Intercept at Origin]
    F --> H[Beta 1 Slope along X1]
    F --> I[Beta 2 Slope along X2]
    G --> J[Equation Y equals Beta 0 plus Beta 1 X1 plus Beta 2 X2]

    I --> J
    J --> K[Residuals e Vertical Distance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In 2D, simple linear regression fits a *line*. With two predictors ($p=2$), MLR fits a *plane* in 3D. With $p \geq 3$, it fits a *hyperplane* in $p+1$ dimensional space — impossible to visualize, but mathematically well-defined.
