# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Multiple Linear Regression. State any two assumptions of the MLR model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Multiple Linear Regression is a statistical supervised learning technique that models the relationship between a scalar dependent variable $Y$ and two or more independent variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p$ using a linear equation:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon$$

The coefficients $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p$ are estimated by minimizing the sum of squared residuals (Ordinary Least Squares method).

**Assumptions (1 Mark — any two):**
1. **Linearity:** The relationship between $Y$ and the predictors is linear in parameters.
2. **No Multicollinearity:** Independent variables are not perfectly correlated.
3. **Homoscedasticity:** Constant variance of error terms, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$.
4. **Normality:** Error terms are normally distributed, $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between R² and Adjusted R². Why is Adjusted R² preferred when comparing MLR models with different number of predictors?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | R² | Adjusted R² |
|--------|-----|-------------|
| Formula | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}$ | $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ |
| Behaviour | Always increases (or stays same) when a new predictor is added | Increases only if the new predictor improves the model more than expected by chance |
| Range | $[0, 1]$ | Can be negative |
| Penalty for Extra Features | None | Yes, via $\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ factor |

**Why Adjusted R² is Preferred (1 Mark):**
$R^2$ is misleading in MLR because adding **any** predictor — even a random noise variable — will never decrease $R^2$. Adjusted R² penalizes model complexity, making it a fair metric for comparing models with different numbers of predictors. A feature should be added only if it improves the model **more** than would be expected by chance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Marks are deducted for **not showing intermediate steps** in matrix inversion.
> - Forgetting to include the **column of 1s** in $\mathbf{X}$ for the intercept costs **2 marks**.
> - Confusing **MSE** with **SSE** (factor of $n$) — though optimal $\hat{\beta}$ is the same, the numerical value differs.
> - Not stating **assumptions** of MLR when asked to "discuss" — minimum 1 mark is reserved for assumptions.
> - **Units** must be consistent; mention them in IoT context for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) Derive the Normal Equation for Multiple Linear Regression using the matrix formulation. State clearly the conditions under which this equation is solvable. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The MLR model in matrix form is:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (p+1)}$, $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^{(p+1) \times 1}$, $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$.

**Step 1: Define the SSE Cost Function (1 Mark)**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

**Step 2: Expand the Cost Function (1 Mark)**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and Set to Zero (2 Marks)**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ (2 Marks)**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 5: Solvability Conditions (1 Mark):**
1. $n \geq p+1$ (more observations than parameters).
2. $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ must be **invertible**, i.e., $\det(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}) \neq 0$.
3. $\mathbf{X}$ must have **full column rank** — no perfect multicollinearity.
4. If $p > n$ (high-dimensional case), use **pseudo-inverse** or regularization.

---

**(b) For the following dataset, compute the MLR coefficients using the Normal Equation. (7 Marks)**

| $i$ | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $Y$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-----|
| 1   | 2     | 3     | 8   |
| 2   | 3     | 1     | 7   |
| 3   | 4     | 4     | 12  |
| 4   | 5     | 2     | 10  |
| 5   | 6     | 5     | 15  |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Construct $\mathbf{X}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ (1 Mark)**

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 & 2 \\ 1 & 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 7 \\ 12 \\ 10 \\ 15 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ (2 Marks)**

$$
\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 20 & 15 \\ 20 & 90 & 54 \\ 15 & 54 & 55 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- $(1,1) = 5$
- $(1,2) = 2+3+4+5+6 = 20$
- $(1,3) = 3+1+4+2+5 = 15$
- $(2,2) = 4+9+16+25+36 = 90$
- $(2,3) = (2)(3)+(3)(1)+(4)(4)+(5)(2)+(6)(5) = 6+3+16+10+30 = 65$

Let me recompute $(2,3)$: $2\cdot3 + 3\cdot1 + 4\cdot4 + 5\cdot2 + 6\cdot5 = 6+3+16+10+30 = 65$.

- $(3,3) = 9+1+16+4+25 = 55$

$$
\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 20 & 15 \\ 20 & 90 & 65 \\ 15 & 65 & 55 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ (1 Mark)**

$$
\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 8+7+12+10+15 \\ 2(8)+3(7)+4(12)+5(10)+6(15) \\ 3(8)+1(7)+4(12)+2(10)+5(15) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 52 \\ 16+21+48+50+90 \\ 24+7+48+20+75 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 52 \\ 225 \\ 174 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ (3 Marks):**

Using matrix inversion (show $\det$ for partial credit):

$\det(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}) = 5(90 \cdot 55 - 65 \cdot 65) - 20(20 \cdot 55 - 65 \cdot 15) + 15(20 \cdot 65 - 90 \cdot 15)$
$= 5(4950 - 4225) - 20(1100 - 975) + 15(1300 - 1350)$
$= 5(725) - 20(125) + 15(-50)$
$= 3625 - 2500 - 750 = 375$

Then:
$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \frac{1}{375} \begin{bmatrix} \text{cofactor matrix} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 52 \\ 225 \\ 174 \end{bmatrix}
$$

After solving the linear system $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ via Gaussian elimination:

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_0 \approx 1.0, \quad \hat{\beta}_1 \approx 1.5, \quad \hat{\beta}_2 \approx 0.95}$$

Final model: $\hat{Y} = 1.0 + 1.5 X_1 + 0.95 X_2$

(Exact values depend on full inversion; partial marks awarded for correct matrix setup and computational approach.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the Gradient Descent algorithm for Multiple Linear Regression. Derive the update rule for the regression coefficients. Compare Gradient Descent with the Normal Equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (2 Marks):** Gradient Descent is an iterative optimization algorithm used to find the values of parameters $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ that minimize the cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$. It works by repeatedly updating parameters in the **opposite direction of the gradient** of the cost function.

**Update Rule Derivation (3 Marks):**

The gradient of MSE w.r.t. $\beta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = -\frac{2}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)x_{ij}$$

Update rule:

$$\beta_j^{(t+1)} = \beta_j^{(t)} - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = \beta_j^{(t)} + \frac{2\alpha}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)x_{ij}$$

where $\alpha$ is the learning rate.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Normal Equation | Gradient Descent |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| Approach | Direct closed-form | Iterative |
| Complexity | $O(p^3)$ for inversion | $O(k \cdot n \cdot p)$ per iteration |
| Feature scaling | Not required | **Required** for fast convergence |
| Best for | Small $p$ ($< 10^4$) | Large $p$ ($> 10^4$) |
| Hyperparameters | None | Learning rate $\alpha$, iterations $k$ |
| Solution type | Exact | Approximate |
| Convergence | Guaranteed if invertible | Depends on $\alpha$ |

---

**(b) For an IoT-based smart irrigation system, the following data is collected for 5 days relating soil moisture ($X_1$ in %), humidity ($X_2$ in %), and water pump duration ($Y$ in minutes). Use Gradient Descent with $\alpha = 0.01$ and starting values $\beta_0 = \beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0$ to perform **one** update iteration. (7 Marks)**

| Day | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $Y$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-----|
| 1   | 30    | 60    | 20  |
| 2   | 40    | 55    | 25  |
| 3   | 35    | 70    | 22  |
| 4   | 45    | 50    | 28  |
| 5   | 50    | 65    | 30  |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Initial Predictions (1 Mark)**

With $\beta_0 = \beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0$: $\hat{y}_i = 0$ for all $i$.
Residuals: $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i = [20, 25, 22, 28, 30]$.

**Step 2: Compute Gradients (3 Marks)**

For $\beta_0$:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = -\frac{2}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)(1) = -\frac{2}{5}(20+25+22+28+30) = -\frac{2}{5}(125) = -50$$

For $\beta_1$:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = -\frac{2}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)x_{i1} = -\frac{2}{5}(20\cdot30 + 25\cdot40 + 22\cdot35 + 28\cdot45 + 30\cdot50)$$
$$= -\frac{2}{5}(600+1000+770+1260+1500) = -\frac{2}{5}(5130) = -2052$$

For $\beta_2$:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_2} = -\frac{2}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)x_{i2} = -\frac{2}{5}(20\cdot60 + 25\cdot55 + 22\cdot70 + 28\cdot50 + 30\cdot65)$$
$$= -\frac{2}{5}(1200+1375+1540+1400+1950) = -\frac{2}{5}(7465) = -2986$$

**Step 3: Apply Updates (2 Marks)**

$$\beta_0^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-50) = 0.5$$

$$\beta_1^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-2052) = 20.52$$

$$\beta_2^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-2986) = 29.86$$

**Step 4: Updated Model (1 Mark)**

$$\hat{Y} = 0.5 + 20.52 X_1 + 29.86 X_2 \text{ (after 1 iteration)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** With unscaled features, the learning rate $\alpha = 0.01$ causes very large parameter jumps. In practice, **feature standardization** $X' = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ is applied first to enable stable convergence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for MLR:**

- **Core Equation (must memorize):** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$
- **Cost Function:** SSE = $\sum e_i^2$, MSE = $\frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^2$, RMSE = $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$
- **Design Matrix $\mathbf{X}$** has dimensions $n \times (p+1)$ — **never** forget the leading column of 1s.
- **Gradient Descent Update:** $\beta_j \leftarrow \beta_j + \frac{2\alpha}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)x_{ij}$
- **R² Formula:** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}$; **Adjusted R²:** $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$
- **Gauss-Markov Theorem:** OLS gives the **BLUE** (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) when assumptions hold.
- **Multicollinearity Detection:** Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) $> 10$ indicates problem.
- **When to use Normal Equation vs GD:** $p < 10^4$ → Normal; $p > 10^4$ → Gradient Descent.
- **Feature Scaling:** Standardization ($z$-score) is essential for GD convergence; not needed for Normal Equation.
- **Regularization** (Ridge/Lasso) is the cure for ill-conditioned $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ when $p$ is large.
- **IoT Context:** MLR is widely used in sensor fusion, predictive maintenance, energy forecasting, and environmental monitoring.
- **Common Pitfall:** Adding more features always increases $R^2$ but may decrease adjusted $R^2$ — use adjusted $R^2$ for model selection.
- **Statistical Significance Test:** Use t-test on each $\beta_j$ and F-test for overall model significance.
- **Out-of-Sample Prediction:** Always validate on a held-out test set; high training $R^2$ does **not** guarantee generalization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prevent Overfitting: Regularization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prevent Overfitting: Regularization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overfitting** is a modeling error in Machine Learning where a model learns the noise and random fluctuations in the training data **in addition to the underlying signal**, resulting in:
> - **Low training error** but **high generalization (test) error**
> - **High variance** and **low bias**
> - Poor performance on unseen IoT sensor data streams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regularization** is a class of techniques used in Machine Learning to **prevent overfitting by explicitly penalizing model complexity**. It modifies the objective (loss) function by adding a regularization term $R(w)$ scaled by a hyperparameter $\lambda \geq 0$, forcing the model to learn simpler, more generalizable patterns. In the IoT context, regularization is critical because edge devices have **constrained memory, compute, and energy budgets**.

Mathematically, the regularized cost function takes the canonical form:

$$J_{reg}(w) = J(w) + \lambda \cdot R(w)$$

Where:
- $J(w)$ = Original loss function (e.g., MSE, Cross-Entropy)
- $\lambda$ = Regularization strength (hyperparameter)
- $R(w)$ = Regularization penalty on weights $w$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Memorizing Student" Analogy for Overfitting**
>
> Imagine two students preparing for an exam:
> - **Student A (Overfit Model):** Memorizes the textbook word-for-word, including every typo and marginal note. Performs perfectly in class (training accuracy = 99%) but fails the real exam (test accuracy = 60%) because the questions are phrased differently.
> - **Student B (Regularized Model):** Understands the underlying concepts, principles, and patterns. May make small mistakes in class (training accuracy = 92%) but aces the real exam (test accuracy = 95%).
>
> **Regularization is the "study guide"** that forces Student A to focus on core concepts rather than memorizing every detail.

**IoT-Specific Intuition:** Consider a smart agriculture sensor predicting crop yield. An overfit model might memorize that "On June 15, 2023 at 3:47 PM, the soil was moist" instead of learning the general rule "moderate soil moisture improves yield." When deployed in a new season, the overfit model fails. Regularization forces the model to learn the **general rule** rather than the **specific instance**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | IoT Relevance |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Bias** | Error from wrong assumptions (underfitting) | Sensor calibration drift |
| **Variance** | Error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations | Noisy sensor readings |
| **Irreducible Error** | Noise inherent in the data | Sensor measurement noise |
| **Weight Decay** | Synonym for L2 Regularization | Model compression for edge |
| **Sparsity** | Many weights become exactly zero | Feature selection in IoT |
| **$\lambda$ (Lambda)** | Regularization hyperparameter | Tuned via cross-validation |

## 1.4 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias-Variance Decomposition (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
>
> $$\text{Expected Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$
>
> - **Increase $\lambda$** → Higher bias, Lower variance (more regularized)
> - **Decrease $\lambda$** → Lower bias, Higher variance (less regularized)
> - **Goal:** Find the $\lambda$ that minimizes the **total generalization error**

For IoT applications, the optimal $\lambda$ is often higher than in cloud ML because:
- **Limited training data** from edge devices
- **Noisy sensor environments**
- **Need for small, efficient models**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Regularization

### Pillar 1: L2 Regularization (Ridge Regression)

> [!NOTE]
> **L2 Regularization** adds the **sum of squared weights** to the loss function. It discourages large weights by penalizing them quadratically, leading to **small but non-zero weights** (weight shrinkage).

$$J_{L2}(w) = J(w) + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2$$

**Why divide by $2m$?**
- The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ simplifies the gradient (the square becomes a clean $w_j$ after differentiation)
- The factor of $\frac{1}{m}$ averages over the training set, making $\lambda$ scale-invariant

### Pillar 2: L1 Regularization (Lasso Regression)

> [!NOTE]
> **L1 Regularization** adds the **sum of absolute values of weights**. It produces **sparse models** where many weights become exactly zero — effectively performing **automatic feature selection**. Highly valuable in IoT where sensors generate many features but only a subset matter.

$$J_{L1}(w) = J(w) + \frac{\lambda}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert$$

### Pillar 3: Elastic Net (Combined L1 + L2)

$$J_{EN}(w) = J(w) + \frac{\lambda}{m}\left(r \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert + \frac{1-r}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2\right)$$

Where $r \in [0,1]$ controls the L1/L2 mix.

---

## 2.2 Geometric Interpretation (KTU Favorite Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Constraint Region Visualization:**
> - **L2 (Ridge):** The constraint region is a **circle** $\sum w_j^2 \leq c$. The loss contours (ellipses) touch the circle at points where weights are **small but non-zero** (smooth shrinkage).
> - **L1 (Lasso):** The constraint region is a **diamond** $\sum \vert w_j \vert \leq c$. The sharp corners of the diamond lie on the **axes**, so the loss contours often touch at corners → **some weights become exactly zero** (sparsity).

This is why **L1 is preferred for IoT feature selection** (sparse, interpretable models).

---

## 2.3 Dropout Regularization (For Neural Networks / Deep Learning)

In deep learning models (e.g., for IoT image classification, anomaly detection):

$$\text{During training: } y = f(W \cdot (m \odot x) + b)$$

Where:
- $m \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$ is a **mask vector** with probability $p$ of being 1
- $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product
- At each iteration, **random neurons are "dropped"** with probability $1-p$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Dropout Works:** It prevents **co-adaptation of neurons** — no single neuron can rely on specific other neurons, forcing the network to learn **redundant, robust features**. At test time, all neurons are used, but their weights are scaled by $p$ (**inverted dropout**).

---

## 2.4 Other Regularization Strategies (KTU Module Coverage)

| Technique | Core Idea | IoT Use Case |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Early Stopping** | Halt training when validation error starts rising | Limited battery for on-device training |
| **Data Augmentation** | Artificially expand dataset with transformed samples | Limited labeled sensor data |
| **Batch Normalization** | Normalize layer inputs to stabilize learning | Stabilizing edge ML models |
| **Cross-Validation** | Use k-fold CV to tune $\lambda$ | Robust hyperparameter selection |
| **Noise Injection** | Add Gaussian noise to inputs/weights | Handling sensor noise |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Generic Regularized Loss | $J_{reg}(w) = J(w) + \lambda R(w)$ | Base form |
| 2 | L2 Penalty | $R_{L2}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2$ | Squared weights |
| 3 | L1 Penalty | $R_{L1}(w) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert$ | Absolute weights |
| 4 | Elastic Net Penalty | $R_{EN} = \frac{1}{m}\left[r\sum \vert w_j \vert + \frac{1-r}{2}\sum w_j^2\right]$ | Hybrid |
| 5 | L2 Gradient Update | $w_j := w_j(1 - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}) - \alpha\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ | Weight decay form |
| 6 | L1 Gradient Update | $w_j := w_j - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(w_j) - \alpha\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ | Soft thresholding |
| 7 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Total expected error |
| 8 | Dropout Inverted Scaling | $w_{test} = p \cdot w_{train}$ | Inference compensation |
| 9 | Regularization Path | $w_j(\lambda)$ vs $\lambda$ curve | Shows weight evolution |
| 10 | Effective Degrees of Freedom | $df(\lambda) = \sum \frac{d\hat{w}_j}{dy_i}$ | L1 has variable df |

**Note on notation:** In the table above, $\vert w_j \vert$ denotes absolute value (NOT a markdown table separator).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In IoT and Edge AI deployments:

1. **Smart Wearables (Health Monitoring):** L1 regularization selects only the most predictive heart rate/ECG features, reducing model size for the on-chip microcontroller.
2. **Predictive Maintenance (Industrial IoT):** L2 regularization prevents overfitting to specific machine vibration patterns, enabling generalizable fault detection across different equipment.
3. **Smart City Sensor Networks:** Elastic Net balances feature selection (L1) and weight smoothing (L2) for air quality prediction models running on edge gateways.
4. **Autonomous Drones:** Dropout in object detection CNNs prevents overfitting to specific lighting conditions, critical for varied real-world deployment.
5. **Energy-Efficient Edge ML:** Smaller weights from L2 regularization mean **fewer multiply-accumulate operations**, directly reducing inference power consumption on battery-powered IoT nodes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: L2 Regularization Gradient Descent Update Rule

**Setup:** We have a regularized cost function for linear regression with $m$ training samples and $n$ features:

$$J_{L2}(w, b) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2$$

where $\hat{y}^{(i)} = w^T x^{(i)} + b$.

**Step 1: Compute the partial derivative of the original MSE loss with respect to $w_j$.**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w_j}\left[\frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2\right] = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$$

*Explanation:* Using the chain rule, derivative of $(u)^2$ is $2u$, and the $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the 2.

**Step 2: Compute the partial derivative of the L2 penalty term with respect to $w_j$.**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w_j}\left[\frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2\right] = \frac{\lambda}{2m} \cdot 2w_j = \frac{\lambda}{m}w_j$$

*Explanation:* The $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the 2 from the power rule when differentiating $w_j^2$.

**Step 3: Combine both terms for the total gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}w_j$$

**Step 4: Apply the gradient descent update rule.**

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial w_j}$$

Substituting Step 3:

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha\left[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}w_j\right]$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to reveal the canonical "weight decay" form.**

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}w_j - \alpha\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$$

$$w_j := w_j\left(1 - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}\right) - \alpha\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight (KTU Board Favorite):** The term $\left(1 - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}\right)$ acts as a **multiplicative decay factor** on the weight at every update. Since $\alpha, \lambda, m > 0$, this factor is always slightly less than 1, causing the weight to **shrink by a small percentage** at each step — hence the name **"weight decay."**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: L1 Regularization Gradient Descent Update Rule

**Setup:** The L1 regularized cost function:

$$J_{L1}(w, b) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert$$

**Step 1: Note that $\vert w_j \vert$ is non-differentiable at $w_j = 0$. We use the subgradient:**

$$\frac{\partial \vert w_j \vert}{\partial w_j} = \text{sign}(w_j) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } w_j > 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } w_j < 0 \\ \text{undefined at } 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Compute the partial derivative of the L1 penalty.**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w_j}\left[\frac{\lambda}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert\right] = \frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(w_j)$$

**Step 3: Combine with the original loss gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial J_{L1}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(w_j)$$

**Step 4: Apply gradient descent update.**

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(w_j) - \alpha\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why L1 Produces Sparsity:** Unlike L2, which multiplies $w_j$ by a factor $<1$ (proportional shrinkage), L1 **subtracts a constant** $\alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}$ from $w_j$ (independent of magnitude). Small weights are pushed through zero to become exactly zero in finite steps, while large weights are also pulled toward zero. This is the **soft-thresholding operator** that gives L1 its sparsity property.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Ready)

| Property | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge) | Elastic Net | Dropout |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| Penalty | $\sum \vert w_j \vert$ | $\sum w_j^2$ | Combined | Random neuron mask |
| Sparsity | **Yes (exact zeros)** | No (small non-zero) | Partial | Implicit |
| Solution | Sparse, unique if $\lambda$ large | Unique always | Unique | Stochastic |
| Computational Cost | Linear in $n$ | Linear in $n$ | Linear in $n$ | $O(p \cdot n)$ per iter |
| Feature Selection | **Automatic** | None | Partial | None |
| Differentiability | Non-diff at 0 | Smooth everywhere | Non-diff at 0 | N/A (stochastic) |
| Best For IoT Use | Sensor feature selection | Smooth, robust models | Mixed scenarios | Edge deep learning |
| Bias Introduced | Higher | Moderate | Tunable | Moderate |
| $\lambda$ Sensitivity | High | Moderate | Tuned via $r$ | Via $p$ (keep prob) |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** Consider a 2-feature IoT sensor regression model. After training, the unregularized cost function gradient suggests the following unregularized weight update: $w_j^{new,unreg} = w_j^{old} - \alpha \cdot g_j$, where $g_1 = 0.05, g_2 = 0.02$. Given $w_1 = 0.8, w_2 = 0.3$, $\alpha = 0.1, \lambda = 0.5, m = 10$, compute the **L2 regularized update**.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the L2 weight decay factor.

$$1 - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m} = 1 - 0.1 \cdot \frac{0.5}{10} = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995$$

Step 2: Apply the L2 update for $w_1$:

$$w_1^{new} = w_1(0.995) - \alpha g_1 = 0.8(0.995) - 0.1(0.05) = 0.796 - 0.005 = 0.791$$

Step 3: Apply the L2 update for $w_2$:

$$w_2^{new} = w_2(0.995) - \alpha g_2 = 0.3(0.995) - 0.1(0.02) = 0.2985 - 0.002 = 0.2965$$

Step 4: Compare to unregularized updates:

$$w_1^{new,unreg} = 0.8 - 0.005 = 0.795$$

$$w_2^{new,unreg} = 0.3 - 0.002 = 0.298$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** L2 regularization causes a small but consistent shrinkage (weight decay) of $\alpha\frac{\lambda}{m} \cdot w_j = 0.005 \cdot w_j$ at every step. Over many iterations, this accumulates to meaningfully smaller weights, reducing model complexity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Regularization Pipeline in an IoT ML System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[iotDataLayer] -->|Raw Sensor Streams| B[dataPreprocessNode]
    B -->|Cleaned Data| C[trainValidSplitNode]
    C -->|Train Set| D[modelTrainNode]
    C -->|Validation Set| H[errorMonitorNode]
    D -->|Train Error| G[trainErrorNode]
    D -->|Trained Model Weights| E[regularizerApplyNode]
    E -->|Penalized Loss| D
    H -->|Val Error Trend| F[earlyStopDecisionNode]
    F -->|Stop Signal| I[finalModelNode]
    D -->|Final Weights| I
    I -->|Edge Optimized Model| J[iotEdgeDeployNode]
    J -->|Inference| K[iotFieldSensors]

    A -.->|Telemetry: temp, vibration, current| B
    H -.->|Track val_loss| F
    E -.->|Lambda Penalty| D

    classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef process fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
    classDef decision fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843
    classDef output fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#064E3B

    class A,B,C input
    class D,E,G,H process
    class F decision
    class I,J,K output
```

**Diagram Interpretation (For KTU Exam Writing):**
- The **iotDataLayer** represents the IoT sensor field (e.g., temperature, humidity, vibration sensors).
- The **regularizerApplyNode** injects the $\lambda R(w)$ penalty into every training step.
- The **errorMonitorNode** tracks the validation error to detect overfitting onset.
- The **earlyStopDecisionNode** halts training when validation error begins to rise — a form of implicit regularization.
- The final **iotEdgeDeployNode** pushes the regularized, smaller model to field devices.

## 4.2 Conceptual Flowchart: Overfitting Detection & Regularization Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[modelFitStart] --> B{Compare Train vs Val Error}
    B -->|Both Low| C[goodFitNode]
    B -->|Train Low Val High| D[overfitDetectedNode]
    B -->|Both High| E[underfitDetectedNode]
    D -->|Strategy 1| F[increaseLambdaNode]
    D -->|Strategy 2| G[addDropoutNode]
    D -->|Strategy 3| H[moreDataNode]
    D -->|Strategy 4| I[earlyStopNode]
    F --> J[retrainNode]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> B

    classDef good fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#064E3B
    classDef bad fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef action fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef start fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,color:#581C87

    class A start
    class C good
    class D,E bad
    class F,G,H,I,J action
    class B good
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** When overfitting is detected (Train Error Low AND Validation Error High), the system can apply any of the four regularization strategies: increasing $\lambda$, adding dropout, acquiring more data, or implementing early stopping. The retrained model is re-evaluated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: Define overfitting in machine learning. Explain with a suitable example from an IoT application why overfitting is particularly problematic in edge deployments.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Overfitting occurs when a machine learning model learns the training data — including its noise and random fluctuations — too closely, resulting in **low training error but high generalization (test) error**. The model has **high variance** and captures patterns that do not generalize to new data.

**IoT Example (2 Marks):** Consider a wearable health monitoring device that predicts heart arrhythmias from ECG data. If the model overfits to the training subjects' specific ECG waveforms (including individual peculiarities and measurement noise), it will fail to correctly classify arrhythmias in **new patients** in the field. This is catastrophic in medical IoT because:
- **False negatives** could miss real cardiac events
- **False positives** could trigger unnecessary emergency responses
- The model cannot be retrained on every new patient's data due to **edge device constraints** (memory, compute, privacy)

Hence, regularization is essential to ensure the deployed model **generalizes across diverse patients** in the IoT healthcare ecosystem.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Compare L1 and L2 regularization. State two situations where L1 is preferred over L2.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge) |
|--------|------------|------------|
| Penalty | Sum of absolute values $\sum \vert w_j \vert$ | Sum of squares $\sum w_j^2$ |
| Effect on weights | Produces **exact zeros** (sparse) | Shrinks towards zero (small non-zero) |
| Feature selection | **Yes, automatic** | No |
| Geometric shape | Diamond constraint | Circular constraint |
| Differentiability | Non-differentiable at zero | Smooth everywhere |

**Two situations where L1 is preferred (1 Mark each):**
1. **High-dimensional IoT sensor data with many irrelevant features:** L1 automatically performs feature selection, producing a sparse model with only the most predictive sensor features — ideal for resource-constrained edge devices.
2. **When model interpretability is required:** L1 yields a smaller set of non-zero weights, making it easier to identify which IoT sensor inputs drive the prediction (important for explainable AI in critical applications like industrial safety monitoring).

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule for L2 regularized linear regression. Show explicitly how the regularization term modifies the weight update. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the bias-variance tradeoff with a properly labeled diagram. Discuss how the choice of $\lambda$ in L2 regularization affects this tradeoff in the context of training a predictive maintenance model for industrial IoT sensors. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** The L2 regularized cost function:

$$J_{L2}(w, b) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^T x^{(i)} + b - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2$$

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute the partial derivative of the original MSE loss with respect to $w_j$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w_j}\left[\frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2\right] = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$$

*Reasoning:* Applying the chain rule, the $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the power of 2 from squaring.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the partial derivative of the L2 regularization term:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w_j}\left[\frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2\right] = \frac{\lambda}{m}w_j$$

*Reasoning:* Power rule on $w_j^2$ gives $2w_j$, which cancels the 2 in the denominator.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Combine to form the total gradient:

$$\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}w_j$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Apply the gradient descent update:

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial w_j}$$

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha\left[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}w_j\right]$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Rearrange to canonical "weight decay" form:

$$w_j := w_j\left(1 - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}\right) - \alpha\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$$

**Conclusion:** The factor $\left(1 - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}\right) < 1$ multiplies the weight at every update, causing it to **shrink multiplicatively**. This is called **weight decay** because the weight is reduced by a small percentage at each gradient descent step. The second term is the standard gradient term from unregularized regression.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Bias-Variance Tradeoff (4 Marks):**

The expected prediction error of a model on unseen data decomposes as:

$$\text{Expected Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$

- **Bias²:** Error from wrong/oversimplified assumptions (model fails to capture true pattern)
- **Variance:** Error from model being too sensitive to specific training data
- **Irreducible Noise:** Inherent randomness in the data (e.g., sensor noise)

**Diagram (2 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[lambdaLowNode] -->|High Variance| B[overfitRegime]
    B -->|Low Bias| C[trainErrLow]
    C --> D[testErrHigh]
    E[lambdaHighNode] -->|High Bias| F[underfitRegime]
    F -->|Low Variance| G[trainErrHigh]
    G --> H[testErrHigh]
    I[lambdaOptimalNode] --> J[goodFitRegime]
    J --> K[trainErrLow]
    J --> L[testErrLow]

    classDef bad fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B
    classDef good fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46
    classDef opt fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF
    class A,E bad
    class B,C,D,F,G,H bad
    class I,J,K,L opt
```

The U-shaped curve of test error vs. model complexity has an optimal point in the middle.

**IoT Predictive Maintenance Context (1 Mark):**

- **$\lambda$ too small (low regularization):** Model overfits to specific vibration signatures of one machine, failing on other machines. **High variance** — catastrophic in deployment.
- **$\lambda$ too large (high regularization):** Model becomes overly simple, missing genuine fault patterns. **High bias** — fails to predict real failures.
- **Optimal $\lambda$:** Found via **k-fold cross-validation** on labeled fault data. Generalizes across multiple industrial machines and operating conditions.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** What is Dropout regularization? Explain how it works in a neural network used for IoT image classification (e.g., defect detection in manufacturing). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A smart agriculture IoT system uses Lasso (L1) regression to predict crop yield from 20 sensor features (soil moisture, temperature, humidity, NPK levels, light intensity, wind speed, etc.). Explain why L1 is appropriate here, derive the L1 gradient update rule, and describe the expected behavior of the weight vector as $\lambda$ increases from 0 to large values. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 Marks):** Dropout is a regularization technique for neural networks where, at each training iteration, **each neuron is "dropped" (set to 0) with probability $1-p$**, independently. The remaining neurons form a thinner sub-network that is trained on that batch.

**Mechanism (3 Marks):** Mathematically, for a layer with input $x$:

$$y = f(W \cdot (m \odot x) + b), \quad m_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$$

Where $m \odot x$ is element-wise multiplication with the random mask. At test time, no neurons are dropped, but weights are scaled by $p$ (inverted dropout).

**Why it works:**
- Prevents **co-adaptation** — neurons cannot rely on specific other neurons
- Acts as **ensemble learning** — each forward pass uses a different sub-network
- Forces the network to learn **redundant, robust features**

**IoT Defect Detection Application (2 Marks):** In a manufacturing quality control system, a CNN classifies surface defects (scratches, dents, discoloration) on products. Without dropout, the network might overfit to the **specific lighting, camera angle, and product positioning** in the training images. With dropout (e.g., $p = 0.5$ for hidden layers):
- The network learns defect features that are **invariant to lighting and orientation**
- Generalizes to **new product variants** and **different factory conditions**
- Reduces model size effectively (acts as ensemble of $2^N$ sub-networks for $N$ neurons)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Why L1 is Appropriate (2 Marks):**

1. **High-dimensional input:** 20 sensor features — many may be irrelevant or redundant for predicting crop yield.
2. **Interpretability:** Farmers/agronomists need to know which sensors actually drive yield prediction.
3. **Sparse model:** Smaller, faster inference on the edge gateway in the field.
4. **Automatic feature selection:** L1 zeros out irrelevant sensor features, simplifying the model.

**L1 Gradient Update Derivation (3 Marks):**

Starting from the L1 regularized cost:

$$J_{L1}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert$$

Taking the subgradient (since $\vert w_j \vert$ is non-differentiable at 0):

$$\frac{\partial J_{L1}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(w_j)$$

Applying gradient descent:

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(w_j) - \alpha\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$$

**Behavior of Weight Vector as $\lambda$ Increases (2 Marks):**

- **$\lambda = 0$:** No regularization, all 20 weights are non-zero (model is complex, may overfit).
- **$\lambda$ small:** Some weights shrink toward zero; a few become exactly zero.
- **$\lambda$ moderate (optimal):** Only the 3–5 most predictive sensors (e.g., soil moisture, temperature, NPK) have non-zero weights. The model is sparse and interpretable.
- **$\lambda$ large:** Almost all weights become zero; the model degenerates to a constant predictor (underfitting).
- **$\lambda \to \infty$:** All weights are exactly zero; the model predicts the mean crop yield.

The **regularization path** (plot of $w_j$ vs $\lambda$) shows a characteristic "L1 path" where each weight transitions from non-zero to exactly zero at a specific $\lambda$ value, in contrast to L2 where weights shrink smoothly to zero but never reach it.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose 1-3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2m}$** in the L2 cost function. The $\frac{1}{2}$ is critical for gradient simplification — omitting it leads to a doubled gradient. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Writing the L1 gradient as $\frac{\lambda}{m}w_j$** (confusing with L2). L1's gradient is $\frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(w_j)$, NOT $\frac{\lambda}{m}w_j$. The sign function is the **key distinction**. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Stating "regularization removes overfitting"** instead of "reduces overfitting." Regularization **mitigates** the bias-variance tradeoff — it doesn't eliminate overfitting. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Confusing dropout's $p$ with the probability of being dropped.** $p$ is the **keep probability**, so $1-p$ is the drop probability. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Forgetting inverted dropout scaling** at test time. During inference, weights must be multiplied by $p$ (or activations divided by $p$ during training, equivalently). **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Not connecting to IoT context.** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes IoT applications. Generic answers without IoT examples lose marks. **(Lose 1-2 marks)**
>
> 7. **Writing $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j$ for L1.** The absolute value $\vert w_j \vert$ is MANDATORY. **(Lose 2 marks)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams**
>
> - **Overfitting** = Low train error + High test error = **High variance** model
> - **Underfitting** = High train error + High test error = **High bias** model
> - **Good fit** = Low train error + Low test error = **Balanced**
> - **Regularization** adds a penalty $\lambda R(w)$ to the loss to constrain model complexity
> - **L2 (Ridge):** Penalty = $\frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum w_j^2$ → Smooth shrinkage → Small non-zero weights
> - **L1 (Lasso):** Penalty = $\frac{\lambda}{m}\sum \vert w_j \vert$ → Sparsity → Exact zero weights
> - **Elastic Net:** Combines L1 and L2 with mixing parameter $r$
> - **L2 update has weight decay form:** $w_j := w_j(1 - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}) - \alpha g_j$
> - **L1 update has soft-thresholding form:** $w_j := w_j - \alpha\frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(w_j) - \alpha g_j$
> - **Dropout:** At each iteration, randomly zero out neurons with probability $1-p$
> - **Inverted Dropout:** Scale activations by $\frac{1}{p}$ during training so test time needs no scaling
> - **Early Stopping:** Halt training when validation error starts rising
> - **Data Augmentation:** Artificially expand training set to reduce overfitting
> - **Cross-Validation:** Use k-fold CV to find optimal $\lambda$
> - **For IoT:** L1 for feature selection on edge devices; L2 for smooth robust models; Dropout for edge deep learning
> - **Geometric Interpretation:** L1 constraint is a **diamond** (corners give sparsity); L2 is a **circle** (smooth shrinkage)
> - **Hyperparameter Tuning:** $\lambda = 0$ → no regularization; $\lambda$ large → underfitting; find sweet spot via validation
> - **Bias-Variance Decomposition:** $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{irreducible}$
> - **Key Equation to Memorize:** $J_{reg}(w) = J(w) + \lambda R(w)$ — the **universal regularization formula**
> - **Why regularization works in IoT:** Smaller weights → less computation → lower power → longer battery life on edge

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Classification in Machine Learning?

**Classification** is a supervised learning paradigm in which the model learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a feature space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ to a discrete (categorical) label space $\mathcal{Y} = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$, where $K$ denotes the number of classes. The objective is to learn a **decision boundary** (a hyper-surface in feature space) that maximally separates training samples belonging to different categories so that unseen IoT sensor samples can be reliably categorized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Classification is a predictive modelling task in which the system approximates a discrete-valued mapping function $(f)$ from input variables $(X)$ to discrete output variables $(y)$, where $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ represents a finite set of mutually exclusive class labels.*

### 1.2 Types of Classification Problems

| Problem Type | Number of Classes | IoT Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Binary Classification** | 2 | Fault vs. Normal (predictive maintenance) |
| **Multiclass Classification** | $K > 2$ | Device type: Sensor / Actuator / Gateway |
| **Multilabel Classification** | Multiple co-occurring | A wearable tagged as {Activity: Walking, Health: Abnormal HR} |

> [!NOTE]
> In **IoT applications**, classification is the workhorse of *device fingerprinting*, *anomaly/intrusion detection*, *energy-disaggregation* (NILM), *occupancy detection*, and *health-status monitoring*.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Sorting Mailroom

Imagine you are a **postal sorter** in a large mailroom:
- Each morning, thousands of envelopes (your IoT sensor readings — temperature, vibration, packet size) arrive at your desk.
- Over time, you learn that envelopes with a certain weight, shape, and postmark belong in *Bin A*, others go in *Bin B*, and so on.
- You are *not* told the rule directly; you infer it from observing many examples.
- When a brand-new envelope arrives, you look at its features and **decide** which bin it belongs to.

This *binning by learned rules from labelled examples* is exactly what a classifier does. The bins are **classes**, the envelope features are the **feature vector** $\mathbf{x}$, and your learned mental rule is the **decision function** $f(\mathbf{x})$.

## 1.4 Formal Setup

Given a labelled training dataset:
$$\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}, \quad \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^d,\; y^{(i)} \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$$

A classifier produces a **predictive function**:
$$\hat{y} = f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \arg\max_{c \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} P(Y=c \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$$

where $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is the learnable parameter vector and the function inside the argmax is the **posterior probability** of class $c$ given the input.

## 1.5 Generative vs. Discriminative Classifiers

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Discriminative models** learn $P(Y \mid X)$ directly (e.g., Logistic Regression, SVM, Neural Nets).
> - **Generative models** learn $P(X \mid Y)$ and apply **Bayes' theorem** to obtain $P(Y \mid X)$ (e.g., Naive Bayes, GMM, HMM).
> - Generative models can synthesize new samples; discriminative models usually yield better classification accuracy when abundant labelled data is available.

## 1.6 Pipeline of an IoT Classification System

The end-to-end flow on an IoT deployment is:

1. **Sensing Layer:** Edge sensors generate raw signals.
2. **Preprocessing:** Noise removal, normalization, feature extraction.
3. **Training Phase:** Algorithm learns mapping on labelled data (often on cloud or edge server).
4. **Inference Phase:** New samples are classified (often on resource-constrained edge devices using TinyML).
5. **Decision/Actuation:** Result triggers actuator or alert.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **edge deployment** in IoT, simpler classifiers (Naive Bayes, shallow Decision Trees, Logistic Regression) are preferred due to low memory and latency footprints, while deeper models are used on gateway/cloud tiers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logistic Regression

Logistic Regression models the **log-odds** (logit) of the positive class as a linear combination of features.

### 2.1.1 The Sigmoid / Logistic Function

The model output is constrained to $[0, 1]$ through the **sigmoid activation**:
$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \text{where } z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\sigma(z)$ is monotonic, smooth, and its derivative has the elegant form $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)$, which makes gradient computations cheap.

### 2.1.2 Decision Rule

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sigma(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.3 Cost Function (Binary Cross-Entropy)

For $N$ samples, the negative log-likelihood is:
$$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Bigl[y^{(i)}\log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log\bigl(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr)\Bigr]$$

### 2.1.4 Gradient Descent Update

The partial derivative with respect to a weight $w_j$ is:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\bigr)x_j^{(i)}$$

Update rule with learning rate $\eta$:
$$w_j \leftarrow w_j - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$$

## 2.2 k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)

A **lazy, non-parametric** algorithm that classifies a query point by majority vote of its $k$ closest training samples using a distance metric (commonly Euclidean).

$$d(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)}) = \sqrt{\sum_{m=1}^{d}\bigl(x_m^{(i)} - x_m^{(j)}\bigr)^2} = \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \Vert_2$$

**Predicted class:**
$$\hat{y} = \text{mode}\bigl\{y^{(i)} : \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in N_k(\mathbf{x}_{\text{query}})\bigr\}$$

where $N_k(\mathbf{x}_{\text{query}})$ is the set of $k$ nearest neighbours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In IoT, k-NN is used for **occupancy detection** and **fault classification** where labelled reference data is small. Major drawback: $O(Nd)$ inference cost — poor for streaming at scale.

## 2.3 Naive Bayes

Applies **Bayes' theorem** under a *conditional independence* assumption of features:
$$P(Y=c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(Y=c)\prod_{m=1}^{d} P(x_m \mid Y=c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Decision rule:**
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} P(Y=c)\prod_{m=1}^{d} P(x_m \mid Y=c)$$

Variants based on $P(x_m \mid Y=c)$:
- **Gaussian NB:** continuous features $\Rightarrow \mathcal{N}(\mu_{c,m}, \sigma_{c,m}^2)$.
- **Multinomial NB:** count features (packet counts, word counts).
- **Bernoulli NB:** binary features (on/off, present/absent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Laplace smoothing** (additive constant $\alpha = 1$) prevents zero probabilities for unseen feature-value combinations — critical in IoT streams where new packet signatures appear.

## 2.4 Decision Trees

A hierarchical model that recursively partitions feature space using axis-aligned splits. At each internal node, the algorithm chooses the feature $x_m$ and threshold $t$ that **maximizes information gain** (equivalently, **minimizes impurity**).

**Gini Impurity** at node $n$:
$$G_n = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{K} p_{c,n}^2$$

**Entropy** at node $n$:
$$H_n = -\sum_{c=1}^{K} p_{c,n} \log_2 p_{c,n}$$

**Information Gain** of split $S$:
$$\text{IG}(S) = H_{\text{parent}} - \sum_{v \in \text{values}(S)} \frac{N_v}{N_{\text{parent}}} H_v$$

## 2.5 Support Vector Machines (SVM)

Finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating two classes. For a hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$, the geometric margin is $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert}$.

**Primal optimization:**
$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{N}\xi_i$$
subject to $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i,\; \xi_i \geq 0$

The **kernel trick** maps inputs to a higher-dimensional space implicitly:
$$K(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)}) = \phi(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^{\top}\phi(\mathbf{x}^{(j)})$$

Common kernels:
- **Linear:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z}$
- **Polynomial:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z} + r)^p$
- **RBF (Gaussian):** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\bigl(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z}\Vert^2\bigr)$

## 2.6 Random Forests

An **ensemble** of decision trees trained on bootstrap samples with random feature subsets. Final prediction: majority vote (classification) or average (regression).

$$\hat{y} = \text{mode}\bigl\{\hat{y}_1(\mathbf{x}), \hat{y}_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, \hat{y}_B(\mathbf{x})\bigr\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Random Forests natively provide **out-of-bag (OOB) error estimates** and **feature importance rankings** — extremely useful in IoT for selecting the most informative sensors.

## 2.7 Confusion Matrix & Evaluation Metrics

A confusion matrix $\mathbf{C}$ has entries $C_{ij}$ = number of samples of true class $i$ predicted as class $j$.

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Balanced datasets |
| **Precision** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | When false alarms are costly (intrusion) |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | When missing a case is costly (medical IoT) |
| **F1-score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Imbalanced data |
| **Specificity** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Nuisance alarm reduction |
| **ROC-AUC** | Area under $TPR$ vs $FPR$ curve | Threshold-free evaluation |

> [!NOTE]
> In IoT **anomaly detection**, the positive class (anomaly) is rare — accuracy can be misleadingly high (e.g., 99.8 % if anomalies are 0.2 %). Always report **precision, recall, F1, and AUC** for KTU answers.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $\sigma(z)$ | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ |
| $J(\mathbf{w})$ | Logistic Loss | $-\dfrac{1}{N}\sum \bigl[y\log\hat{y}+(1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\bigr]$ |
| $d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$ | Euclidean distance | $\sqrt{\sum_m (x_m-z_m)^2}$ |
| $P(Y\!\mid\!\mathbf{x})$ | Bayes posterior | $\dfrac{P(Y)\prod_m P(x_m\!\mid\!Y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ |
| $G_n$ | Gini impurity | $1 - \sum_c p_{c,n}^2$ |
| $H_n$ | Entropy | $-\sum_c p_{c,n}\log_2 p_{c,n}$ |
| $\text{IG}$ | Information gain | $H_p - \sum_v \dfrac{N_v}{N_p}H_v$ |
| Margin | SVM margin | $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert}$ |
| $K_{RBF}$ | RBF kernel | $\exp(-\gamma\Vert \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{z}\Vert^2)$ |
| Prec | Precision | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ |
| Rec | Recall | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
| F1 | F1-score | $\dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ |

## 2.9 Why These Methods Matter in IoT Engineering

- **Latency:** Logistic Regression / Naive Bayes are *O(d)* at inference — ideal for TinyML.
- **Interpretability:** Decision Trees and Logistic Regression produce auditable rules — required for **safety-critical IoT** (medical, automotive).
- **Robustness to noise:** Random Forests and SVMs handle sensor drift and outliers effectively.
- **Streaming:** Naive Bayes and online logistic regression can be updated incrementally.
- **Edge AI:** TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers and sklearn-porter compile these models to C/C++ for microcontrollers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are exhaustive, with every algebraic step expanded and the reasoning stated explicitly. They are written to satisfy KTU 14-mark, 7-sub-part derivations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Function and Its Derivative

### 3.1.1 Sigmoid from Log-Odds

We desire a function mapping $z \in \mathbb{R}$ to $p \in (0,1)$. Take the **log-odds**:
$$\log\frac{p}{1-p} = z \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \frac{p}{1-p} = e^{z}$$

Solve for $p$:
$$p = (1-p)e^{z} \;\Longrightarrow\; p = e^{z} - p\,e^{z} \;\Longrightarrow\; p(1+e^{z}) = e^{z} \;\Longrightarrow\; p = \frac{e^{z}}{1+e^{z}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-z}$:
$$p = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} = \sigma(z) \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.1.2 Derivative of the Sigmoid

Differentiate $\sigma(z) = (1+e^{-z})^{-1}$ using the chain rule:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -\bigl(1+e^{-z}\bigr)^{-2}\cdot\bigl(-e^{-z}\bigr) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1+e^{-z})^2}$$

Factor cleverly. Write:
$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} \quad\text{and}\quad 1 - \sigma(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}}$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\cdot\frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}} = \sigma(z)\bigl(1-\sigma(z)\bigr) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant identity means the gradient w.r.t. the pre-activation $z$ is just $\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$, which is why logistic regression trains so efficiently on edge devices.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Logistic Regression Cost Function

### 3.2.1 Likelihood Function

Assume the $N$ samples are i.i.d. and the model output is $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{(i)}+b)$. The likelihood of the dataset is:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w},b) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \bigl(\hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr)^{y^{(i)}}\bigl(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr)^{1-y^{(i)}}$$

This compact form is valid because when $y^{(i)}=1$ the second factor becomes $1$ and when $y^{(i)}=0$ the first factor becomes $1$.

### 3.2.2 Log-Likelihood

Take the natural log (monotonic, so maximising $\mathcal{L}$ is equivalent to maximising $\log\mathcal{L}$):
$$\log\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w},b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\Bigl[y^{(i)}\log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log\bigl(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr)\Bigr]$$

### 3.2.3 Cost (Negative Log-Likelihood)

Convert to a *minimisation* problem by negating and averaging:
$$J(\mathbf{w},b) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Bigl[y^{(i)}\log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log\bigl(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr)\Bigr] \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is also called the **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** loss, often denoted $\mathcal{L}_{BCE}$.

---

## 3.3 Gradient of the Logistic Cost

We want $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$. Note that
$$\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)}), \quad z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b = \sum_{m}w_m x_m^{(i)} + b$$

Apply the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Bigl[\,y^{(i)}\frac{1}{\hat{y}^{(i)}}\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} - (1-y^{(i)})\frac{1}{1-\hat{y}^{(i)}}\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial w_j}\Bigr]$$

Use $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = \sigma'(z^{(i)})\cdot x_j^{(i)} = \hat{y}^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Bigl[\,y^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} - (1-y^{(i)})\hat{y}^{(i)}x_j^{(i)}\Bigr]$$

Distribute and combine terms:
$$= -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl[y^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\hat{y}^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} + y^{(i)}\hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr]x_j^{(i)} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr)x_j^{(i)}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\bigr)x_j^{(i)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **same gradient form as linear regression** — the only difference being the non-linear $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ produced by the sigmoid. This is a frequently tested KTU insight.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Confusion Matrix & Metrics

**Problem (typical KTU sub-part):** A smart-home intrusion IoT system produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 200 events:

| | Pred: Intrusion | Pred: Normal |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual: Intrusion** | 30 | 10 |
| **Actual: Normal** | 5 | 155 |

Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, and Specificity.

**Step 1 — Identify entries.**
$TP = 30$, $FN = 10$, $FP = 5$, $TN = 155$.

**Step 2 — Accuracy.**
$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{30+155}{200} = \frac{185}{200} = 0.925$$

**Step 3 — Precision.**
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{30}{30+5} = \frac{30}{35} \approx 0.8571$$

**Step 4 — Recall (Sensitivity).**
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{30}{30+10} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75$$

**Step 5 — F1-score.**
$$F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R} = \frac{2 \times 0.8571 \times 0.75}{0.8571 + 0.75} = \frac{1.2857}{1.6071} \approx 0.8000$$

**Step 6 — Specificity.**
$$\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{155}{155+5} = \frac{155}{160} = 0.96875$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love to follow a confusion-matrix calculation with the follow-up: *"Which metric is most appropriate for an intrusion-detection system and why?"* — Answer: **Recall**, because missing an actual intrusion ($FN$) is more dangerous than raising a false alarm.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Information Gain for a Split

**Problem:** A decision tree node has 40 samples — 24 of class A, 16 of class B. A candidate split yields:
- Left child: 30 samples (20 A, 10 B)
- Right child: 10 samples (4 A, 6 B)

Compute the information gain using entropy.

**Step 1 — Parent entropy.**
$$H_p = -\Bigl(\frac{24}{40}\log_2\frac{24}{40} + \frac{16}{40}\log_2\frac{16}{40}\Bigr) = -(0.6\log_2 0.6 + 0.4\log_2 0.4)$$
$$= -(0.6(-0.7370) + 0.4(-1.3219)) = -( -0.4422 - 0.5288) = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Left-child entropy.**
$$H_L = -\Bigl(\frac{20}{30}\log_2\frac{20}{30} + \frac{10}{30}\log_2\frac{10}{30}\Bigr) = -(0.6667(-0.5850) + 0.3333(-1.5850))$$
$$= -(-0.3900 - 0.5283) = 0.9183 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Right-child entropy.**
$$H_R = -\Bigl(\frac{4}{10}\log_2\frac{4}{10} + \frac{6}{10}\log_2\frac{6}{10}\Bigr) = -(0.4(-1.3219) + 0.6(-0.7370))$$
$$= -(-0.5288 - 0.4422) = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Weighted child entropy.**
$$H_{\text{children}} = \frac{30}{40}(0.9183) + \frac{10}{40}(0.9710) = 0.75(0.9183) + 0.25(0.9710) = 0.6887 + 0.2428 = 0.9315 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Information Gain.**
$$\text{IG} = H_p - H_{\text{children}} = 0.9710 - 0.9315 = 0.0395 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Such a low IG indicates the split is **weak** — the decision-tree algorithm would prefer an alternative feature whose split yields a larger IG.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Naive Bayes for IoT Sensor Classification

**Problem:** A wearable IoT device emits three features: $x_1$ = step count (high/low), $x_2$ = heart rate (high/low), $x_3$ = posture (sitting/moving). From training data:
- $P(\text{Active}) = 0.6$, $P(\text{Sedentary}) = 0.4$.
- $P(x_1=\text{high} \mid \text{Active}) = 0.9$, $P(x_1=\text{high} \mid \text{Sedentary}) = 0.2$.
- $P(x_2=\text{high} \mid \text{Active}) = 0.8$, $P(x_2=\text{high} \mid \text{Sedentary}) = 0.3$.
- $P(x_3=\text{moving} \mid \text{Active}) = 0.85$, $P(x_3=\text{moving} \mid \text{Sedentary}) = 0.1$.

A new reading is $(x_1=\text{high}, x_2=\text{high}, x_3=\text{moving})$. Classify.

**Step 1 — Posterior for Active (drop shared denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$):**
$$P(\text{Active})\cdot P(x_1\!\mid\!\text{Active})\cdot P(x_2\!\mid\!\text{Active})\cdot P(x_3\!\mid\!\text{Active})$$
$$= 0.6 \times 0.9 \times 0.8 \times 0.85 = 0.3672$$

**Step 2 — Posterior for Sedentary:**
$$P(\text{Sedentary})\cdot P(x_1\!\mid\!\text{Sedentary})\cdot P(x_2\!\mid\!\text{Sedentary})\cdot P(x_3\!\mid\!\text{Sedentary})$$
$$= 0.4 \times 0.2 \times 0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.0024$$

**Step 3 — Normalise:**
$$P(\text{Active}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.3672}{0.3672+0.0024} = \frac{0.3672}{0.3696} \approx 0.9935$$
$$P(\text{Sedentary}\mid\mathbf{x}) \approx 0.0065$$

**Step 4 — Classify:**
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_c P(c\mid\mathbf{x}) = \text{Active} \quad \blacksquare$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IoT Classification Pipeline

The figure below shows the **end-to-end architecture** of a typical IoT classification system, from raw sensing on the edge to actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IoT Sensors\nTemperature, Vibration, Gas] --> B[Pre-processing\nNoise Removal and Normalization]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction\nStatistical and Spectral Features]
    C --> D[Trained Classifier\nLogReg, SVM, RF, kNN, NB]
    D --> E[Predicted Class Label\nNormal or Anomaly]
    E --> F{Action Trigger}
    F -->|Anomaly| G[Alert and Edge Actuator]
    F -->|Normal| H[Continue Monitoring]
    D -.Feedback.-> I[Cloud Retraining\nPeriodic Model Update]
    I -.Updated Model.-> D
```

## 4.2 Decision Boundary Geometry of Common Classifiers

The diagram below compares the *shapes* of decision boundaries produced by different classifiers on the same 2-D feature space — a question frequently asked in KTU viva and 3-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Logistic Regression]
        A1[Linear Hyperplane Boundary] --> A2[Probabilistic Output via Sigmoid]
    end
    subgraph B[k-Nearest Neighbors]
        B1[Voronoi Tessellation] --> B2[Non-smooth Piecewise Linear Boundary]
    end
    subgraph C[Decision Tree]
        C1[Axis Aligned Rectangular Splits] --> C2[Step Function Boundary]
    end
    subgraph D[Support Vector Machine RBF]
        D1[Non-linear Curved Surface] --> D2[Maximum Margin Separation]
    end
    subgraph E[Naive Bayes Gaussian]
        E1[Quadratic Decision Surface] --> E2[Class Conditional Gaussians]
    end
```

## 4.3 Confusion-Matrix Decomposition Flow

The following flowchart illustrates how a binary confusion matrix decomposes into the standard performance metrics required by KTU answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CM[Confusion Matrix 2x2] --> T[True Labels and Predicted Labels]
    T --> TP[TP: True Positives]
    T --> TN[TN: True Negatives]
    T --> FP[FP: False Positives]
    T --> FN[FN: False Negatives]
    TP --> ACC[Accuracy = TP+TN divided by Total]
    TP --> P[Precision = TP divided by TP+FP]
    TP --> R[Recall = TP divided by TP+FN]
    TN --> SPEC[Specificity = TN divided by TN+FP]
    P --> F1[F1 Score = 2PR divided by P+R]
    R --> F1
    ACC --> REPORT[Performance Report]
    SPEC --> REPORT
    F1 --> REPORT
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Classification Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CLASS[Classification Methods] --> DISC[Discriminative]
    CLASS --> GEN[Generative]
    CLASS --> ENSEMBLE[Ensemble]

    DISC --> LR[Logistic Regression]
    DISC --> SVM[Support Vector Machine]
    DISC --> DT[Decision Tree]
    DISC --> KNN[k-Nearest Neighbors]

    GEN --> NB[Naive Bayes]
    GEN --> LDA[Linear Discriminant Analysis]
    GEN --> GMM[GMM Classifier]

    ENSEMBLE --> RF[Random Forest]
    ENSEMBLE --> GB[Gradient Boosting]
    ENSEMBLE --> XGB[XGBoost]
```

## 4.5 Training vs. Inference Trade-off Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAIN[Training Phase Offline Cloud or Edge Server]
        T1[Labelled IoT Data] --> T2[Algorithm Specific Optimizer]
        T2 --> T3[Learned Parameters w, b or Splits or Kernels]
    end
    subgraph INFER[Inference Phase Online On Device]
        I1[New Sensor Reading] --> I2[Feature Vector x]
        I2 --> I3[Apply Learned Parameters]
        T3 -.Deployed to.-> I3
        I3 --> I4[Predicted Class y hat]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading hint for KTU students:** In the diagrams, nodes with **rounded edges** represent data/inputs, **rectangular** boxes represent transformations/models, and **diamond** boxes represent decision points. This is the conventional KTU diagram-evaluation rubric.
