# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern for PECGT525 — Machine Learning for IoT Applications. Marks are tagged as per KTU guidelines: **Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks** (split into 7 + 7 sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between discriminative and generative classification models. Give one IoT example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Discriminative models** learn the *decision boundary* directly by modelling the conditional probability $P(Y \mid X)$. They are robust when abundant labelled data is available but cannot generate new samples. *IoT example:* Logistic Regression for **wearable activity recognition** (Sitting / Walking / Running).

- **Generative models** learn the joint distribution $P(X, Y)$ by first modelling $P(X \mid Y)$ and then applying **Bayes' theorem** to obtain $P(Y \mid X)$. They can synthesise new data and incorporate prior knowledge. *IoT example:* **Naive Bayes** for **smart-home intrusion detection** (Normal / Intrusion based on packet-header features).

A clean tabular comparison earns the third mark:
| Property | Discriminative | Generative |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Objective | $P(Y \mid X)$ | $P(X \mid Y)$ via Bayes |
| Sample efficiency | Higher with big data | Higher with small data |
| Generative ability | No | Yes |

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the sigmoid function in logistic regression. Why is it preferred over a step function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ squashes the linear output $z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b$ into a probability in $[0, 1]$, making it interpretable as $P(Y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$.

2. It is **differentiable everywhere**, with the elegant derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$, enabling efficient **gradient-based learning** via gradient descent.

3. A step function (Heaviside) is non-differentiable at the threshold, providing *zero gradient* almost everywhere — gradient descent would fail to update weights. The sigmoid's smoothness ensures that even misclassified samples contribute a *useful, non-zero* gradient.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module-2 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the gradient of the binary cross-entropy loss for logistic regression with respect to a weight $w_j$. Show all intermediate steps. (7 marks)**

**(b) A smart-factory IoT system classifies machine states as $\{$Normal, Fault$\}$. On a 250-sample test set it produces the following confusion matrix. Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, and Specificity. State which metric is most appropriate for industrial fault detection. (7 marks)**

| | Pred: Fault | Pred: Normal |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual: Fault** | 40 | 15 |
| **Actual: Normal** | 10 | 185 |

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Setup (1 mark):** Define $z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b$, $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)}) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z^{(i)}}}$, and the cost:
$$J(\mathbf{w},b) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Bigl[y^{(i)}\log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\Bigr]$$

2. **Chain rule decomposition (2 marks):**
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}\cdot\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}}\cdot\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j}$$

3. **Compute each factor (2 marks):**
   - $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\Bigl[\dfrac{\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})}\Bigr]$
   - $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = \hat{y}^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})$
   - $\dfrac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = x_j^{(i)}$

4. **Cancel the quadratic term and combine (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})\,x_j^{(i)}$$

5. **Update rule and convergence remark (1 mark):** $w_j \leftarrow w_j - \eta \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$. Convergence in $O(\text{iterations})$; cost decreases monotonically if $\eta$ is appropriately chosen.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Identify entries (1 mark):** $TP = 40$, $FN = 15$, $FP = 10$, $TN = 185$.

2. **Accuracy (1 mark):**
$$\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{40+185}{250} = \frac{225}{250} = 0.90$$

3. **Precision (1 mark):**
$$P = \frac{40}{40+10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80$$

4. **Recall (1 mark):**
$$R = \frac{40}{40+15} = \frac{40}{55} \approx 0.7273$$

5. **F1-score (1 mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.80 \times 0.7273}{0.80 + 0.7273} = \frac{1.1637}{1.5273} \approx 0.7619$$

6. **Specificity (1 mark):**
$$\text{Spec} = \frac{185}{185+10} = \frac{185}{195} \approx 0.9487$$

7. **Most appropriate metric (1 mark):** For industrial fault detection, **Recall (Sensitivity)** is most critical because *missing* an actual fault ($FN$) can cause expensive equipment damage or safety hazards, whereas occasional false alarms are tolerable. A high recall (≈ 0.73) here is acceptable, but the system should be tuned to push it ≥ 0.95 in production by lowering the decision threshold.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the working of the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm. Discuss the effect of varying $k$ on bias and variance. (7 marks)**

**(b) Apply the Naive Bayes classifier to the following IoT weather-station data and predict the class for a new sample: $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Sunny, Mild, High Humidity})$. Training data is given below. (7 marks)**

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Play |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Yes |

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Working principle (3 marks):** k-NN is a *lazy*, *non-parametric* algorithm. For a query $\mathbf{x}_{\text{query}}$:
   - Compute the distance $d(\mathbf{x}_{\text{query}}, \mathbf{x}^{(i)})$ to every training sample (commonly Euclidean $\Vert \mathbf{x}_{\text{query}} - \mathbf{x}^{(i)}\Vert_2$).
   - Select the $k$ nearest samples; collect their labels.
   - **Predict** the class by majority vote: $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y^{(i)} : \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in N_k(\mathbf{x}_{\text{query}})\}$.

2. **Effect of $k$ on bias-variance (3 marks):**
   - **Small $k$ (e.g., $k=1$):** Decision boundary is highly irregular. **Low bias, high variance** — sensitive to noise.
   - **Large $k$ (e.g., $k = N$):** Always predicts the majority class. **High bias, low variance** — underfits.
   - **Optimal $k$ (often $k = \sqrt{N}$ or by cross-validation):** Balances bias and variance. Typically odd to avoid ties in binary classification.

3. **Practical considerations (1 mark):** Feature scaling (standardisation) is mandatory because distance metrics are sensitive to feature magnitudes. Distance-weighted voting further improves robustness.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Compute class priors (1 mark):**
   $P(\text{Yes}) = 6/10 = 0.6$, $P(\text{No}) = 4/10 = 0.4$.

2. **Likelihoods for each feature (2 marks):**
   - $P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{Yes}) = 1/6$, $P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{No}) = 3/4$.
   - $P(\text{Mild}\mid\text{Yes}) = 2/6 = 1/3$, $P(\text{Mild}\mid\text{No}) = 1/4$.
   - $P(\text{High}\mid\text{Yes}) = 2/6 = 1/3$, $P(\text{High}\mid\text{No}) = 3/4$.

3. **Apply Bayes' rule (2 marks):**
   - $P(\text{Yes})\cdot P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{Yes})\cdot P(\text{Mild}\mid\text{Yes})\cdot P(\text{High}\mid\text{Yes})$
     $= 0.6 \times \tfrac{1}{6} \times \tfrac{1}{3} \times \tfrac{1}{3} = 0.6 \times 0.01852 = 0.01111$.
   - $P(\text{No})\cdot P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{No})\cdot P(\text{Mild}\mid\text{No})\cdot P(\text{High}\mid\text{No})$
     $= 0.4 \times \tfrac{3}{4} \times \tfrac{1}{4} \times \tfrac{3}{4} = 0.4 \times 0.046875 = 0.01875$.

4. **Normalise and decide (1 mark):**
   - Total $= 0.01111 + 0.01875 = 0.02986$.
   - $P(\text{Yes}\mid\mathbf{x}) = 0.01111 / 0.02986 \approx 0.372$.
   - $P(\text{No}\mid\mathbf{x}) = 0.01875 / 0.02986 \approx 0.628$.
   - **Predicted class:** $\hat{y} = \text{No}$ (do not play). $\blacksquare$

5. **Laplace smoothing note (1 mark):** With a small dataset, the term $P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{Yes}) = 1/6$ could vanish for some IoT streams. Applying Laplace smoothing with $\alpha = 1$ yields $P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{Yes}) = (1+1)/(6+3) = 2/9$, providing a more robust estimate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $P(X \mid Y)$ and $P(Y \mid X)$** in Naive Bayes derivations — always anchor to Bayes' theorem explicitly.
> 2. **Using accuracy on imbalanced IoT datasets** (e.g., rare fault events) without flagging precision/recall — KTU expects both the calculation *and* the caveat.
> 3. **Skipping the "why sigmoid" intuition** in logistic regression answers — the examiner awards 1 mark for explaining differentiability.
> 4. **Failing to specify $k$ and distance metric** in k-NN answers — both are part of the 7-mark rubric.
> 5. **Forgetting to standardise features** before computing Euclidean distance in k-NN/SVM — a frequent 2-mark deduction in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **In ensemble questions, omitting the role of bootstrap sampling and feature randomness** — Random Forest is *not* just bagged trees.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Classification Methods**

- [x] Classification is a *supervised* learning task mapping $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ to a discrete label $y \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$.
- [x] Two main families: **Discriminative** ($P(Y\mid X)$) vs. **Generative** ($P(X\mid Y)$ + Bayes).
- [x] **Logistic Regression** uses the **sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ and the **BCE loss** $-y\log\hat{y}-(1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})$.
- [x] The sigmoid derivative simplifies to $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$.
- [x] Logistic regression gradient: $\partial J/\partial w_j = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$.
- [x] **k-NN** is lazy, non-parametric, sensitive to feature scaling; small $k$ ⇒ high variance, large $k$ ⇒ high bias.
- [x] **Naive Bayes** assumes feature conditional independence: $P(Y\mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(Y)\prod_m P(x_m \mid Y)$.
- [x] **Laplace smoothing** ($\alpha=1$) prevents zero probabilities.
- [x] **Decision Trees** split on the feature/threshold that **maximises information gain** (or equivalently, minimises Gini/Entropy).
- [x] **Gini** $= 1 - \sum_c p_c^2$, **Entropy** $= -\sum_c p_c \log_2 p_c$.
- [x] **SVM** finds the maximum-margin hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ minimising $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C\sum\xi_i$.
- [x] **Kernel trick**: linear, polynomial, RBF — RBF kernel $K(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z}) = \exp(-\gamma\Vert \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{z}\Vert^2)$.
- [x] **Random Forest** = bagging + random feature subsets + majority vote; provides OOB error and feature importance.
- [x] **Confusion matrix** entries: $TP, FP, TN, FN$.
- [x] Key metrics: Accuracy, Precision $= TP/(TP+FP)$, Recall $= TP/(TP+FN)$, F1 $= 2PR/(P+R)$, Specificity $= TN/(TN+FP)$.
- [x] For **imbalanced IoT streams** (rare anomalies), report F1 and ROC-AUC — never rely on accuracy alone.
- [x] For **edge/TinyML** deployment, prefer shallow models (LogReg, NB, small DT) due to memory and latency constraints.
- [x] For **interpretability** (safety-critical IoT), prefer Logistic Regression and Decision Trees.
- [x] For **non-linear, high-dimensional** IoT data (vibration, audio), SVM with RBF kernel or Random Forest typically performs best.
- [x] Always **standardise features** before distance-based (k-NN, SVM) and gradient-based (LogReg) methods.
- [x] For **multiclass extension** of binary classifiers, use **One-vs-Rest (OvR)** or **One-vs-One (OvO)** strategies; multinomial logistic regression handles multiclass natively via softmax.

---

**End of Module 2 — Classification Methods Notes.**

topics.title: Decision Trees: Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision Trees: Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning algorithm used for both classification and regression tasks. It constructs a tree-structured model of decisions by recursively partitioning the input feature space based on attribute value tests, producing a flowchart-like structure where each internal node represents a test on a feature, each branch represents the outcome of that test, and each leaf node represents a class label (in classification) or a continuous value (in regression).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A Decision Tree is a hierarchical, recursive partitioning structure used in **CART (Classification and Regression Trees)**, **ID3**, and **C4.5** algorithms. It performs a greedy top-down search through the hypothesis space of all possible decision trees, selecting the *best attribute* at each node using an impurity measure such as **Entropy** or **Gini Index**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "20 Questions" Game

Imagine you are playing the classic guessing game **"20 Questions"** with a friend. Your friend thinks of an animal, and you must guess it by asking yes/no questions. A skilled player does not ask random questions; they ask the **most informative question first** — one that splits the remaining possibilities roughly in half.

For example:
- Q1: *"Is it a mammal?"* → Yes/No
- If **Yes**, Q2: *"Does it live in water?"*
- If **No**, Q2: *"Does it have feathers?"*

Each question **narrows down the search space** dramatically. A decision tree does exactly this with data:
- **Internal nodes** → The questions (attribute tests)
- **Branches** → The answers (attribute values)
- **Leaf nodes** → The final guess (class label)

The algorithm's job is to automatically figure out *which question to ask first* to reduce uncertainty the most.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The decision tree construction process mimics a doctor's differential diagnosis. The doctor first asks the symptom that **best discriminates** between possible diseases (highest information gain), then progressively narrows down based on follow-up symptoms until a confident diagnosis (leaf node) is reached.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Root Node** | The topmost node representing the entire dataset and the first attribute test | "Is Outlook = Sunny?" |
| **Internal Node** | A node that performs an attribute split and has children | "Is Humidity = High?" |
| **Leaf Node (Terminal Node)** | A node that carries a class label or prediction; has no children | "Play = No" |
| **Branch (Edge)** | A possible value of the attribute tested at the parent node | Sunny, Overcast, Rain |
| **Splitting** | The process of partitioning a node's data into subsets based on an attribute test | Dividing by Outlook |
| **Pruning** | The process of removing subtrees to reduce overfitting | Reducing tree depth |
| **Impurity** | A measure of the disorder or heterogeneity of class labels in a node | Entropy, Gini |

## 1.4 Why Decision Trees are Pivotal for IoT Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Relevance (KTU 2024 Module Context):** In IoT systems, decision trees are extremely valuable because:
> 1. They are **lightweight** and can be deployed directly on edge devices (microcontrollers, Raspberry Pi, Arduino with limited RAM).
> 2. They are **interpretable** — domain experts can audit the logic for safety-critical IoT applications (e.g., medical IoT, industrial IoT fault detection).
> 3. They require **minimal preprocessing** (no feature scaling or normalization).
> 4. They handle **mixed data types** (categorical and numerical) which is common in IoT sensor data.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Construction Algorithm: Top-Down Inductive Approach

All major decision tree algorithms (ID3, C4.5, CART) follow a **greedy, top-down, recursive partitioning** strategy known as **TDIDT (Top-Down Induction of Decision Trees)**.

### Algorithm Logic Steps:

1. **Initialize:** Place the entire training dataset at the **root node**.
2. **Compute Impurity:** Calculate the impurity measure (Entropy/Gini) of the current node's data distribution.
3. **Evaluate All Attributes:** For *every* unused feature, compute the **information gain** (or reduction in impurity) if the data were split on that attribute.
4. **Select Best Split:** Choose the attribute that yields the **maximum information gain** (or minimum weighted impurity).
5. **Partition:** Split the data into subsets according to the selected attribute's values, creating child nodes.
6. **Recursive Application:** For each child node:
   - If it is **pure** (all instances belong to one class) → make it a **leaf node**.
   - If it is **impure** and unvisited attributes remain → recurse from step 2.
   - If no attributes remain → create a leaf node with the **majority class**.
7. **Termination Conditions:** Stop when one of the following is met:
   - All instances in a node belong to the same class (pure node).
   - No remaining attributes to split on.
   - The node contains fewer instances than a predefined threshold (e.g., `min_samples_split`).
   - The tree has reached the maximum allowed depth (`max_depth`).

## 2.2 Impurity Measures — The Mathematical Heart of Construction

The decision tree construction problem reduces to: *"Which attribute, when used to split, produces the **purest** child nodes?"* This requires a quantitative measure of impurity.

### 2.2.1 Entropy (Information Theory Foundation)

Entropy measures the **average amount of information** (in bits) needed to identify the class label of a randomly drawn instance from a set $S$.

For a dataset $S$ with $c$ classes and class probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_c$:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

**Properties of Entropy:**
- $H(S) = 0$ when the node is **pure** (all instances belong to one class).
- $H(S) = \log_2 c$ when the node is **maximally impure** (uniform distribution across $c$ classes).
- For binary classification: $H(S) \in [0, 1]$.

### 2.2.2 Information Gain

Information Gain is the **reduction in entropy** achieved by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$.

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

Where:
- $Values(A)$ = the set of all possible values of attribute $A$.
- $S_v$ = the subset of $S$ where attribute $A = v$.
- $\vert S_v \vert / \vert S \vert$ = the weight of subset $S_v$ (proportion of data).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Information Gain?** It quantifies how much "knowing the value of $A$" reduces the uncertainty about the class label. The attribute with the **highest IG** is chosen because it provides the most discriminative power.

### 2.2.3 Gini Index (Used in CART)

The Gini Index measures the **probability of incorrect classification** if a randomly chosen instance is labeled according to the class distribution at that node.

$$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$$

**Properties of Gini:**
- $Gini(S) = 0$ when the node is pure.
- $Gini(S) = 1 - 1/c$ at maximum impurity.
- Computationally **faster** than entropy (no logarithm computation).

The splitting criterion in CART uses the **weighted average Gini** of child nodes:

$$Gini_{split}(S, A) = \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$$

### 2.2.4 Classification Error (Rarely used as splitting criterion)

$$Error(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$$

This is used primarily for **pruning** decisions, not for splitting, as it is not sufficiently sensitive to changes in class probabilities.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Expression | Range | Algorithm | Primary Use |
|---------|------------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | ID3, C4.5 | Impurity measure |
| **Information Gain** | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | ID3 | Splitting criterion |
| **Gain Ratio** | $GR(S,A) = \frac{IG(S,A)}{-\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | C4.5 | Normalized splitting |
| **Gini Index** | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | CART | Impurity measure |
| **Splitting Gini** | $Gini_{split}(S,A) = \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | CART | Splitting criterion |
| **Classification Error** | $Error(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | Pruning | Post-pruning criterion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Note:** When computing entropy or Gini, if any $p_i = 0$, the term $p_i \log_2 p_i$ is defined as **0** (the limit is zero). This is a standard convention from information theory.

## 2.4 Comparison of Tree Algorithms

| Feature | ID3 | C4.5 | CART |
|---------|-----|------|------|
| **Inventor** | Quinlan (1986) | Quinlan (1993) | Breiman et al. (1984) |
| **Splitting Criterion** | Information Gain | Gain Ratio | Gini Index |
| **Handles Continuous Attributes** | No | Yes (threshold-based) | Yes |
| **Handles Missing Values** | No | Yes | Yes (surrogate splits) |
| **Pruning** | No explicit | Error-based post-pruning | Cost-complexity pruning |
| **Output** | Multi-way tree | Multi-way tree | Binary tree |
| **Task** | Classification | Classification | Classification & Regression |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications of Decision Trees:**
> 1. **IoT Predictive Maintenance:** Decision trees deployed on edge devices predict equipment failure based on vibration, temperature, and pressure sensor readings.
> 2. **Medical IoT Diagnosis:** Wearable health monitors use decision trees to classify cardiac arrhythmias from ECG signals.
> 3. **Smart Grid Energy:** Decision trees forecast power demand based on weather, time, and historical consumption data.
> 4. **Network Intrusion Detection:** Decision trees classify network traffic as benign or malicious in IoT gateways.
> 5. **Agricultural IoT:** Decision trees recommend irrigation schedules based on soil moisture, humidity, and temperature sensor data.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: The Classic "Play Tennis" Dataset

We will construct a decision tree step-by-step using the canonical **Play Tennis** dataset (a standard KTU reference example).

### 3.1.1 The Dataset

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------|
| D1  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak   | No  |
| D2  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No  |
| D3  | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak   | Yes |
| D4  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak   | Yes |
| D5  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| D6  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No  |
| D7  | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes |
| D8  | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak   | No  |
| D9  | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| D10 | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| D11 | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes |
| D12 | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes |
| D13 | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| D14 | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No  |

**Class Distribution:** Yes = 9, No = 5, Total = 14

## 3.2 Step 1: Calculate Entropy of the Root Node

The root contains the entire dataset. We compute $H(S)$:

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14}$$

**Textual conversion logic:** We plug in the two class probabilities ($p_{yes} = 9/14$ and $p_{no} = 5/14$) into the entropy formula.

$$H(S) = -0.6429 \times \log_2(0.6429) - 0.3571 \times \log_2(0.3571)$$

**Textual conversion logic:** We convert the fractions to decimals and compute the base-2 logarithms of each.

$$H(S) = -0.6429 \times (-0.6375) - 0.3571 \times (-1.4854)$$

**Textual conversion logic:** We evaluate $\log_2(0.6429) \approx -0.6375$ and $\log_2(0.3571) \approx -1.4854$ using logarithmic identities.

$$H(S) = 0.4098 + 0.5304 = 0.9403 \text{ bits}$$

**Textual conversion logic:** We sum the two positive terms (the negative signs cancel with the negative logarithms) to obtain the final entropy of approximately **0.940 bits**.

$$H(S) \approx 0.940 \text{ bits}$$

## 3.3 Step 2: Calculate Information Gain for "Outlook"

The "Outlook" attribute has three values: Sunny, Overcast, Rain.

### 3.3.1 Subset S_Sunny (Outlook = Sunny)
Rows: D1, D2, D8, D9, D11 → Yes: 2, No: 3, Total: 5

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5}$$

**Textual conversion logic:** Two "Yes" votes and three "No" votes out of 5 instances.

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -0.4 \times \log_2(0.4) - 0.6 \times \log_2(0.6)$$

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -0.4 \times (-1.3219) - 0.6 \times (-0.7370)$$

**Textual conversion logic:** $\log_2(0.4) \approx -1.3219$ and $\log_2(0.6) \approx -0.7370$.

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

### 3.3.2 Subset S_Overcast (Outlook = Overcast)
Rows: D3, D7, D12, D13 → Yes: 4, No: 0, Total: 4

$$H(S_{Overcast}) = -\frac{4}{4} \log_2 \frac{4}{4} - \frac{0}{4} \log_2 \frac{0}{4} = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Textual conversion logic:** A pure node (all Yes) has zero entropy by the convention $0 \log_2 0 = 0$.

### 3.3.3 Subset S_Rain (Outlook = Rain)
Rows: D4, D5, D6, D10, D14 → Yes: 3, No: 2, Total: 5

$$H(S_{Rain}) = -\frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Textual conversion logic:** Symmetric case to Sunny: 3 Yes, 2 No yields the same entropy.

### 3.3.4 Weighted Average Entropy After Split

$$\sum_{v \in Outlook} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)$$

$$= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.6936 \text{ bits}$$

**Textual conversion logic:** We weight each child entropy by its fraction of the parent data.

### 3.3.5 Information Gain for "Outlook"

$$IG(S, Outlook) = H(S) - 0.6936 = 0.9403 - 0.6936 = 0.2467 \text{ bits}$$

## 3.4 Step 3: Calculate Information Gain for "Temperature"

The "Temperature" attribute has three values: Hot, Mild, Cool.

### Hot Subset: D1, D2, D3, D13 → Yes: 2, No: 2, Total: 4
$$H(S_{Hot}) = -\frac{2}{4} \log_2 \frac{2}{4} - \frac{2}{4} \log_2 \frac{2}{4} = -0.5 \times (-1) - 0.5 \times (-1) = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

### Mild Subset: D4, D8, D10, D11, D12, D14 → Yes: 4, No: 2, Total: 6
$$H(S_{Mild}) = -\frac{4}{6} \log_2 \frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6} \log_2 \frac{2}{6}$$

$$= -0.6667 \times \log_2(0.6667) - 0.3333 \times \log_2(0.3333)$$

$$= -0.6667 \times (-0.5850) - 0.3333 \times (-1.5850)$$

$$= 0.3900 + 0.5283 = 0.9183 \text{ bits}$$

### Cool Subset: D5, D6, D7, D9 → Yes: 3, No: 1, Total: 4
$$H(S_{Cool}) = -\frac{3}{4} \log_2 \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4} \log_2 \frac{1}{4}$$

$$= -0.75 \times (-0.4150) - 0.25 \times (-2.0)$$

$$= 0.3113 + 0.5 = 0.8113 \text{ bits}$$

### Weighted Average and Information Gain

$$\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \frac{4}{14}(1.0) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113)$$

$$= 0.2857 + 0.3936 + 0.2318 = 0.9111 \text{ bits}$$

$$IG(S, Temperature) = 0.9403 - 0.9111 = 0.0292 \text{ bits}$$

## 3.5 Step 4: Calculate Information Gain for "Humidity"

### High Subset: D1, D2, D3, D4, D8, D12, D14 → Yes: 3, No: 4, Total: 7
$$H(S_{High}) = -\frac{3}{7} \log_2 \frac{3}{7} - \frac{4}{7} \log_2 \frac{4}{7}$$

$$= -0.4286 \times \log_2(0.4286) - 0.5714 \times \log_2(0.5714)$$

$$= -0.4286 \times (-1.2224) - 0.5714 \times (-0.8074)$$

$$= 0.5239 + 0.4614 = 0.9853 \text{ bits}$$

### Normal Subset: D5, D6, D7, D9, D10, D11, D13 → Yes: 6, No: 1, Total: 7
$$H(S_{Normal}) = -\frac{6}{7} \log_2 \frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7} \log_2 \frac{1}{7}$$

$$= -0.8571 \times \log_2(0.8571) - 0.1429 \times \log_2(0.1429)$$

$$= -0.8571 \times (-0.2224) - 0.1429 \times (-2.8074)$$

$$= 0.1906 + 0.4011 = 0.5917 \text{ bits}$$

### Weighted Average and Information Gain

$$\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \frac{7}{14}(0.9853) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917) = 0.7885 \text{ bits}$$

$$IG(S, Humidity) = 0.9403 - 0.7885 = 0.1518 \text{ bits}$$

## 3.6 Step 5: Calculate Information Gain for "Wind"

### Weak Subset: D1, D3, D4, D5, D8, D9, D10, D13 → Yes: 6, No: 2, Total: 8
$$H(S_{Weak}) = -\frac{6}{8} \log_2 \frac{6}{8} - \frac{2}{8} \log_2 \frac{2}{8}$$

$$= -0.75 \times \log_2(0.75) - 0.25 \times \log_2(0.25)$$

$$= -0.75 \times (-0.4150) - 0.25 \times (-2.0)$$

$$= 0.3113 + 0.5 = 0.8113 \text{ bits}$$

### Strong Subset: D2, D6, D7, D11, D12, D14 → Yes: 3, No: 3, Total: 6
$$H(S_{Strong}) = -\frac{3}{6} \log_2 \frac{3}{6} - \frac{3}{6} \log_2 \frac{3}{6} = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

### Weighted Average and Information Gain

$$\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \frac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0) = 0.4636 + 0.4286 = 0.8922 \text{ bits}$$

$$IG(S, Wind) = 0.9403 - 0.8922 = 0.0481 \text{ bits}$$

## 3.7 Step 6: The Decision — Choose Root Node

### 3.7.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Attribute | Information Gain (bits) | Rank |
|-----------|------------------------|------|
| **Outlook** | **0.2467** | **1st (Winner)** |
| Humidity  | 0.1518                | 2nd  |
| Wind      | 0.0481                | 3rd  |
| Temperature | 0.0292              | 4th  |

**Decision:** Since **Outlook** has the **highest information gain (0.2467)**, it is selected as the **root node** test.

## 3.8 Step 7: Recursive Construction on Child Nodes

### 3.8.1 The Overcast Branch (Pure → Leaf)
Subset S_Overcast has 4 Yes, 0 No. Since it is a **pure node** (all instances have the same class label), it is converted into a **leaf node** with the label **Play = Yes**.

### 3.8.2 The Sunny Branch (Impure → Recurse)
Subset S_Sunny contains D1, D2, D8, D9, D11 → Yes: 2, No: 3.

We now compute the entropy of this subset:
$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

We then evaluate the remaining attributes (Humidity, Wind, Temperature) to find the best split for this subset. By performing the same weighted-entropy calculation procedure:

| Attribute (Sunny branch) | Information Gain |
|--------------------------|------------------|
| Humidity                 | 0.9710 (winner)  |
| Wind                     | 0.0200           |
| Temperature              | 0.5700           |

So **Humidity** is selected as the test for the Sunny branch.

### 3.8.3 The Rain Branch (Impure → Recurse)
Subset S_Rain contains D4, D5, D6, D10, D14 → Yes: 3, No: 2.

Entropy:
$$H(S_{Rain}) = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

The best attribute for this branch is **Wind**, which yields the highest information gain.

## 3.9 Final Decision Tree Structure

The final constructed tree would look as follows (logical representation):

- **Root:** Outlook
  - **Sunny** → Humidity
    - **High** → Play = No
    - **Normal** → Play = Yes
  - **Overcast** → Play = Yes *(leaf)*
  - **Rain** → Wind
    - **Weak** → Play = Yes
    - **Strong** → Play = No

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from the Derivation:** The decision tree construction is a **recursive, greedy, divide-and-conquer** process. At each step, the attribute that **maximally reduces uncertainty** (highest information gain or lowest Gini) is chosen. The process terminates when nodes become pure or when no further splits are beneficial.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Tree Construction Flow (Top-Down Inductive Process)

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **top-down recursive construction algorithm** (TDIDT) used by ID3, C4.5, and CART.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START Entire Training Set at Root Node] --> nodeB[Compute Impurity H S or Gini S of Current Node]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Is Node Pure or No Attributes Left}
    nodeC -->|YES| nodeD[Create Leaf Node Majority Class]
    nodeC -->|NO| nodeE[For Each Remaining Attribute Compute Information Gain or Gini Reduction]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Select Attribute with Highest Information Gain or Lowest Gini]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Split Node into Child Nodes Based on Attribute Values]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Recursively Apply Procedure to Each Child Node]
    nodeH --> nodeI{All Nodes Processed or Stopping Criteria Met}
    nodeI -->|NO| nodeB
    nodeI -->|YES| nodeJ[END Return Fully Constructed Decision Tree]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Decision Tree (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput[Input Feature Vector X1 X2 X3 Xn] --> nodeRoot{Root Node Test Attribute Outlook}
    nodeRoot -->|Sunny| nodeA1{Internal Node Test Humidity}
    nodeRoot -->|Overcast| nodeLeaf1[Leaf Node Class Yes]
    nodeRoot -->|Rain| nodeA2{Internal Node Test Wind}
    nodeA1 -->|High| nodeLeaf2[Leaf Node Class No]
    nodeA1 -->|Normal| nodeLeaf3[Leaf Node Class Yes]
    nodeA2 -->|Weak| nodeLeaf4[Leaf Node Class Yes]
    nodeA2 -->|Strong| nodeLeaf5[Leaf Node Class No]
    nodeLeaf1 --> nodeOutput[Final Prediction]
    nodeLeaf2 --> nodeOutput
    nodeLeaf3 --> nodeOutput
    nodeLeaf4 --> nodeOutput
    nodeLeaf5 --> nodeOutput
```

## 4.3 Impurity Comparison Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Compare Impurity Measures] --> nodeEntropy[ENTROPY H S Uses Logarithm Information Theoretic Foundation]
    nodeStart --> nodeGini[GINI INDEX 1 Sum pi Squared CART Default Computational Efficiency]
    nodeStart --> nodeError[CLASSIFICATION ERROR 1 Max pi Used for Pruning Not Splitting]
    nodeEntropy --> nodeNote1[Pure Node H equals 0 Maximum H equals log2 c]
    nodeGini --> nodeNote2[Pure Node Gini equals 0 Maximum Gini equals 1 minus 1 over c]
    nodeError --> nodeNote3[Pure Node Error equals 0 Maximum Error equals 1 minus 1 over c]
    nodeNote1 --> nodeFinal[Entropy and Gini yield similar splits in practice but differ in sensitivity to probability changes]
    nodeNote2 --> nodeFinal
    nodeNote3 --> nodeFinal
```

## 4.4 Construction Phase Workflow (IoT Deployment Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Phase 1 DATA COLLECTION Gather IoT Sensor Readings Labels] --> nodeB[Phase 2 PREPROCESSING Handle Missing Values Encode Categorical Features]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Phase 3 TREE CONSTRUCTION Recursive Splitting using Information Gain or Gini]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Phase 4 PRUNING Pre pruning with max depth or Post pruning with validation set]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Phase 5 MODEL SERIALIZATION Export Tree to C Code or PMML for Edge Devices]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Phase 6 IoT EDGE DEPLOYMENT Run Inference on Microcontroller with Low Latency]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Phase 7 MONITORING Track Drift and Re train Periodically]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Decision Tree. List any two splitting criteria used for constructing a decision tree.**

**Model Answer:**

A decision tree is a supervised machine learning algorithm that recursively partitions the feature space using a tree-structured model. Each internal node represents a test on an attribute, each branch represents the outcome of the test, and each leaf node represents a class label or prediction.

Two splitting criteria used for constructing a decision tree are:
1. **Information Gain** (used in ID3 algorithm): $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$
2. **Gini Index** (used in CART algorithm): $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A third acceptable answer is **Gain Ratio** (used in C4.5) or **Reduction in Variance** (for regression trees). Marks are awarded for the formula, not just the name.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of entropy in decision tree construction. What is the range of entropy for a binary classification problem?**

**Model Answer:**

Entropy is a measure of **impurity or disorder** in a dataset, borrowed from information theory. It quantifies the average amount of information (in bits) required to identify the class label of a randomly drawn instance. For a dataset $S$ with class probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_c$:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

In decision tree construction, entropy is used to evaluate the quality of a split. An attribute that results in child nodes with **lower entropy** (purer subsets) is preferred.

**Range for binary classification:**
- Minimum value: $H(S) = 0$ when all instances belong to a single class (pure node).
- Maximum value: $H(S) = 1$ when instances are equally split between the two classes (50% Yes, 50% No).

Therefore, the range is $H(S) \in [0, 1]$ for binary classification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Form Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Construct a Decision Tree for the given training dataset using the ID3 algorithm. Show all entropy and information gain calculations.**

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------|
| 1   | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak   | No  |
| 2   | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No  |
| 3   | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak   | Yes |
| 4   | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak   | Yes |
| 5   | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| 6   | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No  |

**Distribution:** Yes = 4, No = 2, Total = 6

#### Part (a): Calculate the Information Gain for the "Outlook" Attribute (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Entropy of Root Node**

$$H(S) = -\frac{4}{6} \log_2 \frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6} \log_2 \frac{2}{6}$$

$$= -0.6667 \times (-0.5850) - 0.3333 \times (-1.5850)$$

$$= 0.3900 + 0.5283 = 0.9183 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Entropy of Each Subset**

*Subset S_Sunny (Day 1, 2):* Yes = 0, No = 2
$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{0}{2} \log_2 \frac{0}{2} - \frac{2}{2} \log_2 \frac{2}{2} = 0 \text{ bits}$$

*Subset S_Overcast (Day 3):* Yes = 1, No = 0
$$H(S_{Overcast}) = -\frac{1}{1} \log_2 \frac{1}{1} - \frac{0}{1} \log_2 \frac{0}{1} = 0 \text{ bits}$$

*Subset S_Rain (Day 4, 5, 6):* Yes = 3, No = 2
$$H(S_{Rain}) = -\frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Weighted Average Entropy**

$$\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \frac{2}{6}(0) + \frac{1}{6}(0) + \frac{3}{6}(0.9710) = 0.4855 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4: Information Gain**

$$IG(S, Outlook) = 0.9183 - 0.4855 = 0.4328 \text{ bits}$$

#### Part (b): Calculate Information Gain for "Humidity" and Identify the Root Node (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Entropy of Each Humidity Subset**

*Subset S_High (Day 1, 2, 3, 4):* Yes = 2, No = 2, Total = 4
$$H(S_{High}) = -\frac{2}{4} \log_2 \frac{2}{4} - \frac{2}{4} \log_2 \frac{2}{4} = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

*Subset S_Normal (Day 5, 6):* Yes = 2, No = 0, Total = 2
$$H(S_{Normal}) = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Weighted Average Entropy**

$$\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \frac{4}{6}(1.0) + \frac{2}{6}(0) = 0.6667 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Information Gain**

$$IG(S, Humidity) = 0.9183 - 0.6667 = 0.2516 \text{ bits}$$

**Decision Matrix:**

| Attribute | Information Gain |
|-----------|------------------|
| **Outlook** | **0.4328 (Winner)** |
| Humidity  | 0.2516           |

**Conclusion:** Since "Outlook" has the **highest information gain**, it is selected as the **root node** of the decision tree. Both "Sunny" and "Overcast" branches become pure leaf nodes (Play = No for Sunny, Play = Yes for Overcast). The "Rain" branch requires further recursive splitting using the remaining attributes (Humidity or Wind).

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Compare the Gini Index and Entropy as impurity measures for decision tree construction. Compute both for a node containing 6 "Yes" and 4 "No" instances.**

#### Part (a): Compute Entropy and Information Gain (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Entropy**

For a node with 6 Yes and 4 No (Total = 10):
$$H(S) = -\frac{6}{10} \log_2 \frac{6}{10} - \frac{4}{10} \log_2 \frac{4}{10}$$

$$= -0.6 \times \log_2(0.6) - 0.4 \times \log_2(0.4)$$

**Textual conversion logic:** Convert fractions to decimal probabilities, then compute base-2 logarithms.

$$= -0.6 \times (-0.7370) - 0.4 \times (-1.3219)$$

$$= 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Suppose we split on attribute A producing two child nodes:**

*Child Node 1 (S1):* 4 Yes, 1 No, Total = 5
$$H(S_1) = -\frac{4}{5} \log_2 \frac{4}{5} - \frac{1}{5} \log_2 \frac{1}{5} = -0.8 \times (-0.3219) - 0.2 \times (-2.3219) = 0.2575 + 0.4644 = 0.7219 \text{ bits}$$

*Child Node 2 (S2):* 2 Yes, 3 No, Total = 5
$$H(S_2) = -\frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Weighted Entropy and Information Gain**

$$\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v) = \frac{5}{10}(0.7219) + \frac{5}{10}(0.9710) = 0.3610 + 0.4855 = 0.8465 \text{ bits}$$

$$IG(S, A) = 0.9710 - 0.8465 = 0.1245 \text{ bits}$$

#### Part (b): Compute Gini Index and Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Gini of Parent Node**

$$Gini(S) = 1 - \left(\frac{6}{10}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{4}{10}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.36 - 0.16 = 0.48$$

**Step 2: Gini of Each Child Node**

*Child Node 1 (S1):* 4 Yes, 1 No
$$Gini(S_1) = 1 - \left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.64 - 0.04 = 0.32$$

*Child Node 2 (S2):* 2 Yes, 3 No
$$Gini(S_2) = 1 - \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.16 - 0.36 = 0.48$$

**Step 3: Weighted Gini (Splitting Criterion)**

$$Gini_{split}(S, A) = \frac{5}{10}(0.32) + \frac{5}{10}(0.48) = 0.16 + 0.24 = 0.40$$

**Step 4: Gini Reduction (Analogous to Information Gain)**

$$\Delta Gini = 0.48 - 0.40 = 0.08$$

**Comparative Analysis Table:**

| Aspect | Entropy | Gini Index |
|--------|---------|------------|
| **Formula** | $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $1 - \sum p_i^2$ |
| **Computational Cost** | Higher (logarithm) | Lower (no logarithm) |
| **Range (binary)** | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 0.5]$ |
| **Algorithm** | ID3, C4.5 | CART |
| **Sensitivity** | More sensitive to probability changes near 0.5 | Less sensitive near extremes |
| **Practical Result** | Yields similar splits in most cases | Slightly faster to compute |
| **Maximum Impurity** | $H = 1$ (binary) | $Gini = 0.5$ (binary) |

**Conclusion:** Both measures are mathematically valid and produce **similar tree structures** in most practical scenarios. The choice between them is often a matter of **computational efficiency** (Gini is faster) or **theoretical preference** (Entropy has a stronger information-theoretic interpretation). CART uses Gini by default for speed, while ID3 and C4.5 use entropy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Weighted Average:** Many students compute entropy/Gini for child nodes but **omit the weighting** by $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$. This results in an incorrect information gain calculation and **loses 3-4 marks**.
>
> 2. **Computing $\log_2 0$ Incorrectly:** The term $0 \log_2 0$ is defined as **0** (by the limit convention), not $-\infty$ or undefined. Writing it as undefined will **lose 1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Selecting the Wrong Splitting Criterion:** ID3 uses **Information Gain**, C4.5 uses **Gain Ratio**, CART uses **Gini Index**. Confusing these in a comparison question costs **2-3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Not Showing Intermediate Steps:** KTU examiners require **explicit step-by-step calculation**. Jumping directly to the final answer without showing the weighted average, intermediate entropies, and the subtraction step will result in **partial marking**.
>
> 5. **Confusing Impurity Range:** Entropy for binary classification ranges in $[0, 1]$, NOT $[0, \log_2 c]$ in a general sense for binary. Gini for binary classification ranges in $[0, 0.5]$, NOT $[0, 1]$. Mixing these up costs **1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Not Terminating the Recursion:** In construction problems, students often forget to mark **leaf nodes** at pure branches and continue splitting unnecessarily. Always explicitly mark leaves with the class label.
>
> 7. **Missing Units in the Answer:** Information Gain and Entropy are measured in **bits**. While this rarely costs marks, writing "Entropy = 0.97" without specifying the unit is technically incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - [x] **Decision Tree** = Supervised, non-parametric, hierarchical model that recursively partitions feature space.
> - [x] **Construction** = Top-down, greedy, recursive (TDIDT algorithm).
> - [x] **Root Node** = First attribute test on the entire dataset.
> - [x] **Leaf Node** = Terminal node carrying a class label; has no children.
> - [x] **Entropy Formula:** $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ — range is $[0, \log_2 c]$ generally, $[0, 1]$ for binary.
> - [x] **Information Gain:** $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ — choose attribute with **maximum** IG.
> - [x] **Gini Index:** $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ — range is $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ generally, $[0, 0.5]$ for binary.
> - [x] **Gain Ratio:** Used in C4.5 to **normalize** information gain and handle bias toward multi-valued attributes.
> - [x] **Pure Node Convention:** $0 \log_2 0 = 0$.
> - [x] **ID3** uses Information Gain; **C4.5** uses Gain Ratio; **CART** uses Gini Index.
> - [x] **Stopping Criteria:** Pure node, no remaining attributes, minimum samples threshold, maximum depth.
> - [x] **Pruning** prevents overfitting: pre-pruning (early stopping) vs. post-pruning (subtree removal).
> - [x] **IoT Relevance:** Decision trees are ideal for edge deployment due to low computational footprint, interpretability, and no need for feature scaling.
> - [x] **Weighted Average Rule:** Always weight child impurities by $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ when computing splitting criteria.
> - [x] **Choose Max IG / Min Weighted Impurity:** The best attribute is the one that **maximizes IG** (or equivalently, **minimizes weighted child entropy/Gini**).

---

**End of Module 2 Topic Notes: Decision Trees — Construction**
