topics.title: Impurity Measures: Gini Index, Entropy, Pruning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impurity Measures: Gini Index, Entropy, Pruning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Decision Tree learning algorithms** (e.g., CART, ID3, C4.5), an **Impurity Measure** is a quantitative metric that estimates the *degree of class heterogeneity* present within a node of a decision tree. A node is said to be **pure** when it contains data points belonging to a single class (impurity = 0). The learning algorithm recursively splits the dataset at the feature–threshold combination that *maximally reduces impurity* (or equivalently, *maximally increases purity*).

The three principal impurity measures used in KTU-aligned ML curricula are:

1. **Gini Index** (used by CART for binary classification)
2. **Entropy / Information Gain** (used by ID3 and C4.5)
3. **Misclassification Error** (used as a stopping/pruning reference)

**Pruning** is the post-construction (or mid-construction) process of *removing sub-trees* that do not contribute significantly to predictive power, in order to combat **overfitting** and improve **generalization** on unseen data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** Impurity = a function $I(t)$ defined over the class-probability distribution $P(c \vert t)$ of samples in node $t$. The decision tree algorithm seeks the split $s^*$ that minimizes the *expected impurity* of the child nodes:
> $$s^* = \arg\min_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \; \sum_{j=1}^{K} \frac{N_j}{N} I(t_j)$$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sorting Classroom" Intuition

Imagine a classroom of 100 students, half wearing **blue** uniforms and half wearing **red** uniforms. A teacher wants to *sort* them into smaller groups (nodes) so that each group has only one colour. She keeps asking yes/no questions: *"Is shoe-size > 40?"*, *"Is height > 160 cm?"*, etc.

* **Impurity** = How *mixed* the colours are in a group.
* A group of 50 blue + 50 red = **highly impure** (impurity is at its maximum).
* A group of 100 blue + 0 red = **completely pure** (impurity = 0).
* **Gini Index** and **Entropy** are simply *different rulers* for measuring "how mixed" a group is. Both rulers return 0 for a pure group and reach their maximum for a 50–50 split.
* **Pruning** = After sorting, the teacher realises that the last few questions were so trivial (e.g., *"Does this student have a left ear?"*) that the resulting tiny sub-groups are useless. She **cuts off** those over-specific branches — that's pruning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Gini and Entropy are *monotonically related* over the $[0, 0.5]$ probability range. They almost always pick the **same split** in practice; KTU questions frequently test the *numerical computation*, not the philosophical difference.

---

### 1.3 Mathematical Preliminaries

Let node $t$ contain $N$ samples distributed across $C$ classes. Let $p_i$ denote the fraction of samples in $t$ belonging to class $i$, where $i = 1, 2, \dots, C$. Then:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i = 1, \qquad 0 \le p_i \le 1$$

The goal of every impurity measure $I(t)$ is to satisfy:

* $I(t) = 0$ when the node is **pure** (one class dominates, $p_k = 1$ for some $k$).
* $I(t)$ is **maximised** when classes are uniformly distributed ($p_i = 1/C$ for all $i$).
* $I(t)$ is **symmetric** — depends only on the probability vector $(p_1, \dots, p_C)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Gini Index — Theoretical Breakdown

The **Gini Index** (sometimes called *Gini impurity* to avoid confusion with the economic Gini coefficient) was introduced by **Breiman et al. (1984)** in the CART algorithm. It measures the probability of *misclassifying* a randomly chosen element from the set if it were labelled according to the **class distribution** of the node.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Choose a sample at random from the node — probability $p_i$ that it belongs to class $i$.
2. Choose another sample at random — probability $p_j$ that it belongs to class $j$.
3. The probability that the two samples belong to *different* classes is the impurity.
4. Mathematically:

$$I_{\text{Gini}}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i (1 - p_i) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

* When $C = 2$ and $p_1 = p_2 = 0.5$, we get $I_{\text{Gini}} = 1 - (0.5^2 + 0.5^2) = 0.5$.
* When the node is pure, $p_k = 1$ and all others = 0, giving $I_{\text{Gini}} = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum Gini value** for $C$ classes occurs at uniform distribution and equals $1 - \tfrac{1}{C}$. For binary classification, this maximum is $\mathbf{0.5}$.

---

### 2.2 Entropy and Information Gain — Theoretical Breakdown

**Entropy** is borrowed from **Information Theory (Shannon, 1948)**. It quantifies the *average amount of information* (in bits) needed to identify the class of a randomly drawn sample from the node.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The *surprise* of seeing a sample from class $i$ is $-\log_2 p_i$.
2. The *expected surprise* (entropy) is the surprise weighted by $p_i$:

$$I_{\text{Entropy}}(t) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

3. By convention, $0 \log_2 0 := 0$ (L'Hôpital limit).

The **Information Gain (IG)** of a split $s$ that partitions node $t$ into $K$ children $\{t_1, \dots, t_K\}$ is defined as the *reduction in entropy*:

$$\text{IG}(s, t) = I_{\text{Parent}}(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{K} \frac{N_j}{N} \, I(t_j)$$

The algorithm (ID3) chooses the split that **maximises** Information Gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Entropy** for $C$ classes at uniform distribution is $\log_2 C$. For binary classification, this is exactly **1 bit**.

---

### 2.3 Misclassification Error

Used primarily as a **pruning criterion** (not for split selection, because it is insensitive to small probability changes):

$$I_{\text{Error}}(t) = 1 - \max_{i} p_i$$

It has a maximum of $(C-1)/C$ at uniform distribution and is useful in the **CART cost-complexity pruning** to define the *resubstitution error*.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day reference** for all KTU 2024 question types on this topic. Memorise it!

| **Measure** | **Formula** | **Range (Binary)** | **Used For** | **Algorithm** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gini Index | $I_G(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$ | $[0, 0.5]$ | Split selection | CART |
| Entropy | $I_H(t) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Split selection | ID3 / C4.5 |
| Information Gain | $\text{IG} = I_{\text{parent}} - \sum_j \frac{N_j}{N} I(t_j)$ | $\ge 0$ | Best-split criterion | ID3 / C4.5 |
| Misclassification Error | $I_E(t) = 1 - \max_i p_i$ | $[0, 0.5]$ | Pruning / stopping | CART, C4.5 |
| Gini Gain (split) | $\Delta I_G = I_G(t) - \sum_j \frac{N_j}{N} I_G(t_j)$ | $\ge 0$ | CART best split | CART |
| Cost-Complexity (CART) | $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ | $\ge 0$ | Post-pruning | CART |

> [!NOTE]
> In the cost-complexity formula above, $\vert T \vert$ denotes the *number of terminal (leaf) nodes* in subtree $T$, and $R(T)$ is the *resubstitution misclassification rate* of $T$ on the training data. The parameter $\alpha \ge 0$ controls the **trade-off between tree size and training accuracy**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **IoT applications** (the focus of PECGT525), impurity-based decision trees are deployed in:

* **Edge device fault diagnosis** (e.g., classifying sensor readings as `normal` vs `anomaly`).
* **Energy-aware routing** in WSNs — choose the split that minimises communication cost.
* **Predictive maintenance** on industrial IoT streams — entropy-based features are extracted from vibration / temperature signals.

**Pruning** is *critical* on edge devices with limited memory (e.g., Arduino, ESP32), where the tree must be compact enough to fit in flash storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gini Index from the "Two-Draw" Probability

We derive the Gini Index rigorously using the probabilistic interpretation.

**Setup:** A node $t$ contains $C$ classes with proportions $(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_C)$. Draw **two** samples independently and uniformly from $t$ (with replacement). The probability that they belong to the *same* class is $\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \cdot p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$.

The probability that they belong to *different* classes is the **complement**:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{Gini}}(t) &= 1 - P(\text{same class}) \\
&= 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i (1 - p_i) \quad \text{(algebraic rearrangement)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum value derivation (binary case, $C = 2$):**

Let $p_1 = p$, $p_2 = 1 - p$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{Gini}}(p) &= 1 - \big[p^2 + (1-p)^2\big] \\
&= 1 - \big[p^2 + 1 - 2p + p^2\big] \\
&= 1 - \big[2p^2 - 2p + 1\big] \\
&= 2p - 2p^2 \\
&= 2p(1 - p)
\end{aligned}$$

To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $p$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d}{dp} I_{\text{Gini}}(p) = 2 - 4p = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad p = 0.5$$

Substituting back: $I_{\text{Gini}}(0.5) = 2(0.5)(0.5) = \mathbf{0.5}$. This is the maximum for binary classification. The function is a downward parabola symmetric about $p = 0.5$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Entropy Maximum for $C$ Classes

We want to maximise $H(p_1, \dots, p_C) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$ subject to the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i = 1$.

Using the **method of Lagrange multipliers** with multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i - \lambda \left(\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i - 1\right) \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_i} &= -\log_2 p_i - \frac{1}{\ln 2} - \lambda = 0 \quad \text{for all } i
\end{aligned}$$

This gives $p_i = 2^{-(1 + \lambda \ln 2)} = \text{const}$ for all $i$. Combined with the constraint, $p_i = 1/C$ for all $i$. The maximum entropy is therefore:

$$H_{\max} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{1}{C} \log_2 \frac{1}{C} = \log_2 C$$

For binary classification, $H_{\max} = \log_2 2 = \mathbf{1}$ bit. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A node $t$ in a decision tree has 30 samples: 18 of class A, 9 of class B, 3 of class C. A proposed split on feature $X$ produces two child nodes:

* **Left child $t_L$** — 20 samples: 16 A, 4 B, 0 C.
* **Right child $t_R$** — 10 samples: 2 A, 5 B, 3 C.

Compute (a) the **Gini gain** of the split, and (b) the **Information Gain** of the split. Which impurity measure recommends the split more strongly?

---

#### (a) Gini Gain Calculation

**Step 1: Parent node Gini.**

$$p_A = \tfrac{18}{30} = 0.6, \quad p_B = \tfrac{9}{30} = 0.3, \quad p_C = \tfrac{3}{30} = 0.1$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_G(t) &= 1 - (0.6^2 + 0.3^2 + 0.1^2) \\
&= 1 - (0.36 + 0.09 + 0.01) \\
&= 1 - 0.46 = 0.54
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Left child Gini.**

$$p_A = \tfrac{16}{20} = 0.8, \quad p_B = \tfrac{4}{20} = 0.2, \quad p_C = 0$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_G(t_L) &= 1 - (0.8^2 + 0.2^2 + 0^2) \\
&= 1 - (0.64 + 0.04) = 0.32
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Right child Gini.**

$$p_A = \tfrac{2}{10} = 0.2, \quad p_B = \tfrac{5}{10} = 0.5, \quad p_C = \tfrac{3}{10} = 0.3$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_G(t_R) &= 1 - (0.2^2 + 0.5^2 + 0.3^2) \\
&= 1 - (0.04 + 0.25 + 0.09) = 0.62
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Weighted child impurity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_G(\text{children}) &= \frac{20}{30} \times 0.32 + \frac{10}{30} \times 0.62 \\
&= 0.6\overline{6} \times 0.32 + 0.3\overline{3} \times 0.62 \\
&= 0.2133 + 0.2067 = 0.4200
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Gini gain.**

$$\Delta I_G = I_G(t) - I_G(\text{children}) = 0.54 - 0.42 = \mathbf{0.12}$$

---

#### (b) Information Gain Calculation

**Step 1: Parent entropy.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(t) &= -\big(0.6 \log_2 0.6 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1\big) \\
&= -\big(0.6 \times (-0.7370) + 0.3 \times (-1.7370) + 0.1 \times (-3.3219)\big) \\
&= 0.4422 + 0.5211 + 0.3322 = 1.2955 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Left child entropy.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(t_L) &= -\big(0.8 \log_2 0.8 + 0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0 \cdot \log_2 0\big) \\
&= -\big(0.8 \times (-0.3219) + 0.2 \times (-2.3219)\big) \\
&= 0.2575 + 0.4644 = 0.7219 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Right child entropy.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(t_R) &= -\big(0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3\big) \\
&= -\big(0.2 \times (-2.3219) + 0.5 \times (-1) + 0.3 \times (-1.7370)\big) \\
&= 0.4644 + 0.5 + 0.5211 = 1.4855 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Weighted child entropy.**

$$H(\text{children}) = \tfrac{20}{30}(0.7219) + \tfrac{10}{30}(1.4855) = 0.4813 + 0.4952 = 0.9765 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5: Information Gain.**

$$\text{IG} = H(t) - H(\text{children}) = 1.2955 - 0.9765 = \mathbf{0.3190 \text{ bits}}$$

---

#### (c) Comparison & Interpretation

| Metric | Parent Impurity | Child Impurity | Gain | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gini | 0.5400 | 0.4200 | 0.1200 | Recommends split |
| Entropy | 1.2955 | 0.9765 | 0.3190 | Recommends split more strongly |

**Conclusion:** Both measures agree the split is worthwhile. The **entropy-based Information Gain** yields a *larger absolute gain* because entropy has a wider dynamic range (max 1.0 vs max 0.5 for binary Gini). When normalised by entropy's max, both convey the same qualitative information.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Measure

| **Criterion** | **Gini Index** | **Entropy / IG** | **Misclassification Error** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Computational cost | Lower (no log) | Higher ($\log_2$) | Lowest |
| Sensitivity to $p_i$ near 0.5 | Moderate | High | Low |
| Sensitivity to $p_i$ near 0 or 1 | Moderate | Very high (due to log) | Low |
| Used in | CART (default) | ID3, C4.5 | Pruning only |
| Symmetry | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Pure-node value | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Binary max | 0.5 | 1.0 | 0.5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Impurity-Based Splitting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Node t with N samples] --> B[Compute class proportions p_i]
    B --> C[Enumerate candidate splits s in S]
    C --> D[For each split, partition node into K children t_1 ... t_K]
    D --> E[Compute impurity I of each child]
    E --> F[Compute weighted child impurity]
    F --> G[Compute gain = I_parent - weighted child impurity]
    G --> H{Select split s* with maximum gain}
    H --> I[Recurse on each child node]
    I --> J{Pruning check triggered}
    J -->|Pre-pruning| K[Stop if min_samples or max_depth exceeded]
    J -->|Post-pruning| L[Grow full tree, then collapse sub-trees]
    K --> M[Return final tree T*]
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow begins with an impure node, evaluates *every* candidate split using both Gini and Entropy criteria, selects the winner, and then applies a **pruning** decision at the recursion boundary. This is the canonical CART / C4.5 pipeline as required by KTU 2024 Module 2.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Pre-Pruning vs Post-Pruning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Training Data] --> B[Decision Tree Inducer]
    B --> C{Full Unpruned Tree T_max}
    C --> D[Pre-pruning Path]
    C --> E[Post-pruning Path]
    D --> D1[Stop early if: max_depth, min_samples_leaf, min_impurity_decrease]
    D1 --> F1[Compact Tree T_pre]
    E --> E1[Evaluate sub-trees bottom-up]
    E1 --> E2[Apply cost-complexity R_alpha or reduced-error pruning]
    E2 --> F2[Pruned Tree T_post]
    F1 --> G[Final Model]
    F2 --> G
```

---

### 4.3 Impurity Function Shape (Binary Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[p = 0: Pure class 0] -->|entropy 0, gini 0| B[p = 0.25]
    B -->|entropy 0.811, gini 0.375| C[p = 0.5: Maximum impurity]
    C -->|entropy 1.0, gini 0.5| D[p = 0.75]
    D -->|entropy 0.811, gini 0.375| E[p = 1: Pure class 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Both Gini and Entropy are **concave**, **symmetric about $p = 0.5$**, and attain their maximum at the uniform distribution. Entropy rises more steeply near the boundaries and has a higher peak (1.0 vs 0.5) for the binary case.

---

### 4.4 Cost-Complexity Pruning Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0["T0: full tree"] --> T1["T1: collapse weakest branch"]
    T1 --> T2["T2: collapse next weakest branch"]
    T2 --> T3["T3: continue collapsing"]
    T3 --> Tn["Tn: single root node"]
    T0 -.alpha 1.-> T1
    T1 -.alpha 2.-> T2
    T2 -.alpha 3.-> T3
    T3 -.alpha n.-> Tn
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cost-complexity parameter $\alpha$ produces a **nested sequence of sub-trees** $T_0 \supset T_1 \supset T_2 \supset \dots \supset T_n$. The optimal $\alpha^*$ is selected by **$k$-fold cross-validation** on a held-out validation set.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Gini Index used in decision tree construction. Show that its maximum value for a binary classification problem is 0.5.*

**Model Answer:**

The Gini Index at a node $t$ measures the impurity of the class distribution and is defined as:

$$I_G(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

where $p_i$ is the proportion of samples in class $i$ at node $t$.

For binary classification, let $p$ be the probability of class 1 and $(1-p)$ the probability of class 0. Then:

$$I_G(p) = 1 - [p^2 + (1-p)^2] = 2p(1-p)$$

Differentiating with respect to $p$ and equating to zero:

$$\frac{d I_G}{dp} = 2 - 4p = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = 0.5$$

Substituting back, $I_G(0.5) = 2(0.5)(0.5) = \mathbf{0.5}$. The second derivative is $-4 < 0$, confirming this is a **maximum**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between **pre-pruning** and **post-pruning** in decision trees. Which is more commonly used in CART and why?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pre-pruning (Early Stopping) | Post-pruning |
|---|---|---|
| **When applied** | During tree construction | After full tree is built |
| **Mechanism** | Halt growth using thresholds (max_depth, min_samples_leaf) | Replace sub-trees with leaves using validation set |
| **Risk** | Underfitting (premature stop) | Higher compute cost |
| **CART default** | No | **Yes (Cost-Complexity Pruning)** |

CART uses **post-pruning** because the full tree contains *all* candidate sub-trees, allowing the algorithm to find the **globally optimal** trade-off between complexity and accuracy via the parameter $\alpha$. Pre-pruning makes *local, myopic* decisions that may miss better sub-trees deeper in the structure. (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Entropy** as an impurity measure in decision tree learning. Derive the maximum entropy for a $C$-class problem. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider a dataset of 20 samples at node $t$: 10 belong to class `+`, 6 to class `-`, 4 to class `?`. A split produces two children:

* $t_1$: 12 samples → 10 `+`, 2 `-`, 0 `?`
* $t_2$: 8 samples → 0 `+`, 4 `-`, 4 `?`

Compute the **Information Gain** of the split and state whether the split is recommended. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Entropy measures the *expected information* (in bits) needed to identify the class of a randomly drawn sample. The mathematical form, along with the full derivation of $H_{\max} = \log_2 C$ using Lagrange multipliers, is given in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The student should present:

1. Definition: $H(t) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$ (1 mark)
2. Boundary convention $0 \log 0 = 0$ (1 mark)
3. Setup of the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i - \lambda(\sum p_i - 1)$ (2 marks)
4. Derivation showing $p_i = 1/C$ at the critical point (2 marks)
5. Final result $H_{\max} = \log_2 C$ (1 mark)

**(b)** Numerical solution:

**Parent entropy:**

$$H(t) = -\left(\tfrac{10}{20}\log_2\tfrac{10}{20} + \tfrac{6}{20}\log_2\tfrac{6}{20} + \tfrac{4}{20}\log_2\tfrac{4}{20}\right)$$

$$= -\big(0.5 \times (-1) + 0.3 \times (-1.7370) + 0.2 \times (-2.3219)\big)$$

$$= 0.5 + 0.5211 + 0.4644 = 1.4855 \text{ bits}$$

**Child $t_1$ entropy** (proportions $10/12, 2/12, 0$):

$$H(t_1) = -\left(\tfrac{10}{12}\log_2\tfrac{10}{12} + \tfrac{2}{12}\log_2\tfrac{2}{12} + 0\right)$$

$$= -\big(0.8333 \times (-0.2630) + 0.1667 \times (-2.5850)\big) = 0.2192 + 0.4308 = 0.6500 \text{ bits}$$

**Child $t_2$ entropy** (proportions $0, 4/8, 4/8$):

$$H(t_2) = -\left(0 + \tfrac{4}{8}\log_2\tfrac{4}{8} + \tfrac{4}{8}\log_2\tfrac{4}{8}\right) = -2 \times 0.5 \times (-1) = 1.0000 \text{ bits}$$

**Weighted child entropy:**

$$H(\text{children}) = \tfrac{12}{20}(0.6500) + \tfrac{8}{20}(1.0000) = 0.39 + 0.40 = 0.79 \text{ bits}$$

**Information Gain:**

$$\text{IG} = 1.4855 - 0.79 = \mathbf{0.6955 \text{ bits}}$$

Since $\text{IG} > 0$, the split **is recommended**. The substantial gain indicates the split creates significantly purer children. (7 marks distributed: 2 for parent entropy, 1.5 for each child entropy, 1 for weighted sum, 1 for final IG, 1 for interpretation)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is **cost-complexity pruning** in CART? Derive the cost-complexity criterion $R_\alpha(T)$ and explain the role of the parameter $\alpha$. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, show how the **Gini Index** is computed for a multi-class problem (at least 3 classes) and demonstrate the computation of **Gini gain** for a candidate split. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Cost-complexity pruning, introduced by **Breiman et al. (1984)** in CART, is a post-pruning technique that balances the *training error* of a tree against its *complexity* (size).

The cost-complexity measure for a tree $T$ is:

$$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$

where:
* $R(T)$ = misclassification rate of $T$ on the training data (resubstitution error).
* $\vert T \vert$ = number of terminal (leaf) nodes in $T$.
* $\alpha \ge 0$ = complexity parameter.

The **role of $\alpha$**:
* When $\alpha = 0$: the criterion selects the full tree $T_0$ (lowest training error).
* When $\alpha \to \infty$: the criterion collapses the tree to a single root node (lowest complexity).
* For each $\alpha$, there exists a unique *minimal sub-tree* $T(\alpha)$ that minimises $R_\alpha$.
* The **optimal $\alpha^*$** is chosen by **$k$-fold cross-validation**: for each fold, grow the tree on the training portion and compute the test error of $T(\alpha)$; select $\alpha$ that minimises average test error.

The algorithm proceeds by:
1. Growing the full tree $T_0$.
2. Computing the *effective $\alpha$* for each internal node: $\alpha_{\text{eff}}(t) = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert T_t \vert - 1}$.
3. Collapsing the node with the smallest $\alpha_{\text{eff}}$ — this produces the next tree in the sequence.
4. Repeating until only the root remains.
5. Selecting the best tree on the validation set.

(7 marks: 2 for the formula, 2 for role of $\alpha$, 3 for the algorithm sequence)

**(b)** Worked example is given in **Section 3.3(a)** of these notes with three classes A, B, C. The student should present:

1. Identify the parent proportions (1 mark)
2. Compute $I_G(t) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ for parent (1 mark)
3. Compute $I_G$ for each child separately (2 marks)
4. Compute weighted child impurity using $N_j/N$ (1 mark)
5. Compute Gini gain = $I_G(\text{parent}) - I_G(\text{children})$ (1 mark)
6. Interpret the result (1 mark)

**Summary answer (from Section 3.3):** Gini gain = $\mathbf{0.12}$, split is recommended as it reduces impurity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\log_2$ base:** Entropy is in *bits*. Using $\ln$ (natural log) is technically a *rescaled* entropy (in nats) and may be marked partially correct only if the examiner is lenient. **Always use $\log_2$.**
> 2. **Missing the $0 \log 0 = 0$ convention:** When a class is absent from a child, students often write $0 \log 0 = 0$ *and* attempt to compute it, losing 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to weight children by $N_j / N$:** A common 2-mark deduction. Always weight by the *sample proportion*, not by 1/$K$.
> 4. **Confusing Gini Index with Gini Coefficient:** They are *different* metrics. Gini Index is for decision trees; Gini Coefficient is for income inequality.
> 5. **Confusing Information Gain with Gain Ratio:** C4.5 uses **Gain Ratio** = IG / SplitInfo, but ID3 and the basic syllabus use plain IG. Verify the question's algorithm context.
> 6. **Pruning direction error:** Students often claim pre-pruning is "removing leaves from a fully grown tree." This is the *definition* of post-pruning. Pre-pruning **stops growth early**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Impurity** quantifies class mixing at a tree node. Pure node → impurity = 0.
- [x] **Gini Index**: $I_G = 1 - \sum p_i^2$. Max = $1 - 1/C$. Binary max = **0.5**.
- [x] **Entropy**: $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$. Max = $\log_2 C$. Binary max = **1.0 bit**.
- [x] **Information Gain** = $H(\text{parent}) - \text{weighted avg } H(\text{children})$.
- [x] **Gini Gain** = $I_G(\text{parent}) - \text{weighted avg } I_G(\text{children})$.
- [x] **Misclassification Error** = $1 - \max_i p_i$. Used for **pruning**, not split selection.
- [x] **Gini is computationally cheaper** (no log); Entropy is more sensitive near boundaries.
- [x] For binary trees, Gini and Entropy give **almost identical splits** (Taylor expansion: $H \approx 2 \cdot I_G$ near $p = 0.5$).
- [x] **Pre-pruning** = stop early (max_depth, min_samples_leaf, min_impurity_decrease).
- [x] **Post-pruning** = grow full tree, then collapse sub-trees (cost-complexity, reduced-error).
- [x] CART default = **post-pruning with cost-complexity** $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$.
- [x] **Optimal $\alpha^*$** is found by **$k$-fold cross-validation**, not by training error.
- [x] Pruning combats **overfitting** and improves **generalisation** to unseen IoT sensor data.
- [x] The **nested sequence** property of cost-complexity pruning: $T_0 \supset T_1 \supset \dots \supset \{root\}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines (SVM): Linear SVM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines (SVM): Linear SVM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised discriminative classifier** that finds an **optimal separating hyperplane** in an N-dimensional feature space by **maximizing the geometric margin** between the closest data points of opposing classes. The points lying precisely on the margin boundaries are called **Support Vectors**, and they alone dictate the position and orientation of the decision surface.

A **Linear SVM** is the base case where the training data is assumed to be (or is forced to be, via a soft-margin relaxation) **linearly separable**, meaning a single straight hyperplane can classify all points without error.

Mathematically, a linear classifier is governed by the decision function:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b$$

where:
- $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **weight vector** (normal to the hyperplane)
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias term** (offset from origin)
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the input feature vector
- The decision boundary (hyperplane) is the locus where $f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wideest Road" Intuition

Imagine two rival villages (Class +1 and Class -1) sitting on a 2D map. You are a town planner tasked with building a **road (the hyperplane)** to separate them. If you draw a line close to one village, that village feels encroached. If you draw it sloppily, the villages might "leak" into the road over time (misclassification under noise).

> [!NOTE]
> **SVM's Philosophy:** Draw the **widest possible road** strictly in the middle, so that even if the villages expand a little, the road still separates them. The **curbs** of this widest road touch the nearest houses — these houses are the **Support Vectors**.

The "width of the road" is the **margin**, and the houses on the curb are the **support vectors**. Only these touch-points matter; the rest of the village can be reshaped and the road won't move. This is the source of SVM's **geometric robustness**.

## 1.3 Why Linear SVM Matters in IoT Applications

In IoT ecosystems (smart health, industrial monitoring, smart agriculture), sensor data is often:
- **Low-dimensional** (few features like temperature, pressure, vibration)
- **Approximately linearly separable** after preprocessing
- **Resource-constrained** (no room for heavy deep models on edge devices)

A **Linear SVM** is ideal here because:
- It is **mathematically closed-form** (no iterative backpropagation)
- It has **predictable inference cost** (single dot product + comparison)
- It is **interpretable** ($\mathbf{w}$ directly reveals feature importance — critical for explainable IoT)

## 1.4 The Three Geometric Entities

| Entity | Symbol | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Hyperplane** | $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | The "median line" that splits the two classes |
| **Positive Margin Boundary** | $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = +1$ | The curb on the +1 class side, touched by support vectors |
| **Negative Margin Boundary** | $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = -1$ | The curb on the -1 class side, touched by support vectors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **1** (not any other value) is a *scaling convention* enabled by the fact that scaling $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ simultaneously does not change the hyperplane. Setting the margin to $\pm 1$ standardizes the math without loss of generality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing the Linear SVM — Step by Step

The construction of a Linear SVM proceeds through **five logical pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — The Decision Rule
For a test point $\mathbf{x}$:
- Predict $y = +1$ if $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b \geq 0$
- Predict $y = -1$ if $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b < 0$

Compactly: $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b)$

### Pillar 2 — The Functional Margin
For a single training example $(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ where $y^{(i)} \in \{-1, +1\}$:

$$\hat{\gamma}^{(i)} = y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)$$

For the entire training set of $m$ samples:

$$\hat{\gamma} = \min_{i=1,\dots,m} \; \hat{\gamma}^{(i)}$$

> **Why?** If $y^{(i)} = +1$ and the prediction is large and positive, $\hat{\gamma}^{(i)}$ is large (good). If $y^{(i)} = -1$ and prediction is large and negative, their product is large positive (also good). A **negative** $\hat{\gamma}^{(i)}$ indicates misclassification.

**Problem with functional margin:** It is **not scale-invariant**. Multiplying $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ by $10$ artificially inflates the margin. Hence we need a *geometric* version.

### Pillar 3 — The Geometric Margin
The **perpendicular Euclidean distance** from a point $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ is:

$$d^{(i)} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

Recognizing the sign from the true label gives the **geometric margin**:

$${\gamma}^{(i)} = y^{(i)} \frac{(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

> **Insight:** The geometric margin **is** scale-invariant, because scaling $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ by any $c > 0$ scales both numerator and denominator equally.

### Pillar 4 — The Canonical Hyperplane (Scaling Trick)
We impose the constraint that for the *closest* training point, the functional margin equals 1:

$$y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, m\}$$

Under this constraint, the **geometric margin becomes** $\dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

### Pillar 5 — The Primal Optimization Problem (Hard Margin)
Maximizing the margin $\dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ subject to all points being correctly classified with margin $\geq 1$:

$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ and the squaring are a *mathematical convenience*. Minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$ is a **strictly convex quadratic program (QP)** with a unique global optimum — this is why SVMs don't suffer from local minima traps like neural networks.

## 2.2 The Lagrangian Dual Formulation

Directly solving the primal QP for $m$ samples (each with $n$ features) scales as $O(n^{3})$. SVM instead solves the **dual problem**, which scales as $O(m^{2})$ or $O(m^{3})$ — *advantageous when $m \ll n$* (common in IoT text/gene data).

Construct the **Lagrangian**:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i}\left[y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) - 1\right]$$

where $\alpha_{i} \geq 0$ are the **Lagrange multipliers**.

Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$$

Substituting back yields the **dual problem**:

$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_{i}\alpha_{j}y^{(i)}y^{(j)}\langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \rangle}$$

subject to:
- $\alpha_{i} \geq 0$ (dual feasibility)
- $\sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$

## 2.3 Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KKT conditions characterize the optimal solution of any convex optimization problem. For SVM, they reveal *why* only support vectors matter.

At optimality:
1. **Stationarity:** $\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ and $\sum_{i} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$
2. **Primal feasibility:** $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1$
3. **Dual feasibility:** $\alpha_{i} \geq 0$
4. **Complementary slackness:** $\alpha_{i}\left[y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) - 1\right] = 0$

> **The Sparsity Revelation:** From (4), if $\alpha_{i} > 0$, the corresponding point **must lie on the margin** (i.e., $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) = 1$). These are the **support vectors**. If a point is far from the margin, $\alpha_{i} = 0$, and it contributes nothing to $\mathbf{w}$. This is the **sparsity property** that makes SVM efficient at inference.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This consolidated table is the **minimum** you must memorize for the KTU University Exam.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decision function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b$ | Scalar output |
| 2 | Class prediction | $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(f(\mathbf{x}))$ | $\hat{y} \in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| 3 | Functional margin (single) | $\hat{\gamma}^{(i)} = y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)$ | Scale-dependent |
| 4 | Geometric margin (single) | $\gamma^{(i)} = y^{(i)} \frac{(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Scale-invariant (units of $\mathbf{x}$) |
| 5 | Geometric margin (set) | $\gamma = \min_{i} \gamma^{(i)}$ | Worst-case distance |
| 6 | Primal (hard margin) | $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$ s.t. $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1$ | Convex QP |
| 7 | Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} - \sum \alpha_{i}[y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) - 1]$ | $\alpha_{i} \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Weight as SV combination | $\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ | Sparsity via $\alpha$ |
| 9 | Bias sum-to-zero | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$ | From $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial b = 0$ |
| 10 | Dual objective | $\max \sum \alpha_{i} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \alpha_{i}\alpha_{j}y^{(i)}y^{(j)}\langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \rangle$ | Maximize over $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ |
| 11 | Complementary slackness | $\alpha_{i}\left[y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) - 1\right] = 0$ | Identifies SVs |
| 12 | Recover $b$ from SVs | $b = y^{(s)} - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(s)}$ for any support vector $s$ | Average for numerical stability |
| 13 | Inference (test point) | $\hat{y} = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x} \rangle + b\right)$ | Only SVs needed |
| 14 | Hinge loss (for soft margin) | $L_{\text{hinge}}(y, f) = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot f(\mathbf{x}))$ | Piecewise linear |
| 15 | Soft margin objective | $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{m} \xi_{i}$ | $C$ = regularization strength |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Linear SVM

| IoT / Engineering Domain | Linear SVM Application | Reason for Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| **Wearable ECG monitors** | Arrhythmia detection (Normal vs. Abnormal) | Low feature dim., high noise → robust margin |
| **Smart manufacturing** | Predictive maintenance (Fault vs. Healthy vibration spectra) | Fast inference on PLCs |
| **Smart agriculture** | Crop disease detection from leaf spectral signatures | Linearly separable in PCA-reduced space |
| **Network intrusion detection** | DDoS packet classification | High-dimensional sparse data |
| **Edge AI microcontrollers** | On-device gesture/activity recognition | Tiny memory footprint of $\mathbf{w}$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Margin Maximization to Primal QP

### Step 1 — Start with the Geometric Margin Definition

For training set $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$, the **perpendicular distance** from point $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ is given by the standard point-to-plane formula:

$$d^{(i)} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the Label Sign

Since the true class $y^{(i)} \in \{-1, +1\}$ should be on the same side as $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b$, the signed geometric margin is:

$$\gamma^{(i)} = y^{(i)} \cdot \frac{(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

The **dataset geometric margin** is the worst-case (smallest) margin over all points:

$$\gamma = \min_{i = 1, \dots, m} \gamma^{(i)}$$

### Step 3 — State the Margin-Maximization Goal

We want to **maximize** $\gamma$ over all valid $(\mathbf{w}, b)$:

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \gamma \quad \text{subject to} \quad y^{(i)} \frac{(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \geq \gamma, \quad \forall i$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Canonical Scaling

Since $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ and $(\lambda \mathbf{w}, \lambda b)$ describe the same hyperplane, we are free to fix a **scale**. We impose the constraint that the *minimum functional margin* equals 1:

$$y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$$

Under this constraint, the geometric margin simplifies to:

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the *key simplification*. The constraint $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1$ for *every* point means that the *closest* point has functional margin exactly 1. Plugging into the geometric formula gives $1 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$.

### Step 5 — Convert Maximization to a Standard QP

Maximizing $\dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$, which (since the square root is monotonic) is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is for clean calculus:

$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, \dots, m}$$

This is a **convex quadratic program (QP)** with a unique global minimum.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: From Primal to Dual via Lagrangian

### Step 1 — Build the Lagrangian

Multiply each inequality constraint by a non-negative Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_{i} \geq 0$ and subtract from the objective:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i}\Big[y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) - 1\Big]$$

The **primal** is:

$$p^{*} = \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha} \geq 0} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha})$$

The **dual** (swapping min and max) is:

$$d^{*} = \max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha} \geq 0} \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha})$$

For convex problems (like our QP) and with **Slater's condition** satisfied (a strictly feasible point exists), we have $p^{*} = d^{*}$ — *strong duality* holds.

### Step 2 — Stationarity with Respect to $\mathbf{w}$

Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

Set to zero:

$$\mathbf{w}^{*} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i}^{*} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is a **linear combination of training points**, weighted by $\alpha_{i} y^{(i)}$.

### Step 3 — Stationarity with Respect to $b$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$$

So we add the **equality constraint**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back into the Lagrangian

Expand the squared norm:

$$\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} = \left(\sum_{i} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}\right)^{T}\left(\sum_{j} \alpha_{j} y^{(j)} \mathbf{x}^{(j)}\right) = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \alpha_{i} \alpha_{j} y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \rangle$$

Expand the constraint term:

$$\sum_{i} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} = \sum_{i} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \left(\sum_{j} \alpha_{j} y^{(j)} \mathbf{x}^{(j)}\right)^{T} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \alpha_{i}\alpha_{j} y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \rangle$$

And the bias term vanishes because of $\sum_{i} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$.

### Step 5 — Assemble the Dual

Plugging in and simplifying:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}^{*}, b^{*}, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i}\sum_{j} \alpha_{i}\alpha_{j} y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \rangle - \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \alpha_{i}\alpha_{j} y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \rangle + \sum_{i} \alpha_{i}$$

$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; W(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_{i}\alpha_{j} y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \rangle}$$

subject to $\alpha_{i} \geq 0$ and $\sum_{i} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Consider 4 training points in 2D:
> - $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (1, 1),\; y^{(1)} = +1$
> - $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = (2, 2),\; y^{(2)} = +1$
> - $\mathbf{x}^{(3)} = (1, 0),\; y^{(3)} = -1$
> - $\mathbf{x}^{(4)} = (0, 1),\; y^{(4)} = -1$
>
> Find the optimal separating hyperplane and the support vectors.

### Step 1 — Identify Support Vector Candidates

By inspection, the closest pair across classes are $(1, 1)$ on the +1 side and $(1, 0)$, $(0, 1)$ on the -1 side. These are likely the support vectors.

### Step 2 — Use the Symmetry Argument

The two -1 points are symmetric about the line $x_1 = x_2$. So the optimal hyperplane must also respect this symmetry, suggesting it lies on the line $x_1 + x_2 = c$ for some constant $c$, i.e., $\mathbf{w} = (1, 1)$ (up to scale).

### Step 3 — Determine the Constant via Margin Condition

For a support vector from +1 class, $y(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b) = 1$:

$$(+1)\big[(1)(1) + (1)(1) + b\big] = 1 \Rightarrow 2 + b = 1 \Rightarrow b = -1$$

Check on the -1 support vector $\mathbf{x}^{(3)} = (1, 0)$:

$$(-1)\big[(1)(1) + (1)(0) + (-1)\big] = (-1)(0) = 0 \neq 1$$

**Problem!** This is not on the margin — the margin is *not yet* $\pm 1$. The geometric margin here is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

### Step 4 — Re-scale to Make Margin Canonical

Let $\mathbf{w} = k(1, 1)$ and $b = -k$ for some scaling $k > 0$. The hyperplane is the same: $x_1 + x_2 = 1$.

For point $(1, 1)$: $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = k(1+1) - k = k$. For margin = 1, we need $y \cdot f = 1$:

$$(+1)(k) = 1 \Rightarrow k = 1$$

So $\mathbf{w} = (1, 1)$, $b = -1$.

### Step 5 — Verify All Constraints

| Point | $f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 1$ | $y \cdot f(\mathbf{x})$ | Constraint $y f \geq 1$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1, 1), y=+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | ✓ On margin (SV) |
| $(2, 2), y=+1$ | $+3$ | $+3$ | ✓ Strictly satisfied |
| $(1, 0), y=-1$ | $0$ | $0$ | ✗ **VIOLATION!** |

The point $(1, 0)$ is **misclassified**! The data is **not linearly separable** as given. This shows the limitation of hard-margin SVM and motivates the **soft-margin** version (slack variables $\xi_{i} \geq 0$ and penalty $C$).

### Step 6 — Add a Soft-Margin Slack

Modify the constraint to:

$$y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1 - \xi_{i}, \quad \xi_{i} \geq 0$$

The objective becomes:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{m} \xi_{i}$$

where $C$ is a **regularization hyperparameter** that trades off margin width against margin violations.

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Linear SVM Training & Inference Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block diagram represents the end-to-end functional flow of a Linear SVM system, as deployed in an IoT analytics pipeline. It traces data from the sensor to the prediction output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Sensor Stream] --> B[Preprocessing Module]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction n-D vector]
    C --> D[Labelled Training Set]
    D --> E[SVM Training Engine]
    E --> F[Solve Convex QP]
    F --> G[Optimal weights w and bias b]
    G --> H[Identify Support Vectors]
    H --> I[Persist Model to Edge Device]
    I --> J[Inference Engine]
    J --> K[Decision Function sign of wT x plus b]
    K --> L[Class Label +1 or -1]
    L --> M[IoT Actuator or Alert System]

```

**Interpretation:**
- **Blue blocks** are IoT data ingress points.
- **Gold blocks** are the *training-time optimization* stage (heavy compute, runs on cloud/server).
- **Green blocks** are the *deployment and inference* stage (lightweight, runs on edge).
- **Red block** is the final actuation/alert — the IoT system's response.

## 4.2 Architecture Flow — Mathematical Operations Inside the SVM Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Training Data Xi, yi] --> B[Construct Primal QP]
    B --> C[Form Lagrangian with multipliers alpha]
    C --> D[Stationarity: w equals sum of alpha i yi xi]
    C --> E[Stationarity: sum of alpha i yi equals 0]
    D --> F[Substitute into Lagrangian]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Dual QP: maximize over alpha]
    G --> H[Apply KKT Conditions]
    H --> I[Identify Support Vectors where alpha i is greater than 0]
    I --> J[Recover w as sum over SVs only]
    I --> K[Recover b from any SV]
    J --> L[Deploy Model]
    K --> L
    style B fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mermaid Graph — Margin Geometry & KKT Roles

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FeatureSpace["2D Feature Space"]
        A[Negative Support Vector on margin -1 line] --- H[Decision Hyperplane wT x plus b equals 0]
        B[Other negative points margin greater than 1] -.-> A
        C[Other positive points margin greater than 1] -.-> D
        D[Positive Support Vector on margin plus 1 line] --- H
        H ---|Geometric Margin 1 divided by norm of w| D
        H ---|Geometric Margin 1 divided by norm of w| A
    end
    A --> K[alpha i greater than 0]
    D --> K
    B --> L[alpha i equals 0: does not affect w]
    C --> L
    K --> M[Contribute to w and b]
    L --> N[Ignored by KKT Complementary Slackness]
    style A fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff

    style K fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Red nodes** = Support Vectors (the only points that influence the model).
- **Blue node** = The decision hyperplane.
- **Gold/Green nodes** = KKT implication: only SVs (with $\alpha_i > 0$) contribute to $\mathbf{w}$; all others are inactive ($\alpha_i = 0$).

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]

**Differentiate between Functional Margin and Geometric Margin in a Linear SVM. Why is the geometric margin considered a more reliable measure?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | Functional Margin $\hat{\gamma}$ | Geometric Margin $\gamma$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)$ | $y^{(i)}\dfrac{(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ |
| Scale dependence | **Scale-dependent** (changes with $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$) | **Scale-invariant** |
| Geometric meaning | Signed confidence (not actual distance) | True perpendicular Euclidean distance |

**Reliability reason (1 mark):** Since the hyperplane equation $(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = 0)$ and $(\lambda\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + \lambda b = 0)$ describe the **same** decision boundary, the functional margin can be artificially inflated by scaling. Only the geometric margin — being normalized by $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ — gives a meaningful physical distance measure, making it the reliable quantity to optimize.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**What are Support Vectors in a Linear SVM? Why is it said that "only the support vectors define the optimal hyperplane"?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** Support vectors are the training samples that lie **exactly on the margin boundaries**, i.e., they satisfy $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) = 1$. Geometrically, they are the closest points of each class to the decision hyperplane.

**Why they alone define $\mathbf{w}$ (2 marks):** From the KKT **complementary slackness** condition:

$$\alpha_{i}\left[y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) - 1\right] = 0$$

- For non-support vectors: $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) > 1 \Rightarrow \alpha_{i} = 0$.
- For support vectors: $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) = 1$, and $\alpha_{i} > 0$.

Since $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$, only the support vectors (with $\alpha_{i} > 0$) contribute. All other training points are **ignored** in the final model — hence the statement that **only support vectors define the hyperplane**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 🔵 Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q. (a)** State and derive the **primal optimization problem** for a hard-margin Linear SVM. Explain the geometric interpretation of every term in the objective and constraints. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Reformulate the above primal problem into its **Lagrangian dual** form using Lagrange multipliers. List the KKT conditions and show how they lead to the **sparsity** of the support vector expansion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Primal Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Margin Definition (1 mark):** The signed geometric margin of training point $(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ is:

$$\gamma^{(i)} = y^{(i)} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

The dataset margin is $\gamma = \min_{i} \gamma^{(i)}$.

**Step 2 — Margin Maximization Goal (1 mark):**

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \gamma \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y^{(i)} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \geq \gamma, \quad \forall i$$

**Step 3 — Canonical Scaling (2 marks):** Since $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ and $(\lambda \mathbf{w}, \lambda b)$ represent the same hyperplane, we choose a scale where the minimum functional margin equals 1. Then $\gamma = 1 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$. Maximizing $1/\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$ (with $\frac{1}{2}$ for calculus convenience):

$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i}$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Interpretation (2 marks):**
- $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$: Squared inverse of the margin. Minimizing it = **maximizing the margin width**.
- The inequality constraints: demand that **every training point** lies on the **correct side** of its respective margin boundary, i.e., no point enters the "no-man's-land" between $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = -1$.

### Part (b) — Dual Formulation & KKT (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Lagrangian Construction (1 mark):**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i}\Big[y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) - 1\Big], \quad \alpha_{i} \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity (2 marks):**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Dual Problem (2 marks):** Substituting $\mathbf{w}$ back into $\mathcal{L}$:

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_{i} - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_{i}\alpha_{j} y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \rangle$$

subject to $\alpha_{i} \geq 0$ and $\sum \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} = 0$.

**Step 4 — KKT & Sparsity (2 marks):**
- **Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_{i}\Big[y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) - 1\Big] = 0$
- For points **strictly beyond** the margin: $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) > 1 \Rightarrow \alpha_{i} = 0$.
- For points **on the margin**: $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha_{i} > 0$ (these are the support vectors).
- Since $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_{i} y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$, only support vectors (with $\alpha_i > 0$) contribute → **sparsity**.

---

### 🟢 Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Alternative Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Consider the 2D dataset: $(\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (3, 3), y^{(1)} = +1)$, $(\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = (4, 3), y^{(2)} = +1)$, $(\mathbf{x}^{(3)} = (1, 1), y^{(3)} = -1)$. **(i)** Identify the support vectors by inspection. **(ii)** Solve the dual problem and find the optimal $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3$ and the separating hyperplane. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **soft-margin SVM** formulation. How are slack variables and the penalty parameter $C$ introduced, and what is the effect of choosing a very large or very small $C$ on the resulting classifier? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Solving a Toy 2D Dual (7 marks)

**(i) Identifying Support Vectors (2 marks):**
By inspection, the closest -1 point is $(1, 1)$ and the closest +1 point is $(3, 3)$ (or possibly $(4, 3)$). The geometric intuition: the line $x_1 = x_2$ (or close to it) likely separates them. The points $(3, 3)$ and $(1, 1)$ are **most likely the support vectors**.

**(ii) Solving the Dual (5 marks):**

The dual for $m = 3$ samples is:

$$W(\alpha) = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \alpha_3 - \frac{1}{2}\Big[\alpha_1^{2}\Vert\mathbf{x}^{(1)}\Vert^{2} + \alpha_2^{2}\Vert\mathbf{x}^{(2)}\Vert^{2} + \alpha_3^{2}\Vert\mathbf{x}^{(3)}\Vert^{2} + 2\alpha_1\alpha_2 y^{(1)}y^{(2)}\langle \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \rangle + 2\alpha_1\alpha_3 y^{(1)}y^{(3)}\langle \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(3)} \rangle + 2\alpha_2\alpha_3 y^{(2)}y^{(3)}\langle \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \mathbf{x}^{(3)} \rangle\Big]$$

Compute dot products:
- $\langle \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(1)} \rangle = 9 + 9 = 18$
- $\langle \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \rangle = 16 + 9 = 25$
- $\langle \mathbf{x}^{(3)}, \mathbf{x}^{(3)} \rangle = 1 + 1 = 2$
- $y^{(1)}y^{(2)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \rangle = (+1)(+1)(12 + 9) = 21$
- $y^{(1)}y^{(3)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(3)} \rangle = (+1)(-1)(3 + 3) = -6$
- $y^{(2)}y^{(3)} \langle \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \mathbf{x}^{(3)} \rangle = (+1)(-1)(4 + 3) = -7$

By inspection, $\alpha_2 = 0$ (since $(4, 3)$ is not a support vector — the margin would not be tight there). With $\alpha_2 = 0$ and the constraint $\alpha_1 - \alpha_3 = 0$ (i.e., $\alpha_1 = \alpha_3$):

$$W = 2\alpha_1 - \frac{1}{2}\Big[18\alpha_1^{2} + 2\alpha_1^{2} + 2\alpha_1 \cdot \alpha_1 \cdot (-6)\Big] = 2\alpha_1 - \frac{1}{2}[20\alpha_1^{2} - 12\alpha_1^{2}] = 2\alpha_1 - 4\alpha_1^{2}$$

Maximize: $\dfrac{dW}{d\alpha_1} = 2 - 8\alpha_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha_1 = \dfrac{1}{4}$.

So $\alpha_1 = \alpha_3 = \dfrac{1}{4}$, $\alpha_2 = 0$.

Compute $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\mathbf{w} = \frac{1}{4}(+1)(3, 3) + \frac{1}{4}(-1)(1, 1) = \left(\frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}, \; \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}\right) = \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

Compute $b$ from support vector $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$:

$$b = y^{(1)} - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{2}\cdot 3 + \frac{1}{2}\cdot 3\right) = 1 - 3 = -2$$

**Hyperplane:** $\dfrac{1}{2}x_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}x_2 - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 + x_2 = 4$.

**Support vectors:** $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (3, 3)$ and $\mathbf{x}^{(3)} = (1, 1)$ (both with $\alpha_i = 1/4 > 0$).

### Part (b) — Soft-Margin SVM (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):** Hard-margin SVM requires **strict linear separability**, which fails when classes overlap or contain outliers (noisy IoT data). Soft-margin SVM allows controlled violations.

**Step 2 — Slack Variables (2 marks):** Introduce non-negative slack variables $\xi_{i} \geq 0$ that measure "how much" point $i$ violates the margin:

$$y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1 - \xi_{i}, \quad \xi_{i} \geq 0$$

- $\xi_{i} = 0$: point is correctly classified beyond the margin.
- $0 < \xi_{i} \leq 1$: point is correctly classified but inside the margin.
- $\xi_{i} > 1$: point is misclassified.

**Step 3 — Modified Objective (2 marks):**

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{m} \xi_{i} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1 - \xi_{i}, \; \xi_{i} \geq 0$$

$C$ is the **box constraint / regularization parameter** that balances margin width against training error.

**Step 4 — Effect of $C$ (2 marks):**

| Value of $C$ | Behaviour | Effect on Classifier |
|---|---|---|
| $C \to \infty$ | Penalty for any $\xi_i$ becomes prohibitive → $\xi_i \to 0$ | **Approaches hard-margin SVM**; narrow margin, no tolerance for outliers. Risk of **overfitting** to noise. |
| $C \to 0$ | Slack variables become nearly free → $\xi_i$ can be large | **Wide margin** with many violations. **Underfitting** — model becomes too permissive. |
| Moderate $C$ | Balanced trade-off | **Generalizes well** — the practical choice, typically tuned via cross-validation. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing the two margins:** A common blunder is using the **functional margin** in the objective instead of the geometric one. Remember: optimization is over $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$, not over $\hat{\gamma}$. (Lose up to **2 marks**.)
> 2. **Forgetting $\frac{1}{2}$ factor:** When writing the primal, omitting the $\frac{1}{2}$ is acceptable mathematically, but the *gradient* derivation later becomes $\mathbf{w}$ instead of $2\mathbf{w}$ — leading to a factor-of-2 error in $\mathbf{w}$ recovery. (Lose up to **2 marks**.)
> 3. **Mixing primal and dual notations:** Many students write $\alpha_i \geq 0$ in the primal (it belongs in the dual) or forget the equality constraint $\sum \alpha_i y^{(i)} = 0$. (Lose up to **1.5 marks**.)
> 4. **Skipping the KKT complementary slackness step:** In any 14-mark question, the KKT block is worth 2–3 marks. Writing only "use Lagrange multipliers" without stating **all four** KKT conditions is incomplete.
> 5. **Mis-stating Soft Margin as "no constraints":** The slack variables do **not** remove constraints — they only **relax** them via $1 - \xi_i$. The $\xi_i \geq 0$ constraint still exists.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this checklist before stepping into the exam hall.**

### 🔹 Definitions
- [ ] **Support Vector Machine (SVM):** Maximum-margin discriminative classifier.
- [ ] **Linear SVM:** Variant restricted to linear decision boundaries.
- [ ] **Hyperplane:** The $(n-1)$-dimensional decision surface in $n$-D feature space.
- [ ] **Margin:** Perpendicular distance between the separating hyperplane and the closest training points.
- [ ] **Support Vectors:** Training samples lying exactly on the margin boundary ($\alpha_i > 0$).
- [ ] **Hinge Loss:** $\max(0, 1 - y \cdot f(\mathbf{x}))$ — piecewise linear surrogate for 0-1 loss.

### 🔹 Key Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] Decision rule: $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b)$
- [ ] Primal QP: $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$ s.t. $y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1$
- [ ] Lagrangian and stationarity conditions
- [ ] Dual objective: $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \langle \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \rangle$
- [ ] Constraints: $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$
- [ ] $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$
- [ ] $b = y^{(s)} - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(s)}$ for any support vector $s$
- [ ] Soft-margin objective: $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum \xi_i$

### 🔹 Theoretical Insights
- [ ] The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ and squaring of $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ are for **convex QP formulation**.
- [ ] Scaling $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ by any positive constant preserves the hyperplane — exploited to set margin = 1.
- [ ] Strong duality ($p^* = d^*$) holds under Slater's condition (linearly separable data).
- [ ] **Sparsity** arises from KKT complementary slackness: $\alpha_i > 0$ only at support vectors.
- [ ] Linear SVM inference requires only the support vectors, not the entire training set — memory-efficient.

### 🔹 Practical / IoT Context
- [ ] Linear SVM is **fast, low-memory, interpretable** — ideal for edge IoT.
- [ ] $\mathbf{w}$ components directly indicate **feature importance** (useful for explainable AI in healthcare/monitoring).
- [ ] Hard margin → use only for guaranteed separable data.
- [ ] Soft margin → use for real-world noisy IoT data; tune $C$ via cross-validation.
- [ ] When the data is **not** linearly separable, the **kernel trick** (covered in the next module) extends SVM to non-linear boundaries without explicitly mapping to high dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kernel Trick
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kernel Trick

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: KERNEL TRICK

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kernel Trick (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The **Kernel Trick** is a mathematical technique in machine learning that enables a learning algorithm operating in a low-dimensional input space to behave as if it is operating in a high-dimensional (potentially infinite-dimensional) feature space, by replacing the inner product of data points with a positive semi-definite **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$, without ever having to explicitly compute the coordinates of the data in the higher-dimensional space.

In formal mathematical notation, given a feature mapping:

$$\phi : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$$

where $\mathcal{X}$ is the original input space and $\mathcal{F}$ is the higher-dimensional feature space, the kernel function is defined as:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\mathbf{y}) \rangle_{\mathcal{F}}$$

The "trick" lies in the fact that we can compute $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ directly from the original inputs $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$, **without** ever computing the explicit transformation $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ or $\phi(\mathbf{y})$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Movie Rating Twin Cities"**
> Imagine you are a sociologist studying whether two people will have similar movie ratings. The *raw* observable features (age, income, city) may not be enough to predict similarity — these are like our **2D input space**. However, the true underlying psychological profile (creative score, emotional score, social score, intellectual score) is a **higher-dimensional representation** (say, 4D).
> 
> Asking "do they have similar psychological profiles?" is equivalent to computing the inner product of two 4D vectors. But you don't have the 4D coordinates! The **Kernel Trick** says: instead of trying to measure the 4D profile, just observe how they rate a few *common movies* and use that as a proxy similarity score. The kernel function is that proxy — a clever shortcut that produces the same answer as the high-dimensional inner product, using only the low-dimensional observable data.

### 1.3 Why the Kernel Trick is Essential

- **Dimensionality Curse Avoidance:** Explicit mapping $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ into a very high-dimensional (or infinite-dimensional) space is computationally infeasible. The kernel function sidesteps this entirely.
- **Linearity in High Dimensions:** Many problems are not linearly separable in their original space but become linearly separable when mapped to a higher-dimensional space.
- **Modularity:** The same kernel can be plugged into multiple algorithms (SVM, PCA, Ridge Regression, Logistic Regression) — the choice of kernel is decoupled from the optimization procedure.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Input Space | $\mathcal{X}$ | Original low-dimensional data space (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^d$) |
| Feature Space | $\mathcal{F}$ | High-dimensional transformed space where data is linearly separable |
| Feature Map | $\phi$ | Transformation function: $\mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$ |
| Kernel Function | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Computes inner product in $\mathcal{F}$ without explicit $\phi$ |
| Gram Matrix | $\mathbf{K}$ | $n \times n$ symmetric matrix where $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric & Mathematical Motivation

The foundational principle of the Kernel Trick rests on the observation that **many linear learning algorithms can be reformulated entirely in terms of inner products between data vectors.** If we can replace every such inner product with a kernel evaluation, the algorithm operates implicitly in a higher-dimensional space.

### 2.2 Why and How It Works — Structured Logic Steps

- **Step 1: Identify the Linear Algorithm.** Algorithms like the Perceptron, Fisher Discriminant, Linear Regression, PCA, and Support Vector Machines have formulations that depend only on $\langle \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \rangle$ (i.e., dot products).

- **Step 2: Map to Higher-Dimensional Space.** Replace each $\mathbf{x}_i$ with $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$ in a higher-dimensional space $\mathcal{F}$ where the problem becomes linearly separable. The decision function becomes:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \langle \mathbf{w}, \phi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle + b$$

- **Step 3: Express $\mathbf{w}$ in Terms of Training Points.** By the Representer Theorem:
$$\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$$

- **Step 4: Substitute and Discover Inner Product Dependency.** The decision function becomes:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$$

- **Step 5: Apply the Kernel Trick.** Instead of computing $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$ and $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly, directly evaluate the kernel $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x})$. This is the essence of the trick.

### 2.3 Mercer's Theorem (Validity Condition for Kernels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A function $K : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a **valid kernel** (i.e., it corresponds to an inner product in some Hilbert space) if and only if:
> 1. **Symmetry:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = K(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{X}$
> 2. **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** The Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is positive semi-definite for all finite samples $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$.

If both conditions hold, there exists a feature map $\phi$ into a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) $\mathcal{H}$ such that $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\mathbf{y}) \rangle_{\mathcal{H}}$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Below is the consolidated formula sheet for the Kernel Trick — preserve these expressions during exam revision.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Kernel Definition | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\mathbf{y}) \rangle$ | Computed implicitly in original space |
| 2 | Linear Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y}$ | No mapping; original space used |
| 3 | Polynomial Kernel (degree $d$) | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y} + r)^{d}$ | $\gamma > 0$, $r \geq 0$ are hyperparameters |
| 4 | RBF (Gaussian) Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert^{2})$ | $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}$; infinite-dimensional feature space |
| 5 | Sigmoid Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \tanh(\gamma \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y} + r)$ | Not always a valid Mercer kernel |
| 6 | Laplacian Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert_{1})$ | Uses L1 norm; useful for histogram data |
| 7 | Representer Theorem | $\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$ | Optimal solution in RKHS lies in span of training data |
| 8 | Kernelized Decision Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$ | Used in kernel SVM, kernel ridge regression |
| 9 | Kernelization Closure Property | $K_{1}(\cdot,\cdot) + K_{2}(\cdot,\cdot)$, $c K_{1}(\cdot,\cdot)$, $K_{1}(\cdot,\cdot) \cdot K_{2}(\cdot,\cdot)$ | Sums, positive scalar multiples, and products of valid kernels are valid kernels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In IoT applications, kernel methods are widely used for **non-linear fault detection** in sensor streams, **anomaly detection** in network traffic, and **classification of high-dimensional time-series data** from sensor arrays (e.g., wearable health monitors, smart-grid meters, and industrial SCADA systems).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Polynomial Kernel as a Worked Example

Let us work through the most pedagogically valuable derivation — proving that the polynomial kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y})^{2}$ corresponds to a particular explicit feature map $\phi$.

**Setup:** Consider input vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, i.e., $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)$ and $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2)$.

**Step 1:** Write out the dot product of $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$.

$$\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y} = x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2$$

**Step 2:** Compute the square of this dot product.

$$(\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y})^{2} = (x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2)^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Algebraically expand the squared expression.

$$= x_1^{2} y_1^{2} + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 + x_2^{2} y_2^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize this as an inner product in $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ (i.e., a 3-dimensional feature space).

$$= (x_1^{2}) \cdot (y_1^{2}) + (\sqrt{2} x_1 x_2) \cdot (\sqrt{2} y_1 y_2) + (x_2^{2}) \cdot (y_2^{2})$$

**Step 5:** Extract the explicit feature map $\phi$ for inputs in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$.

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^{2} \\ \sqrt{2} x_1 x_2 \\ x_2^{2} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \phi(\mathbf{y}) = \begin{bmatrix} y_1^{2} \\ \sqrt{2} y_1 y_2 \\ y_2^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Verify the kernel identity.

$$\langle \phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\mathbf{y}) \rangle = x_1^{2} y_1^{2} + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 + x_2^{2} y_2^{2} = (\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y})^{2} = K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The explicit feature space for $(\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y})^{2}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ is 3-dimensional. For $(\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y})^{d}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{in}}}$, the feature space dimension is $\binom{d + d_{\text{in}} - 1}{d}$ — and computing $\phi$ explicitly becomes prohibitively expensive. The kernel trick, however, requires only the original $\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{y}$ computation plus a power operation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kernelization of the Dual SVM

**Setup:** Consider the soft-margin SVM dual optimization problem over $n$ training points $\{\mathbf{x}_i, y_i\}$.

**Step 1:** Write the primal SVM objective.

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\langle \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{x}_i \rangle + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

**Step 2:** Construct the Lagrangian with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$.

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\langle \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{x}_i \rangle + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 3:** Apply KKT stationarity by taking partial derivatives w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$, $b$, and $\xi_i$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \implies \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \implies 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

**Step 4:** Substitute these back into the Lagrangian to obtain the dual.

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \langle \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \rangle$$

subject to $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**Step 5:** Identify the inner-product dependency — note the term $\langle \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \rangle$.

**Step 6:** Apply the Kernel Trick by replacing $\langle \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \rangle$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

**Step 7:** Derive the kernelized decision function.

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \langle \mathbf{w}, \phi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalence of Two Kernels (Closure Property)

> [!NOTE]
> **Prove:** If $K_1$ and $K_2$ are valid kernels, then $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) + K_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ is also a valid kernel.

**Step 1:** Symmetry. Since $K_1$ and $K_2$ are symmetric, their sum is symmetric.

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) + K_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = K_1(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x}) + K_2(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x}) = K(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2:** Positive Semi-Definiteness. Consider the Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.

$$K_{ij} = K_1(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) + K_2(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = [\mathbf{K}_1]_{ij} + [\mathbf{K}_2]_{ij}$$

Thus $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{K}_1 + \mathbf{K}_2$. For any vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$:

$$\mathbf{v}^{T} \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}^{T} \mathbf{K}_1 \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{v}^{T} \mathbf{K}_2 \mathbf{v} \geq 0 + 0 = 0$$

since $\mathbf{K}_1$ and $\mathbf{K}_2$ are PSD individually. Hence $\mathbf{K}$ is PSD, and the sum is a valid kernel. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common Kernels

| Kernel | Feature Space Dimension | Hyperparameters | Best Use Case | IoT/ML Typical Application |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------|---------------|----------------------------|
| Linear | $d_{\text{in}}$ | None | Linearly separable data | Linear sensor baselines |
| Polynomial | $\binom{d + p}{p}$ (finite) | $\gamma$, $r$, degree $d$ | Moderately non-linear data | Image-like sensor matrices |
| RBF (Gaussian) | Infinite | $\gamma$ (bandwidth) | Highly non-linear, generic | **Most common for IoT** anomaly detection |
| Sigmoid | Not always valid | $\gamma$, $r$ | Neural-network analog | Sometimes used in shallow models |
| Laplacian | Infinite | $\gamma$ | Histogram data, sparse signals | Spectrum analysis, text from sensors |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Kernel Trick

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data X in original space R^d] --> B{Linear Separability Check}
    B -- Separable --> C[Use Linear Classifier]
    B -- Not Separable --> D[Choose Kernel Function K]
    D --> E1[Linear Kernel]
    D --> E2[Polynomial Kernel]
    D --> E3[RBF Gaussian Kernel]
    D --> E4[Sigmoid Kernel]
    D --> E5[Laplacian Kernel]
    E1 --> F[Compute Gram Matrix K_ij]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G{Verify Mercer Conditions}
    G -- Valid --> H[Kernelized Algorithm e.g., Kernel SVM Dual]
    G -- Invalid --> I[Reject Kernel and Retry]
    H --> J[Optimize alpha via Quadratic Programming]
    J --> K[Compute Decision Function f of x]
    K --> L[Output: Class Label or Regression Value]
    I --> D
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping: Original Space to Feature Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: 1D Points on x-axis] -->|Map phi| B[2D Feature Space: x and x^2]
    B --> C[Linear Hyperplane in 2D]
    C -->|Project back| D[Non-linear Curve in 1D]
    E[Kernel K = x_i x_j + x_i^2 x_j^2] -.-> B
    E -.-> C
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:**
- The original 1D points (e.g., $\{x \in \mathbb{R}\}$) are mapped by the feature map $\phi(x) = (x, x^{2})$ into a **2D feature space** (parabola).
- In the 2D space, a **linear hyperplane** can separate the data.
- The kernel $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i x_j + x_i^{2} x_j^{2}$ replicates the inner product of the 2D representation while operating only on the 1D scalars.

## 4.3 Data Flow Diagram: Kernel SVM Classification Pipeline
