## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the backpropagation algorithm. Why is the chain rule of calculus central to its implementation?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Backpropagation is a supervised learning algorithm that computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight in a neural network by propagating the error signal backward from the output layer to the input layer. **(1 mark)**
> The chain rule is central because each weight influences the loss through a *composition* of functions (linear transformation followed by a non-linear activation, repeated for every layer). The chain rule allows us to decompose $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w^{(\ell)}}$ into a product of locally computable derivatives at each layer. **(1 mark)**
> This decomposition makes gradient computation *order of magnitude efficient* — $O(\vert W \vert)$ per sample instead of $O(\vert W \vert^2)$ for naive numerical differentiation. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between forward pass and backward pass in a neural network. Which one is computationally more expensive and why?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Forward pass:** Computes activations layer by layer from input to output, producing the prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ and the loss $E$. It involves one matrix multiplication and one activation evaluation per layer. **(1 mark)**
> **Backward pass:** Computes gradients of $E$ w.r.t. every weight by propagating error signals $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell)}$ from output to input using the chain rule. It involves one matrix multiplication (with the *transpose* of each weight matrix) per layer. **(1 mark)**
> **Computational cost:** The two passes have *identical asymptotic complexity* $O(\vert W \vert)$ — the backward pass is roughly $2\times$ the cost of the forward pass. This efficiency is precisely the *raison d'être* of backpropagation, in contrast to naive finite-difference gradient estimation, which scales as $O(\vert W \vert^2)$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (i) forgetting to include the activation derivative $\phi'(z)$ in the error signal, (ii) confusing the shapes of gradients and applying outer products in the wrong order, (iii) using the wrong sign in the update rule (must be *subtraction* of $\eta \nabla E$, not addition), and (iv) omitting the caching of $\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)}$ values, which makes the backward pass mathematically undefined. **Memorize the recursive formula** $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(\ell+1)})^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell+1)} \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$ — it is worth 4–5 marks on its own.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, End Semester Model Paper]**
*(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a multi-layer feedforward neural network. Define all relevant variables used in the backpropagation algorithm. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Derive the backpropagation algorithm equations for a 2-layer neural network using the Mean-Squared Error loss function and the sigmoid activation. Clearly show the forward pass, loss computation, backward error signal derivation, and weight update rule. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks:**
>
> A multi-layer feedforward neural network consists of an *input layer*, one or more *hidden layers*, and an *output layer*. Information flows strictly in one direction: input $\to$ hidden $\to$ output (no cycles, no feedback). **(2 marks for diagram — see Section 4.1)**
>
> **Variables (each carries 0.5 mark, totalling 3 marks):**
> - $L$ = total number of layers
> - $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$ = input vector of dimension $n$
> - $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}$ = weight matrix between layers $(\ell-1)$ and $\ell$, of shape $(n_\ell \times n_{\ell-1})$
> - $\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$ = bias vector of layer $\ell$, of shape $(n_\ell \times 1)$
> - $\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}\mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$ = pre-activation
> - $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)} = \phi(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$ = post-activation
> - $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)}$ = predicted output
> - $E$ = loss function (e.g., MSE or cross-entropy)
> - $\eta$ = learning rate
> **(Remaining 2 marks):** Discuss that each neuron computes a weighted sum of its inputs, adds a bias, and applies a non-linear activation $\phi(\cdot)$ such as sigmoid, tanh, or ReLU. Note that IoT applications frequently use ReLU for computational efficiency on edge hardware.

> **Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks:**
>
> See **Section 3.2 to 3.6** of these notes for the complete derivation. The key equations the student must reproduce are:
>
> 1. **Forward pass** (1 mark):
>
> $$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}, \quad \mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \phi(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$
>
> $$\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)}\mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}, \quad \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \phi(\mathbf{z}^{(2)})$$
>
> 2. **Loss** (0.5 marks):
>
> $$E = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} \Vert^2$$
>
> 3. **Output error signal** (1.5 marks):
>
> $$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(2)} = (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(2)})$$
>
> 4. **Hidden error signal via chain rule** (2 marks):
>
> $$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(2)} \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$
>
> 5. **Weight gradients and updates** (2 marks):
>
> $$\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(2)}} E = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(2)}(\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{\!\top}, \quad \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(2)} - \eta\,\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(2)}(\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{\!\top}$$
>
> $$\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(1)}} E = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)}\mathbf{x}^{\!\top}, \quad \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(1)} - \eta\,\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)}\mathbf{x}^{\!\top}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester, Alternative Choice]**
*(a)* Explain with suitable equations the role of the *learning rate* $\eta$ and the *activation function* $\phi(\cdot)$ in the backpropagation algorithm. What is the effect of choosing $\eta$ too small or too large? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* For a 2-2-1 network (2 inputs, 2 hidden, 1 output) with sigmoid activations, given:
- $\mathbf{x} = [1, 0]^{\!\top}$, $\mathbf{y} = [1]$
- $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} = [0.1, 0.2]^{\!\top}$
- $\mathbf{W}^{(2)} = [0.5, -0.5]$, $\mathbf{b}^{(2)} = [0.0]$
- $\eta = 0.1$

Compute the output $\hat{y}$ after one forward pass and the updated weights after one backward pass. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Role of learning rate $\eta$ (3 marks):**
> The learning rate scales the magnitude of weight updates: $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}} E$. **(0.5 mark)**
> - If $\eta$ is **too small**, convergence is extremely slow; the network may appear to "stall" and may get trapped in poor local minima. Training time and energy consumption rise — a critical concern for IoT edge-retraining. **(1 mark)**
> - If $\eta$ is **too large**, the updates overshoot the minimum; the loss may oscillate or diverge. The network fails to converge and weights can blow up to infinity (numerical overflow). **(1 mark)**
> - Good practice: start with $\eta \approx 0.01$ to $0.1$ for IoT models; use *learning-rate schedules* (decay, warm restarts) or adaptive optimizers (Adam, RMSProp) for stability. **(0.5 mark)**
>
> **Role of activation function $\phi$ (3 marks):**
> $\phi$ introduces *non-linearity* — without it, any stack of layers collapses to a single linear transformation and cannot learn complex patterns. **(1 mark)**
> - **Sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$: smooth, interpretable, but suffers from *vanishing gradients* for $\vert z \vert$ large ($\sigma'(z) \to 0$). **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Tanh**: zero-centered variant of sigmoid; still suffers from vanishing gradients. **(0.5 mark)**
> - **ReLU** $\phi(z) = \max(0, z)$: cheap to compute, mitigates vanishing gradients, and is the *de facto* standard for IoT edge inference due to its low energy footprint. **(0.5 mark)**
> - In backpropagation, $\phi'$ appears in the error-signal recursion $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(\ell+1)})^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell+1)} \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$ — a saturated or zero derivative blocks learning. **(0.5 mark)**
>
> **(1 mark)** Conclude with a recommendation: for IoT edge models, **ReLU + Adam optimizer with $\eta = 10^{-3}$** is a robust default.

> **Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Forward pass (3 marks):**
>
> $$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.1 \\ 0.2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.3 \\ 0.5\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix}\sigma(0.3) \\ \sigma(0.5)\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5744 \\ 0.6225\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$z^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & -0.5\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0.5744 \\ 0.6225\end{bmatrix} + 0 = 0.2872 - 0.3113 = -0.0241$$
>
> $$\hat{y} = \sigma(-0.0241) = 0.4940$$
>
> **Backward pass (4 marks):**
>
> $$\delta^{(2)} = (0.4940 - 1)\,\phi'(-0.0241) = (-0.5060)(0.4940)(0.5060) = -0.1265$$
>
> $$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 \\ -0.5\end{bmatrix}(-0.1265) \odot \begin{bmatrix}0.5744(1-0.5744) \\ 0.6225(1-0.6225)\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$= \begin{bmatrix}-0.0633 \\ 0.0633\end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix}0.2445 \\ 0.2350\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-0.01547 \\ 0.01487\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Weight updates:**
>
> $$\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(2)}} E = -0.1265 \begin{bmatrix}0.5744 & 0.6225\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-0.0727 & -0.0788\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$\mathbf{W}^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & -0.5\end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \begin{bmatrix}-0.0727 & -0.0788\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5073 & -0.4921\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(1)}} E = \begin{bmatrix}-0.01547 \\ 0.01487\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-0.01547 & 0.0 \\ 0.01487 & 0.0\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> $$\mathbf{W}^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.1\end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \begin{bmatrix}-0.01547 & 0.0 \\ 0.01487 & 0.0\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.2015 & 0.4 \\ 0.2985 & 0.1\end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Bias updates: $\mathbf{b}^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1(-0.1265) = 0.01265$, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = [0.1, 0.2]^{\!\top} - 0.1[-0.01547, 0.01487]^{\!\top} = [0.10155, 0.19851]^{\!\top}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- [ ] **Backpropagation = Chain Rule + Cached Activations + Gradient Descent.** Master this three-part decomposition.
- [ ] **Always cache** $\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)}$ and $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)}$ during the forward pass — they are *mandatory* inputs to the backward pass.
- [ ] **Output error signal** for MSE + sigmoid simplifies to $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ (the $\phi'$ term cancels) — *memorize this simplification!*
- [ ] **Recursive backprop equation** (the most-tested formula): $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(\ell+1)})^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell+1)} \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$.
- [ ] **Weight gradient** is the outer product of the layer's error signal and the previous layer's activation: $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}} E = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell)} (\mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)})^{\!\top}$.
- [ ] **Bias gradient** equals the error signal itself: $\nabla_{\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}} E = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(\ell)}$.
- [ ] **Update rule uses subtraction**, not addition: $w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\partial E/\partial w$. Wrong sign ⇒ loss diverges.
- [ ] **Vanishing gradient problem** arises when $\phi'(\cdot) \to 0$ (sigmoid/tanh saturation) and $\Vert \mathbf{W} \Vert < 1$ — gradients shrink exponentially with depth. **Exploding gradient** is the converse. Mitigations: ReLU, batch norm, gradient clipping, residual connections.
- [ ] **Computational cost** of one backward pass $\approx 2\times$ cost of one forward pass. Total per-epoch cost $\approx 3\times$ forward-pass cost.
- [ ] **IoT context:** Training is offline (cloud/gateway); inference is online (edge). Backpropagation does not run on the MCU during deployment.
- [ ] **Stochastic variants:** *Batch GD* uses the full dataset per update; *SGD* uses one sample; *Mini-batch GD* uses $b$ samples (e.g., $b=32, 64, 128$) — the industry default.
- [ ] **Adaptive optimizers** (Adam, RMSProp, AdaGrad) modify the bare gradient descent rule with per-parameter learning rates — frequently tested in 3-mark questions.
- [ ] **Sign convention check** for numerical problems: $\hat{y} < y \Rightarrow \delta^{(2)} < 0$ for sigmoid outputs, leading to *positive* weight increments on connections from strongly active hidden neurons (intuitive: strengthen the synapses that "tried" to fire).

> **Final Exam Tip:** In any numerical backpropagation problem, the examiner expects the student to: (1) show all forward equations numerically, (2) explicitly compute $\phi'(z)$ values, (3) state the general $\boldsymbol{\delta}$ recursion before substituting numbers, and (4) display the *final* updated weight matrix clearly. Skipping any of these four steps typically costs 1–2 marks per step on a 7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient Descent Optimization: Batch, Mini-batch, and Stochastic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent Optimization: Batch, Mini-batch, and Stochastic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Gradient Descent

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradient Descent (GD)** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the minimum of a differentiable cost (or loss) function $J(\theta)$ by repeatedly updating the model parameters $\theta$ in the direction of **steepest descent**, i.e., opposite to the gradient of the function with respect to the parameters.

Mathematically, the parameter update rule is expressed as:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)
$$

where:
- $\theta_t$ = parameter vector at iteration $t$
- $\eta$ = **learning rate** (a positive scalar, typically in $(0, 1]$)
- $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ = gradient of the cost function with respect to $\theta$

In the context of **Machine Learning for IoT (PECGT525)**, gradient descent is the engine that trains models deployed on edge devices — anomaly detectors in sensor networks, predictive maintenance models in smart factories, and on-device classifiers in wearable health monitors.

## 1.2 The Three Variants — Formal Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly requires the student to distinguish between **Batch**, **Mini-Batch**, and **Stochastic** Gradient Descent (BGD, MBGD, SGD) in terms of computational cost, convergence behaviour, and memory footprint.

### a) Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)
Also called **Vanilla Gradient Descent**, BGD computes the gradient using the **entire training dataset** of size $m$ at every iteration.

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \left[ \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} L\big(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\big) \right]
$$

### b) Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
SGD uses **only one training example** (randomly sampled) per iteration to estimate the gradient.

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} L\big(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\big)
$$

### c) Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD)
MBGD is a **compromise** — it uses a small batch of $b$ training examples (where $1 < b < m$) per iteration.

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \left[ \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i=1}^{b} L\big(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\big) \right]
$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Hiking Down a Mountain in Fog

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine you are standing on a foggy mountain and want to reach the valley (the minimum of the loss function). You cannot see the entire terrain, only the **slope under your feet**.

- **Batch GD**: Before each step, you measure the slope by surveying the **entire mountain** using drones (expensive, slow, but accurate direction).
- **Stochastic GD**: You take a step using only the **single rock beneath your foot** (fast, noisy, zig-zag path).
- **Mini-Batch GD**: You survey a **small cluster of rocks** around you (balanced speed and accuracy — the industry default).

This analogy directly maps to:
| Real World | Optimization Equivalent |
|------------|------------------------|
| Mountain | Loss function $J(\theta)$ |
| Your position | Parameters $\theta$ |
| Slope underfoot | Gradient $\nabla J(\theta)$ |
| Step size | Learning rate $\eta$ |
| Survey area | Batch size $b$ |

## 1.4 Why This Matters in IoT

In IoT, data arrives in **continuous streams** from sensors. Storing the entire dataset in memory is infeasible for edge nodes. Hence:
- **SGD/MBGD** is preferred in **online learning** and **federated IoT** scenarios.
- **BGD** suits **cloud-based training** of centralized IoT data lakes.
- The learning rate $\eta$ directly impacts **energy consumption** of the edge device — a critical constraint in battery-powered IoT.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation

All three variants share the same fundamental principle: **iterative minimization via gradient information**. The key differences lie in the **granularity of gradient estimation**.

### 2.1.1 The Cost Function (Empirical Risk)

For a supervised learning model with parameters $\theta$, the empirical cost is:

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} L\big(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)}\big)
$$

where $L(\cdot)$ is the per-sample loss (e.g., **Mean Squared Error** for regression, **Cross-Entropy** for classification).

### 2.1.2 Gradient Computation

The gradient of $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$ is the partial derivative vector:

$$
\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} \\ \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_2} \\ \vdots \\ \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_n} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The **negative gradient** $-\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta)$ points in the direction of steepest descent, since the gradient always points uphill.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### For **Batch GD**:
1. Initialize $\theta_0$ (randomly or with zeros).
2. **Compute gradient** using ALL $m$ samples: $\nabla J(\theta_t) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla L_i$.
3. **Update** all parameters: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until $\vert \nabla J(\theta_t) \vert < \epsilon$ or a maximum iteration count is reached.

### For **Stochastic GD**:
1. Initialize $\theta_0$ and **shuffle** the training set randomly.
2. For each example $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$:
   - Compute gradient on the single sample $i$.
   - Update: $\theta := \theta - \eta \nabla L_i$.
3. One complete pass over the dataset = **one epoch**.

### For **Mini-Batch GD**:
1. Choose batch size $b$ (commonly 32, 64, 128, 256 — powers of 2 for hardware efficiency).
2. Partition dataset into $\lceil m/b \rceil$ mini-batches.
3. For each mini-batch $k$:
   - Compute averaged gradient over the $b$ samples.
   - Update parameters.
4. One complete pass = **one epoch**.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind the Variants — Bias-Variance Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight (High-Yield for KTU):** The three variants trade off **gradient estimation accuracy** against **computational efficiency**.

- **BGD**: Gradient estimate has **zero variance** (deterministic) but is **computationally expensive** per iteration.
- **SGD**: Gradient estimate has **high variance** (noisy) but each update is **cheap**.
- **MBGD**: Strikes a **balance** — moderate variance, moderate cost, and is **vectorization-friendly** on GPUs/TPUs/edge NPUs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|--------------|------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Parameter update (general) | $\theta_{t+1}$ | $\theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ | Dimensionless (parameter space) |
| Learning rate | $\eta$ | $\alpha$ or $\eta \in (0, 1]$ | Scalar hyperparameter |
| Batch gradient | $\nabla J$ | $\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla L_i$ | $m$ = total samples |
| Mini-batch gradient | $\nabla J_b$ | $\frac{1}{b} \sum_{i=1}^{b} \nabla L_i$ | $b$ = batch size |
| Stochastic gradient | $\nabla L_i$ | $\nabla_{\theta} L(f(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$ | Single sample |
| Convergence criterion | — | $\vert \nabla J(\theta) \vert \le \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$ = tolerance (e.g., $10^{-4}$) |
| Epochs | $E$ | $E = \lceil m / b \rceil \cdot$ (iterations) | Number of full passes |
| MSE Loss | $J(\theta)$ | $\frac{1}{m} \sum (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | Squared error average |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $J(\theta)$ | $-\frac{1}{m} \sum y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)})$ | Classification default |

## 2.5 Learning Rate Scheduling

In modern implementations, $\eta$ is **not constant**. Common schedules include:

$$
\eta_t = \frac{\eta_0}{1 + \text{decay} \cdot t} \quad \text{(Time-based decay)}
$$

$$
\eta_t = \eta_0 \cdot \text{drop}^{\lfloor t / \text{stepsize} \rfloor} \quad \text{(Step decay)}
$$

$$
\eta_t = \eta_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{(Exponential decay)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why schedule?** A large $\eta$ helps escape local minima early; a small $\eta$ enables fine convergence later. This is analogous to taking large strides when far from the valley and small steps when close.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** | **Preferred Variant** |
|------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Cloud ML (AWS SageMaker, Azure ML) | Training CNNs on millions of images | **Mini-Batch (b = 256–1024)** |
| Edge IoT (Raspberry Pi, ESP32) | Online sensor calibration | **Stochastic** |
| Federated Learning (IoT gateways) | Distributed model training across devices | **Mini-Batch (per-device)** |
| Real-time anomaly detection | Streaming IoT time-series | **Stochastic / Online GD** |
| Research benchmarks | Reproducible small-dataset training | **Batch GD** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule

### 3.1.1 Taylor Series Approximation

Consider a function $J(\theta)$ that is differentiable at $\theta_t$. Using a first-order Taylor expansion around $\theta_t$:

$$
J(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx J(\theta_t) + \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)^{\top} \Delta\theta
$$

**Logic:** We want to choose $\Delta\theta$ such that $J(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) < J(\theta_t)$, i.e., we want to *decrease* the function value.

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Descent Direction

Let us set $\Delta\theta = \alpha \cdot d$, where $\alpha > 0$ is a small step size and $d$ is a unit direction vector ($\vert d \vert = 1$).

$$
J(\theta_t + \alpha d) \approx J(\theta_t) + \alpha \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t)^{\top} d
$$

To achieve the maximum decrease, we must minimize the dot product $\nabla J(\theta_t)^{\top} d$. By the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality**:

$$
\nabla J^{\top} d \ge -\vert \nabla J \vert \cdot \vert d \vert = -\vert \nabla J \vert
$$

Equality holds when $d$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla J$, i.e.:

$$
d = -\frac{\nabla J(\theta_t)}{\vert \nabla J(\theta_t) \vert}
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Update Rule

Substituting $d$ back:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)
$$

where $\eta = \alpha / \vert \nabla J \vert$ absorbs both the step size and the gradient magnitude.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Linear Regression with All Three Variants

### Problem Setup
We have $m = 5$ data points and wish to fit $y = \theta_1 x + \theta_0$ (linear regression) using MSE loss:

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y^{(i)} - \theta_1 x^{(i)} - \theta_0)^2
$$

**Data:**

| $i$ | $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 3 | 5 |
| 4 | 4 | 4 |
| 5 | 5 | 5 |

**Gradients:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{2}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\theta_1 x^{(i)} + \theta_0 - y^{(i)})
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{2}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\theta_1 x^{(i)} + \theta_0 - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}
$$

**Initial parameters:** $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$. **Learning rate:** $\eta = 0.01$.

---

### 3.2.1 Batch GD — Iteration $t = 0$

**Step 1:** Compute predictions $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \theta_1 x^{(i)} + \theta_0 = 0$ for all $i$.

**Step 2:** Compute residuals: $r^{(i)} = \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} = (0 - 2, 0 - 4, 0 - 5, 0 - 4, 0 - 5) = (-2, -4, -5, -4, -5)$.

**Step 3:** Compute $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0}$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{2}{5} \cdot (-2 - 4 - 5 - 4 - 5) = \frac{2}{5} \cdot (-20) = -8
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{2}{5} \cdot [(-2)(1) + (-4)(2) + (-5)(3) + (-4)(4) + (-5)(5)]
$$

$$
= \frac{2}{5} \cdot [-2 - 8 - 15 - 16 - 25] = \frac{2}{5} \cdot (-66) = -26.4
$$

**Step 5:** Update parameters:

$$
\theta_0^{(1)} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-8) = 0.08
$$

$$
\theta_1^{(1)} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-26.4) = 0.264
$$

This single Batch GD step used **all 5 samples** simultaneously.

---

### 3.2.2 Stochastic GD — Iteration $t = 0$ (sample $i = 1$)

**Step 1:** Randomly shuffle the data; pick $i = 1$ with $(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}) = (1, 2)$.

**Step 2:** Per-sample loss gradient:

$$
\frac{\partial L_1}{\partial \theta_0} = 2(\theta_1 \cdot 1 + \theta_0 - 2) = 2(0 + 0 - 2) = -4
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L_1}{\partial \theta_1} = 2(\theta_1 \cdot 1 + \theta_0 - 2) \cdot 1 = -4
$$

**Step 3:** Update:

$$
\theta_0^{(0,1)} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-4) = 0.04
$$

$$
\theta_1^{(0,1)} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-4) = 0.04
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The SGD update for sample 1 only uses **one** data point — much faster per step but produces a noisier trajectory.

---

### 3.2.3 Mini-Batch GD — Iteration $t = 0$ (batch size $b = 2$)

**Step 1:** Take samples $i = 1, 2$ as one mini-batch: $\{(1, 2), (2, 4)\}$.

**Step 2:** Per-sample residuals with current $\theta = (0, 0)$: $r^{(1)} = -2$, $r^{(2)} = -4$.

**Step 3:** Compute averaged gradients:

$$
\frac{\partial J_b}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{2}{2} \cdot \frac{(-2) + (-4)}{2} = -6
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J_b}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{2}{2} \cdot \frac{(-2)(1) + (-4)(2)}{2} = \frac{-10}{1} = -10
$$

**Step 4:** Update:

$$
\theta_0^{(0,b)} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-6) = 0.06
$$

$$
\theta_1^{(0,b)} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-10) = 0.10
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Batch GD** | **Mini-Batch GD** | **Stochastic GD** |
|--------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Samples per update | All $m$ | $b$ (e.g., 32–256) | 1 |
| Gradient variance | **Zero** (deterministic) | **Low** | **High** |
| Per-update cost | **High** | **Moderate** | **Very low** |
| Convergence path | Smooth, direct | Mild oscillations | Very noisy zig-zag |
| Memory required | $O(m)$ | $O(b)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Hardware fit | CPU sequential | GPU/TPU vectorized | Edge / online |
| Epochs to converge | Few | Moderate | Many |
| Escapes local minima? | **No** | Sometimes | **Yes (often)** |
| Best for IoT streaming? | No | Yes (with small $b$) | **Yes** |
| Convergence guarantee | Strong (convex) | Strong | Weak (sub-optimal) |

## 3.4 Why Mini-Batch GD is the Industry Default

The reason MBGD dominates deep learning and IoT-edge training pipelines:

1. **Vectorization**: Modern hardware (GPUs, edge NPUs like Google Edge TPU) can compute $b$ parallel dot products efficiently.
2. **Stable convergence**: Lower variance than SGD, faster than BGD.
3. **Memory-efficient**: Only $b$ samples need to be in RAM at any time.
4. **Online-friendly**: When new IoT sensor data arrives, it can be incorporated into the next mini-batch without retraining from scratch.

The choice $b$ is a **hyperparameter** — too small → noisy, too large → wasted compute.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Three Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initialize Parameters theta] --> B0[Set Learning Rate eta]
    B0 --> C0[Set Batch Size b]

    C0 --> D0{Select Variant}
    D0 -->|b equals m| E1[Batch GD]
    D0 -->|1 less than b less than m| E2[Mini-Batch GD]
    D0 -->|b equals 1| E3[Stochastic GD]

    E1 --> F1[Load ALL m samples]
    F1 --> G1[Compute FULL gradient]
    G1 --> H1[Update parameters]
    H1 --> I1{Converged?}
    I1 -->|No| F1
    I1 -->|Yes| J1[Output trained model]

    E2 --> F2[Load b samples in mini-batch]
    F2 --> G2[Compute average gradient over b]
    G2 --> H2[Update parameters]
    H2 --> I2{End of epoch?}
    I2 -->|No| F2
    I2 -->|Yes| J2[Output trained model]

    E3 --> F3[Shuffle dataset]
    F3 --> G3[Pick ONE sample i]
    G3 --> H3[Compute gradient on sample i]
    H3 --> H3a{More samples?}
    H3a -->|Yes| G3
    H3a -->|No| J3[Output trained model after one epoch]
```

## 4.2 Convergence Path Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start at theta_0] --> B[Iterate]
    B --> C{Gradient noise level}
    C -->|Zero| D[Batch Path - straight line to minimum]
    C -->|Low| E[Mini-Batch Path - gentle oscillation]
    C -->|High| F[SGD Path - erratic zig-zag]
    D --> G[Global Minimum]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

## 4.3 Learning Rate vs Convergence Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Choose Learning Rate eta] --> B0{eta magnitude}
    B0 -->|eta too small| C0[Slow convergence - many epochs]
    B0 -->|eta optimal| D0[Fast stable convergence]
    B0 -->|eta too large| E0[Oscillation around minimum]
    B0 -->|eta extremely large| F0[Divergence - loss explodes]
    C0 --> G0[Underfitting risk]
    D0 --> H0[Best generalization]
    E0 --> I0[Sub-optimal final loss]
    F0 --> J0[Training failure]
```

## 4.4 IoT-Edge Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Sensors generate data] --> B[Pre-process on edge]
    B --> C{Memory budget}
    C -->|Large gateway| D[Mini-Batch GD on edge]
    C -->|Tiny sensor| E[Online SGD on chip]
    C -->|Cloud aggregator| F[Batch GD in data center]
    D --> G[Update local ML model]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Global model update]
    G --> I[Anomaly prediction]
    H --> I
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
> **Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Descent. Mention two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Batch Gradient Descent** | **Stochastic Gradient Descent** |
|---|---|---|
| Data used per update | Entire dataset ($m$ samples) | Single sample ($i$) |
| Computation per update | High — processes all $m$ samples | Low — processes 1 sample |
| Convergence | Smooth, deterministic | Noisy, zig-zag |
| Memory | High (stores all $m$) | Low (stores 1) |

**Two advantages of BGD:** (i) Stable, monotonic convergence. (ii) Deterministic gradient — reproducible results.
**Two advantages of SGD:** (i) Fast per-update — suitable for streaming/online IoT data. (ii) Can escape shallow local minima due to noise.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **What is a mini-batch in gradient descent? Why is it preferred over pure batch and pure stochastic variants in deep learning pipelines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **mini-batch** is a small subset of the training data (with $b$ samples, where $1 < b < m$) used to estimate the gradient at each iteration.

It is preferred because:
1. It balances **gradient accuracy** (less noisy than SGD) and **computational efficiency** (cheaper than BGD).
2. It exploits **hardware parallelism** — GPUs and edge NPUs process $b$ samples simultaneously using vectorized operations (SIMD).
3. It enables **stable convergence** while remaining feasible for large datasets and streaming IoT data.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule from the first-order Taylor approximation. Clearly state the conditions under which convergence is guaranteed. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the loss function $J(\theta) = \theta^2$ and initial parameter $\theta_0 = 5$. Perform **two iterations** of (i) Batch GD and (ii) Stochastic GD, with learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, and compare the trajectories. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Write the first-order Taylor expansion of $J(\theta)$ around $\theta_t$:

$$
J(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx J(\theta_t) + \nabla J(\theta_t)^{\top} \Delta\theta
$$

**Step 2:** Require that $J$ decreases: $J(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) < J(\theta_t)$, so $\nabla J^{\top} \Delta\theta < 0$.

**Step 3:** To make $J$ decrease **as fast as possible**, minimize $\nabla J^{\top} d$ subject to $\vert d \vert = 1$. By Cauchy-Schwarz:

$$
\nabla J^{\top} d \ge -\vert \nabla J \vert
$$

with equality at $d = -\frac{\nabla J}{\vert \nabla J \vert}$.

**Step 4:** Choose step size $\eta > 0$ and set $\Delta\theta = \eta \cdot d$:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t)
$$

**Step 5 — Convergence conditions:**
- $J$ is **continuously differentiable** and **convex** (or at least locally smooth).
- Learning rate $\eta$ satisfies the **Robbins-Monro conditions**:
$$
\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty
$$
- Gradient must be **Lipschitz continuous** with constant $L$, and $\eta < 2/L$ for guaranteed descent.

---

#### Part (b) — Iterative Computation (7 marks)

**Setup:** $J(\theta) = \theta^2$, $\nabla J(\theta) = 2\theta$, $\theta_0 = 5$, $\eta = 0.1$.

##### (i) Batch GD

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\theta_1 = \theta_0 - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_0) = 5 - 0.1 \cdot (2 \cdot 5) = 5 - 1.0 = 4.0
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\theta_2 = \theta_1 - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_1) = 4.0 - 0.1 \cdot (2 \cdot 4.0) = 4.0 - 0.8 = 3.2
$$

**Trajectory (BGD):** $5 \to 4.0 \to 3.2$ — smooth, monotonic decrease.

##### (ii) Stochastic GD

For $J(\theta) = \theta^2$, the per-sample loss is identical: $L_i = \theta^2$, so the gradient is still $2\theta$. The numerical values match in this toy case, but the **trajectory interpretation** differs: SGD performs an update after **every single sample** (here, $m$ updates per epoch).

If we instead model $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum (y_i - \theta x_i)^2$ with stochastic sampling, the per-sample gradient would fluctuate.

**Comparison Table:**

| Step | Batch GD $\theta$ | SGD $\theta$ (loss-form) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 5.0 | 5.0 |
| 1 | 4.0 | 4.0 (if $L_i = \theta^2$) |
| 2 | 3.2 | 3.2 (if $L_i = \theta^2$) |

**Conceptual comparison:** BGD computes gradient over the full loss, whereas SGD samples one stochastic gradient — even when numerically equivalent here, SGD generalises to **streaming IoT** where $m$ is unbounded.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Internal Choice Alternative, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain Mini-Batch Gradient Descent in detail. Discuss how the batch size $b$ affects convergence speed, gradient noise, and memory footprint. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A logistic regression model is trained on $m = 10000$ IoT sensor readings using mini-batch GD with batch size $b = 100$. The learning rate is $\eta = 0.05$. Calculate: (i) the number of mini-batches per epoch, (ii) the number of parameter updates per epoch, and (iii) how many total samples are processed in 5 epochs. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — MBGD Explanation (7 marks)

**Mini-Batch GD** computes the gradient over a small batch of $b$ training samples per update:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i \in B_t} \nabla L_i(\theta_t)
$$

**Effect of batch size $b$:**

| $b$ value | Convergence Speed | Gradient Noise | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|
| $b = 1$ (SGD) | Many iterations needed | **Very high** | Minimal |
| $b = 32$ | Fast | Moderate | Low |
| $b = 128$ | Faster | Low | Moderate |
| $b = m$ (BGD) | Few iterations | **Zero** | Very high |

**Key trade-off:** Increasing $b$ reduces gradient variance but increases per-update cost. **Optimal $b$** is hardware-dependent: powers of 2 (32, 64, 128, 256) align with CPU/GPU cache lines.

**IoT relevance:** Edge devices with limited RAM (e.g., ESP32 with 320 KB SRAM) use small $b$ (8 or 16) to fit batches in memory.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $m = 10000$, $b = 100$, $\eta = 0.05$.

**(i) Number of mini-batches per epoch:**

$$
N_{batches} = \lceil m / b \rceil = \lceil 10000 / 100 \rceil = 100 \text{ mini-batches}
$$

**(ii) Number of parameter updates per epoch:**

Each mini-batch triggers exactly **one parameter update**. So:

$$
N_{updates} = 100 \text{ updates per epoch}
$$

**(iii) Total samples processed in 5 epochs:**

$$
\text{Total samples} = 5 \times m = 5 \times 10000 = 50000 \text{ samples}
$$

**Final answer tabulated:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Mini-batches per epoch | $N_{batches}$ | 100 |
| Updates per epoch | $N_{updates}$ | 100 |
| Total samples in 5 epochs | $S_{total}$ | 50000 |
| Learning rate per update | $\eta$ | 0.05 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "iteration" and "epoch":** An **epoch** = one full pass over the dataset; an **iteration** = one parameter update. In BGD, 1 epoch = 1 iteration. In MBGD, 1 epoch = $\lceil m/b \rceil$ iterations. In SGD, 1 epoch = $m$ iterations.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the average in MBGD:** The mini-batch gradient must be the **sum divided by $b$**, not just the sum. Failing to normalize inflates the gradient by factor $b$.
>
> 3. **Sign error in update rule:** Writing $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \eta \nabla J$ instead of the **minus** sign causes divergence, not convergence.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Robbins-Monro conditions:** Simply stating "$\eta$ is small" is incomplete — KTU expects the two sum conditions for rigorous SGD convergence proofs.
>
> 5. **Mixing up formulas in numerical problems:** Students often use $m$ instead of $b$ in MBGD cost expressions, leading to a $\times 100$ error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):**

- **Gradient Descent** = first-order iterative minimisation; update rule is $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$.
- **Three variants** are distinguished purely by the **number of samples $b$** used per gradient estimate.
- **Batch GD** ($b = m$): smooth convergence, high cost, no noise, needs all data in memory.
- **Stochastic GD** ($b = 1$): noisy zig-zag, cheap updates, escapes local minima, ideal for online IoT streams.
- **Mini-Batch GD** ($1 < b < m$): industry default, balances cost and accuracy, hardware-friendly.
- **Epoch ≠ Iteration**: Epoch = one full dataset pass; iteration = one parameter update.
- **Learning rate $\eta$**: too small → slow; too large → oscillation; just right → fast convergence.
- **Convergence conditions**: $\eta$ must satisfy Robbins-Monro (sum to $\infty$, sum of squares $< \infty$); $J$ must be smooth and ideally convex.
- **IoT-specific**: prefer **SGD** on resource-constrained edge, **MBGD** on edge gateways, **BGD** only in cloud.
- **Hyperparameter $b$**: typical values are powers of 2 (32, 64, 128, 256) for hardware alignment.
- **Memory complexity**: $O(m)$ for BGD, $O(b)$ for MBGD, $O(1)$ for SGD.
- **Gradient variance**: BGD = 0, MBGD = low, SGD = high — controls trajectory smoothness.
- **For the exam**: always state the **update rule**, the **loss function form**, the **batch size assumption**, and the **convergence caveat** in long-answer derivations.
- **Common mistake to avoid**: forgetting to divide by $b$ in MBGD averaged gradient; mixing iteration/epoch terminology; sign error in the descent step.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis**: distinguish variants by *convergence behaviour*, *computational cost*, and *memory footprint* — these three axes are routinely tested in Part A and Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Improving Convergence: Learning Rate Schedules, Momentum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Improving Convergence: Learning Rate Schedules, Momentum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Convergence Enhancement in Gradient Descent

In the context of training Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) models — particularly those deployed in **Internet of Things (IoT)** edge-intelligence applications — plain **Vanilla Gradient Descent** often suffers from three critical pathologies:

1. **Oscillation** in narrow ravines of the loss landscape.
2. **Slow convergence** in flat regions (plateaus).
3. **Divergence** when the learning rate $\alpha$ is set too high.

To overcome these pathologies, KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 prescribes two principal families of techniques:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Learning Rate Schedules** — dynamically reduce $\alpha$ as training progresses to fine-tune the weights near the minimum.
>
> **Momentum** — injects "inertia" into the update rule, accelerating motion in consistent directions and damping oscillations in orthogonal directions.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

### A. Learning Rate ($\alpha$)
The **learning rate** $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^+$ is the hyperparameter that scales the magnitude of the parameter update against the negative gradient of the loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ with respect to parameters $\theta$.

$$
\theta_{t+1} \;\leftarrow\; \theta_t \;-\; \alpha_t \,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_t)
$$

### B. Learning Rate Schedule
A **learning rate schedule** is a deterministic or adaptive function $\eta(t)$ (or $\eta(\text{epoch})$) that monotonically (or quasi-monotonically) decreases $\alpha$ during training. KTU 2024 recognises the following canonical schedules:

| Schedule | KTU Designation |
|----------|-----------------|
| Time-Based Decay | $\eta_\text{time}$ |
| Step Decay | $\eta_\text{step}$ |
| Exponential Decay | $\eta_\text{exp}$ |
| Cosine Annealing | $\eta_\text{cos}$ |
| Cyclical Learning Rate (CLR) | $\eta_\text{cyc}$ |

### C. Momentum
**Momentum** is a first-order optimisation accelerator proposed by **Polyak (1964)**. It maintains a velocity vector $v_t$ that accumulates an exponentially decaying moving average (EMA) of past gradients. Two variants are KTU-mandated:

- **Classical (Heavy-Ball) Momentum**
- **Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG)** — Nesterov (1983)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Schedules:** Schedules modify $\alpha$ (the *step size*). Momentum modifies the *direction and magnitude of accumulation* via the velocity $v_t$. The two are **complementary**, not mutually exclusive.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Analogy 1: "The Blindfolded Hiker" → Learning Rate
> Imagine a blindfolded hiker descending a foggy mountain (the loss surface $\mathcal{L}$). The **learning rate** $\alpha$ is the **length of each step** the hiker takes. Big steps (high $\alpha$) get you down fast, but you may overshoot the valley. Tiny steps (low $\alpha$) are safe but painfully slow. A **schedule** is like telling the hiker: *"Take giant steps at the top, but progressively shorter steps as you near the bottom."*

### Analogy 2: "The Bowling Ball on a Slope" → Momentum
> A **bowling ball** rolling down the same mountain will not stop at every pebble. It **gathers speed** when the slope is consistent, and **inertia** carries it through small bumps. The **velocity** $v_t$ is the ball's accumulated speed. **Nesterov momentum** is the same ball, but the hiker **peeks ahead** at where the ball *would* be before choosing the direction — like braking slightly before a sharp turn.

### Analogy 3: "IoT Edge Analogy" → Why This Matters in IoT
> In **IoT ML** (e.g., TinyML on a microcontroller), training or fine-tuning on-device is **energy-constrained**. Better convergence = **fewer iterations** = **less battery drain** = **longer device lifetime**. Hence these techniques are not academic luxuries — they are **deployment necessities**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Hyperparameter Ranges (Industry Standard / KTU Approved):**
> - **Initial learning rate** $\alpha_0$: $\mathbf{0.1}$, $\mathbf{0.01}$, or $\mathbf{0.001}$ depending on optimiser.
> - **Momentum coefficient** $\gamma$ (a.k.a. $\beta$): typically $\mathbf{0.9}$ or $\mathbf{0.99}$.
> - **Decay rate**: $\mathbf{0.01}$ to $\mathbf{0.1}$ per epoch.
> - **Drop factor** in Step Decay: $\mathbf{0.5}$ (halve every $k$ epochs).
> - **Convergence tolerance** $\epsilon$: typically $\mathbf{10^{-6}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-8}}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vanilla Gradient Descent Baseline

For a parameter vector $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and a loss $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$, the update is:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \,\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)
$$

**Limitations:**
- **Fixed $\alpha$** — cannot adapt to local curvature.
- **High variance** — noisy gradients in mini-batch settings cause zig-zag.
- **No memory** — each step is independent of the trajectory.

---

## 2.2 Learning Rate Schedules — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Time-Based Decay (Inverse Time)

$$
\alpha_t = \frac{\alpha_0}{1 + \beta_{\text{decay}} \cdot t}
$$

- **Why it works:** As $t \to \infty$, $\alpha_t \to 0$, satisfying the **Robbins-Monro conditions** for stochastic convergence:
  $$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$$
- **Pitfall:** Decay is often too slow early and too fast late.

### 2.2.2 Step Decay

$$
\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \cdot \rho^{\lfloor t / s \rfloor}
$$

- $\rho \in (0,1)$ is the **drop factor** (commonly $0.5$).
- $s$ is the **step size** in epochs.
- **Why it works:** Mimics manual learning-rate annealing; widely used in **ResNet** and **Inception** training pipelines.

### 2.2.3 Exponential Decay

$$
\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
$$

- $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (in units of $\text{epoch}^{-1}$).
- **Why it works:** Smooth, continuous, and easier to tune than step decay; the $\log\alpha$ decreases *linearly* with $t$.

### 2.2.4 Cosine Annealing

$$
\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min} + \frac{1}{2}\left(\alpha_{\max} - \alpha_{\min}\right)\left(1 + \cos\left(\frac{T_{\text{cur}}}{T_{\max}}\pi\right)\right)
$$

- **Why it works:** The half-cosine shape provides **aggressive decay early** (broad exploration) and **gentle settling late** (fine convergence). Used heavily in **SGDR (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017)**.

### 2.2.5 Cyclical Learning Rate (CLR)

$\alpha$ oscillates between $\alpha_{\min}$ and $\alpha_{\max}$ over a cycle length $M$. Two policies:
- **Triangular:** linear ramp-up and ramp-down.
- **Triangular2:** amplitude halved each cycle.
- **Exp range:** amplitude shrinks exponentially.

$$
\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min} + (\alpha_{\max} - \alpha_{\min}) \cdot \left|\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{M}\right)\right|
$$

**Why it works (Smith, 2017):** High $\alpha$ acts as a *regulariser*, allowing the model to **escape sharp local minima** and find **flatter, more generalisable** minima — critical for IoT models facing distribution shift.

---

## 2.3 Momentum — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Classical (Heavy-Ball) Momentum

Polyak's heavy-ball method adds an exponentially weighted history of past updates:

$$
v_t = \gamma \, v_{t-1} + \alpha \,\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t
$$

**Where:**
- $v_t$ = **velocity** vector (accumulated gradient direction).
- $\gamma$ = **momentum coefficient** (typically $0.9$). This is a **first-order low-pass filter** on the gradient.
- $\alpha$ = learning rate.
- $v_{-1} = 0$ (initial condition).

**Intuition:** In a narrow ravine, the gradient component *along* the ravine (the descent direction) is **reinforced** by $\gamma$, while the *across* the ravine (oscillation direction) **averages out** to zero. This yields faster net progress.

### 2.3.2 Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG)

Nesterov (1983) corrects the "look-ahead" error by evaluating the gradient at a *projected* future point:

$$
v_t = \gamma \, v_{t-1} + \alpha \,\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1})
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t
$$

**Key Difference:** The gradient is computed at $\theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1}$ (the **anticipatory position**) rather than at $\theta_t$. This gives the optimiser a "preview" — if the lookahead point is on a steep slope, it **brakes early**, preventing overshoot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation of NAG:** Think of the ball computing a "ghost gradient" $\gamma$ steps ahead. If that ghost position is *worse* (higher loss), NAG reduces the effective step. This is mathematically equivalent to a **second-order correction** without computing the Hessian.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise the closed-form expressions and the conditions on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$.**

| # | Technique | Update Rule | Key Parameter | Convergence Condition |
|---|-----------|-------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Vanilla GD | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla \mathcal{L}$ | $\alpha$ | $\alpha \in (0, 2/L)$ where $L$ = Lipschitz constant of $\nabla \mathcal{L}$ |
| 2 | Time-Based | $\alpha_t = \dfrac{\alpha_0}{1+\beta t}$ | $\beta$ | Robbins-Monro |
| 3 | Step Decay | $\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \,\rho^{\lfloor t/s \rfloor}$ | $\rho,\,s$ | $\rho \in (0,1)$ |
| 4 | Exponential | $\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \,e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda > 0$ |
| 5 | Cosine Anneal | $\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min} + \tfrac{1}{2}(\alpha_{\max}-\alpha_{\min})(1+\cos(\tfrac{T_{\text{cur}}}{T_{\max}}\pi))$ | $T_{\max}$ | $\alpha_{\min} \ge 0$ |
| 6 | Cyclical | $\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min}+(\alpha_{\max}-\alpha_{\min})\vert\sin(\pi t/M)\vert$ | $M$ | $\alpha_{\min} < \alpha_{\max}$ |
| 7 | Heavy-Ball Momentum | $v_t=\gamma v_{t-1}+\alpha \nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_t);\quad \theta \leftarrow \theta - v_t$ | $\gamma$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$, typically $0.9$ |
| 8 | Nesterov (NAG) | $v_t=\gamma v_{t-1}+\alpha \nabla\mathcal{L}(\theta_t-\gamma v_{t-1});\quad \theta \leftarrow \theta - v_t$ | $\gamma$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$, $0 < \alpha < 1/L$ |

### Conditions & Boundary Values (CRITICAL for Numerical Problems)

- $\alpha > 0$ always.
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ for **stability**; $\gamma \ge 1$ causes divergence.
- For a quadratic $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\theta^\top Q \theta$, Heavy-Ball is **optimal** at $\gamma = \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{L} - \sqrt{\mu}}{\sqrt{L} + \sqrt{\mu}}\right)^2$ where $L,\mu$ are the largest and smallest eigenvalues of $Q$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Schedule + Momentum |
|---|---|
| **TinyML on Microcontrollers** (ARM Cortex-M) | Reduces total iterations → lower energy per training cycle. |
| **Federated Learning in IoT** (1000s of devices) | Momentum compensates for **non-IID** data across clients. |
| **Time-Series Forecasting on Edge** (predictive maintenance) | Schedules prevent overfitting to recent sensor drifts. |
| **Anomaly Detection in IoT** (autoencoders) | Cosine annealing + NAG → flatter minima → better OOD generalisation. |
| **CNN Training on Embedded GPUs** (Jetson Nano) | Step decay is the de-facto standard (matches ResNet paper). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unrolling the Momentum Recurrence (Closed-Form)

The momentum update is a linear recurrence. We can **unroll** it to obtain a closed-form expression for $v_t$ in terms of all past gradients.

### Starting Recurrence

$$
v_t = \gamma \, v_{t-1} + \alpha \, g_t
$$

where $g_t \equiv \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$.

### Step 1 — Substitute $v_{t-1}$ in terms of $v_{t-2}$

$$
v_t = \gamma \left(\gamma v_{t-2} + \alpha g_{t-1}\right) + \alpha g_t = \gamma^2 v_{t-2} + \alpha \gamma g_{t-1} + \alpha g_t
$$

**Conversion logic:** Distribute the $\gamma$ operator across the recurrence at $t-1$.

### Step 2 — Continue unrolling back to $v_0$

$$
v_t = \gamma^3 v_{t-3} + \alpha \gamma^2 g_{t-2} + \alpha \gamma g_{t-1} + \alpha g_t
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the General Pattern

After $k$ unrollings, the coefficient of $g_{t-k}$ is $\alpha \gamma^{k-1}$ (for $k \ge 1$), and the residual term is $\gamma^k v_{t-k}$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Initial Condition $v_0 = 0$

$$
v_t = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \gamma^{\,i} \, g_{t-i} + \gamma^{\,t} v_0
$$

With $v_0 = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\,v_t = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \gamma^{\,i} \, g_{t-i}\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Closed Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** $v_t$ is a **weighted sum of past gradients** with geometric weights $\alpha \gamma^{i}$. The most recent gradient $g_t$ has weight $\alpha$; the gradient one step back $g_{t-1}$ has weight $\alpha \gamma$; two steps back has weight $\alpha \gamma^2$; etc. Since $\gamma \in (0,1)$, the older the gradient, the smaller its influence — this is the **Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA)** behaviour.

### Step 6 — Effective Step-Size Analysis

Rewrite the velocity as:

$$
v_t = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \gamma^{\,i} \, g_{t-i} = \alpha \left(g_t + \gamma g_{t-1} + \gamma^2 g_{t-2} + \cdots \right)
$$

The **sum of weights** is:

$$
S = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \gamma^i = \frac{1}{1-\gamma}
$$

So, in the **steady-state** (assuming all $g_{t-i} \approx g$):

$$
\boxed{\,v_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha}{1-\gamma} \, g\,}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This shows that momentum **amplifies the effective learning rate by a factor of $\dfrac{1}{1-\gamma}$**. For $\gamma = 0.9$, the effective rate is $10\times$ larger than the nominal $\alpha$. This is why practitioners *reduce* $\alpha$ by a factor of 10 when adding momentum.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: NAG from First-Order Taylor Expansion

We want to derive the Nesterov update **rigorously** using a first-order Taylor expansion of $\mathcal{L}$.

### Step 1 — Approximate $\mathcal{L}$ Around $\theta_t$ Using the Lookahead

Consider the point $\tilde{\theta}_{t+1} = \theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1}$. A first-order Taylor expansion of $\mathcal{L}$ around $\theta_t$ gives:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\tilde{\theta}_{t+1}) \approx \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) + \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)^\top (\tilde{\theta}_{t+1} - \theta_t)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Lookahead Displacement

$$
\tilde{\theta}_{t+1} - \theta_t = -\gamma v_{t-1}
$$

So:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\tilde{\theta}_{t+1}) \approx \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) - \gamma \, \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)^\top v_{t-1}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Gradient at the Lookahead Point

For NAG, we evaluate the gradient at $\tilde{\theta}_{t+1}$:

$$
\nabla \mathcal{L}(\tilde{\theta}_{t+1}) \approx \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) - \gamma \, H_t \, v_{t-1}
$$

where $H_t$ is the Hessian $\nabla^2 \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$. **Dropping the Hessian term** (first-order approximation) gives:

$$
\nabla \mathcal{L}(\tilde{\theta}_{t+1}) \approx \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) = g_t
$$

### Step 4 — NAG Velocity Update

Substituting $g_t$ into the momentum template:

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha \, g_t
$$

But in NAG, we want to use the gradient at the **anticipatory point**. Nesterov's original formulation (1983) yields:

$$
\boxed{\,v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha \, \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1})\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Reformulation as Two Coupled Updates

Define an **intermediate variable** $\tilde{\theta}_t = \theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1}$. Then:

$$
\tilde{\theta}_t = \theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1}
$$

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha \, \nabla \mathcal{L}(\tilde{\theta}_t)
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t
$$

This three-line update is the form implemented in production frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Convergence Rate Comparison

For a **strongly convex quadratic** objective $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\theta^\top Q \theta - b^\top \theta$ with condition number $\kappa = L/\mu$ (ratio of largest to smallest eigenvalue of $Q$):

| Method | Convergence Rate | Optimal $\gamma$ | Optimal $\alpha$ |
|--------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Vanilla GD | $\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{L+\mu}\cdot\alpha L\right)^t$ | N/A | $\alpha^* = \dfrac{2}{L+\mu}$ |
| Heavy-Ball Momentum | $\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}\right)^{2t}$ | $\gamma^* = \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}\right)^2$ | $\alpha^* = \dfrac{4}{(\sqrt{L}+\sqrt{\mu})^2}$ |
| Nesterov (NAG) | $\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}\right)^t$ | $\gamma^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}$ | $\alpha^* = \dfrac{1}{L}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** The **NAG rate** is $\mathcal{O}\left((1-1/\sqrt{\kappa})^t\right)$ which is **provably faster** than Heavy-Ball's $\mathcal{O}\left((1-1/\kappa)^t\right)$ when $\kappa \gg 1$ (ill-conditioned problems). NAG is the method of choice for IoT models trained on ill-conditioned loss surfaces.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A loss function has gradient $g_t = 0.5$ (constant in magnitude, alternating sign: $+0.5, -0.5, +0.5, \ldots$). Using **Classical Momentum** with $\alpha = 0.1$ and $\gamma = 0.9$, compute $v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4$ starting from $v_0 = 0$.

### Step 1 — Compute $v_1$

$$
v_1 = \gamma v_0 + \alpha g_1 = (0.9)(0) + (0.1)(0.5) = 0.05
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $v_2$ (with $g_2 = -0.5$)

$$
v_2 = \gamma v_1 + \alpha g_2 = (0.9)(0.05) + (0.1)(-0.5) = 0.045 - 0.05 = -0.005
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $v_3$ (with $g_3 = +0.5$)

$$
v_3 = \gamma v_2 + \alpha g_3 = (0.9)(-0.005) + (0.1)(0.5) = -0.0045 + 0.05 = 0.0455
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $v_4$ (with $g_4 = -0.5$)

$$
v_4 = \gamma v_3 + \alpha g_4 = (0.9)(0.0455) + (0.1)(-0.5) = 0.04095 - 0.05 = -0.00905
$$

### Step 5 — Observe the Damping

| $t$ | $g_t$ | $v_t$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| 1 | $+0.5$ | $+0.0500$ |
| 2 | $-0.5$ | $-0.0050$ |
| 3 | $+0.5$ | $+0.0455$ |
| 4 | $-0.5$ | $-0.0091$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Notice $v_2$ and $v_4$ are **much smaller in magnitude** than $v_1$ and $v_3$. The momentum is **damping the oscillation** by carrying forward the "memory" of the previous step's direction. Without momentum, $|v_t| = 0.05$ for all $t$. With momentum, the alternating gradients nearly cancel. This is the **damping effect** that makes momentum so powerful in narrow ravines.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 SET A]

**Define momentum in gradient-based optimisation. Why is the momentum coefficient $\gamma$ typically restricted to the range $[0,1)$?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** Momentum is a first-order optimisation technique that augments the standard gradient descent update by maintaining a velocity vector $v_t$ which is an exponentially decaying moving average of past gradients. The update rule is:

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t
$$

**Range Justification (2 Marks):** The momentum coefficient $\gamma$ must lie in $[0,1)$ for the following reasons:
1. **Stability:** If $\gamma \ge 1$, the magnitude of $v_t$ grows unboundedly over iterations (since each term is multiplied by $\gamma$ in the unrolled sum), causing the optimiser to **diverge**.
2. **Convergence:** $\gamma < 1$ ensures the geometric series $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\gamma^i = \frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ converges, guaranteeing that older gradients have **diminishing influence** on the current update — exactly the desired "memory decay" behaviour.
3. **Empirically:** $\gamma = 0.9$ is the de-facto default because it amplifies the effective learning rate by a factor of $10$ (since $1/(1-0.9) = 10$), compensating for the typical $10\times$ reduction in nominal $\alpha$ when adding momentum.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 SET B]

**List and briefly explain ANY THREE learning rate scheduling strategies used to improve convergence in deep learning.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):

**1. Step Decay:** The learning rate is reduced by a multiplicative factor $\rho \in (0,1)$ after every $s$ epochs:
$$
\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \cdot \rho^{\lfloor t/s \rfloor}
$$
Commonly $\rho = 0.5$, halving $\alpha$ every 10 epochs. Easy to implement and interpret; widely used in ResNet and Inception.

**2. Exponential Decay:** The learning rate decays continuously and smoothly as:
$$
\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
$$
where $\lambda$ is the decay rate. Avoids the discontinuous "drops" of step decay. $\log(\alpha)$ is linear in $t$, simplifying hyperparameter search.

**3. Cosine Annealing:** The learning rate follows a half-cosine curve from $\alpha_{\max}$ down to $\alpha_{\min}$ over $T_{\max}$ epochs:
$$
\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min} + \tfrac{1}{2}(\alpha_{\max}-\alpha_{\min})\left(1 + \cos\left(\tfrac{T_{\text{cur}}}{T_{\max}}\pi\right)\right)
$$
Provides aggressive decay early (broad exploration) and gentle settling late (fine convergence). Used in **SGDR** and modern transformer training pipelines.

*(Acceptable alternative: Time-Based Decay, Cyclical Learning Rate)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Momentum & Nesterov Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper | Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derive the closed-form expression for the velocity update in Classical (Heavy-Ball) Momentum. Starting from the recurrence $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha g_t$ with $v_0 = 0$, show that:**

$$
v_t = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \gamma^{\,i} \, g_{t-i}
$$

**Then compute the effective steady-state velocity $v_\infty$ and interpret the factor $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$.**

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Unroll the recurrence one step (1 Mark):**

$$
v_t = \gamma(\gamma v_{t-2} + \alpha g_{t-1}) + \alpha g_t = \gamma^2 v_{t-2} + \alpha \gamma g_{t-1} + \alpha g_t
$$

**Step 2 — Continue unrolling to general $k$ steps (2 Marks):**

After $k$ unrollings:

$$
v_t = \gamma^k v_{t-k} + \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \gamma^{\,j} g_{t-j}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply initial condition $v_0 = 0$ (2 Marks):**

Setting $k = t$ and using $v_0 = 0$:

$$
v_t = \gamma^t \cdot 0 + \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{t-1} \gamma^{\,j} g_{t-j} = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \gamma^{\,i} g_{t-i}
$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state analysis (2 Marks):**

If the gradient is approximately constant ($g_{t-i} \approx g$ for all $i$):

$$
v_\infty = \alpha g \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{\,i} = \alpha g \cdot \frac{1}{1-\gamma}
$$

**Interpretation:** Momentum amplifies the effective learning rate by the factor $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$. For $\gamma = 0.9$, the effective rate is $10\alpha$; for $\gamma = 0.99$, it is $100\alpha$. This is why practitioners must **reduce** the nominal $\alpha$ by the same factor when adding momentum.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) algorithm. With the help of a labelled diagram, show how NAG differs from Classical Momentum in the location where the gradient is evaluated. Prove that NAG achieves a convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}\!\left(\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa}}\right)^t\right)$ for $\kappa$-conditioned strongly convex quadratics.**

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — NAG Update Equations (1 Mark):**

$$
v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1})
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t
$$

**Step 2 — Diagram & Key Difference (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Classical Momentum | NAG |
|--------|-------------------|-----|
| Gradient evaluated at | $\theta_t$ (current position) | $\theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1}$ (anticipatory position) |
| Computational cost | 1 gradient eval | 1 gradient eval (at lookahead) |
| Correction | None | **Lookahead correction** — uses future-projected gradient |

**Conceptual Diagram (text-based, 2 Marks):**

```
Classical Momentum Path:
   theta_(t-1) ---> theta_t ---> theta_(t+1)
                       |
                    g evaluated HERE

Nesterov Path:
   theta_(t-1) ---> theta_t ---> theta_(t+1)
                       |
                       |---> theta_tilde = theta_t - gamma*v_(t-1)  [LOOKAHEAD]
                              |
                           g evaluated HERE
```

The gradient is taken at the **lookahead point** $\tilde{\theta}_t = \theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1}$, giving the optimiser a "preview" of the curvature — it brakes early if the projected position is on a steep upward slope.

**Step 3 — Convergence Rate Proof (2 Marks):**

For a strongly convex quadratic $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\theta^\top Q\theta - b^\top\theta$ with eigenvalues in $[\mu, L]$ and condition number $\kappa = L/\mu$, Nesterov (1983) proved that with $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}$ and $\alpha = \frac{1}{L}$:

$$
\|\theta_t - \theta^*\|^2 \;\le\; \|\theta_0 - \theta^*\|^2 \left(1 - \frac{2}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}\right)^t
$$

For large $\kappa$ (ill-conditioned), $1 - \frac{2}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1} \approx 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa}}$, yielding the rate:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathcal{O}\!\left(\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa}}\right)^t\right)\,}
$$

This is **strictly faster** than Vanilla GD ($\mathcal{O}((1-1/\kappa)^t)$) for $\kappa \gg 1$, which is the typical regime in IoT-trained neural networks with ill-conditioned loss surfaces.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Learning Rate Schedules Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper | Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Consider a deep learning model trained using Exponential Learning Rate Decay. The initial learning rate is $\alpha_0 = 0.1$ and the decay constant is $\lambda = 0.05$ per epoch. Calculate the learning rate at epoch 5, 10, and 20. Verify the Robbins-Monro conditions for stochastic convergence.**

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Recall the Exponential Decay Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\alpha$ at Required Epochs (3 Marks):**

**At $t = 5$:**

$$
\alpha_5 = 0.1 \cdot e^{-0.05 \times 5} = 0.1 \cdot e^{-0.25} = 0.1 \cdot 0.7788 = 0.07788
$$

**At $t = 10$:**

$$
\alpha_{10} = 0.1 \cdot e^{-0.05 \times 10} = 0.1 \cdot e^{-0.5} = 0.1 \cdot 0.6065 = 0.06065
$$

**At $t = 20$:**

$$
\alpha_{20} = 0.1 \cdot e^{-0.05 \times 20} = 0.1 \cdot e^{-1.0} = 0.1 \cdot 0.3679 = 0.03679
$$

**Step 3 — Robbins-Monro Verification (3 Marks):**

The Robbins-Monro conditions for convergence of stochastic gradient methods require:

**Condition 1:** $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty$ (the schedule must be "non-summable" — large enough to reach any point).

**Condition 2:** $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ (the schedule must be "square-summable" — small enough to dampen noise).

**Verify Condition 1:**

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_0 e^{-\lambda t} = \alpha_0 \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} = \alpha_0 \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda}}{1-e^{-\lambda}}
$$

This is a **finite geometric series** summed, but to infinity it equals $\frac{\alpha_0 \, e^{-\lambda}}{1-e^{-\lambda}}$ which is **finite**. Thus the *unconstrained* exponential schedule **violates Condition 1** in the strict sense, but in practice a **lower-bound clipping** $\alpha_t \ge \alpha_{\min} > 0$ is applied to satisfy it.

**Verify Condition 2:**

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_0^2 e^{-2\lambda t} = \alpha_0^2 \cdot \frac{e^{-2\lambda}}{1-e^{-2\lambda}}
$$

This is a **finite** geometric sum (since $2\lambda > 0$), so **Condition 2 is satisfied**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** In strict stochastic theory, pure exponential decay may fail Condition 1. In practical deep learning, this is **mitigated by early stopping** or by adding a non-decaying baseline $\alpha_{\min}$. This is the engineering reality behind the mathematical ideal.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Compare and contrast FOUR learning rate schedules (Step Decay, Exponential Decay, Cosine Annealing, and Cyclical Learning Rate) in terms of (i) mathematical form, (ii) hyperparameter complexity, (iii) typical use case, and (iv) suitability for IoT edge deployment.**

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

| Schedule | (i) Mathematical Form | (ii) Hyperparameters | (iii) Typical Use | (iv) IoT Edge Suitability |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| **Step Decay** | $\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \rho^{\lfloor t/s \rfloor}$ | $\alpha_0, \rho, s$ | ResNet, Inception, transfer learning | **High** — simple integer arithmetic, low memory |
| **Exponential Decay** | $\alpha_t = \alpha_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\alpha_0, \lambda$ | RNNs, LSTMs, older frameworks | **Medium** — requires $\exp(\cdot)$ evaluation (or lookup table) |
| **Cosine Annealing** | $\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min} + \tfrac{1}{2}(\alpha_{\max}-\alpha_{\min})(1+\cos(\tfrac{T_{\text{cur}}}{T_{\max}}\pi))$ | $\alpha_{\min}, \alpha_{\max}, T_{\max}$ | Transformers (ViT, BERT), SGDR, modern CNNs | **Medium** — needs $\cos(\cdot)$ and floating-point ops |
| **Cyclical LR (CLR)** | $\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min} + (\alpha_{\max}-\alpha_{\min})\vert\sin(\pi t / M)\vert$ | $\alpha_{\min}, \alpha_{\max}, M$ | FastAI, GANs, super-convergence (Smith 2017) | **Low** — oscillating $\alpha$ increases variance; higher iteration counts |

**Conclusion (in 2-3 sentences for 1 Mark):**
For **IoT edge training** where energy and determinism are critical, **Step Decay** is the most suitable due to its integer-only operations and predictable behaviour. **Cosine Annealing** offers superior final accuracy at the cost of additional $\cos(\cdot)$ evaluation. **CLR** is powerful but its oscillation increases total iteration count, making it **less suitable** for battery-constrained IoT devices.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in NAG:** Many students write $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t + \gamma v_{t-1})$ (using $+$ instead of $-$). The correct sign is $\theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1}$. This single sign error costs **2-3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Schedule vs Momentum effects:** When asked "how does convergence improve?", students often give a generic answer. The examiner expects: **Schedule → finer step size near minimum; Momentum → dampens oscillations and accelerates consistent gradients.** Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Robbins-Monro conditions:** Exponential/Step schedules without verification of $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ are considered incomplete answers. **Always include both conditions.**
>
> 4. **Wrong $\gamma$ optimal value for Heavy-Ball:** The optimal $\gamma$ for a quadratic is $\left(\frac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}\right)^2$ (squared), but for NAG it is $\frac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}$ (**not** squared). This is a classic trap. **Memorise both.**
>
> 5. **Missing units in numerical problems:** For exponential decay, $\lambda$ has units of $\text{epoch}^{-1}$. Always specify the units to avoid 0.5-mark deductions.
>
> 6. **Diagrams without labels:** A Mermaid diagram without clear labels for $\theta_t$, $v_t$, $\gamma$, and the gradient point will lose 1-2 marks. KTU examiners award partial marks for *labelled* schematics.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS:**

- [x] **Vanilla GD update:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla \mathcal{L}$. Single hyperparameter $\alpha$.
- [x] **Learning Rate Schedule** = dynamic modification of $\alpha$ over training. 5 KTU-mandated types: **Time, Step, Exponential, Cosine, Cyclical**.
- [x] **Step Decay formula:** $\alpha_t = \alpha_0 \rho^{\lfloor t/s \rfloor}$. Drop factor $\rho \in (0,1)$.
- [x] **Exponential Decay formula:** $\alpha_t = \alpha_0 e^{-\lambda t}$. $\log$-linear in $t$.
- [x] **Cosine Annealing formula:** $\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min} + \frac{1}{2}(\alpha_{\max}-\alpha_{\min})(1+\cos(\pi T_{\text{cur}}/T_{\max}))$.
- [x] **Cyclical LR formula:** $\alpha_t = \alpha_{\min} + (\alpha_{\max}-\alpha_{\min})|\sin(\pi t/M)|$. Aids generalisation.
- [x] **Momentum = velocity-augmented GD.** Maintains $v_t$ as EWMA of past gradients.
- [x] **Heavy-Ball update:** $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha g_t$; $\theta \leftarrow \theta - v_t$.
- [x] **NAG update:** $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \alpha g(\theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1})$; $\theta \leftarrow \theta - v_t$.
- [x] **Key difference NAG vs Heavy-Ball:** NAG evaluates gradient at **lookahead** $\theta_t - \gamma v_{t-1}$.
- [x] **Closed-form velocity:** $v_t = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \gamma^i g_{t-i}$. EWMA with weights $\alpha \gamma^i$.
- [x] **Effective rate amplification:** Momentum amplifies $\alpha$ by $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$. For $\gamma = 0.9$, factor is $10$.
- [x] **Stability constraint:** $\gamma \in [0,1)$ mandatory; $\gamma \ge 1$ ⇒ divergence.
- [x] **Robbins-Monro conditions:** $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ AND $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ ⇒ guaranteed stochastic convergence.
- [x] **Convergence rates for $\kappa$-conditioned quadratics:** GD = $\mathcal{O}((1-1/\kappa)^t)$; Heavy-Ball = $\mathcal{O}((1-1/\sqrt{\kappa})^{2t})$; **NAG = $\mathcal{O}((1-1/\sqrt{\kappa})^t)$** (fastest).
- [x] **Optimal Heavy-Ball $\gamma^*$:** $\left(\frac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}\right)^2$ (squared).
- [x] **Optimal NAG $\gamma^*$:** $\frac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}$ (not squared).
- [x] **IoT relevance:** Fewer iterations ⇒ lower energy ⇒ longer battery life on edge devices.
- [x] **TinyML, Federated Learning, Predictive Maintenance** are the primary IoT application domains.
- [x] **Schedule + Momentum are complementary**, not exclusive — production code uses both.
