# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define clustering. List and briefly explain any two applications of clustering in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Clustering is an unsupervised machine learning technique that groups unlabeled data points into $K$ clusters such that intra-cluster similarity is maximized and inter-cluster similarity is minimized.

**Application 1 — Anomaly Detection in Sensor Networks (1 mark):** IoT deployments generate massive streams from temperature, pressure, and vibration sensors. Clustering the normal sensor readings allows any new reading that does not belong to any cluster (a "noise" point in DBSCAN) to be flagged as a potential fault or attack.

**Application 2 — Device Profiling and Segmentation (1 mark):** In a smart-home IoT network, clustering groups devices (lights, ACs, refrigerators) by their energy-consumption signatures. This enables dynamic load balancing and personalized automation rules.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is DBSCAN? Differentiate between core points, border points and noise points with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**DBSCAN Definition (1 mark):** DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) is a density-based clustering algorithm that groups together points that are closely packed (high density) and marks points in low-density regions as outliers.

**Core Point (1 mark):** A point $p$ is a core point if its $\varepsilon$-neighborhood contains at least $\text{minPts}$ points: $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert \ge \text{minPts}$. Example: If $\varepsilon = 1$ and $\text{minPts} = 4$, and point $p$ has 5 points within radius 1, then $p$ is a core point.

**Border Point (1 mark):** A point that lies within the $\varepsilon$-neighborhood of a core point but itself has fewer than $\text{minPts}$ neighbors. It belongs to a cluster but is on the periphery.

**Noise Point (1 mark):** A point that is neither a core point nor a border point — i.e., it is in a sparse region. It is labeled as an outlier and is not assigned to any cluster.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Module 4 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** in detail. State its objective function and describe the iterative procedure to optimize it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the following 2-D dataset, perform **K-Means clustering with $K = 2$** for **two complete iterations** using Euclidean distance. Initial centroids are $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$ and $\mu_2 = (5, 5)$.

$$
D = \{ (1, 1),\; (2, 1),\; (1, 2),\; (8, 8),\; (9, 8),\; (8, 9) \}
$$

Plot the final cluster assignments. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — K-Means Explanation:**

The K-Means clustering algorithm (Lloyd's algorithm, 1957/1982) is a **centroid-based, partitioning** unsupervised learning method. It divides a dataset $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, \dots, x_N\}$ into $K$ non-overlapping clusters.

**Objective Function (2 marks):** The goal is to minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:

$$
J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert_2^2
$$

**Iterative Procedure (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_K\}$ using either the Forgy method, Random Partition, or K-Means++ (recommended).

**Step 2 — Assignment Step:** For each data point $x_i$, compute the squared Euclidean distance to every centroid and assign $x_i$ to the cluster of the nearest centroid:

$$
C_k^{(t)} = \{x_i : \lVert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \rVert_2^2 \le \lVert x_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \rVert_2^2 \;\; \forall j\}
$$

**Step 3 — Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the arithmetic mean of all points currently in that cluster:

$$
\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t)}} x_i
$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Check:** Repeat Steps 2 and 3 until the centroids stop moving (or movement is below a tolerance $\tau$, typically $10^{-4}$) or until a maximum iteration count $T_{\max}$ is reached. K-Means is **guaranteed to converge** to a local minimum, but not necessarily a global one — multiple restarts (`n_init`) are recommended.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Worked Numerical Example:**

**Initial Setup:** $\mu_1^{(0)} = (1, 1)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = (5, 5)$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment Step:**

Compute squared distances from each point to both centroids:

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2$ | Assignment |
|-------|------------------|------------------|------------|
| $(1, 1)$ | $0$ | $(4^2+4^2) = 32$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2, 1)$ | $(1^2+0) = 1$ | $(3^2+4^2) = 25$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1, 2)$ | $(0+1) = 1$ | $(4^2+3^2) = 25$ | $C_1$ |
| $(8, 8)$ | $(7^2+7^2) = 98$ | $(3^2+3^2) = 18$ | $C_2$ |
| $(9, 8)$ | $(8^2+7^2) = 113$ | $(4^2+3^2) = 25$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8, 9)$ | $(7^2+8^2) = 113$ | $(3^2+4^2) = 25$ | $C_2$ |

So $C_1^{(1)} = \{(1,1), (2,1), (1,2)\}$ and $C_2^{(1)} = \{(8,8), (9,8), (8,9)\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Update Step:**

$$
\mu_1^{(1)} = \left( \frac{1+2+1}{3},\; \frac{1+1+2}{3} \right) = \left( \frac{4}{3},\; \frac{4}{3} \right) \approx (1.333, 1.333)
$$

$$
\mu_2^{(1)} = \left( \frac{8+9+8}{3},\; \frac{8+8+9}{3} \right) = \left( \frac{25}{3},\; \frac{25}{3} \right) \approx (8.333, 8.333)
$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment Step:**

Recompute squared distances with the new centroids:

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1^{(1)}$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2^{(1)}$ | Assignment |
|-------|------------------------|------------------------|------------|
| $(1, 1)$ | $(0.111+0.111) = 0.222$ | $(54.222+54.222) = 108.444$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2, 1)$ | $(0.444+0.111) = 0.556$ | $(40.111+53.778) = 93.889$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1, 2)$ | $(0.111+0.444) = 0.556$ | $(53.778+40.111) = 93.889$ | $C_1$ |
| $(8, 8)$ | $(44.444+44.444) = 88.889$ | $(0.111+0.111) = 0.222$ | $C_2$ |
| $(9, 8)$ | $(58.778+44.444) = 103.222$ | $(0.444+0.111) = 0.556$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8, 9)$ | $(44.444+58.778) = 103.222$ | $(0.111+0.444) = 0.556$ | $C_2$ |

Assignments are unchanged: $C_1^{(2)} = \{(1,1), (2,1), (1,2)\}$ and $C_2^{(2)} = \{(8,8), (9,8), (8,9)\}$.

**Iteration 2 — Update Step:**

Centroids remain $(1.333, 1.333)$ and $(8.333, 8.333)$. **Convergence achieved in 2 iterations.**

**Final Answer (2 marks):** The two final clusters are perfectly separated — $C_1$ represents the "low-temperature, low-humidity" cluster and $C_2$ represents the "high-temperature, high-humidity" cluster. The WCSS is $J = 3 \times 2 \times 0.222 = 1.333$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 4 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Explain **Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering** with a neat diagram of a dendrogram. Discuss the **single, complete, average and Ward's linkage** methods with suitable formulas. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **DBSCAN algorithm** in detail. How does it handle noise? Describe the role of the parameters $\varepsilon$ and $\text{minPts}$. Show how DBSCAN can be used for **anomaly detection in IoT sensor data**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — Hierarchical Clustering:**

**Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering (4 marks):** Agglomerative clustering is a "bottom-up" approach. It starts with $N$ singleton clusters (each containing exactly one data point) and repeatedly merges the two most similar clusters until only one cluster containing all $N$ points remains. The result is a binary tree called a **dendrogram**, which records the sequence of merges and the distance at which each merge occurred. To obtain $K$ clusters, the dendrogram is "cut" with a horizontal line at an appropriate height.

**Linkage Criteria (3 marks):** The "similarity" between two clusters is measured by a **linkage function** $\mathcal{L}(C_a, C_b)$:

1. **Single Linkage (MIN):** $d_{\text{single}}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x_i \in C_a,\, x_j \in C_b} d(x_i, x_j)$. Tendency to produce *chaining* — long, thin clusters.

2. **Complete Linkage (MAX):** $d_{\text{complete}}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x_i \in C_a,\, x_j \in C_b} d(x_i, x_j)$. Produces compact, roughly spherical clusters of similar diameter.

3. **Average Linkage (UPGMA):** $d_{\text{avg}}(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_a} \sum_{x_j \in C_b} d(x_i, x_j)$. A compromise between single and complete linkage.

4. **Ward's Linkage:** $\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \frac{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \lVert \bar{x}_a - \bar{x}_b \rVert_2^2$. Minimizes the increase in total within-cluster variance — most similar in objective to K-Means.

**Dendrogram Diagram (Description for Drawing):**
```
Height
  |       ___________________
  |      |                   |
  |   ___|___           _____|
  |  |       |         |     |
  | _|___  __|___   __|___  |
  | |   | |     |  |     | |
  | |   | |     |  |     | |
  p1  p2  p3   p4 p5    p6 p7
```
The vertical axis is the merge distance; horizontal lines represent merges. Cutting the dendrogram horizontally at any height yields a specific number of clusters.

---

**Model Solution (b) — DBSCAN:**

**Algorithm Description (3 marks):** DBSCAN (Ester et al., KDD-1996) clusters data based on the **density** of points. It defines a *core point* as a point having at least $\text{minPts}$ points (including itself) within a circular neighborhood of radius $\varepsilon$. Clusters are formed as maximal sets of *density-connected* core points. Points that are not reachable from any core point are labeled as *noise* (outliers).

**Handling of Noise (2 marks):** Unlike K-Means and Hierarchical clustering — which *force* every point into some cluster — DBSCAN explicitly **rejects** points that are in low-density regions by labeling them as **noise points**. This makes it uniquely suited to anomaly detection tasks.

**Role of $\varepsilon$ and $\text{minPts}$ (1 mark):** $\varepsilon$ controls the *size* of the local neighborhood, while $\text{minPts}$ controls the *minimum density* required to declare a region "dense." Together, they act as a density threshold. Increasing $\varepsilon$ merges nearby clusters but reduces noise; increasing $\text{minPts}$ tightens the definition of "dense" and may convert border points into noise.

**IoT Anomaly Detection Use Case (1 mark):** In an IoT temperature sensor network, DBSCAN is run on the streaming (or batched) sensor readings. Normal sensor behavior forms *dense clusters*; malfunctioning sensors transmitting garbage values appear in low-density regions and are **automatically flagged as noise** without any prior labeling — a critical advantage in zero-shot fault detection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Before Exam!):**
> 1. **Confusing the objective of K-Means with that of K-NN.** K-Means is **unsupervised** and clusters; K-NN is **supervised** and classifies. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks instantly for this mix-up.
> 2. **Writing "minPts is the minimum distance between points" — it is NOT.** $\text{minPts}$ is the **minimum number of points** within the $\varepsilon$-neighborhood.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the convergence guarantee in K-Means.** Always state: *"K-Means is guaranteed to converge to a local minimum in a finite number of iterations, but NOT to a global minimum."*
> 4. **Using absolute-value vertical bars $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table.** This will break the table rendering. Use `\vert` in LaTeX or write "abs(x)" in tables.
> 5. **For hierarchical clustering, not specifying the linkage.** A vague answer like "merge similar clusters" gets zero. You **must** state which linkage (single, complete, average, or Ward's) and provide its formula.
> 6. **Saying DBSCAN needs $K$ in advance.** It does **not** — this is a major distinguishing feature and a guaranteed follow-up question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Clustering is unsupervised** — no labels are used; the algorithm discovers structure in data on its own.
- **K-Means** minimizes **WCSS** using an iterative two-step (assign, update) EM-style procedure; it requires the user to **specify $K$** in advance.
- The **centroid update rule** $\mu_k = \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ is the **unique global minimum** of the within-cluster squared error (proven by setting $\nabla J = 0$).
- K-Means is sensitive to **initialization** — always use **K-Means++** and `n_init ≥ 10` restarts.
- The **Elbow Method** and the **Silhouette Score** are the two most popular ways to choose the optimal $K$.
- **Hierarchical clustering** produces a **dendrogram** — a tree that can be cut at any level to obtain any desired number of clusters; uses $O(N^2)$ memory.
- The four major **linkage criteria** are **Single, Complete, Average, and Ward's**; **Ward's** is the KTU favorite because it shares the WCSS objective with K-Means.
- **DBSCAN** is **density-based**, requires **two parameters** ($\varepsilon$ and $\text{minPts}$), does NOT need $K$, and **explicitly handles noise**.
- DBSCAN classifies points into **core**, **border**, and **noise** — only KTU's three-class taxonomy is acceptable in answers.
- DBSCAN can find **arbitrarily shaped clusters** (unlike K-Means, which assumes spherical clusters).
- **Complexity summary:** K-Means is $O(N K T d)$ — best for large data. Hierarchical is $O(N^2 \log N)$ — best for small data. DBSCAN is $O(N \log N)$ — best for spatial/anomaly data.
- **Standardization (Z-score scaling) is mandatory** before applying any distance-based clustering algorithm, otherwise features with larger scales will dominate.
- In **IoT applications**, use **K-Means** for regime segmentation, **Hierarchical** for taxonomies of device types, and **DBSCAN** for anomaly and fault detection in sensor streams.
- **In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic carries 14 marks** in the End Semester Exam (Module 4 internal choice) and is also a **Part A** favorite for 3-mark short questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction: PCA, t-SNE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction: PCA, t-SNE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dimensionality Reduction — Formal Definition

**Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a meaningful low-dimensional representation that preserves the most important structural, statistical, or relational properties of the original dataset. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, it is treated as a **feature engineering and unsupervised learning technique** that mitigates the *Curse of Dimensionality*, reduces computational cost, suppresses noise, and enables visualization for IoT analytics (e.g., sensor stream clustering, anomaly visualization, edge-device inference).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECGT525, Module 4):**
> "Dimensionality Reduction: PCA, t-SNE — principle, mathematical formulation, algorithm steps, and IoT use cases."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Google Maps" View of Data

Imagine you are standing in a 3D world (latitude, longitude, altitude). To show it on a phone screen, you project it onto a 2D map. You **lose one dimension**, but the *most informative* features (city layout, road structure) are preserved.

Dimensionality Reduction does the same thing to data:

- A 1000-sensor IoT deployment produces data with **1000 features** per sample.
- A dimensionality reduction algorithm compresses this into, say, **2 or 3 features**, while keeping clusters, anomalies, and class separations visible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Curse of Dimensionality:** As the number of features (dimensions) grows, the data becomes extremely sparse, distances lose meaning, and models overfit. Reduction is a direct countermeasure.

### 1.3 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Definition

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a *linear*, *deterministic*, *unsupervised* dimensionality reduction technique that projects data onto a new orthogonal basis such that the **first principal component (PC1) captures the maximum variance**, the second (PC2) captures the next maximum variance orthogonal to PC1, and so on.

- **Linearity:** PCA finds linear combinations of features.
- **Orthogonality:** All principal components (PCs) are mutually perpendicular.
- **Variance Maximization:** Each PC is oriented to retain as much information (variance) as possible.
- **Deterministic:** Same input → same output every time.

### 1.4 t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) — Definition

**t-SNE** is a *non-linear*, *probabilistic*, *unsupervised* technique developed by **Laurens van der Maaten and Geoffrey Hinton (2008)**. It models pairwise similarities in high-dimensional space using a **Gaussian distribution** and in low-dimensional space using a **Student's t-distribution** (hence *t*-SNE), then minimizes the **Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence** between the two distributions.

- **Non-linearity:** Captures complex, curved manifolds.
- **Local structure preservation:** Excellent for *clustering visualization*.
- **Stochasticity:** Uses random initialization, so results vary across runs (fixed seed for reproducibility).

> [!NOTE]
> **t-SNE vs PCA — High-Level Distinction:**
> - **PCA** preserves **global** structure (variance) and is *linear*.
> - **t-SNE** preserves **local** structure (neighborhood) and is *non-linear*.
> - In IoT, PCA is used for *compression* on the edge; t-SNE is used for *offline visualization* in dashboards.

### 1.5 Why Dimensionality Reduction Matters in IoT

| IoT Challenge | How Dimensionality Reduction Helps |
|---|---|
| High-frequency multi-sensor data | Compresses features before cloud upload |
| Limited edge-device memory | Reduces model input size |
| Anomaly detection | Visualizes high-dim anomalies in 2D/3D |
| Training cost on stream data | Speeds up downstream ML models |
| Noise in sensor readings | PCA discards low-variance noise PCs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PCA — Conceptual Logic Steps

1. **Center the data:** Subtract the mean vector $\mu$ from every sample so the data is zero-mean. This is the **mandatory preprocessing step** for PCA.
2. **Standardize (optional):** Divide each feature by its standard deviation if features are on different scales — critical for IoT sensors with mixed units (°C, Pa, lux).
3. **Compute the Covariance Matrix:** Captures how features co-vary.
4. **Eigendecomposition of the Covariance Matrix:** Extracts eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
5. **Rank Eigenvectors by Eigenvalue:** Eigenvalue magnitude = variance captured.
6. **Project Data:** Multiply centered data by the top-*k* eigenvectors (principal components).
7. **Reconstruct / Analyze:** The projected data is the reduced representation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind each step:**
> - *Mean-centering* ensures PC1 passes through the centroid, making variance the only thing to maximize.
> - *Eigendecomposition* mathematically yields the directions of maximum variance because the covariance matrix is symmetric and positive semi-definite.
> - *Sorting by eigenvalue* orders the new axes from "most informative" to "least informative."

### 2.2 PCA — Mathematical Foundations

Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ be the centered data matrix ($n$ samples, $d$ features).

**Mean vector:**

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$$

**Centered matrix:**

$$\mathbf{X}_{c} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{1}\mu^{T}$$

where $\mathbf{1}$ is an $n \times 1$ column of ones.

**Covariance matrix:**

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_{c}^{T} \mathbf{X}_{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$$

**Eigendecomposition:**

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, d$$

where $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues and $\mathbf{v}_i$ are eigenvectors (orthonormal basis).

**Variance explained by the $i$-th PC:**

$$\text{Var}(PC_i) = \lambda_i$$

**Cumulative variance explained (scree plot basis):**

$$\text{Cumulative}_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$$

**Projection onto top-*k* PCs:**

$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_{c} \mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$$

where $\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k]$.

### 2.3 t-SNE — Conceptual Logic Steps

1. **Compute pairwise similarities in high-dim space** using a Gaussian (conditional probability that $x_j$ would be picked as neighbor of $x_i$):

$$p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\frac{\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^{2}}{2 \sigma_i^{2}}\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lVert x_i - x_k \rVert^{2}}{2 \sigma_i^{2}}\right)}$$

2. **Symmetrize:** $p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2n}$.

3. **Initialize low-dim embeddings** $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 2}$ (usually random or PCA-initialized).

4. **Compute pairwise similarities in low-dim space** using a Student t-distribution (1 degree of freedom → Cauchy):

$$q_{ij} = \frac{\left(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2}\right)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}\left(1 + \lVert y_k - y_l \rVert^{2}\right)^{-1}}$$

5. **Minimize KL divergence** between $P$ and $Q$ using **gradient descent**:

$$C = \text{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$$

6. **Update Y** iteratively with momentum and adaptive learning rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the heavy-tailed t-distribution?** In low dimensions, points tend to clump at the boundary of the embedding space. The t-distribution has heavier tails than a Gaussian, so it gives **moderate distances more probability mass**, which counteracts the crowding problem.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Mean vector | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_{ij}$ | $n$ = samples, $j$ = feature index |
| Centered data | $\mathbf{X}_{c} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{1}\mu^{T}$ | Removes mean |
| Covariance matrix | $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c}$ | $d \times d$ symmetric PSD matrix |
| Eigen-equation | $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | $\lambda$ = variance of PC |
| Proportion of variance | $\text{PoV}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d}\lambda_j}$ | Fraction retained by PC $i$ |
| Reconstruction | $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{W}_k^{T} + \mu$ | Approximate original data |
| Reconstruction error | $\sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i$ | Lost variance |
| High-dim similarity (t-SNE) | $p_{j \mid i} \propto \exp(-\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^{2}/2\sigma_i^{2})$ | Gaussian kernel |
| Perplexity | $\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$ | Effective neighbor count |
| Low-dim similarity (t-SNE) | $q_{ij} \propto (1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2})^{-1}$ | Student t (df=1) |
| KL cost | $C = \sum p_{ij}\log(p_{ij}/q_{ij})$ | Objective minimized |
| t-SNE gradient | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial y_i} = 4\sum_{j}(p_{ij}-q_{ij})(y_i - y_j)(1+\lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2})^{-1}$ | Update direction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconstruction error** is the sum of discarded eigenvalues. This is the central loss metric examiners love to test in PCA numericals.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCA in IoT Edge Devices:** Compresses 128-channel EEG/audio/IMU streams on microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino Nicla Sense ME) before transmission.
- **PCA in Industrial Monitoring:** Identifies the few dominant vibration modes in rotating machinery.
- **t-SNE in IoT Dashboards:** Visualizes 50+ sensor clusters (e.g., smart-city pollution data) on a 2D scatter for operator inspection.
- **t-SNE in Bioinformatics/Healthcare IoT:** Clusters wearable ECG and SpO2 patterns to detect arrhythmias.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 PCA — Derivation of the Direction of Maximum Variance

**Goal:** Find a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ (with $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert = 1$) such that the projected data $\mathbf{X}_{c}\mathbf{w}$ has maximum variance.

**Step 1 — Define the variance of projections.**

The variance of the projected scalar values $\mathbf{X}_{c}\mathbf{w}$ is given by:

$$\text{Var}(\mathbf{X}_{c}\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{n-1}(\mathbf{X}_{c}\mathbf{w})^{T}(\mathbf{X}_{c}\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the covariance matrix definition.**

Since $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c}$, we obtain:

$$\text{Var}(\mathbf{X}_{c}\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3 — Formulate the constrained optimization problem.**

We want to maximize $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}$ subject to the constraint $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} = 1$.

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Apply the method of Lagrange multipliers.**

The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} - 1)$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w}$$

This is precisely the **eigenvalue equation**. The variance is then:

$$\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{T}(\lambda\mathbf{w}) = \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w}) = \lambda$$

So the maximum variance equals the largest eigenvalue, and the direction is its eigenvector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric meaning:** The eigenvectors of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ are the principal axes of the ellipsoidal data cloud, and the eigenvalues are the squared lengths of those axes.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — PCA on a 2D Toy Dataset

Consider 5 centered samples:

$$\mathbf{X}_{c} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad n = 5, \quad d = 2$$

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c}$.**

$$\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Element-wise:

$$\begin{aligned}
(\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c})_{11} &= 2^{2}+(-1)^{2}+0^{2}+1^{2}+(-2)^{2} = 4+1+0+1+4 = 10 \\
(\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c})_{12} &= (2)(1)+(-1)(-2)+(0)(1)+(1)(-1)+(-2)(1) = 2+2+0-1-2 = 1 \\
(\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c})_{22} &= 1^{2}+(-2)^{2}+1^{2}+(-1)^{2}+1^{2} = 1+4+1+1+1 = 8
\end{aligned}$$

So:

$$\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 1 \\ 1 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute covariance matrix** $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{X}_{c}$ with $n-1 = 4$:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 1 \\ 1 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & 2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the characteristic equation** $\det(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda\mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\det\!\begin{pmatrix} 2.5-\lambda & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & 2.0-\lambda \end{pmatrix} &= 0 \\
(2.5-\lambda)(2.0-\lambda) - 0.0625 &= 0 \\
\lambda^{2} - 4.5\lambda + 5.0 - 0.0625 &= 0 \\
\lambda^{2} - 4.5\lambda + 4.9375 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the quadratic using the formula** $\lambda = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{4.5 \pm \sqrt{20.25 - 19.75}}{2} = \frac{4.5 \pm \sqrt{0.5}}{2} \\
\lambda &= \frac{4.5 \pm 0.7071}{2} \\
\lambda_1 &\approx 2.6036, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 1.8964
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Find eigenvector for $\lambda_1$.**

Solve $(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda_1 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_1 = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2.5-2.6036 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & 2.0-2.6036 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1036 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & -0.6036 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}$$

From the first row: $-0.1036 v_{1,1} + 0.25 v_{1,2} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{1,2} = 0.4144 v_{1,1}$.

Normalize:

$$\lVert \mathbf{v}_1 \rVert = \sqrt{v_{1,1}^{2} + (0.4144 v_{1,1})^{2}} = v_{1,1}\sqrt{1.1718} = 1.0825 v_{1,1}$$

Taking $v_{1,1} = 1/1.0825 \approx 0.9238$:

$$\mathbf{v}_1 \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.9238 \\ 0.3828 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute explained variance ratio.**

$$\text{Total variance} = 2.6036 + 1.8964 = 4.5$$

$$\text{PoV}_1 = \frac{2.6036}{4.5} \approx 0.5786 \;(57.86\%)$$

$$\text{PoV}_2 = \frac{1.8964}{4.5} \approx 0.4214 \;(42.14\%)$$

**Step 7 — Reduce to 1D by selecting PC1.**

$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_{c}\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0.9238) + 1(0.3828) \\ -1(0.9238) - 2(0.3828) \\ 0 + 1(0.3828) \\ 1(0.9238) - 1(0.3828) \\ -2(0.9238) + 1(0.3828) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.230 \\ -1.689 \\ 0.383 \\ 0.541 \\ -1.464 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **1D reduced representation** of the original 2D data, capturing **~57.86% of total variance**.

### 3.3 t-SNE — Derivation of the Gradient

**Step 1 — Recap the KL cost:**

$$C = \text{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $q_{ij}$.** Recall:

$$q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2})^{-1}}{Z}, \quad Z = \sum_{k \neq l}(1+\lVert y_k - y_l \rVert^{2})^{-1}$$

Define $d_{ij} = 1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2}$ and note $q_{ij} = d_{ij}^{-1}/Z$.

**Step 3 — Compute partial derivative of $\log q_{ij}$ w.r.t. $y_i$.**

$$\frac{\partial \log q_{ij}}{\partial y_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y_i}\left[-\log d_{ij} - \log Z\right] = -\frac{2(y_i - y_j)}{d_{ij}} - \frac{1}{Z}\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y_i}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\partial Z / \partial y_i$.**

Since $Z = \sum_{k \neq l} d_{kl}^{-1}$ and $y_i$ appears in pairs $(i, k)$ and $(k, i)$:

$$\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y_i} = -2\sum_{k \neq i}\frac{2(y_i - y_k)}{d_{ik}^{2}} = -\sum_{k \neq i}\frac{4(y_i - y_k)}{d_{ik}^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the gradient.**

Using the symmetry of the summand and the fact that $p_{ij}=p_{ji}$:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial y_i} = 4 \sum_{j}\left(p_{ij} - q_{ij}\right)\left(y_i - y_j\right)\left(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2}\right)^{-1}$$

This is the famous **t-SNE gradient** used in the iterative update:

$$y_i^{(t+1)} = y_i^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\partial C}{\partial y_i} + \alpha(t)\left(y_i^{(t)} - y_i^{(t-1)}\right)$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate and $\alpha(t)$ is the momentum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the gradient:**
> - If $p_{ij} > q_{ij}$ (high-dim neighbors are too far in low-dim), the term $(p_{ij} - q_{ij}) > 0$ pulls $y_i$ and $y_j$ **closer**.
> - If $p_{ij} < q_{ij}$ (low-dim are too close), they are **pushed apart**.

### 3.4 Perplexity — The Critical t-SNE Hyperparameter

$$\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}, \quad H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \mid i} \log_2 p_{j \mid i}$$

- Perplexity is set by the user (typical: **5 to 50**).
- It indirectly determines $\sigma_i$ (the bandwidth) for each point.
- **Higher perplexity** → larger $\sigma_i$ → considers more global structure.
- **Lower perplexity** → smaller $\sigma_i$ → focuses on finer local clusters.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** Mixing the formulas. Examiners frequently test whether students know that $p_{ij}$ uses a Gaussian, $q_{ij}$ uses a Student's t, and the optimization is **KL divergence**, not MSE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PCA — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw IoT Data Matrix X n x d] --> B[Mean Centering Xc = X - 1 mu transpose]
    B --> C{Standardize?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Divide by std dev]
    C -->|No| E[Compute Covariance Sigma]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Eigendecomposition Sigma v = lambda v]
    F --> G[Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    G --> H[Select Top k Eigenvectors Wk]
    H --> I[Project Z = Xc Wk]
    I --> J[Reduced Representation n x k]
    J --> K[Downstream Task: Classification Clustering Anomaly Detection]
```

### 4.2 t-SNE — Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Dim Data X] --> B[Compute Pairwise Euclidean Distances]
    B --> C[Set Perplexity Perp]
    C --> D[Binary Search sigma_i for each point]
    D --> E[Compute Conditional Probabilities p j given i Gaussian]
    E --> F[Symmetrize p_ij]
    F --> G[Random Init Low Dim Embedding Y]
    G --> H[Compute Low Dim Similarities q_ij Student t]
    H --> I[Compute KL Divergence Cost]
    I --> J[Backprop Gradient 4 sum p_ij minus q_ij times y_i minus y_j times 1 plus norm squared inverse]
    J --> K[Update Y with Gradient Descent and Momentum]
    K --> L{Converged?}
    L -->|No| H
    L -->|Yes| M[Final 2D or 3D Embedding]
```

### 4.3 PCA vs t-SNE — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PCA_BLOCK[PCA Characteristics]
        P1[Linear Transformation]
        P2[Deterministic]
        P3[Global Variance Preservation]
        P4[O n d squared Eigen Decomposition]
        P5[Output: Full Axis Set Reusable]
    end
    subgraph TSNE_BLOCK[t-SNE Characteristics]
        T1[Non-Linear Manifold Learning]
        T2[Stochastic Random Init]
        T3[Local Neighborhood Preservation]
        T4[O n squared Pairwise Computation]
        T5[Output: Embedding Only No Inverse Map]
    end
    PCA_BLOCK --> DECIDE{Use Case}
    TSNE_BLOCK --> DECIDE
    DECIDE -->|Edge Compression| PCA_OUT[Use PCA on Device]
    DECIDE -->|Dashboard Visualization| TSNE_OUT[Use t-SNE Offline]
```

### 4.4 Scree Plot — Visual Concept

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Eigenvalue Index 1 to d] --> B[Bar Heights = lambda_i]
    B --> C[Sharp Drop = Optimal k]
    C --> D[Keep k PCs before the elbow]
```

### 4.5 Mathematical Symbol Glossary — Quick Reference Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    X[Symbol: Sigma Covariance Matrix] --> XDEF[d x d Symmetric Matrix]
    Y[Symbol: lambda_i Eigenvalue] --> YDEF[Variance Captured by PC i]
    Z[Symbol: v_i Eigenvector] --> ZDEF[Direction of PC i Unit Vector]
    W[Symbol: p_ij] --> WDEF[High Dim Joint Probability]
    Q[Symbol: q_ij] --> QDEF[Low Dim Joint Probability]
    C[Symbol: KL P parallel Q] --> CDEF[Objective Minimized by t-SNE]
    P[Symbol: Perp] --> PDEF[Effective Number of Neighbors]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
**Define Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Explain the role of eigenvalues and eigenvectors in PCA.**

**Model Answer:**

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a linear dimensionality reduction technique that transforms data into a new orthogonal coordinate system such that the greatest variance lies along the first axis (PC1), the second greatest along PC2, and so on.

In PCA, the **eigenvectors** of the covariance matrix define the **directions** (principal axes) of the new coordinate system, while the corresponding **eigenvalues** quantify the **amount of variance** captured by each principal component. The eigenvector associated with the largest eigenvalue is PC1, the second largest is PC2, and so on. Components with very small eigenvalues are often discarded as noise.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for eigenvector role, 1 for eigenvalue role.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**What is t-SNE? Why is a Student's t-distribution used in the low-dimensional space instead of a Gaussian?**

**Model Answer:**

t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) is a non-linear dimensionality reduction technique that models pairwise similarities in high-dimensional and low-dimensional spaces and minimizes the Kullback–Leibler divergence between the two probability distributions to produce a 2D/3D embedding.

A **Student's t-distribution with 1 degree of freedom** (i.e., a Cauchy distribution) is used in the low-dimensional space because it has **heavier tails** than a Gaussian. This is essential to overcome the **crowding problem** — when many moderately distant high-dimensional points are mapped to a low-dimensional space, they tend to clump at the boundary. The heavier tails of the t-distribution assign more probability mass to moderate distances, allowing dissimilar points to be modeled far apart in the embedding.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for t-distribution justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Typical]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step algorithm of PCA. Derive the eigenvector equation using the Lagrangian method. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the centered 2D data $\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the covariance matrix, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and the proportion of variance explained by PC1. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PCA Algorithm & Derivation (7 marks):**

**Algorithm steps:**

1. Compute the mean of each feature: $\mu_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i x_{ij}$.
2. Center the data: $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{1}\mu^T$.
3. Compute covariance: $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$.
4. Solve $\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$.
5. Sort eigenvalues in descending order; select top $k$.
6. Project: $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}_k$.

**Derivation (Lagrangian method):**

We want to maximize variance $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{w} = 1$.

Form the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} - 1)$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w}$$

This is the eigenvector equation. The variance in direction $\mathbf{w}$ is $\lambda$.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(\mathbf{X}_c^T\mathbf{X}_c)_{11} &= 1^2 + 2^2 + (-1)^2 + (-2)^2 = 1+4+1+4 = 10 \\
(\mathbf{X}_c^T\mathbf{X}_c)_{12} &= (1)(2) + (2)(1) + (-1)(-2) + (-2)(-1) = 2+2+2+2 = 8 \\
(\mathbf{X}_c^T\mathbf{X}_c)_{22} &= 2^2 + 1^2 + (-2)^2 + (-1)^2 = 4+1+4+1 = 10
\end{aligned}$$

So:

$$\mathbf{X}_c^T\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Covariance matrix** with $n-1 = 3$:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/3 & 8/3 \\ 8/3 & 10/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic equation:**

$$\det(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda\mathbf{I}) = (10/3 - \lambda)^2 - (8/3)^2 = 0$$

$$(10/3 - \lambda)^2 = 64/9 \;\Rightarrow\; 10/3 - \lambda = \pm 8/3$$

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{10-8}{3} = \frac{2}{3}, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{10+8}{3} = 6$$

Wait — re-ordering in descending order: $\lambda_1 = 6$, $\lambda_2 = 2/3$.

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 6$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 10/3 - 6 & 8/3 \\ 8/3 & 10/3 - 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8/3 & 8/3 \\ 8/3 & -8/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $-\frac{8}{3}v_1 + \frac{8}{3}v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2$. Normalized:

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2/3$:**

Similarly, $v_1 = -v_2$:

$$\mathbf{v}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Proportion of variance:**

$$\text{Total variance} = 6 + 2/3 = 20/3$$

$$\text{PoV}_1 = \frac{6}{20/3} = \frac{18}{20} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

So PC1 alone captures **90%** of the variance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Typical]**

**(a)** Explain the t-SNE algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of perplexity and the KL divergence objective. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between PCA and t-SNE in terms of linearity, objective function, complexity, scalability, and IoT use cases. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) t-SNE Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — High-dim similarities.** For each point $x_i$, compute conditional probabilities $p_{j \mid i}$ using a Gaussian kernel centered at $x_i$ with variance $\sigma_i^2$ (tuned via perplexity). This represents the probability that $x_i$ would pick $x_j$ as its neighbor.

**Step 2 — Symmetrization.** Convert conditional probabilities to joint probabilities:

$$p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2n}$$

**Step 3 — Initialize low-dim embeddings** $\mathbf{Y}$ randomly (often PCA-init for stability).

**Step 4 — Low-dim similarities** using Student t-distribution (df=1):

$$q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2})^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \lVert y_k - y_l \rVert^{2})^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — KL divergence objective:**

$$C = \text{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$$

**Step 6 — Gradient descent** to update $\mathbf{Y}$ with the gradient:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial y_i} = 4\sum_{j}(p_{ij} - q_{ij})(y_i - y_j)(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2})^{-1}$$

plus momentum term.

**Role of perplexity:** Perplexity $\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$ is a smooth measure of the effective number of neighbors. It controls $\sigma_i$ via binary search. Typical values: 5–50. Higher perplexity → more global focus; lower → more local.

**Role of KL divergence:** KL is asymmetric; it heavily penalizes placing similar (high $p_{ij}$) points far apart in low-dim, but tolerates placing dissimilar points close. This is exactly the local-structure-preservation behavior we want.

**(b) PCA vs t-SNE Comparison (7 marks):**

| Criterion | PCA | t-SNE |
|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | Linear projection | Non-linear manifold learning |
| **Objective** | Maximize variance | Minimize KL divergence |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic (same input → same output) | Stochastic (random init) |
| **Preserves** | Global variance | Local neighborhood structure |
| **Computational complexity** | $O(d^3)$ for eigendecomposition | $O(n^2)$ pairwise |
| **Scalability** | Scales to $10^6+$ samples | Limited to $\sim 10^4$ samples |
| **Inverse transform** | Yes: $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{W}_k^T + \mu$ | No inverse mapping |
| **IoT use case** | Edge compression, feature extraction | Dashboard visualization, offline analytics |
| **Output reuse** | Reusable for new samples | Embedding must be recomputed |
| **Interpretability** | Components are linear combos of original features | Embedding axes have no direct meaning |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Examiner Insights:**
> 1. **Forgetting mean-centering** before computing the covariance matrix. KTU examiners explicitly deduct for this.
> 2. **Using $n$ instead of $n-1$** in the denominator of the covariance formula (Bessel's correction). This is a 1-mark penalty trap.
> 3. **Confusing $p_{ij}$ and $q_{ij}$**: $p_{ij}$ uses a **Gaussian**; $q_{ij}$ uses a **Student-t**. Mixing them up costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to symmetrize** $p_{j \mid i}$ into $p_{ij}$ in t-SNE.
> 5. **Saying t-SNE is deterministic** — it is stochastic, fix the random seed for reproducibility.
> 6. **Claiming t-SNE preserves global distances** — it does not; only local neighborhoods.
> 7. **In numericals, writing eigenvectors without normalizing** them to unit length.
> 8. **Saying PCA is a "feature selection" technique** — it is **feature extraction / transformation**, not selection.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Dimensionality Reduction**

- **PCA = Linear, Deterministic, Variance-Maximizing.** Uses eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix.
- **Mandatory pre-step:** Mean-centering (subtract feature means).
- **Optional pre-step:** Standardization (divide by std dev) when features are on different scales.
- **Eigenvalues = variance of PCs.** Always sort in descending order.
- **Proportion of variance:** $\text{PoV}_i = \lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$. Use a **scree plot** to choose $k$.
- **Reconstruction error** = sum of discarded eigenvalues.
- **PCA has an inverse transform** $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{W}_k^T + \mu$ → reusable on new IoT samples.
- **t-SNE = Non-linear, Stochastic, Local-NL-preserving.** Uses KL divergence minimization with gradient descent.
- **$p_{ij}$ uses Gaussian** (high-dim); **$q_{ij}$ uses Student-t with df=1** (low-dim).
- **Perplexity** controls the effective number of neighbors (typical: 5–50). Tune via binary search on $\sigma_i$.
- **Crowding problem** is solved by the heavy-tailed t-distribution in the low-dim space.
- **t-SNE has no inverse transform** — embedding must be recomputed when new data arrives (limitation for streaming IoT).
- **Computational cost:** PCA $O(d^3)$; t-SNE $O(n^2)$ — use Barnes-Hut t-SNE for $n > 5000$.
- **PCA use cases in IoT:** sensor fusion compression, feature engineering on the edge, denoising vibration/temperature signals.
- **t-SNE use cases in IoT:** anomaly cluster visualization, exploratory data analysis on cloud dashboards, post-hoc model interpretation.
- **Common alternatives:** Kernel PCA, LDA (supervised), UMAP (faster than t-SNE), Autoencoders (deep non-linear).
- **For the exam:** Always derive the eigenvector equation via Lagrange multipliers if asked, and always normalize the final eigenvector.
- **Examiner loves numericals:** Practice at least one 2D PCA computation and one t-SNE algorithmic walkthrough before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Evaluation, Validation, and Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT525 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Evaluation, Validation, and Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Evaluation, Validation, and Optimization** is the systematic process of assessing the performance, generalizability, and predictive reliability of a trained machine learning model using quantitative metrics, statistical resampling techniques, and hyperparameter tuning methodologies. In the context of IoT applications, this triad ensures that models deployed on resource-constrained edge devices maintain high accuracy, low latency, and robustness against noisy sensor data.

The three pillars can be formally defined as:

- **Model Evaluation**: The use of quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, RMSE) computed on a held-out test set to measure how well a trained model generalizes to unseen data.
- **Model Validation**: The application of resampling techniques such as k-Fold Cross-Validation, Stratified k-Fold, and Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) to estimate the model's performance on unseen data while mitigating overfitting.
- **Model Optimization**: The process of systematically searching for the optimal combination of hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, regularization strength, tree depth) using methods like **Grid Search**, **Random Search**, and **Bayesian Optimization** to maximize performance.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Driving Test" for ML Models**
>
> Imagine you are a driving instructor who has taught a student (your ML model) to drive.
>
> 1. **Model Evaluation** is the **final road test** — you measure how well the student drives on a NEW route they have never seen (the *test set*) using a scoring rubric (accuracy, F1, RMSE).
> 2. **Model Validation** is **mock tests during training** — you repeatedly test the student on different practice routes (cross-validation folds) to ensure they aren't just memorizing one specific path (overfitting).
> 3. **Model Optimization** is **fine-tuning the car** — adjusting seat position, mirror angles, and tire pressure (hyperparameters) to find the smoothest ride before the final test.
>
> Without all three, the student might pass the test but crash on the highway — just like a model that scores 99% on training data but fails on real IoT sensor streams.

### Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following bold metrics are **mandatory** for KTU board examinations:

- **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$
- **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Recall (Sensitivity)** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-Score** = $2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$
- **ROC-AUC** = Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve
- **MSE** = $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
- **RMSE** = $\sqrt{MSE}$
- **MAE** = $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$

Where **TP** = True Positive, **TN** = True Negative, **FP** = False Positive, **FN** = False Negative.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of Classification Metrics

The **Confusion Matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ (binary) or $N \times N$ (multi-class) tabular representation comparing predicted labels against actual labels.

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in IoT**: In anomaly detection for IoT sensor networks (e.g., detecting faulty temperature sensors), a False Negative (missing a real fault) can be far more costly than a False Positive. Hence, **Recall** is often prioritized over Accuracy.

### 2.2 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff

Every model's prediction error can be decomposed as:

$$\text{Total Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$

- **Bias**: Error from wrong assumptions (underfitting). Model is too simple.
- **Variance**: Error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations (overfitting). Model is too complex.
- **Irreducible Noise**: Inherent randomness in data that no model can eliminate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Implication**: IoT data is often noisy due to sensor drift, interference, and packet loss. A high-variance model will memorize this noise, leading to catastrophic failure when deployed in a new environment. **Cross-validation** is the primary defense against variance.

### 2.3 Cross-Validation Methodology

**k-Fold Cross-Validation** splits the dataset into $k$ equal partitions. The model is trained on $k-1$ folds and tested on the remaining 1 fold, repeating $k$ times.

$$\text{CV Score} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} M_i$$

where $M_i$ is the metric (e.g., accuracy) on the $i$-th test fold.

**Special cases**:
- **Leave-One-Out CV (LOOCV)**: $k = n$ (each fold is one sample). Computationally expensive.
- **Stratified k-Fold**: Preserves the class distribution in each fold (critical for imbalanced IoT datasets like fault detection).

### 2.4 Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies

| Method | Search Space | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grid Search** | Exhaustive over discrete grid | Finds true optimum in grid | Exponential cost; "curse of dimensionality" |
| **Random Search** | Random samples from distributions | Better in high-dim spaces than Grid | No guarantee of optimum |
| **Bayesian Optimization** | Probabilistic model (Gaussian Process) | Sample-efficient; converges fast | Complex to implement |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Accuracy | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Recall | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | F1-Score | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 5 | Specificity | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True Negative Rate |
| 6 | MSE | $\frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Squared units of $y$ |
| 7 | RMSE | $\sqrt{MSE}$ | Same units as $y$ |
| 8 | MAE | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same units as $y$ |
| 9 | R² Score | $1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Coefficient of determination |
| 10 | Bias-Variance | $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Decomposition |
| 11 | CV Score | $\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} M_i$ | Mean over $k$ folds |
| 12 | Log Loss | $-\frac{1}{n}\sum [y \log(p) + (1-y)\log(1-p)]$ | Penalizes confident wrong predictions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility in IoT Engineering**:
> - **Smart Agriculture**: F1-score is used to evaluate crop disease classification from sensor + image data.
> - **Predictive Maintenance**: RMSE is used to evaluate remaining-useful-life (RUL) prediction models for industrial motors.
> - **Healthcare IoT**: Recall is paramount for wearable ECG anomaly detection — missing a heart attack prediction is unacceptable.
> - **Smart Grid**: ROC-AUC is used to evaluate electricity theft detection classifiers.

### 2.6 Learning Curves and Validation Curves

- **Learning Curve**: Plots training score vs. cross-validation score as a function of training set size. Diagnoses **high bias** (both curves converge to low score) vs. **high variance** (large gap between curves).
- **Validation Curve**: Plots training score vs. validation score as a function of a single hyperparameter. Helps identify overfitting/underfitting regions.

### 2.7 Regularization for Optimization

To control variance, regularization adds a penalty term to the loss function:

$$J(\theta) = L(\theta) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)$$

- **L1 (Lasso)**: $\Omega(\theta) = \sum \vert \theta_i \vert$ — induces sparsity (feature selection)
- **L2 (Ridge)**: $\Omega(\theta) = \sum \theta_i^2$ — shrinks coefficients smoothly
- **Elastic Net**: Combines L1 + L2

Where $\lambda$ is the **regularization strength** — a critical hyperparameter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the F1-Score from Precision and Recall

The F1-score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision (P) and Recall (R), which (unlike the arithmetic mean) penalizes extreme imbalances between P and R.

**Step 1: Definition of Harmonic Mean**

The harmonic mean of two positive numbers $a$ and $b$ is:

$$H = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $a = P$ and $b = R$**

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 3: Find common denominator**

The denominators $\frac{1}{P}$ and $\frac{1}{R}$ combine over the common denominator $PR$:

$$\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R} = \frac{R + P}{PR}$$

**Step 4: Invert the fraction**

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{P + R}{PR}} = \frac{2 \cdot PR}{P + R}$$

**Step 5: Final KTU-standard form**

$$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}}$$

**Step 6: Why Harmonic Mean and not Arithmetic Mean?**
Suppose $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.01$ (a model that predicts one perfect positive but misses 99% of others).
- Arithmetic mean: $(1.0 + 0.01)/2 = 0.505$ (misleadingly high)
- Harmonic mean: $F_1 = 2(1.0)(0.01)/(1.01) \approx 0.0198$ (correctly punitive)

This is why **F1-score is preferred over simple accuracy for imbalanced IoT datasets**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Bias-Variance Decomposition (Squared Loss)

For a regression model with squared error loss, the expected prediction error at point $x$ decomposes as:

**Step 1: Define the expected squared error**

$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2]$$

**Step 2: Add and subtract the expected prediction $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$**

$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})^2]$$

**Step 3: Expand using $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ where $a = y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]$ and $b = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}$**

$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2] + 2\mathbb{E}[(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})] + \mathbb{E}[(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})^2]$$

**Step 4: The cross-term vanishes** because $y$ is independent of $\hat{f}$ and $\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}] = 0$:

$$= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2]}_{\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Irreducible Noise}} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})^2]}_{\text{Variance}}$$

**Step 5: Further decompose the first term** as $(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - f_{true})^2 + \mathbb{E}[(y - f_{true})^2]$:

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \underbrace{(f_{true} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\hat{f} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Noise}}}$$

This is the **fundamental bias-variance tradeoff**: reducing bias typically increases variance and vice-versa.

### 3.3 Derivation of k-Fold Cross-Validation Estimate

Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ split into $k$ equal folds $\mathcal{D}_1, \mathcal{D}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{D}_k$:

**Step 1: For fold $j$**, train on $\mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{D}_j$ and evaluate on $\mathcal{D}_j$:

$$M_j = \text{Metric}(\text{Model}_j, \mathcal{D}_j)$$

**Step 2: Repeat for all $k$ folds**, yielding $k$ scores $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_k$.

**Step 3: The CV estimate is the arithmetic mean**:

$$\hat{\mu}_{CV} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} M_j$$

**Step 4: The standard error** of the estimate (used for confidence intervals):

$$\hat{\sigma}_{CV} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1}\sum_{j=1}^{k}(M_j - \hat{\mu}_{CV})^2}$$

**Step 5: 95% Confidence Interval**:

$$\text{CI}_{95\%} = \hat{\mu}_{CV} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{\hat{\sigma}_{CV}}{\sqrt{k}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Metric

| Problem Type | Imbalance | Recommended Metric | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| IoT Anomaly Detection | High (1% anomalies) | **Recall, F1, PR-AUC** | Accuracy is misleading; missing anomalies is costly |
| Energy Load Forecasting | Low (continuous) | **RMSE, MAE, R²** | Penalizes large errors (grid stability) |
| Multi-class Sensor Classification | Moderate | **Macro-F1, Cohen's Kappa** | Treats all classes equally |
| Binary Health Monitoring | High (rare events) | **MCC, Balanced Accuracy** | Robust to imbalance |
| Regression on Sensor Drift | Low | **MAE** | Robust to outliers from sensor glitches |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem**: A binary classifier for IoT intrusion detection produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 1000 samples:
- TP = 80, FP = 20, FN = 40, TN = 860

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Accuracy**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{80 + 860}{80 + 860 + 20 + 40} = \frac{940}{1000} = 0.94$$

**Step 2: Compute Precision**

$$P = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 3: Compute Recall**

$$R = \frac{80}{80 + 40} = \frac{80}{120} \approx 0.6667$$

**Step 4: Compute F1-Score**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.6667}{0.80 + 0.6667} = \frac{1.0667}{1.4667} \approx 0.7273$$

**Step 5: Interpretation for IoT Security**
- High accuracy (94%) is misleading — it just reflects class imbalance.
- Recall of 66.67% means we miss 1 in 3 intrusions — **unacceptable for critical infrastructure**.
- The model should be retrained with class weights or resampling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End ML Model Evaluation and Optimization Pipeline (IoT Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw IoT Sensor Data] --> B[Data Cleaning and Preprocessing]

    C --> D[Train Validation Test Split]

    D --> F[Validation Set]
    D --> G[Test Set]
    E --> H[Model Training]
    H --> I[Hyperparameter Space Definition]
    I --> J{Optimization Strategy}
    J --> K[Grid Search]
    J --> L[Random Search]
    J --> M[Bayesian Optimization]
    K --> N[Best Hyperparameters]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Retrain Model with Best Params]
    F --> P[K-Fold Cross Validation]
    P --> Q[Compute CV Score and Std Dev]
    Q --> R{Model Meets Target Metric}
    R -->|No| S[Adjust Features or Algorithm]
    S --> H
    R -->|Yes| T[Final Evaluation on Test Set]
    T --> U[Compute Accuracy F1 RMSE AUC]
    G --> T
    U --> V{Acceptance Threshold Met}

    V -->|No| X[Trigger Model Retraining Loop]
    X --> H
```

### 4.2 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Visualization

```mermaid

    B --> C[Underfitting]

    P --> I
```

### 4.3 K-Fold Cross-Validation Data Partitioning Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complete Dataset N samples] --> B[Shuffle and Partition]
    B --> C1[Fold 1 Test]
    B --> C2[Fold 2 Test]
    B --> C3[Fold 3 Test]
    B --> C4[Fold 4 Test]
    B --> C5[Fold 5 Test]
    C1 --> D1[Train on Folds 2 3 4 5]
    C2 --> D2[Train on Folds 1 3 4 5]
    C3 --> D3[Train on Folds 1 2 4 5]
    C4 --> D4[Train on Folds 1 2 3 5]
    C5 --> D5[Train on Folds 1 2 3 4]
    D1 --> E1[Score M1]
    D2 --> E2[Score M2]
    D3 --> E3[Score M3]
    D4 --> E4[Score M4]
    D5 --> E5[Score M5]

    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F

```

### 4.4 Confusion Matrix to Metric Derivation Flow

```mermaid

    A[Predictions and Ground Truth] --> B[Build Confusion Matrix]
    B --> C[Extract TP TN FP FN]
    C --> D[Compute Accuracy]

    C --> F[Compute Recall]
    C --> G[Compute Specificity]
    E --> H[Compute F1 Score]
    F --> H
    D --> I[Threshold Adjustment]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    I --> J[Plot ROC Curve]
    I --> K[Plot Precision Recall Curve]
```
